```
Grinder:
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows printing from COBOL

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2002-05-01T19:29:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3cd03edf.23965755@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<3ccc8231_2@corp-news.newsgroups.com> <3ccd46a0.1829167@news.epix.net> <aak1ou0290f@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```
"Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com> wrote:
[snip]

Grinder:

I have a few answers to your questions:

>A suggestion that might be easier to implement on FormPrint's side, but
>would be a big improvement -- some way to go to a specific page, rather than
>having to sequentially move through each one.  (A textbox that you type a
>page number into would be low-tech, but would get the job done.)

Allowing the end user to go to a specific page is on the enhancement
list, but it won't be in the next version.

The enhancements to the print preview function concern remaining in
preview and printing.  Otherwise, recent other enhancements cover
everything else.  Here are the recent enhancements:

Recent enhancements:

1.  Allow printer selection after preview
2.  Return more printer specific incormation - Physical margins,
resolution and the like.
3.  Automatic get all pages (Option)
4.  Automatic maximize preview (option)
5.  Progress indication (retrieveing page xxx, printing page xxx etc).

Coming soon!

Print from preview and don't exit preview. Includes print current page
and/or range of pages.

>By the time the print preview is open, is that back-end data already
>paginated?

No.  Only the first page need be generated to begin the preview - the
remaining pages are either generated during the preview as you page,
or if a "get-all-pages" is done - it's another thread while you
preview.

Hope that this answered your questions.



Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows printing from COBOL

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-02T20:37:57-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aaspls09tq@enews4.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<3ccc8231_2@corp-news.newsgroups.com> <3ccd46a0.1829167@news.epix.net> <aak1ou0290f@enews4.newsguy.com> <3cd03edf.23965755@news.epix.net>`

```

"Bob Wolfe" <rtwolfe@flexus.com> wrote in message
news:3cd03edf.23965755@news.epix.net...
> "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com> wrote:
> [snip]
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> >would be a big improvement -- some way to go to a specific page, rather
than
> >having to sequentially move through each one.  (A textbox that you type a
> >page number into would be low-tech, but would get the job done.)
>
> Allowing the end user to go to a specific page is on the enhancement
> list, but it won't be in the next version.

I'm curious -- from the tech side, there can be a good reason why this
shouldn't be implemented.  Unless you can determine exactly what goes on
each page, without paginating, you'll suffer a performance hits trying to
jump to any page.

Word wrapped text is a good example.  If you try to jump to page 1000, you
essentially have to format pages 1 to 999 before you'll know where page 1000
starts.  Does FormPrint have any way of randomly accessing the report data?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows printing from COBOL

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-03T08:26:45-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ojvA8.18325$1k5.2707093@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<3ccc8231_2@corp-news.newsgroups.com> <3ccd46a0.1829167@news.epix.net> <aak1ou0290f@enews4.newsguy.com> <3cd03edf.23965755@news.epix.net> <aaspls09tq@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```
"Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com> wrote in message
news:aaspls09tq@enews4.newsguy.com...
>
> "Bob Wolfe" <rtwolfe@flexus.com> wrote in message
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> > >having to sequentially move through each one.  (A textbox that you type
a
> > >page number into would be low-tech, but would get the job done.)
> >
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> essentially have to format pages 1 to 999 before you'll know where page
1000
> starts.  Does FormPrint have any way of randomly accessing the report
data?
>
>
>

Let me get this straight.  It takes more time to format a report onto a
screen to read it than to format it onto paper to read it, therefore it is
inefficent to format it onto the screen first, as if you do not print it,
then you will have wasted the computer time.  Is that the "performance hit"
you are refering too?

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows printing from COBOL

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-04T22:30:27-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ab290p0bd5@enews3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<3ccc8231_2@corp-news.newsgroups.com> <3ccd46a0.1829167@news.epix.net> <aak1ou0290f@enews4.newsguy.com> <3cd03edf.23965755@news.epix.net> <aaspls09tq@enews4.newsguy.com> <ojvA8.18325$1k5.2707093@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```

> Let me get this straight.  It takes more time to format a report onto a
> screen to read it than to format it onto paper to read it, therefore it is
> inefficent to format it onto the screen first, as if you do not print it,
> then you will have wasted the computer time.  Is that the "performance
hit"
> you are refering too?

Nope, it's a question of whether or not your report information is
pre-paginated.  Consider two grids: one with fixed row heights, and another
with variable row heights.  In the fixed example I can easily tell you what
page row 10000 is on, by dividing the number of antecedant rows by the
number of rows that fit on a page.

For the variable row height case, I would have to scan through each row
(looking at its height) to determine what page the 10000th row will be on.
This example does not suggest a great difference in performance, but as you
add complexities: word wrap, embedded images, widow control, etc, it gets
more and more profound.
```

```
I think maybe you've missed the antecedant messages in this thread.  I was
just curious if a jump to page function was not built into PrintForm's print
preview because of a technical limitation.  Lots of report writers have this
"problem" because of taking a virtual view on the data.  (It's not so much a
problem as a trade-off.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows printing from COBOL

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "JJ" <jj@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-01T02:06:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s4Iz8.4523$v7.488988@bin6.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3ccc8231_2@corp-news.newsgroups.com> <3ccd46a0.1829167@news.epix.net> <2255982.0204291339.3a61ed9c@posting.google.com> <3cce28d4_1@corp-news.newsgroups.com>`

```
You can take a look at VSVIEW from ComponentOne (which bought Videosoft, the
original author of VSVIEW).

If you can directly use an OCX control, you can use it for printing/print
preview.  If you are interested in PDF output (that's what we use it for),
it has the ability write PDF files and has a vsPDF control buried in it.  I
say "buried" because it is not well documented - I had to download a sample
that used it from their web site to get the docs.   But it works and is
royalty free.

I use it to convert Cobol reports to PDF, with a little fancy formatting.
The Cobol program writes the report to an ASCII file, then I run it through
a VB program (using the vsPDF control).  It pulls out the headings and makes
them "pretty" and writes the whole thing to a PDF file, then starts Acrobat
and views it.

"Newsgroups" <thepla@mail.com> wrote in message
news:3cce28d4_1@corp-news.newsgroups.com...
> I think that I looked at this program also, is it the same as TXT2PDF? The
> problem is that all users that I send the software out to need a license.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows printing from COBOL

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Mike Kinasz" <mkinasz@cbspayroll.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-01T05:53:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Another is http://www.visual.co.uk
Their PCL2PDF product is not cheap but it's excellent.  Our largest Cobol
program outputs very nice PCL reports and this has absolutely no trouble
converting them to PDF........ we use both the command line version and the
windows dlls.   Then we do the same thing JJ does when we're operating in
Windows..... we spawn off Acrobat Reader and everyone's happy......

We even use their OCX on a couple of our web servers (iis) as well to do on
demand stuff for web users......

(no I don't work for them...  <g>)
Mike Kinasz
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows printing from COBOL

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** jmiedreich@sbcglobal.net (Jeremy Miedreich)
- **Date:** 2002-05-01T22:58:35-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<755320fc.0205012158.291ab2d5@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3ccc8231_2@corp-news.newsgroups.com> <3ccd46a0.1829167@news.epix.net> <2255982.0204291339.3a61ed9c@posting.google.com> <3cce28d4_1@corp-news.newsgroups.com> <s4Iz8.4523$v7.488988@bin6.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
Another possibliy is to write out your data in postscript, which is
very simple.  Then use a utilie called Ghostscript, there is a
gsview.exe which allows you to printer the postcript to all printers, 
not just postscript enabled printers, plus you could launch the
application to have a preview if you liked.  I use it for all my
applications that run on PC's and it does work fine.  But there are
other updated technologies from the COBOL vendors that can make life
easier, but they will also put a drain on your pocket.  General
postsript is easier to learn, basically you just tell it to print at x
and y cords, draw lines...etc.
```

```
> On Mon, 29 Apr 2002 21:33:28 UTC, "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com>
> wrote:
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> or by both said punishments, in the discretion of the court."

This does have a form of the word "monopoly" in it's description.  Take a
look at Penfield's finding of fact in the case -- the grievances are
entirely couched in terms of their anti-competitive practices.  There's a
lot of legal lingo to cover, but a monopoly in of itself is not illegal, as
there may be legitimate "barriers to entry."

One example of this would be those switches used in atomic bombs.  GE took
fire from lefties for being the only manufacturer of this essential
component to weapons of mass destruction.  I doubt GE was ever at risk of
litagation under the Sherman Act, it's just that they were in a position
(that no others were) to make them.

I wouldn't claim that Microsoft is in the same position with Windows, but
would still maintain it's they're practices, not their girth, that's put
them in hot water.  As an incidental note, I think they're guilty as hell.
```

```
"Mark Dodel" <madodel@ptdprolog.net> wrote in message
news:yRNWO1cJ1gUw-pn2-OnHd6uFpdP4R@localhost...
> On Wed, 1 May 2002 16:38:24 UTC, "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
> wrote:
>
> -)
> -)This does have a form of the word "monopoly" in it's description.  Take
a
> -)look at Penfield's finding of fact in the case -- the grievances are
> -)entirely couched in terms of their anti-competitive practices.  There's
a
> -)lot of legal lingo to cover, but a monopoly in of itself is not illegal,
as
> -)there may be legitimate "barriers to entry."
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Let me type slowly so even the dim will understand: Monopolies are not
illegal.

A quote in a previous post:

"15 U.S.C. 2: Monopolizing trade a felony; penalty

"Every person who shall monopolize, or attempt to monopolize,
or combine or conspire with any other person or persons,
to monopolize any part of the trade or commerce among the
several States, or with foreign nations, shall be deemed guilty
of a felony, and, on conviction thereof, shall be punished by
fine not exceeding $10,000,000 if a corporation, or, if any other
person, $350,000, or by imprisonment not exceeding three years,
or by both said punishments, in the discretion of the court."

A 'monopoly' is a condition of status. A monopoly cannot be illegal or
legal: it just is.

The Act cited pertains to 'actions' not status. Further, only certain
actions tending toward a monopoly are illegal, others not. For example, the
Constitution protects certain acts in furtherance of a monopoly (trademarks
and patents). It is not the end result (monopoly) that determines whether
someone violated of the Sherman Act - the violation occurs totally based on
the path taken.

Let me say it again: whether the actions in question lead to a monopoly is
irrelevant; the main determinant is whether the actions were designed and
carried out with eventual monopoly status as a goal. Of course, if a
monopoly results from the actions, a reasonable person may presume intent.
BUT, if it can be shown that a company reached it's monopoly status by legal
means (such as internal growth or the protected method of patent), the
company is blameless. There is no crime in just BEING a monopoly.

In Microsoft's situation, since they already WERE a monopoly, and since
there's no penalty for acting to PRESERVE a monopoly position, the case can
be made there was no foul.
```

```
I forget why I thought this, but I think there was some evidence that
IBM wanted OS/2 to be good, but not so good that it would eat
into their mainframe revenues. In summary, they couldn't make up
their minds as to where they wanted the product to go. Microsoft,
on the other hand, didn't have any ambivalence about what the future
of Windows should be.

And I think I heard once that IBM thought it was a good thing
that some people thought you had to have an IBM PS/2 PC
in order to run OS/2.
```

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### COBOL FAQ

- **From:** wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
- **Date:** 2002-05-01T09:35:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,news.answers,comp.answers,alt.answers
- **Message-ID:** `<computer-lang/cobol-faq_1020245719@rtfm.mit.edu>`

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2000/07/02
Version: 3.01
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Sunday, July 2, 2000

The COBOL FAQ has moved.  To view the most recent version, please check out the
web version at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send private
email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

The following is Appendix B.5 - Changes to create Version 3.01

 o Recorded new location of the FAQ
 o Updated some old (bad) web links
 o Added some new COBOL books (particularly those written or contributed to - by
frequent comp.lang.cobol submitters).
 o Added information about LegacyJ (PERCobol) and changed the company name from
"Synkronix" to "LegacyJ".
 o Updated information for changes in product and company names and addresses
where available
 o Updated the URLs for downloading the draft of the next COBOL Standard and
CobCy
 o Started the Samples/Example Section
 o Tried to get rid of most specific references to Windows/NT, Windows/95,
Windows/98, and Windows/2000.
 o Added links to Xinotech
 o Added information on Norcom and GUI ScreenIO
 o Added (back) information on Wang
 o Added (back) information on the Tiny-COBOL ('74 Standard) project
```

<a id="t-69b301851e"></a>
## Thread: How to print a GIF or TIF logo in a COBOL report? — 7 this month / 9 total

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

_Thread started [2002-04](2002-04.md#t-69b301851e)._

#### ↳ Re: How to print a GIF or TIF logo in a COBOL report?

- **From:** "Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-01T11:13:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<75Qz8.3684$1j1.226268184@newssvr16.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<3ccf0f99$0$3721$39cecf19@news.twtelecom.net>`

```
Back in the days when I was using Micro Focus COBOL, I had to do something
similar.  Please keep in mind that there may have been a better way to do
this. But being a novice in the language, not doing exactly how to do it,
and having to meet a deadline,  I did the following:

1.  Dug out a HP Laserjet PCL book to get the printer control codes that I
would need for my report.
2.  Imported my graphic into MS Word.
3.  From Word, saved the graphic as mypict.prn, making sure that the printer
was set to the printer I needed for my report.  In my case, the graphic was
at the beginning of the report.
4.  Used the MF COBOL call (x'91'?)
        MOVE PRT-ID TO COPY-LJDSK1-NAME.   (PRT-ID would be myreport.prn or
whatever)
        DISPLAY COPY-LJHDG1-DSK UPON COMMAND-LINE.
        CALL X"91" USING RESULT, FUNC, COMMAND-LIN.
    WS looked like:
        01  COPY-LJHDG1-DSK.
              03  FILLER           PIC X(19)
                    VALUE "COPY /B MYPICT.PRN ".
              03  COPY-LJDSK-NAME  PIC X(12).
           03  FILLER           PIC X(06) VALUE " > NUL".
5.  Opened PRT-ID Extend and continued with the report output.

Not exactly pretty code, but it worked quite well.  Makes me really thankful
for Fujitsu Powerform now, though.

Denny

"Bookie" <bobsmith@netnet.net> wrote in message
news:3ccf0f99$0$3721$39cecf19@news.twtelecom.net...
> I need to print a small logo at a specific location in a report that I
> produce with a COBOL program.   I have the logo in a GIF format and also
in
> a TIF format.   I need to know how to write the logic to print this logo
in
> the report.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
meaning of which I did not overall understand I must admit)

Dieter
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to print a GIF or TIF logo in a COBOL report?

- **From:** "Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-02T03:13:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<I92A8.4257$3w5.254716138@newssvr16.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<3ccf0f99$0$3721$39cecf19@news.twtelecom.net> <75Qz8.3684$1j1.226268184@newssvr16.news.prodigy.com> <3CD032BE.A1E76092@shaw.ca>`

```
Jimmy,

Ah yes, I didn't care for using PCL at the time either.  And the project was
all for an employee evaluation program someone from HR had designed.  It was
to be typed on a form that had no definable line spacing.  I.E., if you
typed it on a typewriter (I told you this was a long time ago, HA), you
couldn't just hit return and line up with the next line on our "preprinted"
evaluation forms.  The Micro Focus compiler I was using at the time had just
been upgraded from the Microsoft (i.e. Micro Focus in disguise) product.
Since my expertise at the time was in Burroughs/Unisys mainframe COBOL,
needless to say, this was "one step beyond" for me.  I didn't know Windows
OLE, API or whatever, but I did understand my supervisor's PDQ.  I'm glad I
stuck with it though, since I'm still actively employed as both a
mainframe/PC COBOL programmer.  I currently have programs that interface
with Lotus 123, Excel, Access and Winfax Pro, but back then, I was one lost
puppy!

Denny
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How to print a GIF or TIF logo in a COBOL report?

- **From:** jmiedreich@sbcglobal.net (Jeremy Miedreich)
- **Date:** 2002-05-01T22:52:11-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<755320fc.0205012152.66a9ef98@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3ccf0f99$0$3721$39cecf19@news.twtelecom.net> <3cd07cd2.1718340@enews.newsguy.com>`

```
This kind of only applies if you have a postscript printer.  One of
the ways I do lasered reports is to write out the postscript from the
cobol program.  As pertaining to the logo, you could use a graphics
program and convert the logo to postscript.  Then with some editing of
it, and combining it with the data you wrote in in postscript
previously and send the file to the printer - and it prints.

The whole process gets very old fast, but postscript does allow for
some really nice forms and reports, logo's become a little bit of
pain, but once you get it down, its pretty good.  The major
disadvantage of that, is that you can only use postscript printers,
but I have found away around that.

To make a really long story short, if you use v5.1 of Acucobol, they
provide all the functionality of doing lasered reports and puting
logos on your forms.
There addresss is www.cobol.com.
```

## Thread: (OT) Would you believe ??? — 2 this month / 3 total

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

_Thread started [2002-04](2002-04.md#t-134493ca43)._

#### ↳ Re: (OT) Would you believe ???

- **From:** "Greg Condon" <nospam@me.thanks>
- **Date:** 2002-05-01T11:26:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<xhQz8.4057$xS2.273981@news1.calgary.shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<aan5a2$8t2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Now that brings back memories.

I took an APL course (1978) before I graduated high school (1980).  It was
the "coolest thing" out there at the time.  All the funky characters
probably doomed it as a general purpose language.  It was amazing how much
you could do in one line of code.

The professor at the University had custom-built a portable computer with a
built-in APL interpreter with a 3-line plasma display.  Naturally it was in
a hand-made aluminum case.  Got pretty warm, too.  I think it ran at 1Mhz or
a bit less.  This guy was way ahead of his time.


Regards,

Greg
```

## Thread: Open Source Code or Free code — 10 this month / 11 total

_Thread started [2002-04](2002-04.md#t-e435d43590)._

#### ↳ Re: Open Source Code or Free code

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-01T11:59:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oMQz8.20915$mF4.20805@nwrddc01.gnilink.net>`
- **References:** `<755320fc.0204301909.7168792a@posting.google.com>`

```
Jeremy Miedreich <jmiedreich@sbcglobal.net> wrote in message
news:755320fc.0204301909.7168792a@posting.google.com...
> I'm trying to find out if there are any open source cobol programs out
> there ...Specifically for accounting....   thinking of intergrating it
with an
> accouting backend...

While 'Accounting' is not my strong suit (once I get past the number of
beans I can count on two hands, I'm lost), it seems 'Accounting' is an
awfully broad term and subject to lots of interpretation.

I mean, does 'accounting' include.... Invoicing? Accounts Receivable/Cash
Receipts posting? Fixed Asset Depreciation? Payroll? Job Costing? Accounts
Payable? Cash Flow Forecasting? Inventory Analysis?

My point is, you may have better luck in your search if  either...

A. You can specify what you mean by 'accounting'
  OR
B. You can get people like me beyond the ten beans barrier.

MCM
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Open Source Code or Free code

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-01T18:35:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CD03509.F6A16CF2@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<755320fc.0204301909.7168792a@posting.google.com> <JNQz8.19768$sh6.1598698@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```


Donald Tees wrote:

> I have a similar package, and am in a similar position.  I also have an old
> accounting package that works, though it is a mess, and I am about to start
> over with it.  I would be interested in *starting* an open source AR system

> that would act as a "back end" for custom packages.

Don,

Is it truly worth it ? Ages ago I had a General Ledger package, (allowed for
different companies, branches and cost accounting). I even tried selling it for
$39 - one taker. It worked very successfully for the one user, no problems.
(It's not the product that sells itself, it requires you to be a bit of a
motor-mouth = salesman).

Considering something like Quickbooks, which I use - it is superb. Other than
specializing/customizing as you did with Inco - seems to me 'general accounting
packages' are a bit of a lost cause for somebody wishing to make some money.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Open Source Code or Free code

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-01T15:10:45-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<g2Xz8.12398$1k5.1854582@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<755320fc.0204301909.7168792a@posting.google.com> <JNQz8.19768$sh6.1598698@news20.bellglobal.com> <3CD03509.F6A16CF2@shaw.ca>`

```

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3CD03509.F6A16CF2@shaw.ca...
>
>
> Donald Tees wrote:
>
> > I have a similar package, and am in a similar position.  I also have an
old
> > accounting package that works, though it is a mess, and I am about to
start
> > over with it.  I would be interested in *starting* an open source AR
system
>
> > that would act as a "back end" for custom packages.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Is it truly worth it ? Ages ago I had a General Ledger package, (allowed
for
> different companies, branches and cost accounting). I even tried selling
it for
> $39 - one taker. It worked very successfully for the one user, no
problems.
> (It's not the product that sells itself, it requires you to be a bit of a
> motor-mouth = salesman).
>
> Considering something like Quickbooks, which I use - it is superb. Other
than
> specializing/customizing as you did with Inco - seems to me 'general
accounting
> packages' are a bit of a lost cause for somebody wishing to make some
money.
>
> Jimmy
>
>

Not as a stand-alone package, for sure.  However, the money to be made is in
generating the transactions.  The real problems with most of the stand-alone
packages are threefold.  They are not big enough for a larger company (say
500,000 invoices a year), they are very difficult to export data into, and
they are impossible to break items down correctly inside them. For example,
I need a ledger not only in dollars, but also in tonnes of ore. Full debits
and credits.  I also have to calculate tax based on a split for each load
... one rate for the material, and a different rate for the shipping.  That
load must appear as one item on the invoice, as shipping rates are
considered trade secrets.  The portion of the amount that is shipping also
has to generate a payable to the trucker, and so forth.  Once an actual
invoice is generated, then a standard package could handle open items,
payments, aging, etc., but getting the invoices cut is very non-standard.

I suspect that a similar situation exists for many industries ... where the
generation of invoices is actually a complete system that does something
like keep timesheets, or manufactures a product ...  I use to computerize
concrete plants.  I produced a bill of lading, and then forwarded it to an
invoicing system.  A computerized concrete plant runs in the $75,000 range.
I could give the accounting away free.

That kind of integration is industry specific, and unless there is open
source, a stadard package is just not going to cut it.

Donald
```

- **From:** jmiedreich@sbcglobal.net (Jeremy Miedreich)
- **Date:** 2002-05-01T22:41:37-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<755320fc.0205012141.de5588e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<755320fc.0204301909.7168792a@posting.google.com> <JNQz8.19768$sh6.1598698@news20.bellglobal.com> <3CD03509.F6A16CF2@shaw.ca> <g2Xz8.12398$1k5.1854582@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Donald,

I completly agree with you on all your points that you have made, and
with everyone else.  I would be interested on an open source package
that you mentioned.  The general accouting like A/R, etc..etc.. is
actually what I am looking for because the invoicing is very custom,
and to be able to send the data to A/R would be great, Its really no
problem to write A/R systems, but carefully planning would be needed
to write a backend accounting system to intergrate to other packages,
but have it open enough to allow for customization by the front end
customzied applicaton.  Also lets rememeber that accouting is
accounting no matter how u look at it.  Also i not sure I understood
you totally correct - you are willing to give the accouting portion
away? If that is so, that would help out a great deal.  I also have
been looking over other posiblites of intergration into other
packages, like quickbooks, and they provide and XML intergration, but
mainly with VB.  There would be ways using existing enhancements from
the COBOL vendors like Acucorp which i use, and Fujitso, which
starting to have some nice offerings.  But I' also am on the same line
as trying to provide a complete solution.  Also at the same time i'm
not in the business to provide accouting software.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Open Source Code or Free code

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-04T10:34:13-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vhSA8.25206$1k5.3238464@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<755320fc.0204301909.7168792a@posting.google.com> <JNQz8.19768$sh6.1598698@news20.bellglobal.com> <3CD03509.F6A16CF2@shaw.ca> <g2Xz8.12398$1k5.1854582@news20.bellglobal.com> <3CD31F70.C6EDFDE4@shaw.ca>`

```
Actually, all mining companies keep tonnes in their general ledger at some
level ... their major asset is always reserves, and without it there is no
value for the comapny.  With gravel pits, "pit run" starts as your main
asset account, while stuff like "washed stone", and "pea stone" look like
inventory accounts.  You debit the pit run, and credit the various piles of
product as you screen and wash. It all has to balance, as you pay resource
tax on the weights.

At the code level, you actually issue invoices and refunds to a customer by
shipping trucks with positive or negative weights.  That ensures that all
taxes are reversed, as well as the inventory being adjusted correctly.

At Inco, I never got involved at the accounting level ... I just ran the
trains and counted/weighted boxcars with the computer.  I am still getting
over the fact that someone gave me a real electric train with a few hundred
miles of track to run with my computer, and even paid me for playing with
it. I really cannot imagine anybody doing that for a Java programmer <G>.

Donald


"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3CD31F70.C6EDFDE4@shaw.ca...
>
>
> Donald Tees wrote:
>
> Serves me right <G>. Knowing you I should have realized there was already
a lot
> of deep thinking behind you initial short statement. Problem -  OO or
non-OO ? I
> guess the other guys are thinking non-OO.
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Not as a stand-alone package, for sure.  However, the money to be made
is in
> > generating the transactions.  The real problems with most of the
stand-alone
> > packages are threefold.  They are not big enough for a larger company
(say
> > 500,000 invoices a year), they are very difficult to export data into,
and
> > they are impossible to break items down correctly inside them. For
example,
> > I need a ledger not only in dollars, but also in tonnes of ore. Full
debits
> > and credits.  I also have to calculate tax based on a split for each
load
> > ... one rate for the material, and a different rate for the shipping.
That
> > load must appear as one item on the invoice, as shipping rates are
> > considered trade secrets.  The portion of the amount that is shipping
also
> > has to generate a payable to the trucker, and so forth.  Once an actual
> > invoice is generated, then a standard package could handle open items,
> > payments, aging, etc., but getting the invoices cut is very
non-standard.
> >
> > I suspect that a similar situation exists for many industries ... where
the
> > generation of invoices is actually a complete system that does something
> > like keep timesheets, or manufactures a product ...  I use to
computerize
> > concrete plants.  I produced a bill of lading, and then forwarded it to
an
> > invoicing system.  A computerized concrete plant runs in the $75,000
range.
> > I could give the accounting away free.
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

- **References:** `<755320fc.0204301909.7168792a@posting.google.com> <JNQz8.19768$sh6.1598698@news20.bellglobal.com> <3CD03509.F6A16CF2@shaw.ca> <g2Xz8.12398$1k5.1854582@news20.bellglobal.com> <3CD31F70.C6EDFDE4@shaw.ca> <vhSA8.25206$1k5.3238464@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
"Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:vhSA8.25206$1k5.3238464@news20.bellglobal.com...
> Actually, all mining companies keep tonnes in their general ledger at some
> level ... their major asset is always reserves, and without it there is no
> value for the comapny.  With gravel pits, "pit run" starts as your main
> asset account, while stuff like "washed stone", and "pea stone" look like
> inventory accounts.  You debit the pit run, and credit the various piles
of
> product as you screen and wash. It all has to balance, as you pay resource
> tax on the weights.

It gets even deeper (pardon the pun). I once had an opportunity to buy into
a 'Sand Pit.' The sand in the ground was, of course, the main company asset
which depreciated as you sold the stuff.

When you sold out of sand, you are left with a hole in the ground.

The sand-selling company converts itself into a land-fill company and
accepts material to bury. The company's main asset, remember, is a hole. The
hole now depreciates as you fill the hole with tree stumps, broken concrete,
Jimmy Hoffas, and the odd car.

When the hole fills up, you cover the area with dirt and sell the land for a
low-cost housing development.

I passed on the opportunity. The accounting was just too weird.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Optimization Techniques.

- **From:** Edward Reid <edward@paleo.org>
- **Date:** 2002-05-02T21:16:13-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.B8F75D1D000F3A740DAA47E0@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<c78087de.0205020801.2c91b200@posting.google.com> <WogA8.27510$mF4.20474@nwrddc01.gnilink.net>`

```
On Thu, 2 May 2002 15:25:42 -0400, Michael Mattias wrote
> The biggest 'catch' is poor software design.

I most emphatically agree. Code tuning typically achieves a few percent 
improvement, and the improvement is linear. The major improvements are 
almost always the result of improving the algorithm. To get better than 
linear improvements you must improve the algorithm. The most general 
aspects of the design are the most important, because those are the 
most difficult to change later.

If you ever have to write your own sort, read Knuth first. I don't know 
how many times I've seen programs where someone had written a bubble 
sort -- because it's intuitively appealing. (For the uninitiated, 
bubble sorts are appealing but appalling -- not the worst sort 
possible, but close.) Once many years ago I slipped a copy of Knuth to 
a programmer who was running a program with a bubble sort -- I knew 
because of the console displays. He put in the code for a Quicksort, 
and it ran so much faster (5 seconds vs 15 minutes) that he added code 
to check the results ... which of couse he should have done to begin 
with, even with the bubble sort.

That said, I recently helped with a program optimization where the 
problem turned out to be that the code was initializing a 325,000 byte 
working storage area for every transaction, even though most of it 
wasn't used for any given transaction. Regained about 90% of the CPU 
time in a program which had been using I think 3 CPUs for a couple of 
hours every night for many years. Ironically, this program contained a 
bubble sort, which I immediately suspected, but it was NOT the problem.

Most COBOL programmers just aren't oriented to the kinds of tools 
needed to track down something like this. Most also aren't oriented to 
thinking about algorithmic complexity, a good reason to have analysts 
with some knowledge of algorithms review the program and system design. 
Most COBOL systems escape the worst problems only because in the 
applications for which COBOL is typically used, the most obvious 
algorithms are usually OK.

Edward Reid
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Optimization Techniques.

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-04T07:49:22+00:00

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Optimization Techniques.

- **From:** cesullivan@aol.comsf (Cathy Sullivan)
- **Date:** 2002-05-04T19:51:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020504155103.19386.00001172@mb-fw.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<01HW.B8F75D1D000F3A740DAA47E0@news-east.usenetserver.com>`

```
In article <01HW.B8F75D1D000F3A740DAA47E0@news-east.usenetserver.com>, Edward
Reid <edward@paleo.org> writes:

>That said, I recently helped with a program optimization where the 
>problem turned out to be that the code was initializing a 325,000 byte 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>bubble sort, which I immediately suspected, but it was NOT the problem.

Wow, this must problem must be more common that I thought.  A guy at my job did
this to program and it went from 2.5 hours CPU time to 10 minutes CPU time. 
This is a nightly program.  

This savings was done by replacing a statement that moved spaces to a large
working storage table with code that initialized the parts that the program was
going to use.  The savings become notable when the program is processing a
million transactions.

Cathy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Optimization Techniques.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** cesullivan@aol.comsf (Cathy Sullivan)
- **Date:** 2002-05-09T02:45:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020508224551.01654.00000773@mb-mh.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3CD6C408.76B6F95E@optonline.net>`

```
In article <3CD6C408.76B6F95E@optonline.net>, Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.net>
writes:

>Cathy Sullivan wrote:
>> 
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Is it careless coding?  That's how they learned in COBOL 101 when they had to
read a file of 10 records.  No training in scaling up.  Why not write "MOVE
SPACES TO LARGE-TABLE" instead of PERFORM SPACE-OUT-TABLE 1000 TIMES and then
writing the procedure?   Isn't it the same?   

At first look, one statement is more efficent than 10.  I bet that a good
number of COBOL programmers have associate degrees.  They've never taken any
courses in how the operating system works or how to write efficient code.  And
they don't get encouragement on learning more.  When they need an example, they
look at their beginning COBOL textbook.  

I bet a number of COBOL programmers took a 13 week training program that their
company set up for people who passed an aptitude test.  I know that some folks
I have worked with learned that way.  Or they took one college course in COBOL
and their employer let them be a programmer.  

Think about it.

Cathy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Optimization Techniques.

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-09T11:31:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<G6tC8.5474$721.331852@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3CD6C408.76B6F95E@optonline.net> <20020508224551.01654.00000773@mb-mh.aol.com>`

```
Actually, I think that it might be a case of the compiler "falling down on the job".
There are ways to initialize just about any amount of storage efficiently, essentially
by setting up register with the starting and ending address and a mask. Otherwise,
you have to initialize it in 256 character chunks with a MVC's. (On the mainframe that is, workstations do it differently.)


"Cathy Sullivan" <cesullivan@aol.comsf> wrote in message news:20020508224551.01654.00000773@mb-mh.aol.com...
> In article <3CD6C408.76B6F95E@optonline.net>, Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.net>
> writes:
…[49 more quoted lines elided]…
> Cathy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Optimization Techniques.

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Edward Reid <edward@paleo.org>
- **Date:** 2002-05-10T11:07:23-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.B9015A6B0177D4090DA4B290@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<3CD6C408.76B6F95E@optonline.net> <20020508224551.01654.00000773@mb-mh.aol.com> <G6tC8.5474$721.331852@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
On Thu, 9 May 2002 7:31:50 -0400, Paul Raulerson wrote
> Actually, I think that it might be a case of the compiler "falling 
> down on the job". There are ways to initialize just about any amount 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> mainframe that is, workstations do it differently.) 

Not all mainframes say "IBM" on the side. The mainframe Cathy and I are 
talking about does the entire move in a single instruction -- the 
length of the move instruction is only limited by the size of an 
integer.

In this cast, the cost was purely in the memory write time for 325,000 
bytes.

As for whether programmers *ought* to be trained in design ... yes, 
they should. But they aren't. And given the climate in which most work, 
it's unreasonable to blame the individuals for lack of training. They 
are doing what's expected of them, which is what most people want to 
do. Blame the organizations that don't provide training.

Edward Reid
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Optimization Techniques.

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-09T12:25:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hVtC8.6834$Sh.1463@nwrddc01.gnilink.net>`
- **References:** `<3CD6C408.76B6F95E@optonline.net> <20020508224551.01654.00000773@mb-mh.aol.com>`

```
Cathy Sullivan <cesullivan@aol.comsf> wrote in message
news:20020508224551.01654.00000773@mb-mh.aol.com...
> At first look, one statement is more efficent than 10.

I'm glad you added that "at first look" qualifier, because there is no
general rule which says there is any relationship between number of source
code statements and the size/efficiency of the machine code created by the
compiler.

Compilers exist to make it easy for the programmer to execute many machine
instructions  with few programmer instructions.

Look at your own code: what is more 'efficient?'

CALL BIG-ROUTINE
or
<the 200 lines of source code which make up BIG-ROUTINE placed in-line>

The 200 lines of source code, executed in-line, is more efficient - from the
machine's perspective. Same thing applies to COBOL verbs like READ,
INITIALIZE and ADD: the compiler expands each verb into some number of
machine instructions. How many? Depends on the compiler.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Optimization Techniques.

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-05-09T14:45:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<abe1p8$lk1$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3CD6C408.76B6F95E@optonline.net> <20020508224551.01654.00000773@mb-mh.aol.com>`

```

On  8-May-2002, cesullivan@aol.comsf (Cathy Sullivan) wrote:

> >> This savings was done by replacing a statement that moved spaces to a
> >> large
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> then writing the procedure?   Isn't it the same?

In general, the one move is more efficient - unless it does more stuff than
is necessary.  And either technique can be worse if it isn't obvious to
maintenance programmers what is being initialized and why.

It doesn't matter what type of training one gets - as long as one's
education doesn't stop when he starts working.   Obviously, initializing
more stuff takes up more computer time than initializing less stuff.  Less
obvious is how much work is taken by coding specific checks to see what
should be initialized or not.  So you learn to design to minimize the amount
of checking necessary.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Optimization Techniques.

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2002-05-09T15:28:00-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0205091428.7df04a58@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3CD6C408.76B6F95E@optonline.net> <20020508224551.01654.00000773@mb-mh.aol.com> <abe1p8$lk1$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
To continue the discussion, I think that optimisation is very
important, but that the original enquiry was too narrow in its
approach.  As others have mentioned good system design is the major
factor and is therefore arguably the starting point of optimisation
for specific tasks.  Having said that it is often the case that
optimisation is found to be necessary rather later in the development
process, or often even later, when the program or system is found to
be underperforming in the circumstances in which it is to be used.

I think optimisation is about getting the job done effectively at a
reasonable cost in the particular circumstances of the specific task,
for which the subsidiary factors to which optimisation is applicable
are as follows.

Time it takes to train staff to do their jobs reasonably effectively.

Time and cost to develop a software product.

Time a software product takes to run.

Other resources a software product needs for execution, e.g. disk
space, softward libraries, etc.

Time it takes to debug a program/system.

Comprehensibility of a program/system to those who design,
maintain/enhance and use it, the harder something is to understand,
the longer it takes to work upon it.  This is often as or more
important than the minimisation of the time it takes to execute.

Maintenance of staff morale in the provision of continuing career
development by improving their design and programming ability and
style, this may improve the retention of staff in an organisation,
i.e. those who know how the organisation's systems work and can
therefore debug problems, apply enhancements and develop associated
new facilities rapidly.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Optimization Techniques.

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-05-10T13:51:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<abgiv2$5n7$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3CD6C408.76B6F95E@optonline.net> <20020508224551.01654.00000773@mb-mh.aol.com> <abe1p8$lk1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <fcd09c56.0205091428.7df04a58@posting.google.com>`

```

On  9-May-2002, robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones) wrote:

> Having said that it is often the case that
> optimisation is found to be necessary rather later in the development
> process, or often even later, when the program or system is found to
> be underperforming in the circumstances in which it is to be used.

A trouble with having good programmers is that when you get late in the
development process and find out that your programs are too slow - the code
can't be optimized because they were already written well.

If you start off with bad programmers, you can always improve the code!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Optimization Techniques.

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Drake Coker" <drake@san.rr.com.nospm>
- **Date:** 2002-05-15T14:26:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<FeuE8.5408$DF2.1015562@twister.socal.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3CD6C408.76B6F95E@optonline.net> <20020508224551.01654.00000773@mb-mh.aol.com>`

```
Just for fun, I ran a million moves of spaces to a 325000 byte area and
timed it under a couple of languages:

Visual C++: 360 seconds
ACUCOBOL-GT (native): 27 seconds.

This is just a home PC (a pretty good one), so it will have quite different
CPU usage and characteristics than
whatever is running the jobs reported below.

It is interesting that a roughly 8400 seconds are reported below for a
similar operation. (2.5 hours - 10 minutes).
Presumably, the 2.5 hours, the 10 minutes and the "million transactions" are
rough approximates and there
are other very substantial differences that make a comparison not
meaningful.  But its sort of fun to do!

I wouldn't completely "diss" those that learn COBOL on the job.  I didn't
even get a "13 week training course",
just was handed a COBOL compiler and a screen management program to port to
a new operating system.
That start lead me to my ultimate career: writing a new COBOL compiler ;)

Drake
```

#### ↳ Re: Optimization Techniques.

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-02T21:50:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_wiA8.33511$q8.3562730@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<c78087de.0205020801.2c91b200@posting.google.com>`

```

"Chidananda Reddy" <Chidananda.P.Reddy@aexp.com> wrote in message
news:c78087de.0205020801.2c91b200@posting.google.com...
> hi All,
> Can any one please let me know all the optimizing techniques for Cobol
> and JCL.
> Can also send me any document which explains the catches in the
> programs which consume lots of CPU and I/O.

That's easy. For all practical, economical, and measurable purposes, there
aren't any.

In modern systems, the vast, vast majority of searches and fixes for
inefficiency cost more than they ultimately save. To paraphrase a famous
senator (Everett Dirkson, if memory serves) "A millisecond here, a
millisecond there, and pretty soon you're talking about a full second!"

There may be mission-critical situations (like life-and-death) where cost
does not matter and for those instances the search may be warranted.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Optimization Techniques.

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>

```
I respectfully disagree, at least about life-and-death situations.  I
once fixed a batch job that couldn't make its batch window.  By
optimizing a table search, the wall-clock runtime was reduced from ten
hours to two hours.  This was a job that ran twice a week, and the
cost-saving was signficant.

It's mentioned on the following page:
http://home.att.net/~arnold.trembley/cob-rte.htm
```

```
Sorry, Jerry Mouse, but it sounds as if you have never worked on batch
jobs for a big bank or broker (or other kinds of transaction-intensive
industries).  I once knocked reduced a job from 3 hours to 20 minutes
by changing file access for multiple files to skip-sequential from
plain sequential.

There are a number of us here who have made a decent buck doing
application tuning.

The IBM COBOL Programmer's Guide always has a section on coding
efficiency.

Check Bookmanager; I think IBM recently added a separate book on

Stephen J Spiro
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Optimization Techniques. ==>>> STROBE

- **From:** Charles Haatvedt Jr. <clastname@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-06T01:55:43-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.173fe4ea1ba0241998968d@news.charter.net>`
- **References:** `<c78087de.0205020801.2c91b200@posting.google.com> <_wiA8.33511$q8.3562730@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <4145699b.0205051338.5ef5ab1a@posting.google.com>`

```

	I work as a lead analyst in an application performance tuning group. 
The single most valuable tool we use is STROBE from Compuware. It is 
indespensable. You would be amazed at some of the simple techniques 
which have saved considerable CPU and wall clock time in application 
programs...   little things in applications which process huge w/s 
tables indexes, designing applications to avoid table initialization, 
PERFORM n TIMES instead of using the UNTIL option checking the index 
(only if the number of interations is known in advance)...    

The real benefit of STROBE is that it identifies what section of your 
code is using resources. Thus allowing you to focus on those areas of 
the program which are using significant amounts of resources.

In article <4145699b.0205051338.5ef5ab1a@posting.google.com>, 
stephenjspiro@hotmail.com says...
> "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com> wrote in message news:<_wiA8.33511$q8.3562730@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>...
> > "Chidananda Reddy" <Chidananda.P.Reddy@aexp.com> wrote in message
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
```

#### ↳ Re: Optimization Techniques.

- **From:** "Schroeder" <jfriedman@nc.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-11T01:53:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vQ_C8.61036$gd5.25189801@typhoon.southeast.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<c78087de.0205020801.2c91b200@posting.google.com>`

```
I remember back in my early days of programming when my manager told me to
implement an algorithm which our customer had "invented" so solve a problem.
After looking at it I found that all it was doing was division by repeated
subtractions!  But my manager insisted that since the customer wanted it
that way, I should do it that way.  So I am sure there is a lot of
algorithmically poor code our there.  And in general that is probably much
worse than a poor coding style.

But there is another side of the issue too: for typical business
applications, maintenace costs are the major expense, and the need to
quickly implement changes a important requirement.  Again, way back when in
my programming career I wrote a simple piece of code to replace a month
number by its name (I had an array of month names defined in working
storage) and I coded something like:

move month-name(month-number) to result.

Seemed pretty simple to me.  But lo-and-behold, once again my manager has
something to say about it.  He strenuously objected to it and insisted that
it should be coded:

IF month-number is equal to 01
    MOVE "January" to result.
IF month-number is equal to 02
    MOVE "February" to result.
etc..

And note that each IF ended with a period assuring that all 12 comparisons
would always be done (I could not even convince him to add the ELSEs to at
least stop looking once the right month was found).  I argued how
inefficient it was but my manager's response was:  "Is there any doubt in
your mind what that piece of code is doing?"  Of course the answer is NO -
so he said: "Then it is the better code!".

I would hope most programming managers don't take maintenance requirements
to quite that extreme - but I learned to agree that maintenance Must be
considered when writing a piece of code - and complexity of the code
dramatically affects maintenance costs.  So before you optimize the heck out
of what you are working on, think of the poor schnook who will have to work
on it next.

Just MHO

Jeff Friedman







"Chidananda Reddy" <Chidananda.P.Reddy@aexp.com> wrote in message
news:c78087de.0205020801.2c91b200@posting.google.com...
> hi All,
> Can any one please let me know all the optimizing techniques for Cobol
> and JCL.
> Can also send me any document which explains the catches in the
> programs which consume lots of CPU and I/O.
```

```
has not kept up with the language), I tell him/her: "I will be very
happy to teach a course to your staff.  Your company is paying for the
whole compiler, your staff should be familiar with all of it, and
should be able not only to recognize, but to use the better
techniques."

Stephen J Spiro
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Optimization Techniques.

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-12T16:50:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<J3xD8.3865$9z5.489004@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
I guess everyone has horror stories. But chances as a lot of them are related to previous bad
experiences with communications between the programmer(s) and the manager(s). If the program is
so buggy that it regularly abends at 2am in the morning, they should be looking towards other problems
than that.

But given that, it is amazing how people just absolutely love EVALUATE once they discover it. :)

-Paul
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Optimization Techniques.

- **From:** epc8@juno.com (E P Chandler)
- **Date:** 2002-05-13T08:50:51-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7f64e2ff.0205130750.5edc8a78@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<c78087de.0205020801.2c91b200@posting.google.com> <vQ_C8.61036$gd5.25189801@typhoon.southeast.rr.com>`

```
"Schroeder" <jfriedman@nc.rr.com> wrote in message news:<vQ_C8.61036$gd5.25189801@typhoon.southeast.rr.com>...
> But there is another side of the issue too: for typical business
> applications, maintenace costs are the major expense, and the need to
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> so he said: "Then it is the better code!".

This brought to mind an example in Jon Bentley's book "Programming
Pearls." It's a classic programming text which discusses optimization
and other important topics. At the start of chapter 3, Bentley
describes a COBOL program which he once saw and improved:

IF THISINPUT IS EQUAL TO 001 ADD 1 TO COUNT001.
IF THISINPUT IS EQUAL TO 002 ADD 1 TO COUNT002.
....
up to 500.

He says (page 23):

"Although the program accomplished a slightly more complicated task,
it isn't misleading to view it as counting how many times each integer
between 1 and 500 was found in a file. It contained about 1600 lines
of code: 500 to define the variables COUNT001 through COUNT500, the
above 500 to do the counting, 500 to print out how many times each
integer was found, and 100 miscellaneous statemets."

Of course a simple array would have done the job.

Later on on page 25 he says:

"... the programmers thought about their problems in COBOL, and many
COBOL programmers think of arrays as fixed tables that are initialized
at the beginning of a program and never altered."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Optimization Techniques.

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-05-13T17:12:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<abortd$oqk$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<c78087de.0205020801.2c91b200@posting.google.com> <vQ_C8.61036$gd5.25189801@typhoon.southeast.rr.com> <7f64e2ff.0205130750.5edc8a78@posting.google.com>`

```
I have a few programs that read in a set of subcodes and then compare the
database data with those subcodes.  The subcodes range from 00000 to 99999.


Users provide the parms and I check if they are numeric and mark a hash
table with "Y" if it is selected.  Or if a subcode is '*****', I move 'Y' to
the whole table.   This was soooo very much more efficient than the old way
it was done that I was able to convince people of the change - but needed to
document it well.  (even though it seems awfully simple to me)

The only difficulty is the CoBOL won't allow me to define
   05  MY-TABLE     OCCURS 100000 TIMES FROM 0 to 99999.
So I have to do some coding to handle subcode 00000.

To me, this is a limitation of CoBOL which should have been fixed a long
time ago.


Another code I need to document well is the ALSO clause of the EVALUATE
command.  This is interesting in that the efficiency is really in the
maintenance - it is easier to clone EVALUATE code accurately than nested if
code.  But someone who hasn't done many EVALUATE can get confused by the
ALSO clause - so I add on an explanation.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Optimization Techniques.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-13T12:20:31-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aboslb$hd$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<c78087de.0205020801.2c91b200@posting.google.com> <vQ_C8.61036$gd5.25189801@typhoon.southeast.rr.com> <7f64e2ff.0205130750.5edc8a78@posting.google.com> <abortd$oqk$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:abortd$oqk$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
 <snip>
> Another code I need to document well is the ALSO clause of the EVALUATE
> command.  This is interesting in that the efficiency is really in the
> maintenance - it is easier to clone EVALUATE code accurately than nested
if
> code.  But someone who hasn't done many EVALUATE can get confused by the
> ALSO clause - so I add on an explanation.

Maybe I am "out of touch" with real world problems, but I find that most
programmers understand the "ALSO" clause a LOT easier then they do multiple
WHEN clauses (with no imperative) which really are (implicit?) "OR"
conditions.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Optimization Techniques.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-05-13T18:15:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aboviq$qvp$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<c78087de.0205020801.2c91b200@posting.google.com> <vQ_C8.61036$gd5.25189801@typhoon.southeast.rr.com> <7f64e2ff.0205130750.5edc8a78@posting.google.com> <abortd$oqk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <aboslb$hd$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

On 13-May-2002, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:

> > code.  But someone who hasn't done many EVALUATE can get confused by the
> > ALSO clause - so I add on an explanation.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> conditions.

I can't avoid multiple WHEN clauses - but I have seen people try to use "OR"
without luck before I showed them how the multiple WHEN clauses worked.  
One can get around some ALSO clauses with AND, but this can get ugly.

When we write
	WHEN "A"
	WHEN "B"
	WHEN "C"
		PERFORM FOUND-ABC
	WHEN OTHER
		PERFORM OTHER

The implicit "OR" is pretty obvious.

Hmmm.   It's obvious to the reader.   That's the important part here.  A
coder who's unfamiliar with the EVALUATE syntax might have a harder time
writing it in the first place, but I'm more concerned with the person who
reads and maintains it.

I would much rather have a language that is easy to read than one that's
easy to write.  (C= write only language)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Optimization Techniques.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Edward Reid <edward@paleo.org>
- **Date:** 2002-05-15T10:23:58-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.B907E7BE0025E63D0DFF1E70@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<c78087de.0205020801.2c91b200@posting.google.com> <vQ_C8.61036$gd5.25189801@typhoon.southeast.rr.com> <7f64e2ff.0205130750.5edc8a78@posting.google.com> <abortd$oqk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <aboslb$hd$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
On Mon, 13 May 2002 13:20:31 -0400, William M. Klein wrote
> Maybe I am "out of touch" with real world problems, but I find that most
> programmers understand the "ALSO" clause a LOT easier then they do multiple
> WHEN clauses (with no imperative) which really are (implicit?) "OR"
> conditions.

Interesting. I've seen the opposite -- programmers who dislike ALSO 
despite lots of explanation and some practice, but who take to multiple 
WHENs like a fish to water. I wonder why the difference? Perhaps the 
programmers of your experience were exposed to specific cases of ALSO 
which were easier to understand than the general case? I don't know.

I've found EVALUATE TRUE to be by far the most valuable version of 
EVALUATE in programs converted from old COBOL74 programs (which were 
converted from COBOL68 programs). I haven't had sufficient opportunity 
to see whether typical COBOL programmers will adapt their programming 
style to use more EVALUATE data-name and less EVALUATE TRUE. I really 
don't expect most to use anything more complex than EVALUATE data-name. 
My own coding practice has in the past not proven to be a good 
indicator of what the typical COBOL programmer will do (to put it 
mildly).

Edward Reid
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Optimization Techniques.

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-20T03:44:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Qi_F8.95930$9F5.5748467@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<c78087de.0205020801.2c91b200@posting.google.com> <vQ_C8.61036$gd5.25189801@typhoon.southeast.rr.com> <7f64e2ff.0205130750.5edc8a78@posting.google.com> <abortd$oqk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <aboslb$hd$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <01HW.B907E7BE0025E63D0DFF1E70@news-east.usenetserver.com>`

```
Might be surprised. :)

Evaluate is particularly useful in CICS programs, but using it comes natural to people moving from C, or one of the languages more
or less ALGOL based; like Ada, Java, Pascal, or Modula.

It also makes the programs a bit more concise and easy to read if done correctly. Older COBOL code
seems as cryptic to me as C does to a COBOL programmer. YMMV.

One note- I was dealing with a 'COBOL-85' compiler a few weeks ago that was not COBOL-85
compliant. The person who was supposed to be dealing with that issue just sneered at me and tried to
tell me that real COBOL programmers don't need COBOL-85 features. (Literally sneered - he has some financial interest in the
compiler product. Very annoying.)

Worse yet, the compiler documentation detailed the use of things like Intrinsic Functions and
ADDRESS OF, but none of that was available. Yet this is considered (by some people!) to be a
production class compiler. This is the kind of stuff that leads to COBOL getting a bad name, and
to the newer features being ostracized. I'm really big on a compiler doing what it says it will do, and
doing so efficiently, accurately, and consistently.

Nope, this was not a Unisys or IBM compiler- it was a compiler from a company that should have been at the leading edge of compiler
technology, and at one time, was pretty well was
.
It was shame too, because the compiler is cost efficient, fast, and has a really nice user interface to it.

Aw well...

-Paul
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Optimization Techniques.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamDD@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-15T15:43:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CE28222.2000908@optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<c78087de.0205020801.2c91b200@posting.google.com> <vQ_C8.61036$gd5.25189801@typhoon.southeast.rr.com> <7f64e2ff.0205130750.5edc8a78@posting.google.com> <abortd$oqk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <aboslb$hd$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:

> "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
> news:abortd$oqk$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> conditions.


My experience has been the opposite, e.g.:

	EVALUATE something
	WHEN CASE-1
	WHEN CASE-2
	WHEN CASE-4
		do something
	.
	.
	.
	END-EVALUATE

was the easier understood construct. Coding ALSOs can get very messy 
very quickly and is often not required if the programmer (me, for 
instance) understands that the above is legal. Learning that I could 
stack multiple WHENs was a breakthrough moment for me when I was 
learning COBOL II many moons ago.

I guess it's one of these YMMV issues.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Optimization Techniques.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-13T18:01:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CDFFF6E.C4328E31@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<c78087de.0205020801.2c91b200@posting.google.com> <vQ_C8.61036$gd5.25189801@typhoon.southeast.rr.com> <7f64e2ff.0205130750.5edc8a78@posting.google.com> <abortd$oqk$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```


Howard Brazee wrote:

> I have a few programs that read in a set of subcodes and then compare the
> database data with those subcodes.  The subcodes range from 00000 to 99999.
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> ALSO clause - so I add on an explanation.

Sorry Howard - but I'm more than glad that tables start at element #1. It may
possibly be more mathematically correct (???), but I *hated* BASIC for starting
at element # 0.

Is it really a big deal  -  if Element #1 = spaces or zeroes then you have zero
elements ?

Agree with BIll - I have no problem with ALSO in EVALUATES.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Optimization Techniques.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-05-13T19:12:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<abp2te$sp8$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<c78087de.0205020801.2c91b200@posting.google.com> <vQ_C8.61036$gd5.25189801@typhoon.southeast.rr.com> <7f64e2ff.0205130750.5edc8a78@posting.google.com> <abortd$oqk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3CDFFF6E.C4328E31@shaw.ca>`

```

On 13-May-2002, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:

> Sorry Howard - but I'm more than glad that tables start at element #1. It
> may
> possibly be more mathematically correct (???), but I *hated* BASIC for
> starting at element # 0.

BASIC should have given us a choice.  So should CoBOL.  If the subcodes
range from 80000-89999, I would like to define my table that way to, so that
I don't have to translate (the computer can translate displacements much
faster than my code can)

> Is it really a big deal  -  if Element #1 = spaces or zeroes then you have
> zero elements ?

I have no idea what you're referring to here.  My example was converting
subcodes to positions in a table.

> Agree with BIll - I have no problem with ALSO in EVALUATES.

I don't either - but I have had to document my command more to account for
programmers who aren't familiar enough with them.

Programming for programmers is a major reason for not using REPORT WRITER as
well - keep things familiar.
```

```
Disregard mine and others criticisms of J4 - but they DO take account and give

from vendor staff. Now of course, just like people who don't vote and still
bitch about their government, if you say "Can't be bothered...." then you
haven't got a case for saying "COBOL should....."

Let you in on something - I have made suggestions to J4 in the past, but I got a
gentle pat on the head from Bill, "You still don't get it, do you ?". My
response to that one was, with reference to seaside postcards, with an army
platoon marching by, one cheery chappie says, "Hi Mum. They're all out of step
except me". <G>

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Optimization Techniques.

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-13T22:00:45+00:00

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3CE03766.63FE1389@shaw.ca...
 <snip>
>
> Didn't appreciate it was WG4 calling the initial shots, (now there's
*another*
> story..... One committee ????). However, by being specific, I think you
muddied
> the waters a little indicating those domiciled in US can submit to J4 -
Robert
> of Gloucester in UK, Tim Josling in Australia etc.,  or myself in the
Great
> White North, can ALSO submit ideas to J4.
>
> Jimmy
>

It is true that people from anywhere MAY submit ideas to J4.  HOWEVER, my
(personal) suggestion would be that you submit your ideas (for enhancements)
to the Standards group in your own country. It is my IMPRESSION that you are
more likely to carry additional "weight" if your ideas come in via the
Standards body for your own country.  If, on the other hand, your country
doesn't have any "easy to reach" Standards body, then do feel free to submit
them via J4.

Just because it has been a while, let me repeat the URL with the list of
National Standards Groups:

http://www.iso.ch/iso/en/aboutiso/isomembers/MemberList.MemberSummary?MEMBER
CODE=10
```

```
James J. Gavan <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3CE03766.63FE1389@shaw.ca...
>
> However, by being specific, I think you muddied
> the waters a little indicating those domiciled in US can submit to J4 -
Robert
> of Gloucester in UK, Tim Josling in Australia etc.,  or myself in the
Great
> White North, can ALSO submit ideas to J4.
>
While I respect your (and everybody else's) right to send mesages to
whomsoever you please, Jimmy, I have to ask:

"What is the point in making submissions to a body which has shown it is
totally ineffective, is unresponsive to user feedback anyway, and is NOT a
credible custodian of COBOL?"

You might as well send submissions to the Housing Committee of Ulan Bator,
for all the good it is likely to do COBOL.

The sooner these people disband (or get disbanded through lack of funding)
the better it will be for COBOL.

Sending them messages only encourages them and makes them think they are
even more important than they do already (if that were possible...).

COBOL has managed 17 years without them; I say it can manage indefinitely
without them.

Pete.
```

```
James J. Gavan <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3CE05EE3.970E2D5F@shaw.ca...
>
>
> Complete change of topic but more than semi-related. Your 'Doom and Gloom'
re
> US programming. People can summarily dismiss what you wrote and your
> viewpoints - clever bastard etc...
>
My post was not intended to be 'Doom and Gloom'. It is intended to be
realistic. Just because news is not like one would like to hear DOESN'T mean
it is simply negativity. Personally, I am not depressed by the situation.
I'd like it to be different, but I'll simply deal with it. (Am doing so, in
fact...)

As for being "summarily dismissed" I think most people will argue their
position if tey disagree or see a flaw in the reasoning. I sure hope so.

> But there isn't anything like statistics - and I mean REAL statistics,
like
> you just quoted, 100 applying for the same job as you and, not your
> preference, still looking for a job.
>
Well, there were over 400 for one job I applied for...<G>

The jobs are there, but the jobs are "different" than they were even 3 years
ago. That was what my post was trying to identify.


> Why oh why can the COBOL community, not just the Standards, but people
here in
> this group, be so bloody complacent about the future. I have LOVED my
years in
> EDP and to me COBOL became the icing on the cake, plus a real BIG dollop
of
> cream was added with OO !.
>
I agree. There is musch complacency (even smugness...) but the World is
changing and IT is changing also, as I outlined. It is important that young
people looking at computer programming as a future career should be aware of
the true situation. It isn't that you CANNOT do it, it is just that the
demand for it is in decline and looks like continuing to be so. In other
words, unless you REALLY want to program computers, you could probably make
more money doing Accountancy or Business Management.

> I thank God I am in my Indian Summers - for many reasons, but certainly
the
> picture you paint, I would hate to be a youngster starting out now.
>
> Meanwhile back at the ranch - you can still send 'get well cards' to J4,
if
> that's your bent <G>
>
Well, I like the idea of sending a get well card to them...<G>.

However, I would rather see them simply die...

Pete.

> Jimmy
>
>
```

- **References:** `<c78087de.0205020801.2c91b200@posting.google.com> <vQ_C8.61036$gd5.25189801@typhoon.southeast.rr.com> <7f64e2ff.0205130750.5edc8a78@posting.google.com> <abortd$oqk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3CDFFF6E.C4328E31@shaw.ca> <abp2te$sp8$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
On Mon, 13 May 2002 19:12:27 GMT, "Howard Brazee"
<howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote:

>
snip
> If the subcodes
>range from 80000-89999, I would like to define my table that way to, so that
>I don't have to translate (the computer can translate displacements much
>faster than my code can)
>
This is one (of many) feature I like about PL/I. You can define the
bounds of your array/table to meet the  problem. If you are collecting
temperatures just define (off my head) TEMP(-100:100) then assign
TEMP(READING) = TEMP(READING) + 1;
 A bit contrived but you get the idea. BTW you can do something
similar in REXX.
But this is c.l.c. not c.l.p. :-) and current COBOL does not allow you
to define an array/table by stating the bounds. 
John
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Optimization Techniques.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Charles Haatvedt Jr. <clastname@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-13T19:03:04-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.174a1037b58063398968e@news.charter.net>`
- **References:** `<c78087de.0205020801.2c91b200@posting.google.com> <vQ_C8.61036$gd5.25189801@typhoon.southeast.rr.com> <7f64e2ff.0205130750.5edc8a78@posting.google.com> <abortd$oqk$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
	Howard,

I have run into the same situation many times when using "direct index" 
tables...   my solution is fairly simple...

   05  MY-TABLE     OCCURS 100000 TIMES
                    INDEXED BY MY-INDEX.

SET MY-INDEX               TO subcode.
SET MY-INDEX               UP BY +1.

===>>> This will allow you to still use the entire universe of possible 
values. It does require an extra "SET" statement when accessing the 
table. I use this technique quite frequently in combination with 
"indirection" tables in Cobol...  it is very fast...

 
In article <abortd$oqk$1@peabody.colorado.edu>, howard.brazee@cusys.edu 
says...
> I have a few programs that read in a set of subcodes and then compare the
> database data with those subcodes.  The subcodes range from 00000 to 99999.
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Optimization Techniques.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-05-14T13:43:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<abr3vu$3rg$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<c78087de.0205020801.2c91b200@posting.google.com> <vQ_C8.61036$gd5.25189801@typhoon.southeast.rr.com> <7f64e2ff.0205130750.5edc8a78@posting.google.com> <abortd$oqk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <MPG.174a1037b58063398968e@news.charter.net>`

```

On 13-May-2002, Charles Haatvedt Jr. <clastname@nospam.com> wrote:

>    Howard,
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> "indirection" tables in Cobol...  it is very fast...

That works for the 00000-99999 tables.  I tend to use subscripts and have
done the same thing.  I have also checked for 00000 and translated it to
100000 - I am not sure which is faster.

But I also have had occasion to work with 80000-89999.  But for this we need
to do more bounds checking than IF NUMERIC and your code above.
```

```
etc required from here on
```
