```
"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message news:<abortd$oqk$1@peabody.colorado.edu>...
> I have a few programs that read in a set of subcodes and then compare the
> database data with those subcodes.  The subcodes range from 00000 to 99999.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> document it well.  (even though it seems awfully simple to me)

But it might not be obvious to someone who looks at arrays as fixed
tables.

Expanding your ways of thinking is one reason that working in various
languages and environments is good for programmers, along with
widening the job options, of course.

I rarely get to make an 'aha' programming change that speeds things up
by an order of magnitude, but when it happens, it's quite a thrill.


> The only difficulty is the CoBOL won't allow me to define
>    05  MY-TABLE     OCCURS 100000 TIMES FROM 0 to 99999.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

At least you have arrays which allow you to simulate some other sort
of data structure that might be more suited to your task. It was
painful, but I've done binary trees in BASIC, for example. I'm going
to enjoy thinking about how I might simulate a bitmap in COBOL.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OCCURS with "base" (was: Optimization Techniques.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-14T12:24:32-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<abrh8n$bto$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<c78087de.0205020801.2c91b200@posting.google.com> <vQ_C8.61036$gd5.25189801@typhoon.southeast.rr.com> <7f64e2ff.0205130750.5edc8a78@posting.google.com> <abortd$oqk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <7f64e2ff.0205140902.4d99085e@posting.google.com>`

```
Just in case anyone posting in this thread has missed the "obvious".  In the
'85 Standard; one can use EITHER relative "subscripting" or "indexing".  I
believe that this is why the ANSI/ISO committees have never looked
"favorably" on adding syntax to the OCCURS clause to "mean" that the 1st
thru Nth index/subscript values are actually Y to Z (as suggested earlier in
the thread).

With the *NEXT* COBOL Standard two enhancements are introduced that make
(IMHO) any such enhancement even less "required" - CONSTANTs and "arithmetic
expressions  as subscripts).

Today, to get the "equivalent" of 10,000 thru 99,999 for "subscript values",
simply have an occurs of

  05  SubscriptValue  Pic 99999.
        88 ValidSubscipts  Value 10000 thru 99999.
  05 MyTable occurs 89,999 times.
      10 EachElem  Pic X.
        ...
Move EachElem (SubscriptValue - 9999) to SomeOtherField.

    ***
With the next Standard, that "9999" can be defined as a constant (to make
maintenance easier) or can actually be another data item - if the value is
"determined" at run-time.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OCCURS with "base" (was: Optimization Techniques.

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>

```
On 14-May-2002, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:

> With the *NEXT* COBOL Standard two enhancements are introduced that make
> (IMHO) any such enhancement even less "required" - CONSTANTs and
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> "determined" at run-time.

One would think that such a subscript with a constant or a constant variable
would compile very efficiently.

I have seen instances where people define a table OCCURS 10000, but only use
1000 elements - figuring the space taken is less costly than the arithmetic
needed.  This doesn't help where they need to have the zeroeth element
though.  Your example will help though.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OCCURS with "base" (was: Optimization Techniques.

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-05-14T18:50:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<abrlvv$ec7$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
>   05  SubscriptValue  Pic 99999.
```

```
Welcome to the WONDERFUL world of the new VALIDATE statement.  All that (and
more) can "easily" be done by this facility defined in the draft Standard.
You might want to check out the examples in the CONCEPTS annex of the draft
Standard.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Optimization Techniques.

- **From:** Edward Reid <edward@paleo.org>
- **Date:** 2002-05-15T10:16:11-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.B907E5EB002578880DFF1E70@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<c78087de.0205020801.2c91b200@posting.google.com> <vQ_C8.61036$gd5.25189801@typhoon.southeast.rr.com> <7f64e2ff.0205130750.5edc8a78@posting.google.com>`

```
> This brought to mind an example in Jon Bentley's book "Programming
> Pearls." [...] page 25 he says:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> at the beginning of a program and never altered."

Bentley's implication is that COBOL programmers understand static 
tables. My experience is that COBOL programmers who have never received 
any training in programming -- which especially means those whose 
training has been entirely OJT -- often don't understand tables (which 
of course are called arrays in almost every other programming language) 
at all. A very simple static table subscripted by constants, maybe ... 
but table-driven code based on a static table will be even more foreign 
to them than a simple table with dynamic values.

Not too long ago, someone mentioned that some COBOL code I had written 
for them was rather complex. My answer was that complex problems often 
require complex code. They had contracted with us because their staff 
wasn't comfortable doing the task themselves. That's no reflection on 
their programmers, who are generally quite good -- they simply aren't 
trained in programming techniques. Of course, I explained the code in 
comments quite thoroughly.

Edward Reid
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Optimization Techniques.

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-05-13T15:54:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<abonb2$m1l$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<c78087de.0205020801.2c91b200@posting.google.com> <vQ_C8.61036$gd5.25189801@typhoon.southeast.rr.com>`

```

On 10-May-2002, "Schroeder" <jfriedman@nc.rr.com> wrote:

> And note that each IF ended with a period assuring that all 12 comparisons
> would always be done (I could not even convince him to add the ELSEs to at
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> so he said: "Then it is the better code!".

That's what comments are for.
```

```
Hi community.

This message is to offer you our free tool for report development: Rpv
Printing System. 

Rpv is a tool that lets you create great reports using any programming
language (Windows, Dos). Since we had great results with VB and
Liberty Basic, we wanted to let you know about this tool to the Cobol
community too. I am personally a COBOL (Rm-Cobol 85) developer and I
have developed reports using Rpv from Dos to Windows and the
application works great.

Please note that the application is FREE. Free means that you can
distribute it with your software freely. (not like non-commercial use
or demo).

Application is available in english & spanish (for now) at
http://www.rpvreport.com

Thanks for your time.
Tom
```

<a id="t-514a4ec147"></a>
## Thread: .dat and .idx file conversion — 2 this month / 6 total

_Thread started [2002-04](2002-04.md#t-514a4ec147)._

#### ↳ Re: .dat and .idx file conversion

- **From:** "Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-03T02:37:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OJmA8.6252$YU6.234302724@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<9e862e20.0204300803.f652244@posting.google.com>`

```
Mike,

I'll throw this out as a "give it a try" option only.  If I can recall
correctly, the MicroFocus Isam .dat file had the first 128 bytes as the Isam
info record.  As to what the layout of the fields in that 128 bytes is, I'm
not really sure, but open the .dat file in Wordpad or Notepad and see if you
see something that looks like a date (yymmdd) starting in position 9 of the
first record.  I seem to recall that this was somewhat standard in Micro
Focus Isam files, but I haven't used Micro Focus for a number of years now.
If so, then look at position 129 for a couple of bytes of weird looking
characters, i.e. "B�",  "E?", or whatever.  Then continue scanning down the
file, counting each byte until you see the same characters repeat
themselves.  This will give you the actual data "record" length, including
the "weird" characters.  Assuming you have a compiler that can read byte
stream files, i.e. 128 bytes first, then <whatever length counted above>
next, you can possibly create a new file that is more user friendly to use.
In this case, the .idx file doesn't really matter, since it's just pointing
you to the data records in the .dat file anyhow.  However, you still won't
know the contents of each field in the record.  But I have found if the old
application still runs and you can produce the output from it, you can
usually figure out what data is where, give or take a couple of bad guesses.
However, if the data is in comp/binary format, the job becomes, shall we
say, a bit more challenging, but still doable!

Good luck,

Denny

"Mike" <mike@dsacentral.com> wrote in message
news:9e862e20.0204300803.f652244@posting.google.com...
> We are reviewing an old application for a client.  Our assumption is
> that it was written in MicroFocus Cobol and uses .dat and .idx
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Murf
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: .dat and .idx file conversion

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-03T15:31:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<A3yA8.2869$vT1.259646@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<9e862e20.0204300803.f652244@posting.google.com> <OJmA8.6252$YU6.234302724@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com>`

```
    It depends on exactly WHICH microfocus indexed file format you have,
but if you have a micros focus compiler, try defineing the data portion of
the file
as a variable length sequential file.  Be sure to move spaces to
the record before EVERY read.

    Some really, really old files used to be relative files.

    If you do have a microfocus compiler, you could use the rebuild
utility to copy the file to a sequential file.   Rebuild is one of the files
that
they allow you to ship with an application, so you should be able to find
a copy.


"Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net> wrote in message
news:OJmA8.6252$YU6.234302724@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com...

the
```

## Thread: Why'd they pay so much? — 2 msgs

### Why'd they pay so much?

- **From:** "Rufio" <davecawdell@cox.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-03T02:47:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9TmA8.14523$9c5.517599@news2.west.cox.net>`

```
WOW, and again WOW !

Started as an employee of Charles Schwab last week, after 8 years
contracting. Good to be coding COBOL, after 6 months of PL/I.

I wondered why Schwab would offer so great a package - but then my first
project covers CICS, Datacom, DB2, Oracle MVS, Unix & MQ-Series "just to
give me a feel of the customer apps". The learning curve is still almost
vertical. But isn't it odd, that a job that is intellectually stimulating is
also the most lucrative. That the easy, dull, boring jobs are also the worst
paying jobs !!!

 Also, I get to remove all those recruitment & external training bookmarks
from my browser too!!

P.S. Anyone want to give me an update on weather, restaruants etc. in SF &
escpecially Nob Hill? Flying in next week, for a head to head, etc.

P.P.S. Especially want to get to a Giants game or 2 - D'backs are a bit sick
at the moment - but it only counts in October !
```

<a id="t-d0875ebb49"></a>
## Thread: Dumb Code - What Was I Smoking? — 16 msgs

### Dumb Code - What Was I Smoking?

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2002-05-03T03:29:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020502232928.14065.00004738@mb-ce.aol.com>`

```
This was one of those days I shouldn't have gotten out of bed.  Several things
happened before I got to work that should have given me a clue it wasn't going
to be pretty.

We have a client/server set of programs that because I finally figured out how
to do it I'm the expert in.  The batch side (client) does all the computations
and data manipulation and the CICS (server) side writes the records to the
on-line VSAM file.  This particular VSAM file is being converted from variable
length to fixed length (yeah! finally!) and my cubemate was given the task to
test the changes. 

The only thing changed in the programs was the length of the commarea to
accomodate the change in the length of the records (I know - famous last
words). She was merrily testing away when the whole thing blew up.  After
drilling down through the code of both programs (the return code received by
the batch program indicated the CICS program had failed but there was no dump
and we couldn't for the life of us find an abend code that made any sense) we
determined that part of the commarea contained spaces when there should have
been data in it.  The worst part was that particular area was smack dab in the
middle of the commarea.

Several hours later and many many displays later I finally decided to put in
one final display and we had it.  

The commarea is defined thus:

01  WS-COMMAREA.
      05  WS-COMM-TYPE   PIC X(6).
      05  WS-COMM-SEQ    PIC S999 COMP-3.
      05  WS-COMM-HEAD  PIC X(350)   ****
      05  WS-COMM-DETAIL OCCURS 4 TIMES PIC X(350) *****

*****  fields were originally PIC X(250) and PIC X(90) respecitvely.


In the procedure division I had this:

PERFORM 4 TIMES
     MOVE SPACES TO WS-COMM-DETAIL (SUB)
END-PERFORM

Well needless to say the subscript was never incremented and WS-COMM-DETAIL (0)
was spaced out.

The first time the CICS program is entered a file containing the invoice
numbers is read and the field in the Header is populated and the Header written
to the VSAM and then returned to the batch.  The next time the CICS program is
entered the Details are written out and only the first 7 positions of the
Header are used.  Since in the old version of the program WS-COMM-DETAIL was
only 90 positions long, the last 90 positions of the 250 were spaced and the
bug wasn't caught.

How long had this been going on?   About 18 months.

Pass the weed please.

Eileen
```

#### ↳ Re: Dumb Code - What Was I Smoking?

- **From:** Jason Adams <jsa@kona.homeip.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-02T23:12:03-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ud47q6efkap7d@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<20020502232928.14065.00004738@mb-ce.aol.com>`

```
YukonMama wrote:

> This was one of those days I shouldn't have gotten out of bed.  Several
....
> Header are used.  Since in the old version of the program WS-COMM-DETAIL
> was only 90 positions long, the last 90 positions of the 250 were spaced
> and the bug wasn't caught.
> 
> How long had this been going on?   About 18 months.

Oh Gawd, Now you've done it....!!
You've started another "TOP THIS" thread of bone headed bugs.

Run Away!!
```

#### ↳ Re: Dumb Code - What Was I Smoking?

- **From:** Edward Reid <edward@paleo.org>
- **Date:** 2002-05-03T09:52:15-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.B8F80E4F0038DFAD0DAA47E0@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<20020502232928.14065.00004738@mb-ce.aol.com>`

```
On Thu, 2 May 2002 23:29:28 -0400, YukonMama wrote
> PERFORM 4 TIMES
>      MOVE SPACES TO WS-COMM-DETAIL (SUB)
> END-PERFORM

This actually relates to the optimization thread.

This is a good example of why subscript range checking should always be 
active. But this is expensive unless it's designed as part of the 
instruction set architecture, so that it can be done in parallel 
without delaying the CPU. Thus good design up front promotes not only 
efficiency but also correctness. It's a feature that cannot be added on 
later without incurring large performance costs.

Unisys (previously Burroughs) MCP systems are designed with subscript 
range checking always active. My experience with these systems is that 
it helps programmers and reduces costs considerably.

Edward Reid
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Dumb Code - What Was I Smoking?

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-03T08:18:08-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aau9ng$p28$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<20020502232928.14065.00004738@mb-ce.aol.com> <01HW.B8F80E4F0038DFAD0DAA47E0@news-east.usenetserver.com>`

```

"Edward Reid" <edward@paleo.org> wrote in message
news:01HW.B8F80E4F0038DFAD0DAA47E0@news-east.usenetserver.com...

> Unisys (previously Burroughs) MCP systems are designed with subscript
> range checking always active. My experience with these systems is that
> it helps programmers and reduces costs considerably.

I agree with the sentiment, but the details are not quite right.

The MCP/AS hardware is designed such that it is impossible to access outside
the physical array that contains the logical record, that's true.  In the
example cited, the subscript would have to be outside of the bounds 0
through +4 to encounter this interrupt.  To get the "granularity" required
here requires explicit object code from the compiler.

By default, MCP/AS COBOL74 does not do "detail" range checking *on indices*
(relying on the "container limits" and the various hardware index-limits
interrupts).   It does do "detail" range checking on subscripts, and that
logic can be omitted by setting $OPTIMIZE.   If "detail" range checking is
not specified, SUB would have to be -1 or less (or +5 or greater) for the
hardware interrupt to occur.  If it is specified, the lower limit is +1.

MCP/AS COBOL85, on the other hand, does explicit range checking by default
for both indices and subscripts.  This is partly because its storage
allocation algorithms make reliance on the container boundaries pretty much
ineffective, and it's difficult to say exactly what the lower and upper
limits provided by the hardware without compiler assistance are.  The detail
range-check logic can be omitted (for both subscripts and indices) by $RESET
BOUNDS (INDEX).

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dumb Code - What Was I Smoking?

- **From:** Edward Reid <edward@paleo.org>
- **Date:** 2002-05-03T22:32:14-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.B8F8C06E0062BC9E0DAA47E0@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<20020502232928.14065.00004738@mb-ce.aol.com> <01HW.B8F80E4F0038DFAD0DAA47E0@news-east.usenetserver.com> <aau9ng$p28$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
On Fri, 3 May 2002 11:18:08 -0400, Chuck Stevens wrote
> I agree with the sentiment, but the details are not quite right.

Ah, yes. Chuck is right of course. I forget that the implementation has 
changed.

With COBOL on the Burroughs B6700 -- circa 1970 -- there was indeed 
architectural support for combining address calculation with subscript 
checking in this kind of situation. This was 1968 COBOL of course -- at 
that time no one thought of distinguishing one version of the standard 
from another.

However, this architectural support proved rather inflexible and was 
gradually dropped over the years. The particular instruction still 
appears to be supported in the ISA, but as Chuck says, the COBOL 
compilers no longer generate it, at least not in this context.

So the current implementation proves that you can get excellent 
performance even WITH subscript range checking and WITHOUT 
architectural support.

Edward Reid
```

## Thread: Is COBOL the answer? — 5 msgs

### Is COBOL the answer?

- **From:** "News Netvisao" <alexandre.albino.2@netvisao.pt>
- **Date:** 2002-05-03T21:48:16+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1020458774.382552@newsfront1>`

```
Hi,  I'm new to the newsgroup and to COBOL. I'm a Delphi and VB6
programmer... I want to know... is COBOL an answer to fast and small
programs. Is it worth it for me to learn it? I currently develop database
aplications using either VB6 or Delphi, both with Access or SQL Server... So
many people tell me cobol is history...is it true? Or is it worth to develop
under it for Windows and DOS?

Thanks
Alex
```

```
COJ
```

#### ↳ Re: Is COBOL the answer?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-03T23:18:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CD31A78.59966191@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<1020458774.382552@newsfront1>`

```


News Netvisao wrote:

> Hi,  I'm new to the newsgroup and to COBOL. I'm a Delphi and VB6
> programmer... I want to know... is COBOL an answer to fast and small
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> under it for Windows and DOS?

A background to a lot of your questions can be found if you go to
cobolreport.com and take a look at Bill Klein's COBOL FAQ - lists compiler
vendors, sites with examples and other useful links on COBOL.

Some short answers to specifics :-

1.  'fast and small programs ' - YES, plus 'fast and BIG programs'.

2.  DBs - COBOL handles, subject to compiler - I for instance currently use a
COBOL wizard which generates necessary COBOL fields from MS Access files, and
even better, the tool allows me to generate the queries and auto paste into my
COBOL source. (Nothing to stop you including SQL in your Business Logic
programs, but typically, using OO, I segregate reference to MS Access tables
into separate classes).

3. You mentioned both Windows and DOS. Any particular reason for DOS ? If so, in
a text environment, as a separate 'text window' within Windows, you can have
text applications - in this respect COBOL Screen Section would be appropriate,
though I confess I think it a  somewhat backward step for somebody already
conversant with VB and Delphi.

4. As is apparent from my comments, OO is available in COBOL - specifically
POWERCOBOL from Fujitsu, and Net Express from Micro Focus.

5. GUIs - GUI classes built into some of the compilers, plus there are GUI
add-on tools from Flexus and Norcom, specifically geared to COBOL programmers.
Nothing to stop COBOL invoking VB or vice versa. Don't know about Delphi, but
assuming it fits into the OLE 'glove', no reason why you can't do the same for
that.

6. COBOL obviously offers the :-

            if
            else if
            End-if ,        syntax, plus 'IFs" can be nested

One area where I think COBOL beats the other languages hands down is what you
call CASE:  Ours has a greater degree of flexibility - the EVALUATE :-

    Evaluate true also true also true also true etc...
        when A = B and C < ??? and D < ???
                        do this.....
        when A > B also any
                        do this
        when etc....
    End-Evaluate

Like "IFs", EVALUATEs can also be nested.

7. Assuming you still want to research, suggest your starting point should be
Bill Klein's COBOL FAQ, which will link you to the different vendors.

OK - one favour deserves another - now you <G>. Just out of curiosity - what has
been the total price you have paid for VB and Delphi, including any packages to
support them ?
.
Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Is COBOL the answer?

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@NO-SPAM.hot.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-04T16:34:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<m4UA8.33796$9F5.1775958@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<1020458774.382552@newsfront1>`

```
It almost leads me to wonder if your question is more:

 Do big central information systems still have a place in a world of PC's and distributed
network systems?

The answer to that is less than obvious, and is usually expressed as 'Does COBOL have ...'
etc. The answer to that is somewhat non-intuitive. When you narrow the scope of an
issue down, it usually appears that either one or the other is the best solution. That is, however,
nothing more than an illusion of scale. Every system needs some kind of central coordination
and some kind of distributed processing. In some systems, one is very much more important
than the other, while in some systems, they tend to balance much more closely.

COBOL fits easily into systems where the emphasis is closely balanced, or weighted towards
a centralized control. It also fits very well into the *parts* of a distributed system than
deal with the necessary centralized controls.

This is of course, no a comprehensive overview - it is just something that I and others have found to be
generally true. I am sure there are exceptions, but in truth, I haven't really *found* any exceptions,
in more than 20 years of doing this stuff...
.


"News Netvisao" <alexandre.albino.2@netvisao.pt> wrote in message news:1020458774.382552@newsfront1...
> Hi,  I'm new to the newsgroup and to COBOL. I'm a Delphi and VB6
> programmer... I want to know... is COBOL an answer to fast and small
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Expo and Standards process

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>

```
Jeanette kindly invited me to be a panellist.

As many of you will know, I have some extremely strong feelings about the
way in which the COBOL Standards have been developed.

Unfortunately, as I am now in Europe and slowly gathering work committments,
it is unlikely I shall be able to attend.

This is one session I would LOVE to be in on and I strongly urge all those
of you who ARE attending to go along to it.

It might be interesting to find out how the Chairman of a Committee that has
failed to deliver anything can accept honours for this non-achievement and
still sleep nights...

OR,

How a Committee that is not answerable to anybody can be expected to deliver
anything.

OR,

How a Committee that is supposed to be supporting COBOL could allow an
Organization like ANSI to try and Copyright the Language.

OR,

Why Bill Klein was blamed and publicly castigated by four senior members of
J4 for simply pointing out a failure in the procedures.

OR,

But enough... don't start me on this... Just go along and take no
prisoners....<G>

Pete.
```

```
> It might be interesting to find out how the Chairman of a Committee that
has
> failed to deliver anything can accept honours for this non-achievement
and
> still sleep nights...
> 
> OR,
> 
> How a Committee that is not answerable to anybody can be expected to
deliver
> anything.
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Why Bill Klein was blamed and publicly castigated by four senior members
of
> J4 for simply pointing out a failure in the procedures.
> 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> > For those of you planning on being at the COBOL Expo in Chicago in
June,
> > *and* who are interested in the Standards process, you might want to
look
> at
> > the abstract at:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Expo and Standards process

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-05T16:54:43-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ab49n8$cv7$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<aavb0p$a3j$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3cd3b90b_4@Usenet.com> <4145699b.0205051323.7e26e84f@posting.google.com>`

```
Stephen,
  I am afraid you are slightly mistaken on the copyright issue.

If you contact the name in the FCD (or FDIS) concerning the copyright issue,
you will find that ISO is "deferring" to ANSI (or more accurately ITI) for
the fact that they are ASSUMING (as in taking over) the *existing* ANSI/ITI
copyright.

NOTE TO ALL:
  If you look at any (all) copies of the '85 Standard or either the '89 or
'91 amendments, they DO already have copyright statements on them.  The
issue/problem is that they ALSO include the "acknowlemenge" which seems (to
many/most) to indicate that the copyright has "minimal" impact.
```

```
> Stephen,
>   I am afraid you are slightly mistaken on the copyright issue.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…

Bill, the copyright statements in previous standards acknowledged that
the standard was based on copyrighted material owned by other
companies, who were allowing it to be used.  It was NOT copyrighted by
NCITS, ITI, ANSI, ISo, etc...

What a bureaucrat SAYS about the copyright is of little consequence.
If ISO claims a copyright, they can enforce it at any time.  We EXPECT
a FREEly downloadable copy of the final standard.

I will repeat: If they CLAIM a copyright, I will file criminal
charges.

Stephen J Spiro
```

- **References:** `<aavb0p$a3j$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3cd3b90b_4@Usenet.com> <4145699b.0205051323.7e26e84f@posting.google.com> <ab49n8$cv7$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <4145699b.0205062318.8da9f45@posting.google.com>`

```
"Stephen J Spiro" <stephenjspiro@hotmail.com> wrote in message
<snip>
> >
> Bill, the copyright statements in previous standards acknowledged that
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Stephen,
  You are confusing the "Acknowledgment" page with the COPYRIGHT statement,
read the bottom of the "back" of the page with the acknowledgement (of the
'85 Standard) which states rather explicitly,

"Copyright (c) 1985 by American National Standards Institute, Inc
All rights reserved.
No part of this publication may be reproduced in any form,
in an electronic retrieval system or otherwise, without
the prior written permission of the publisher."

Again, this SEEMS to conflict with the "Acknowledgement" itself, but it does
show that what is in the draft Standard is NOT (particularly) new - nor
something that ISO has "thought up".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Expo and Standards process

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-07T12:57:02-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<udg1hnjt2egaea@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<aavb0p$a3j$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3cd3b90b_4@Usenet.com> <4145699b.0205051323.7e26e84f@posting.google.com> <ab49n8$cv7$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <4145699b.0205062318.8da9f45@posting.google.com> <ab8gv3$j0f$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:ab8gv3$j0f$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net...
>
> "Stephen J Spiro" <stephenjspiro@hotmail.com> wrote in message
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>   You are confusing the "Acknowledgment" page with the COPYRIGHT
statement,
> read the bottom of the "back" of the page with the acknowledgement (of the
> '85 Standard) which states rather explicitly,
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Again, this SEEMS to conflict with the "Acknowledgement" itself, but it
does
> show that what is in the draft Standard is NOT (particularly) new - nor
> something that ISO has "thought up".

I think the conflict involves "fair use" of copyrighted material.

I see no problem with the ANSI and ISO copyrights; provided that both
organizations accept that "fair use" is defined by the acknowledgement.
That is, "... authorization extends to the reproduction and use of COBOL
specifications in programming manuals or similar publications."

Hence, reproduction of the standard, as a standard and in competition
with ANSI or ISO, is infringement of their respective copyrights and,
therefore, requires permission and may be subject to royalty payments.

Reproduction, of those parts of the standard that define COBOL, when
used in a programming manual, does not infringe, does not require
permission, and is not subject to royalty payments.
----------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Expo and Standards process

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-05-07T01:43:46+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3cd726e3_8@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<aavb0p$a3j$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3cd3b90b_4@Usenet.com> <4145699b.0205051323.7e26e84f@posting.google.com>`

```

Stephen J Spiro <stephenjspiro@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:4145699b.0205051323.7e26e84f@posting.google.com...
> "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote
> <snip>
> > It might be interesting to find out how the Chairman of a Committee that
has
> > failed to deliver anything can accept honours for this non-achievement
and
> > still sleep nights...
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Thanks for the response, Stephen.

As always, my comments should not be taken personally (except where I intend
them to be...<G>) and I know you have been a faithful and steadfast member
of J4 for some time. (In fact, you are on record as saying that such
membership has allowed you to increase your chargeout rate, so I guess your
interest hasn't been entirely altruistic...Nevertheless, I'm sure you have
contributed wherever you were able, and your stand on the Copyright issue is
admirable and commendable.)

However, your response above raises two questions in my mind:

1. So how come NO VENDOR currently complies with the Draft Standard?

Could it be that they have actually been researching the Market Place and
providing what people WANT, rather than what J4 has suggested? I take it you
have concrete evidence of Vendors incorporating work done by J4?  If this
work was completed by J4 (and it is unlikely that vendors would implement it
if it wasn't complete), then why wasn't it published? If, on the other hand,
some
ideas being explored by J4 were passed to vendors who then refined them and
implemented them, then it is hardly fair for J4 to take the credit, and
again, I have to ask, if a vendor can implement it, why can't J4 publish it?

2. If Vendors actually rely on J4 for ideas why aren't J4 answerable to
them? Has no vendor commented on the remarkable lack of material coming from
J4, or is it simply true that corporate sponsorship of J4 has been steadily
declining, resulting in a marked lessening of Boondoogles and corresponding
drop in Committee morale?

Now, if you can show that Vendors are really positive about the work of J4
and have actually increased Corporate sponsorship over the past 5 years
(actual figures please...) then I'd be forced to withdraw my comment and
review my position. (I would then have to wonder why Vendors are behaving so
stupidly... but that's another issue...<G>).

I would also apologise. (I always do when, and only when, I am genuinely
sorry...).


> > How a Committee that is supposed to be supporting COBOL could allow an
> > Organization like ANSI to try and Copyright the Language.
> >
> First of all, it's ISO, not ANSI.  If you are going to pass out
> insults, get your facts right.

Well, actually, it's you who hasn't got your facts right on this one. (See
Bill K's post in this thread...)

Irrespective, I shall pass out "insults" wherever I see fit whether what you
call the "facts", are right or not (obviously, I make every effort to be
accurate because it weakens my argument if I'm not...<G>).

That's what the Internet and open unmoderated forums (like this one), are
for... So there is no "if" about it. Rest assured, where I see things that
really piss me off, I'll be commenting on it. Some of these unpleasantries
may be construed as "insults"...that's up to you. J4 has really pissed me
off.(And BTW, I'm not the ONLY one...).

> Second of all, J4 has not "allowed" it, but has objected strenuously,
> and in writing, numerous times.  If you are going to pass out insults,
> get your facts right.

OK, I'll take your word on that (based on past experience, I have found you
to be honest (though sometimes mistaken <G>)).

Again, there is no way I'll be stopped from commenting. In this case, you
are assuring the rest of the
Forum that J4 has "objected strenuously, and in writing, numerous times."
So, why did the Standards body not change its position or publicly reassure
all interested parties that the Copyright issue was a non starter?

They could have posted a simple message here, f'rinstance. Given that there
was no change in position, why didn't J4 remove itself from the ANSI/ISO
arena? (I can draw my own conclusions on that one...it has to do with geese
and golden eggs...).

Given there was no public commitment to keep COBOL in the spirit in which it
was intended, given that the "strenuous objections" from J4 were not made
public (or, if they were, I certainly missed it...), can you not see that
the data an outside observer (such as myself) would be operating on (my
"facts"), would lead me to the conclusion I expressed? Given that you can
see that, would you then allow that I was NOT simply passing out insults
without getting my facts right, rather, I was expressing dismay at what
appeared to be the case - COBOL was being hijacked?

Well, whether you allow it or not, that is what happened. It comes back
again to no answerability. There was no public statement from J4 or ANSI/ISO
(why should they bother to involve the general COBOL public...?). All I can
say is that if I had been running that committee there would have been. (And
some improved general communication could have averted much wrath...)

> Third of all, I have warned NCITS and ISO that if they try to enforce
> a copyright, including the distribution of the new standard with a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

This is really excellent, Stephen. (I'm sure they're all trembling in their
boots...<G>)

Actually, you have done the decent and honourable thing. But imagine how
much MORE effective this could have been if EVERYONE on J4 publicly adopted
this position? Why only Stephen Spiro? Are we to infer that the other
Committee members have no real objection to COBOL losing its Public Domain s
tatus?

I wanna know...WHO'S RESPONSIBLE!!! <G>

Pete.





 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Expo and Standards process

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<aavb0p$a3j$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3cd3b90b_4@Usenet.com> <4145699b.0205051323.7e26e84f@posting.google.com> <3cd726e3_8@Usenet.com> <bfdfc3e8.0205070529.15867556@posting.google.com>`

```

Thane Hubbell <thaneh@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:bfdfc3e8.0205070529.15867556@posting.google.com...
> With all due respect Pete ....
>
OK, likewise, Thane...<G>
> "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:<3cd726e3_8@Usenet.com>...
> > 1. So how come NO VENDOR currently complies with the Draft Standard?
> >

and

you

hand,

> > ideas being explored by J4 were passed to vendors who then refined them
and
> > implemented them, then it is hardly fair for J4 to take the credit, and
> > again, I have to ask, if a vendor can implement it, why can't J4 publish
it?
> >
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I think this simply reinforces what I said... Why weren't these "stable"
parts published?
(Something like a prgressive release, leading up to a final release...)

> > 2. If Vendors actually rely on J4 for ideas why aren't J4 answerable to
> > them? Has no vendor commented on the remarkable lack of material coming
from
> > J4, or is it simply true that corporate sponsorship of J4 has been
steadily
> > declining, resulting in a marked lessening of Boondoogles and
corresponding
> > drop in Committee morale?
> >
>
> This is all just doublespeak gobbldegook and doesn't mean anything in
> the real world of the standards process as I observe it.

Sorry if this is not clear enough for you. I've been accused of many things
(sometimes quite accurately), but never of "doublespeak gobbledygook".... (I
believe that is redundant  <G>) I avoid traps like that when I write English
<G>.
 My suggestion (rephrased) is that Corporate sponsorship of the Standards
process has been declining as Vendors realise it has no credibility and is
not really producing anything. Of course I can't (or won't...) prove it but
nevertheless, it IS my belief.

I understand and accept your opposition to this suggestion. I also know that
your intentions are honourable.

> I know of at
> least one vendor who has increased (infinitely) their spending on the
> Standards process in the last 5 years.

"Infinitely"?  I don't think so.

While I can accept a degree of rhetorical hyperbole here, your argument
would be much more persuasive if you could quote actual figures or even
percentages. At the moment, this argument is a tie... neither of us being
prepared to back our positions with hard evidence.


>Others have been consolidated
> via merger.  But the committee is NOT made up primarily of vendor
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > They could have posted a simple message here, f'rinstance. Given that
there
> > was no change in position, why didn't J4 remove itself from the ANSI/ISO
> > arena? (I can draw my own conclusions on that one...it has to do with
geese
> > and golden eggs...).
> >
>
> "Here" is an inappropriate forum for such a notification.

No it isn't. It is a Public COBOL Forum. Not the ONLY one but a quite
important one nevertheless. Notification should have been published to ALL
interested COBOL parties. (It would have been if I had been running it.)

One of my major criticisms of the whole J4/ANSI/ISO/Alphabet Soup
establishment is the failure to communicate with the grass roots of COBOL.
Instead they have adopted a "closed doors" approach with decisions regarding
the Language being taken by unelected elite committees who can buy their way
in for the sake of what looks good on a CV.

Even if they were ALL highly dedicated altruistic people (and I believe some
of them (certainly, present company <G>) are, it is NOT the way to go about
it.)

Small wonder that  many of the rest of us see it as high handed and out of
touch.

The fact is that they BEHAVED as if the hoi polloi were of no account.

Governments who behave in this way have consistently been overthrown by said
hoi polloi...<G>

> Contrary to
> the assumption of members of this group - Comp.lang.cobol is not
> demographically representative of the COBOL community at large.  It's
> not even a microcosm of the community.
>

No, it isn't. And you are very right to make that point. However, it IS
Public and it IS frequented by people with a vested interest in COBOL.

If you consider the subscribers to this NG as a percentage of the claimed
3,000,000 COBOL programmers World Wide, your comment is fair and
irrefutable.

However, lately I've been having some doubts about this claimed 3,000,000...
Where are they? Many of them are retiring or dying. I have been keeping an
eye on the European job scene over the last month and I can tell you that
COBOL requirements are less than .001% (actually, in 28,000 IT jobs, today
there were 9 who required COBOL).

So maybe CLC has more influence than you might think. (It MAY represent a
larger percentage of the "COBOL Community" than we are giving it credit for.
There are MANY people who lurk here regularly and never post. I know because
I get private mail from some of them...)

> > Given there was no public commitment to keep COBOL in the spirit in
which it
> > was intended, given that the "strenuous objections" from J4 were not
made
> > public (or, if they were, I certainly missed it...), can you not see
that
> > the data an outside observer (such as myself) would be operating on (my
> > "facts"), would lead me to the conclusion I expressed? Given that you
can
> > see that, would you then allow that I was NOT simply passing out insults
> > without getting my facts right, rather, I was expressing dismay at what
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
The original Charter published with COBOL 59 (and which I shouldn't need to
remind you of, Mr Hubbell <G>) said (and I am paraphrasing), that the
Organisations involved in CODASYL were donating their time and expertise in
the spirit of co-operation on achieving a platform independent "high level"
language. As such it would NOT be the property of any Organisation or
entity. (This was an early attempt at what we now recognise as "Public
Domain").

We then have an Organisation come along and slap their Copyright on it and
charge $100 to get a copy of it. I call that a "hi-jack". (And that's being
polite... when I first read about this my actual words were: ..."downright
fucking diabolical liberty"...<G>)

Anyway, as we are being civilised here, I'll drop that subject.

> >
> > Actually, you have done the decent and honourable thing. But imagine how
> > much MORE effective this could have been if EVERYONE on J4 publicly
adopted
> > this position? Why only Stephen Spiro? Are we to infer that the other
> > Committee members have no real objection to COBOL losing its Public
Domain s
> > tatus?
> >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> can't lose a status it never had.

Good try. See above. Better still, read the original Charter which is
SUPPOSED to be reprinted in every COBOL Manual...

Despite our differences on this, Thane, I do accept that there ARE people
involved with (or have been involved with) the Standard who do mean well
(You and Bill for a start, and probably Stephen too...) Nevertheless, the
record shows that no new standard has been produced for 17 years and it has
a continual history of postponement. Adding insult to this injury has been a
parade of self congratulation and back patting, intellectual snobbery (in my
book the very worst kind of snobbery), a scurrillious  and unwarranted
attack on Bill Klein as a reason for non-delivery, and a total disregard for
anyone other than large corporations with clout (read "money"). It's like
they have worked to the Golden Rule: "Whoever has the gold, makes the
rules...".

Fortunately, it contains the seeds of its own destruction. As credibility
shrinks, vendors will go their own way (those that opt to stay in the COBOL
business at all) and eventually the "COBOL Standard" will stop generating
revenue for ANSI/ISO so they'll move it to the back burner... As the
essential requirement to have COBOL in the Corporation diminishes, so there
will be less pressure from the Users for more features. With or without a
Standard the language is in decline. It can be argued that if there had been
a more lively and dynamically responsive Standards body this decline might
have been slowed or even stopped, but we'll never know now.

It is becoming apparent that if vendors can produce the product for 17 years
without a viable Standard, why should they need one now?

The ONLY ones who emerge with credit from this whole sorry mess are the
vendors. Driven by the Market Place (not the Standards Authority) they have
consistently provided what the Marketplace needed. Many of them have shown
faith in COBOL even when it looked like going into decline and the fact that
I can compile COBOL components for today's cutting edge technology is a
tribute to them. I, for one, am grateful. However, forces beyond the power
of even the vendors are coming into play and we will see more COBOL compiler
vendors leaving the arena within the next 5 years.

IBM have already voted with their feet towards Java. They will mantain
fortress COBOL until it dies by natural attrition (say, 10 - 15 years) and
in the interim we will see no COBOL innovation from them.

This "Gotterdammerung" we are currently witnessing has been attributable in
no small part to the hopeless inefficiency of the COBOL Standards authority.
The language has lost credibility as the Standard has lost credibility. (In
fact, there are even Academic papers which have been published showing how
COBOL does not meet its own design criteria because of the plethora of
different implementations of it... due to historical lack of
Standardisation. While I don't agree with the authors of these papers, I
have to admit that papers like this, published from a reputable University,
will cut some ice. Eventually, Acadaemia DOES influence the Market Place...)

To be fair, the Standards Authority cannot be entirely blamed for the demise
of COBOL.

But they can be blamed for speeding it. And for all the other things I
mentioned above.

Do we see any recognition of fault on their part? Any apology? Any
reconstruction of internal processes, evaluation or analysis of why delivery
failed? Any moves to improve communication and open the Standards process?

Nope. Just the opposite. "Didn't we do well..." Let's have another
boondoggle and pat ourselves on the back...

It makes me sick.

Pete.
```

```
Interesting news for Mr. Dashwood et all below...

I'm going to snip a lot and only answer a few points - in an effort
only to correct misinformation and not in any effort to discredit or
denegrate anyone or their opinions.

"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message news:<3cd80b61_6@Usenet.com>...

> I think this simply reinforces what I said... Why weren't these "stable"
> parts published?
> (Something like a prgressive release, leading up to a final release...)
> 

Nothing is sacred until the fat lady sings.  She's warming up her
voice now.

> > > 2. If Vendors actually rely on J4 for ideas why aren't J4 answerable to
> > > them? Has no vendor commented on the remarkable lack of material coming
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

You assume Corporate sponsorship has declined.  No evidence of that. 
You assume Morale is low - no evidence of that either.  You assume it
lacks credibility - no evidence of that - and in fact evidence to the
fact that an ISO standard is DESIRED - look at .NET, and the CIL and
C#.


> I understand and accept your opposition to this suggestion. I also know that
> your intentions are honourable.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Well - yes - Infinite.  From ZERO dollars to X - (I am prohibited by
contract for sharing $$) - but even if it is $1.00 - it's an infinite
increase. <G>


> One of my major criticisms of the whole J4/ANSI/ISO/Alphabet Soup
> establishment is the failure to communicate with the grass roots of COBOL.
> Instead they have adopted a "closed doors" approach with decisions regarding
> the Language being taken by unelected elite committees who can buy their way
> in for the sake of what looks good on a CV.

This is just patently FALSE.  The proceedings are public record - and
the meetings are OPEN to any interested in attending.  Observer
opinions are even counted in straw votes.  (Though not binding).  What
I am saying is that the information is NOT hidden - it's just not
within the financial means of the organizations to spend the
advertising $$ you might want them to spend!  There is nothing hidden
here.

> 
> Small wonder that  many of the rest of us see it as high handed and out of
> touch.

This is a misperception.

>> 
> However, lately I've been having some doubts about this claimed 3,000,000...
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Busy working.
 
> So maybe CLC has more influence than you might think. (It MAY represent a
> larger percentage of the "COBOL Community" than we are giving it credit for.
> There are MANY people who lurk here regularly and never post. I know because
> I get private mail from some of them...)

It's influence is out of proportion to its representation of the
community.  Squeeky wheel and all.

Keep reading for the important information...

> >
> > Hijacked?  By whom?  What are the demands of the hijackers?
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>


No - let's not drop it.  The acknowledgment remains in the FDS as does
this *NEW* statement from ISO:

Copyright notice

This ISO document is a draft International Standard and is
copyright-protected by ISO.

ISO grants permission to the reproduction, storing, or transmittal in
any form of this draft International Standard
in whole or in part, without charge, provided that this Copyright
Notice is included.

-----
Whats that sound I hear?  Bubble bursting?  Balloon Deflating?  
 
This is GOOD for COBOL and it is THANKS not to those who RAILED
against the copyright with anger and vitrol, but actually thanks to
the WG4 Convener who, through proper ISO channels quietly and
respectfully requested that this be allowed.
```

```
You assume it hasn't. No evidence of that. You assume morale is not low. No

As for ISO standardization being DESIRED, well there are many areas where it

continues to be the case, with particles popping in and out of "reality" all
the time. Nevertheless, the Universe as we understand it is NOT infinite. It
is simply boundless. Full marks for humour (always welcome...) but none in

Touche! <G>   I wish it were true... Today there were suddenly 37 jobs
requiring COBOL so it may not be so dismal as it appears. It is still a very
low percentage of 28,000...
>
> > So maybe CLC has more influence than you might think. (It MAY represent

How dare you! Squeaky wheels may well get the grease but that is because
they need it.  What you are saying is that the "Real" COBOL Community is not
involved here. Your definition of "real" would be those who meekly accept
the situation regarding Standards and don't make a fuss about it.

So, CLC is a bunch of malcontents who exercise much more influence than they
should? Long may it remain so.

You may dismiss the objections voiced here by saying it is not
representative of the COBOL community, but the only one you are fooling is
yourself. This is EXACTLY the attitude I was writing about in my previous
post... If it isn't Corporate it doesn't matter; it is only the Great
Unwashed...the hoi polloi. They have no say because they have no funding so
just ignore them. Besides, they really don't know enough about the issues
for their contribution to matter and we CERTAINLY aren't going to be
accountable or answerable to THEM...

The FACT is that the people who subscribe to this NG are the ones who are
using COBOL every day. They SHOULD have a voice in the Standardization of
the Language and the Standards Authority should be pressing them to do so,
not haughtily allowing that some things may be made public, depending on the
good graces of the Committee.

Instead, we are treated with contempt and dismissed as an unimportant,
unrepresentative sample who simply make a noise.

Then you wonder why I am anti-Alphabet Soup...
```

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3cd89fdd_5@Usenet.com...
> Your definition of "real" would be those who meekly accept
> the situation regarding Standards and don't make a fuss about it.

You didn't ask me, but I'd have to weigh in that for every COBOL programmer
that spends more than ten minutes per day examining the contents of
comp.lang.cobol there are many hundreds who are actually developing and
maintaining COBOL code around the world.

The real COBOL programmers are the ones who are spending their time
programming in COBOL, not the ones who are debating the processes by which
the rules of the language are developed.  Precious few of the former group
have time to bother.  Real COBOL programmers spend most of their
COBOL-related energy writing real COBOL programs for real companies, not
discussing international politics.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Expo and Standards process

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-08T12:56:45-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<abbot7$hnd$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<aavb0p$a3j$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3cd3b90b_4@Usenet.com> <4145699b.0205051323.7e26e84f@posting.google.com> <3cd726e3_8@Usenet.com> <bfdfc3e8.0205070529.15867556@posting.google.com> <3cd80b61_6@Usenet.com> <bfdfc3e8.0205071700.79cf9831@posting.google.com> <3cd89fdd_5@Usenet.com> <abbljp$158r$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
Chuck,
  I agree with your assessment of the "percentages" involved - if not the
reasons for it.

I would ask, however, given that "real COBOL programmers" don't have (or
make) the time to participate in comp.lang.cobol (much less directly in the
Standards process),

  HOW,

do you think that the Standards groups (J4, WG4, anyone else) can get
"input" on WHAT to add into revisions - or if what they have proposed
actually meets their needs/desires?

If you took every "official" and unofficial review from 1993-2002 and
counted the INDIVIDUALS who actually commented on what was in (was going to
be in) the next COBOL Standard (much less what they WANTED to be in), I
think you would find a SMALLER number than those who participate regularly
in CLC.

I can't speak for MOST vendors, but from my personal knowledge of IBM and
Micro Focus "user groups" and "conventions" - it seems to me that the number
of people providing input/direction to vendors (via these channels) is
greater than those who participate in J4 and/or CLC, but NOT MUCH greater -
and certainly below the 2-5% of the total "real COBOL programmer" community.

It is my "best guess" that J4 (and WG4) take THEIR best guesses as to what
programmers SHOULD want (in the next 5-10 years), but I still don't see
there being much "real world programmers" input to the existing process.
(There have been frequent comments here and elsewhere on how FEW of the J4
"vendor participants" have written a "real" production COBOL program in the
last decade.  Certainly SOME have, but equally certainly not all.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Expo and Standards process

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Edward Reid <edward@paleo.org>
- **Date:** 2002-05-10T10:00:00-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.B9014AA001741DA90DA4B290@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<aavb0p$a3j$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3cd3b90b_4@Usenet.com> <4145699b.0205051323.7e26e84f@posting.google.com> <3cd726e3_8@Usenet.com> <bfdfc3e8.0205070529.15867556@posting.google.com> <3cd80b61_6@Usenet.com> <bfdfc3e8.0205071700.79cf9831@posting.google.com> <3cd89fdd_5@Usenet.com> <abbljp$158r$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3CD96FED.68DA7E5A@shaw.ca>`

```
On Wed, 8 May 2002 14:36:28 -0400, James J. Gavan wrote
> Sorry Chuck,
> 
> You blew it. I'll give you the benefit of the doubt, but at first read, the
> above comes across as bloody condescending.

Jimmy,

I certainly did not read Chuck's comments as condescending. I felt he 
was just stating facts. In the large world of COBOL, we need people 
doing various kinds of tasks -- and this requires various kinds of 
people. For the most part, people are doing the kinds of tasks they 
prefer, and have a strong lack of interest in many of the other tasks.

For example, some of the tasks are:

-- Writing new COBOL programs for company-specific applications
-- Maintaining and modifying those COBOL programs
-- Discussing applications written in COBOL
-- Discussing the process of writing COBOL
-- Writing general-purpose software
-- Writing program generators
-- Writing compilers
-- Extending the language
-- Standarizing the language

These tasks require a variety of skills and personal preferences, and 
you just don't find the skills and preferences for all tasks in any one 
person. The person is is both happy and competent at developing 
applications that support company operations often has little or no 
interest in discussing the process, or in hypothesizing how the process 
might be better, etc. This takes nothing away from that person's 
abilities -- it's just like saying that a bus driver is a different 
person from a farmer. But we very much need all these different tasks, 
and the different kinds of people who orient to these tasks.

If you graphed the number of people engaged in each of these tasks, 
you'd find (very roughly!) rapidly decreasing numbers from the top of 
the list to the end.

Now, I don't think these distinctions have very much to do with who has 
time. If someone who works at writing new custom COBOL programs wants 
to spend a few hours a week working in standardizing the language, 
he/she can certainly do so. Most programmers have many hours per week 
of personal time. Yes, it's hard to get paid for such work, but there 
aren't a lot of people interested either -- not compared with the 
number interested in doing basic programming. As it turns out, the 
numbers of people needed for the various tasks roughly (very roughly) 
corresponds to the number interested. And which came first, the chicken 
or the egg?

The controversies arise with the last two categories in my list -- 
those who extend the language and those who standardize the language -- 
because their work has a profound effect on the others. They bear much 
more of a responsibility for other parts of the COBOL community than do 
people acting in the other capacities listed. It's thus important that 
the people empowered to do these tasks take responsibility for their 
broad effect, and show this in a variety of ways, including good 
communication (both in and out!). When people in such positions become 
solipsistic or try to feather their own nests, they abrogate their 
responsibility.

I do not intend the above discussion to imply any comments about 
current instantiations of any of the categories, but only to help 
provide a bit of framework for discussing the broad variety of tasks 
and people.

Edward Reid
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Expo and Standards process

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2002-05-09T21:02:42-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0205092002.42d857e3@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<aavb0p$a3j$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3cd3b90b_4@Usenet.com> <4145699b.0205051323.7e26e84f@posting.google.com> <3cd726e3_8@Usenet.com> <bfdfc3e8.0205070529.15867556@posting.google.com> <3cd80b61_6@Usenet.com> <bfdfc3e8.0205071700.79cf9831@posting.google.com> <3cd89fdd_5@Usenet.com> <bfdfc3e8.0205090531.cde2eab@posting.google.com>`

```
Just a couple of comments on various posts...

In North America, a "boondoggle" is an expensive, badly planned,
unsuccessful or unnecessary  project.  Such as building a school in a
swamp, or almost any "defense" project of which you can think, or
paving the dirt road past the County Supervisor's farm... One of my
favorites in the corporate world was Planter's "Chocolate covered
cashews".  Planter's went public with a huge inventory for the
expected enormous sales.  They had been tried out in the company
stores (for employees) and were a FABULOUS success.  The product
BOMBED.  Afterwards, someone pointed out that everything in the
company stores are sold at about 25% of retail.....


COBOL Draft Standards have been published every few months since I
joined the committee in 1995, and certainly before that.  Almost all
of the compiler makers who are not ON the committee nevertheless
subscribe to the documentation, and get the drafts, as well as the
discussion papers (now it's all posted on the web, but you could get
paper copies of everything until quite recently).
Most (not all!) of the new stuff in COBOL compilers came from J4 (or
it's predecessors), not the other way around.  Some compiler-makers
have already implemented almost all of the new standard.   IBM's new
Enterprise COBOL is not missing much, for instance.

I intend to keep making an uproar about the copyright; if I don't they
will do nothing to preserve OUR rights.

Stephen J Spiro
Member, J4 COBOL  Committee
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Expo and Standards process

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-09T23:38:06-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<abfiro$tb6$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<aavb0p$a3j$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3cd3b90b_4@Usenet.com> <4145699b.0205051323.7e26e84f@posting.google.com> <3cd726e3_8@Usenet.com> <bfdfc3e8.0205070529.15867556@posting.google.com> <3cd80b61_6@Usenet.com> <bfdfc3e8.0205071700.79cf9831@posting.google.com> <3cd89fdd_5@Usenet.com> <bfdfc3e8.0205090531.cde2eab@posting.google.com> <4145699b.0205092002.42d857e3@posting.google.com>`

```
"Stephen J Spiro" <stephenjspiro@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:4145699b.0205092002.42d857e3@posting.google.com...
  <snip>   Some compiler-makers
> have already implemented almost all of the new standard.   IBM's new
> Enterprise COBOL is not missing much, for instance.
>

Stephen,
  What makes you think this?

A) The OO is DECIDELY not what is in the FCD (or FDIS)
B) They have left out all (most) of the new USAGEs (such as bit and
true-binary and floating-point)
C) There is no STANDARD ARITHMETIC
D) There is no "common" EXCEPTION HANDLING
E)  There NATIONAL support doesn't match what is in the FCD/FDIS
F) Even with the "add-on" they don't support the (required) Report Writer
enhancements
G) They don't have user-defined functions
H) They don't have conditional compilation
I) They don't have (your favorite <G>) free-format reference format
J) They don't have any of the new EXIT features (that most PC compilers have
had for years)

and, of course, this doesn't even start on IBM mainframe desirable
"processor dependent" items such as READ PREVIOUS and (possibly)
record-locking/file-sharing

GENERALLY, IBM Enterprise COBOL for z/OS & OS/390 (of course not even
available on VM, VSE, OS/2, Windows, or AIX) is a RELATIVELY minor
enhancement over the previous IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM - with changed (but
not matching the FCD) OO Support (that doesn't even run except under USS),
no CMPR2 support, and initial one-way XML support.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Expo and Standards process

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Edward Reid <edward@paleo.org>
- **Date:** 2002-05-10T09:18:49-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.B90140F90171D8F90DA4B290@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<aavb0p$a3j$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3cd3b90b_4@Usenet.com> <4145699b.0205051323.7e26e84f@posting.google.com> <3cd726e3_8@Usenet.com> <bfdfc3e8.0205070529.15867556@posting.google.com> <3cd80b61_6@Usenet.com> <bfdfc3e8.0205071700.79cf9831@posting.google.com> <3cd89fdd_5@Usenet.com> <bfdfc3e8.0205090531.cde2eab@posting.google.com> <4145699b.0205092002.42d857e3@posting.google.com>`

```
On Fri, 10 May 2002 0:02:42 -0400, Stephen J Spiro wrote
> In North America, a "boondoggle" is an expensive, badly planned,
> unsuccessful or unnecessary  project. 

Well, purely to carp on the semantics of slang ...

"Boondoggle" usually implies in addition a significant personal benefit 
to one of the participants. A large committee meeting in Bermuda might 
often be viewed as a boondoggle; the same meeting in Baltimore would 
seldom be so viewed. Stephen's example of paving the road past the 
County Supervisor's farm clearly qualifies. But the Planter's chocolate 
covered cashews example probably does not, as at least from this 
description, it does not appear to have conferred any personal 
benefits.

Goodness but I'm feeling picky this morning.

Edward Reid
```

```
enlightened us:

>On Fri, 10 May 2002 0:02:42 -0400, Stephen J Spiro wrote
>> In North America, a "boondoggle" is an expensive, badly planned,
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well since we are talking semantics here, The American Heritageï¿½
Dictionary of the English Language, Fourth Edition gives two
definitions for boondoggle and neither has anything to do with
personal profit or benefit.  Stephen's explanation is one possible
definition.  Here are both:

1.  An unnecessary or wasteful project or activity. 

2.  A braided leather cord worn as a decoration especially by Boy
Scouts. A cord of braided leather, fabric, or plastic strips made by a
child as a project to keep busy. 

Regards,



          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

"How come we choose from just two people for 
president and 50 for Miss America?"
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

```
"When I use a word", said Humpty Dumpty, "it means exactly what I choose it
to mean.".

"The question is", said Alice, "whether you CAN use words that way.".

"The question is", replied Humpty Dumpty, "who is to be the Master. That is
all.".
---Lewis Carroll---

Comments below, Steve...

SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com> wrote in message
news:0B7C8C86F5C28047.4B57415AF2D91BBD.5396B5B649ED4276@lp.airnews.net...
> On Fri, 10 May 2002 9:18:49 -0400, Edward Reid <edward@paleo.org>
> enlightened us:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I submit that my original post complies with meaning '1' above.

Here it is again:
======================= quote from previous post to this thread
=======================
"I was introduced to this term many years ago by an American colleague while
working in Australia.

It may have a different meaning in Canada, but when I use it, it means:

"An excuse to get off real work and go somewhere exotic at Corporate
expense. An undeserved reward."

For example, Sales are down but the Marketing Manager decides for all Sales
people to go to a luxury hotel at Corporate expense for the weekend to run a
"Motivational projection" seminar. They all know they don't deserve it, but
they all go anyway. Two hours are spent in the Seminar and the rest in the
pool and bars...The Staff justify it by saying: "Well, if they're stupid
enough to do this, I'll go along..." No actual motivation is achieved and
money that could be used for much more worthwhile purposes is actually
wasted..."
====================  End quote from previous post  ================

The second meaning you provide relates to what is known to Kiwi Boy Scouts
as a "Lanyard and toggle" or, sometimes, a "floggletoggle".  It is
interesting to see the subtle differences in similar terms around the World.

The one about being a pointless exercise for a child is new to me. Why would
you give a child a pointless exercise? Especially when they keep screening
Teletubbies on TV...<G>

(No reflection on Teletubbies; it is EXCELLENT entertainment for adults and
I love it... Tubby toast! Tubby toast!)

Pete.





 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services

----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Expo and Standards process

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2002-05-07T18:13:46-07:00

```
I find that the Copyright statement *MAY* be subject to change.  This
is still quite controversial - and I suggest that quiet acceptence of
how it is NOW may be the best way to proceed -- but to each his own.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Expo and Standards process

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-08T03:41:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CD89E17.136DDD3D@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<aavb0p$a3j$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3cd3b90b_4@Usenet.com> <4145699b.0205051323.7e26e84f@posting.google.com> <3cd726e3_8@Usenet.com> <bfdfc3e8.0205070529.15867556@posting.google.com> <3cd80b61_6@Usenet.com> <bfdfc3e8.0205071713.45640d41@posting.google.com>`

```


Thane Hubbell wrote:

> And now for the grain of salt.....
>
> I find that the Copyright statement *MAY* be subject to change.  This
> is still quite controversial - and I suggest that quiet acceptence of
> how it is NOW may be the best way to proceed -- but to each his own.

Well so far as the 'famous' copyright is concerned, it was generally
understood by the COBOL community that following on the 40-odd year,
"This is copyrighted by Univac , Flowmatic etc.." whatever the wording
was, that we ALL had access to COBOL. Suddenly, the 'intrusion' on that
copyright, first pointed out by Bill and Stephen. The latter got vocal -
and damn well should have done ! So from 'peace', Stephen saw the
justification for putting the gloves on and going to war.

My thanks to Ann, behind the scenes doing her bit, but recall that Don,
Chair of J4,  THANKED THEM BOTH, for triggering this observation. Having
the error of ISO's ways pointed out, it would have made even more sense
had J4 as a GROUP,  strenuously objected to the ISO intrusion into what
was already a non copyright issue. (Just think of that stupid boondoggle,
where due to a clerical error, somebody forgot to mention, "Sorry guys,
your license has expired").

This is one where quiet acceptance SHOULD NOT be used to compromise.
First off, I'll go along with the ISO statement PROVIDED it fully
acknowledges that they are acting as "successors" to the "This is Univac
and Flowmatic etc...", statement, and quite clearly confirm you can
continue to extract from the Standard, as you have been able to do for
the past forty years !
(See quote below).

With your suggestion of  'quiet acceptance', there is always the
possibility that we could finish up the losers because of a further
bureaucratic boondoggle.

Now this extract from Jerome Garfunkel's "COBOL 85 Example Book" - he
used to be a member of J4/CODASYL :-

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------

COBOL is an industry language and is not the property of any company or
group of companies, or of any organization or group of organizations.

No warranty, expressed or implied, is made by any contributor or the
CODASYL COBOL Committee as the accuracy and functioning of the
programming system and language. Moreover, no responsibility is assumed
by any contributor, or by the committee, in connection therewith.

The authors and copyright holders of copyrighted material used herein

    FLOW-MATIC (trade mark of Sperry-Rand Corporation), Programming for
the
    Univac I and II, Data Automation Systems copyrighted 1958, 1959 by
Sperry
    Rand Corporation; IBM Commercial Translator Form  No. F28-8013,
    copyrighted 1959 by IBM; FACT, DS1 27A5260-2760, copyrighted 1960 by
    Minneapolis-Honeywell

have specifically authorized the use of this material in whole or in
part, in the COBOL specification. Such authorization extends to the
reproduction and use of COBOL specifications in programming manuals and
similar publications.
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

AND as the ISO statement now appears - see a previous reference that Bill
posted,. I can't damn-well cut and paste from the Adobe document, dated
2001 Dec 04, but when I look at the ISO acknowledgment, it looks
identical to the above quote.

So what's the BIG DEAL "it may be subject to change". If they (ISO)
follow through on the above quote - game finito ! Or am I missing
something ?

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Expo and Standards process

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-05-08T05:18:07+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3cd8a775_2@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<aavb0p$a3j$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3cd3b90b_4@Usenet.com> <4145699b.0205051323.7e26e84f@posting.google.com> <3cd726e3_8@Usenet.com> <bfdfc3e8.0205070529.15867556@posting.google.com> <3cd80b61_6@Usenet.com> <bfdfc3e8.0205071713.45640d41@posting.google.com> <3CD89E17.136DDD3D@shaw.ca>`

```
Glad you weighed in, Jimmy.

Just a brief note on the use of "boondoggle".

I was introduced to this term many years ago by an American colleague while
working in Australia.

It may have a different meaning in Canada, but when I use it, it means:

"An excuse to get off real work and go somewhere exotic at Corporate
expense. An undeserved reward."

For example, Sales are down but the Marketing Manager decides for all Sales
people to go to a luxury hotel at Corporate expense for the weekend to run a
"Motivational projection" seminar. They all know they don't deserve it, but
they all go anyway. Two hours are spent in the Seminar and the rest in the
pool and bars...The Staff justify it by saying: "Well, if they're stupid
enough to do this, I'll go along..." No actual motivation is achieved and
money that could be used for much more worthwhile purposes is actually
wasted...

Some of us (the ones who have consciences) won't accept these little
perquisites until they have been legitimately earned by effort and producing
something. I always throw a large and expensive party at the END of a
project when it has been delivered. During the project we have working
lunches but these are NOT boondoggles because we actually achieve stuff
during these sessions. If the Company will not pay for this, I do it out of
my own pocket. It is a way of saying "Thanks" to team members.

I take great care when building Project teams to try and detect the
Boondogglers... they don't get on board.

I never have difficulty in recruiting people to my Project teams...<G>

Pete.
```
