<a id="t-27f0f90f46"></a>
## Thread: Websphere where its at? — 14 msgs

### Websphere where its at?

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-05-04T11:46:12+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3cd3bb97_1@Usenet.com>`

```
I have been doing some research into IBM's Websphere product and am very
favourably impressed.

It seems fairly certain that IBM is going this way for OO programming.

I won't write a blurb here for Websphere (you can read IBM's own blurb on
their Web Site) but this is a useful way to deploy OO Components.

Sadly, the preferred environment is Java (they use J2EE and Enterprise Java
Beans) and I find no mention anywhere of OO COBOL. Their Visual Age Java and
Java environment (associated with WebSphere) seems excellent, although I
haven't used it personally.

 I say "sadly", not as a criticism of Java, but rather because it is pretty
apparent that IBM have no more faith in COBOL (apart from Fortress COBOL,
which has a limited lifetime as more and more Companies opt for ERP and
business solutions like SAP, Chordiant, and Siebel).

This seems to confirm what we have suspected for some time now; IBM are
dropping support for OO COBOL.
(The inevitable corollary to this is the demise of COBOL. The future is in
OO, for all programming languages.)

Nevertheless, Websphere is a good product and those of you on mainframe
sites who realise the need to expand your skill sets would be well advised
to grab some of the tutorials and get stuck into it.

The demand in Europe for people with this experience is increasing and, as
component technology takes over (I predicted this some years ago), this will
become a valuable (that means more money in your pocket) skill.

It is very apparent in looking at job requirements here that there is an
increasing demand for Component technologies and we are seeing projects
advertised with UML, Rational Tools and even RAD/DSDM being specified. All
of these function best in the OO environment.

I'd be interested to hear comments from people in this NG who are actually
using Websphere in the workplace. (Maybe the wrong forum, but hopefully
not...<G>).

What do you think of it?

Pete.



 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Websphere where its at?

- **From:** none@none.com (freewheelin)
- **Date:** 2002-05-04T13:10:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3cd3e28f.1157035@news.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3cd3bb97_1@Usenet.com>`

```
Pete, here is a comment, from a project management point of view,
rather than the technical.

I work at one of the largest corporations in the world.  They use
Websphere and had expected good things from it.  They found themselves
managing websphere projects that blew budgets out the window.  Their
biggest complaint was the rapid changes to the product and keeping
previously written code working.  According to the analysts who worked
on it, they rewrote the projects, several times, before completion and
long overdue.  The programmers became cynical, the bean counters said
stop and management shelved development.  They are looking for another
way.


On Sat, 4 May 2002 11:46:12 +0100, "Peter E. C. Dashwood"
<dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>I have been doing some research into IBM's Websphere product and am very
>favourably impressed.
…[48 more quoted lines elided]…
>                http://www.usenet.com
```

```
I love corporations like that - management and IT did not communicate correctly.
They are a great source of income for people who know how to communicate and
how to get the systems to work well (i.e. We Software Engineering types...)...

-Paul
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Websphere where its at?

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-05-07T01:57:52+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3cd726e5_8@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<3cd3bb97_1@Usenet.com> <3cd3e28f.1157035@news.earthlink.net>`

```

freewheelin <none@none.com> wrote in message
news:3cd3e28f.1157035@news.earthlink.net...
> Pete, here is a comment, from a project management point of view,
> rather than the technical.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Thanks very much for this. It is valuable feedback and my interest is from a
PM point of view as well as technical.

Changes to the product I guess are to be expected, as the whole Component
world is an evolving technology. The impact of .NET and a new version of
CORBA are yet to be evaluated, and Websphere would need to deploy in both MS
and non-MS environments.

It is unusual (and a pity, I think) for changes in a technology to require
rewriting a project. I wonder if proper OOD was applied from the start...?
Were the analysts properly trained in OOD?

See, If Business Objects are identified correctly from the start, along with
their Properties, Methods, and Interfaces, the actual technology used to
develop and implement them is not too important.

I'm very interested in hearing your experience in this area, but I'd also
like to hear what IBM's take on it was...Could they give a reason for the
obvious failure?

Thanks for the post.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Websphere where its at?

- **From:** none@none.com (freewheelin)
- **Date:** 2002-05-07T13:28:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
On Tue, 7 May 2002 01:57:52 +0100, "Peter E. C. Dashwood"
<dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>
>freewheelin <none@none.com> wrote in message
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
>Pete.

Unfortunately I cannot answer your questions.  I was not involved
directly.  The reasons that I know of were as I said, time and money.
I can inquire, but a lot of them have left.

You are correct.  Training was certainly an issue, especially with an
evolving product.  IMO, the real skill with OOD and 3-tier
architecture is knowing when not to use it.  

The problem I see eveywhere is that technical resources trained today
cannot understand software in general, and of course only their way
will suffice, regardless of the cost.  Same with the dinosars.   It is
not enough to train anyone in the latest technology.  It depends on
who is trained and their previous knowledge and I suspect this is at
the root of the failure.

I saw so-called senoir technical analysts claim systems they are being
shown can't possibly work because the SQL is embedded and 'must be' a
stored procedure.  The same bunch proposed to replace a file transfer
by defining every field from two databases in web services.  Publish
hashed accounting records?   Imagine the costs.  Pete, I feel futility
that wasn't there before and I think the bean counters would agree.
Of course Websphere can work.  I don't believe the software was
entirely responsible for the failure.

>
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>                http://www.usenet.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Websphere where its at?

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@NO-SPAM.hot.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-04T16:23:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PVTA8.33731$9F5.1771120@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3cd3bb97_1@Usenet.com>`

```
Well, don't count out COBOL yet. :)

Just one instance - in WebSphere environments, databases tend to be the
primary data source, especially (not coincidentally!) DB/2. Guess what legacy
language is the easiest for most legacy programmers to write stored procedures in?

(And why do you *want* stored procedures? Because you can encode business
logic in them that is completely insensitive to the tiers above it, and it makes
the best sense to keep your data processing as close to the data source as
possible. Not to mention they are trivially easy to write. :)

Yep - COBOL - on *all* supported DB/2 platforms, either from the IBM compiler or
from MicroFocus, PerCOBOL, AcuCOBOL, etc.

-Paul

"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message news:3cd3bb97_1@Usenet.com...
> I have been doing some research into IBM's Websphere product and am very
> favourably impressed.
…[48 more quoted lines elided]…
>                 http://www.usenet.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Websphere where its at?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-07T07:28:44-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ab8ha4$quo$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3cd72e00_6@Usenet.com> <20020506214359.21990.00007586@mb-fh.aol.com> <3cd79c30$1_1@Usenet.com>`

```
Pete,
  I think that there might be some confusion between "green screen" vs GUI
vs "heads down" applications.

Many "traditional" mainframe "green screen" applications are primarily (if
not exclusively) data-entry systems that really ARE best served by a "text
only" input environment. Although some (most?) of these do involve SOME
fields that might be "best" served by drop-down boxes or similar "GUI"
approaches, I do think that they may need "enhancements" while still NOT
needing a GUI approach.

This is NOT intended to imply that there isn't OFTEN user resistance to
"new" (but more productive) interfaces.  I just want to state this isn't
ALWAYS the problem.  (Not that I think you think <G> that it is)
```

```
Hi all... I'm from Portugal, my name is Alex and I'm new to the list...

I am starting to study the COBOL language. I've been developing in VB and
Delphi for some years now...
I have this request...(I think it's a modest one) Does anybody can provide
me with a sample COBOL program with the basic DB statments... (Insert,
Delete, Modify and Search) ? I've been searching for a sample demo like this
and can't seem to find one. I don't know any cobol programmers....so it
makes it diffucult to have such examples...


Thanks in advance,
Alex
```

#### ↳ Re: Sample COBOL Program

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-05T12:43:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<EN9B8.2973$Ez1.206@nwrddc03.gnilink.net>`
- **References:** `<1020600196.927637@newsfront2>`

```
News Netvisao <alexandre.albino.2@netvisao.pt> wrote in message
news:1020600196.927637@newsfront2...
>
> I am starting to study the COBOL language. I've been developing in VB and
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Delete, Modify and Search) ?

Unlike VB and Delphi, COBOL does not have any "database" functions beyond
OPEN, CLOSE, READ and WRITE. Inserts, Deletes, Modifies and Searches must be
constructed from combinations of these lower-level verbs.

That said, some COBOL compiler vendors do offer extensions to 'standard'
COBOL; however, the syntax for database access are specific to the brand
name of COBOL compiler.

There is one exception to this, however. Many vendors offer an "embedded
SQL" option; while the method for including this varies slightly from vendor
to vendor, the SQL language is common; and the syntax for embedding SQL
statments is also common:

EXEC SQL
    sql_statement
END-EXEC

Compilers which support embedded SQL do so by converting the 'EXEC SQL
sql_statement   END-EXEC' code blocks into 'standard' COBOL verbs (e.g.,
MOVE, CALL).

I'm pretty sure there are examples of this available on the Internet: do a
search on "+COBOL +embedded +SQL" using your faviorite search engine; and
check out the COBOL FAQ at http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm to
see if there is anything Bill Klein may have included in that document.

(Bill Klein:  if this question does not appear in the FAQ, it might be a
good addition)
```

- **Message-ID:** `<uda8ts7cghib60@corp.supernews.com>`

```
I was surfing somewhere the other day and ran across a small article for a 
book about using COBOL and the new MS .NET runtime and I would assume 
framework. Has anyone here any experience with this yet? Can anyone point 
me to more information. A language that self documents as nicely as cobol 
does in .NET could be very nice (not to mention legacy code that could be 
brought forward).

Thanks and sorry to all if this is off topic here.
```

#### ↳ Re: .NET and Fujitsu Cobol

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-05-06T08:26:56+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CD63040.3604F75F@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<uda8ts7cghib60@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Rico wrote:
> 
> I was surfing somewhere the other day and ran across a small article for a
> book about using COBOL and the new MS .NET runtime and I would assume
> framework. 

It is just a way of writing C# programs using some of the syntax of
Cobol.  It is likely to wind up as many CALLs or INVOKEs of .NET
components with a few Cobol lines scattered amongst it.

The actual language for managed code is a superset (ie extensions) of a
sub-set (ie not all Cobol is supported) of OO Cobol.

> Can anyone point me to more information. 

www.adtools.com and www.netcobol.com

> A language that self documents as nicely as cobol
> does in .NET could be very nice (not to mention legacy code that could be
> brought forward).

I very much doubt that any 'legacy' code would be suitable.  What were
you thinking ? Batch ? CICS ?

"""ASP.NET Example 1

The following is a sample ASP.NET page written using COBOL that displays
"Welcome to ASP.NET (now in COBOL!)" in increasing font sizes:

<%@ page language="COBOL" %>
<script runat="server">
           OBJECT.
           DATA DIVISION. 
           WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
           77 Font-Size PIC S9(9) COMP-5.
           END OBJECT.
</script>
<% PERFORM VARYING Font-Size FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL Font-Size > 7 %>
<font size="<%=Font-Size%>"> Welcome to ASP.NET (now in  COBOL!) </font>
<br>
<% END-PERFORM. %>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Max value possible in COMP field ??

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-06T13:55:27-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ab6qjv$2pmu$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<d88238ab.0204191433.27443f4a@posting.google.com> <FRhw8.123985$K5.10565071@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <rrlw8.23150$o86.928895@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <01HW.B8EC309A0073FED70DB3DC60@news-east.usenetserver.com> <XFIx8.9118$d17.387384@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```

"Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@NO-SPAM.hot.rr.com> wrote in message
news:XFIx8.9118$d17.387384@typhoon.austin.rr.com...

> [COMP]UTATIONAL does NOT mean packed decimal, it means BINARY. In fact,
> COMPUTATIONAL is synonymous with BINARY.  If the MCP systems are storing
> COMP items in some non-computational form, I would seriously take it up
with
> the
> compiler writers.

Ummm ... Paul, I'm afraid your information is rather out of date.

It is true that in the MCP/AS COBOL(68) compiler COMP (and for that matter
COMP-1) was a word-oriented integer data type analogous to INTEGER in ALGOL.
COMP-4 and COMP-5 corresponded to ALGOL REAL and DOUBLE data types.  COMP-2
in that dialect was a packed-decimal format.

But support for MCP/AS COBOL(68) ended, as I recall, on 12/31/1999, and with
it died the appropriateness of the supposition that COMP represented a
word-oriented format on MCP/AS systems (and their predecessors).

Accoding to the standards, "COMPUTATIONAL" means nothing more nor less than
what the compiler implementor decides it means.

Burroughs management decided sometime in the 1970's that while an equivalent
of ALGOL's "INTEGER" data type could be used to get the absolute last shred
of performance on the Burroughs Large System, expecting everybody who was
being encouraged to migrate from systems like the B1700 and the Medium
System (both of which had packed decimal arithmetic, but neither of which
had a word-oriented format suitable for COBOL) to reformat all their records
was not reasonable.  Accordingly, for all Burroughs plants developing COBOL
compilers compilant with any ANSI standard from ANSI-74 onward, COMP items
would be in a packed-decimal format.  And that has been true since the very
introduction of COBOL74 and COBOL85 on MCP/AS systems, as well as the
COBOL74 compilers for both B1000 and B2/3/4000 systems.

For the current COBOL dialects on MCP/AS systems, if you want an item to be
USAGE BINARY (which is basically the same format as an ALGOL integer on that
system), the appropriate thing to do is to tell the compiler that in the
USAGE clause.

The supposition that packed-decimal items were expensive to manipulate was a
great deal more tenable on the likes of the B6700 system.  But our hardware
engineers worked long and hard to improve the hardware associated with
conversion to and from both DISPLAY and packed-decimal data types, and while
it's true that retrieving a packed-decimal field for arithmetic purposes
from a record isn't quite as rapid as retrieving a word-aligned integer for
the same purposes from the same record, it's not demonstrable that in
real-world programs the difference is generally significant.   That's been
pretty much true since the introduction of the larger Burroughs A Series
systems in the 1980's.

Moreover, there's now over two decades of precedent for the internal format
for COMP items on MCP/AS systems.  For that reason, if you decided to
"seriously take this up with the compiler writers", I suspect strongly that
you would not make a lot of headway unless you *first* convinced pretty much
the entire MCP/AS user base to support your position and agree to swallow
the costs to reformat all their files and revise all their
currently-operating programs.  I have serious doubts that that level of
support for your position from the MCP/AS COBOL user base is likely to
materialize.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
I know of one system where COMP used exactly the same representation as
DISPLAY NUMERIC. This was the ex Singer 10 based ICL System 25 which was
a decimal machine - its hardware did aritmetic on decimal numbers in 6
bit characters, 10 to each 60bit 'word'.

Someone 'borrowed' my Cobol manual for this range many years ago, and it
is still not returned.
```

#### ↳ Re: Max value possible in COMP field ??

- **From:** "Georgi Kozinakov" <gjokok@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-24T19:36:21+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3cee798f@news.mt.net.mk>`
- **References:** `<d88238ab.0204191433.27443f4a@posting.google.com>`

```
Sandy,

On my VAX/VMS COBOL I use this simple tecnique
to put 5 digit number in a pic XX:
FD ...
01 Record1.
    03 id pic xx. (here goes the 5 digit converted number with upper limit
65535!)
    03 name pic x(10).
    03 salary pic 9(7)v99 comp.

working-storage.

01 nid pic 9(5) comp.
01 wid redefines nid pic xx.


procedure division.

    display "Enter the id: (<65535) ". accept nid conversion.
    display "name ? ". accept name.
    display "salary ? ". accept salary conversion.
...
    write record1 invalid ....

This conserves 3 bytes in every record. I have implemented this many times.
Of course that you'll have to take care that the limit is 65535 (2^16-1).

HTH
Georgi Kozinakov
Skopje, Macedonia

"sandy" <sandythesatan@rediffmail.com> wrote in message
news:d88238ab.0204191433.27443f4a@posting.google.com...
> Hi Folks,
> Am a new born baby in the world of COBOL ;-)
>
> Wanted to know if I can store a 5 digit numeric value in a PIC 9(4) COMP
field ??
>
> Thanks in advance guyz
```

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-05-24T18:57:46+00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<acm25j$rc7$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

On 24-May-2002, "Georgi Kozinakov" <gjokok@yahoo.com> wrote:

> On my VAX/VMS COBOL I use this simple tecnique
> to put 5 digit number in a pic XX:

I use similar logic to retrieve an IDMS dbkey.  But only because IDMS uses a
dbkey that is larger than the CoBOL picture in its copymember allows.

> This conserves 3 bytes in every record. I have implemented this many
> times.
> Of course that you'll have to take care that the limit is 65535 (2^16-1).


Why would I want to convolute my code to save three bytes/record?  Many
times?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Max value possible in COMP field ??

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-05-25T07:43:09+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CEF327D.AFF2548B@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<d88238ab.0204191433.27443f4a@posting.google.com> <3cee798f@news.mt.net.mk>`

```
Georgi Kozinakov wrote:
> 
> 01 nid pic 9(5) comp.
> 01 wid redefines nid pic xx.

On my systems a PIC 9(5) COMP would occupy 3 or 4 bytes depending on
compiler directives.  The redefine as PIC XX would take the first two
bytes of these and the low order bytes would be lost.  

      01  N-Id         PIC S9(8) COMP.
      01  N-Id-RDF REDEFINES N-Id.
          03  FILLER   PIC X(2).
          03  W-Id     PIC X(2).

should actually work.
```

### Usings CICS

- **From:** leo_fleming2002@yahoo.com (Leo Fleming)
- **Date:** 2002-05-06T15:21:10-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2556ac1e.0205061421.1fae7e3@posting.google.com>`

```
Can somebody tell me what exactly is involved in programming CICS for
Cobol? I have experience testing in CICS regions, as well as
supporting some purchased packages (GEAC) that had online CICS
regions, but I've never actually programmed CICS.

Is it difficult to pick up? I'm thinking of applying for a job that is
telling me I need CICS experience but I'm trying to figure out what
exactly that means. Is there actually a CICS programming language? And
is it fairly easy to learn, seeing as how I already know Cobol? Thanks
for the help.

Leo
```

#### ↳ Re: Usings CICS

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-06T17:54:28-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ab71jb$i7v$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<2556ac1e.0205061421.1fae7e3@posting.google.com>`

```
Basic CICS coding isn't THAT difficult, but learning "good" coding
techniques may be.  I certainly would NOT claim to have "CICS coding
experience" - if you haven't actually used it.

There are a "couple" of places to start looking to see what is involved, See
(for example),

http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/DFHP3P00/1.4?

Although, it is fairly out of date (about 3 or 4 VERSIONS ago), you might
want to look at some of the source code in the "PRIMER" at:


http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/DFHZP104/CCONTENT
S?

to see what COBOL/CICS programs can look like.
```

#### ↳ Re: Usings CICS

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2002-05-07T00:55:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c1f561$cfee0540$61caf943@chottel>`
- **References:** `<2556ac1e.0205061421.1fae7e3@posting.google.com>`

```
If you already know Cobol then I world recommend Mike Murach's CICS book.
(www.murach.com) I taught myself CICS by reading this book and it is very
good for the basics.

Maps/screens are defined using BMS - basic mapping services - i.e.
assembler language macros.  A physical map (load module) and a Cobol copy
book for the screen fields are produced by assembly of the screen.  Cursor
positioning is controlled via attribute bytes.  You can set initial values
in the map and can override them in the program.

Within the Cobol program  you  have EXEC CICS cics_command END-EXEC.  These
get translated into the appropriate CALL statements. CICS provides a lot of
different services and in particular services for accessing files.  If you
use a dbms already then that will help reduce the learning curve.  Many
programs simply SEND/RECEIVE maps, handle PF keys, edit screen field data,
and add, delete, or update files/databases.  You have to worry about things
like:

    Is this the first time thru?
    Has the transaction code changed?
    Has the key changed?
    Do I transfer to another program?
    In a pseudo-conversational program how does the program  remember
important data (commarea or     temporary storage)?
    What exceptional conditions do I need to handle for this cics command? 
etc.

Like I said the book gives you the basic coding techniques and example
programs of the most common type of transactions.

One one the "hard" things about CICS is that it uses certain tables to
define transaction ids and program names etc.  The  "hard" part is that
usually a system programmmer is involved in setting these things up and
this can be block to experimenting with practice programs. Some places make
this easier for the programmer to do this himself. You will have to check
out your local environment.  Having a sys prog friend may be helpful.

Leo Fleming <leo_fleming2002@yahoo.com> wrote in article
<2556ac1e.0205061421.1fae7e3@posting.google.com>...
> Can somebody tell me what exactly is involved in programming CICS for
> Cobol? I have experience testing in CICS regions, as well as
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Usings CICS

- **From:** "kurimao" <kelpogs@singnet.com.sg>
- **Date:** 2002-05-10T23:54:55+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<abgqgt$8lo$1@dahlia.singnet.com.sg>`
- **References:** `<2556ac1e.0205061421.1fae7e3@posting.google.com>`

```
CICS is  the online system for mainframe machines like that of IBM,
AMDAHL...etc. It's just a system where the users
can enter data, enquire, update files, etc. Just like any Windows data entry
screens, except that you are
presented usually with a 3270 terminal - a dumb terminal (Usually used by
dumb users too!). A terminal with an 80X24 dimension.
CICS is an ordinary COBOL program.If you see a compilation of CICS, u can
see that it'll comment out the EXEC CICS ..
statements and create the COBOL (batch) equivalent and compiled just like
any other batch programs (with CICS libs linked to it of course).

So here it is, I've been programming CICS, batch COBOL, ASM-H for the past 6
years and have written some
pretty nice programs myself. This is what I learned first. Try looking in
ibm.com too.

1. Learn only how to write pseudo-conversational CICS programs. It saves
resources and your boss will like you.
2. Learn how files are accessed thru CICS, basically they are analogous to
batch programs BUT to check or handle
    file status, you use the CICS return code. So, learn a lot about
handles.
3. Next, learn how to create maps/screen maps thru BMS.
4. Learn how to use COMMAREA - this is usually LINKAGE SECTION in batch
4. Try to dissect the EIB resources
5. Since most CICS programs deals with data entry, and file browsing, it is
imperative that you learn paging.
6. Learn about queues - TSQs
7. Learn the above first, all of them. Then try the TWA, CWA stuff




"Leo Fleming" <leo_fleming2002@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:2556ac1e.0205061421.1fae7e3@posting.google.com...
> Can somebody tell me what exactly is involved in programming CICS for
> Cobol? I have experience testing in CICS regions, as well as
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Leo
```

```
object and it will transfer the file for you.
```

```
> double quotes as part of the data.

IMNSHO, anyone who uses CSV files for data which may contain quotes or
commas ought to have his head examined.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CSV Files

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@paralynx.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-07T07:54:07-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CD7EA8F.7A2A@paralynx.com>`
- **References:** `<ude2mijsl0od06@corp.supernews.com> <aSGB8.9109$vT1.775341@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3CD7E604.4AB1F423@Azonic.co.nz> <znOB8.22$Sh.16@nwrddc01.gnilink.net> <udfdo9g3k3b958@corp.supernews.com> <2oQB8.2976$EY1.543@nwrddc02.gnilink.net>`

```
Michael Mattias wrote:
> 
> <jasman@theoffice.net> wrote in message
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> commas ought to have his head examined.

You can't control what users will send you.  You need to consider context - treat a 
quote as a literal rather than a delimiter unless it is either
1. Adjacent to a comma
2. At the beginning of a record
3. At the end of a record

Resolve for the commas to split the fields - with a comma inside a quoted string 
(identified using the rules above) being considered a literal.

When you trim quotes, remember that there not be any for numeric fields.

If you don't mind translating from C, here is a routine that works for me:
----------------------8<--------------------------
 char linework[MAXLINE];
 char *letter;
 char *nextcomma;
 int fieldcount=0;
 strcpy(linework,InputLine);
 if(*(linework+strlen(linework)-1)==34){*(linework+strlen(linework)-1)=0;}
 letter=linework;
 while(*letter&&fieldcount<MAXDELIMFIELDS)
		{
		fieldcount++;
		if (*letter!=34)                      /* its not a quote */
			{
			nextcomma=strstr(letter,",");
			if(nextcomma>=letter)             /* it found one */
				{
				*nextcomma=0;
				strcpy(f[fieldcount],letter);
				letter=nextcomma+1;
				}
			else
				{
				strcpy(f[fieldcount],letter);  /* use the rest of the 
line */
				*letter=0;                     /* terminate loop */
				}
			}
		else
			{	/* it is a quote */
			char *nextquote;
			letter++;                          /* skip over it */
			nextquote=strstr(letter,"\",");    /* find the next */

			if(nextquote>=letter)             /* it found one */
				{
				*nextquote=0;
				strcpy(f[fieldcount],letter);
				letter=nextquote+1;         /*should be a comma*/
				if(*letter)letter++;        /* to next field   */
				}
			else
				{
				strcpy(f[fieldcount],letter);  /* use the rest of the 
line */
				*letter=0;				/* terminate 
loop */
				}
			}
		}

------------------------------------------8<------------------------
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CSV Files

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Anonymous <Nobody>
- **Date:** 2002-05-07T19:06:41-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3cd7b2d4_3@anonymous>`
- **References:** `<ude2mijsl0od06@corp.supernews.com> <aSGB8.9109$vT1.775341@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3CD7E604.4AB1F423@Azonic.co.nz> <znOB8.22$Sh.16@nwrddc01.gnilink.net> <udfdo9g3k3b958@corp.supernews.com> <2oQB8.2976$EY1.543@nwrddc02.gnilink.net>`

```
Some (most?) software which produces delimited files allows you to choose
what the delimiter is.  It doesn't generally have to be a comma.  If you
receive files on a regular basis, you could simplify the issue by requesting
that a different delimiter be used.

Otherwise, suck it into Excel and resave it yourself with a different
delimiter.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CSV Files

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** jasman@theoffice.net
- **Date:** 2002-05-08T03:01:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<udh5909fs1m19d@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<ude2mijsl0od06@corp.supernews.com> <aSGB8.9109$vT1.775341@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3CD7E604.4AB1F423@Azonic.co.nz> <znOB8.22$Sh.16@nwrddc01.gnilink.net> <udfdo9g3k3b958@corp.supernews.com> <2oQB8.2976$EY1.543@nwrddc02.gnilink.net> <3cd7b2d4_3@anonymous>`

```
In the particular case I'm dealing with, the file is output from a MS Excel
spreadsheet that is then FTPed to my IBM Mainframe.

This precludes somewhat any PC based solution on my end as I don't want to FTP
it down to an NT server, try to trigger a PC process to "Un-CSV" the data and
then get it back up to the mainframe. Besides we don't own a wonderful tool like
ParseRat (they would rather I burn time creating a solution than spend a few
"real" dollars - don't ask, the story will bore you and piss me off).

Yes I know that Excel can save a fixed format record, but as someone has already
noted that makes the file larger and there is a greater risk. If the user, in
Spain, happens to alter the Excel column width, the format of my fixed file will
suddenly change and I'm toast.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CSV Files

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** rob lee <roblee@nospam.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-10T00:00:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CDB0C17.BD341D41@nospam.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<ude2mijsl0od06@corp.supernews.com> <aSGB8.9109$vT1.775341@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3CD7E604.4AB1F423@Azonic.co.nz> <znOB8.22$Sh.16@nwrddc01.gnilink.net> <udfdo9g3k3b958@corp.supernews.com> <2oQB8.2976$EY1.543@nwrddc02.gnilink.net> <3cd7b2d4_3@anonymous> <udh5909fs1m19d@corp.supernews.com>`

```
It seems only a few of the respondents here realize that in the real
world we often get files handed to us without the option of picking and
choosing which format we'd prefer. In other words, when the task falls
upon you, you can't just send it back. And oftentimes some
characteristic of the data file precludes some quick workaround
(filesize comes to mind ... a "small" Cobol file can be overwhelming for
MS Office).

As for spending hours rather than dollars, well I feel your pain.  Just
try to look upon it as a technical challenge and a personal growth
opportunity.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CSV Files

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-07T22:49:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CD859BC.72AA99ED@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<ude2mijsl0od06@corp.supernews.com> <aSGB8.9109$vT1.775341@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3CD7E604.4AB1F423@Azonic.co.nz> <znOB8.22$Sh.16@nwrddc01.gnilink.net> <udfdo9g3k3b958@corp.supernews.com> <2oQB8.2976$EY1.543@nwrddc02.gnilink.net> <3CD8DAF3.1069803B@Azonic.co.nz> <3CD8592E.3E22@paralynx.com>`

```


Ed Guy wrote:

> Richard Plinston wrote:
> >
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Whichever way you go, commas or quotes, you get hosed on data entry
inconsistencies. My own particular bugaboo :-

123456,"V-BAND ON 10 INCH ELBOW", 003.45 etc...
123456,"V-BAND ON 10 IN. ELBOW", 003.45 etc...
123456,"V-BAND ON 10" ELBOW", 003.45 etc...

(Mind you above *can't*  happen any more - I generate the descriptions from an
8-character mnemonic code, stringing the 'pieces', together !)

That's why for preference, if you can get it, I go for a fixed length record, at
least you can take it into an editor, view it column by column, and do some
judicious editing.

I haven't tried it, but taking your O'Bloggs example above, given a series of those
with a fixed length record, something like MS Access *may* say "I recognize your
records as being fixed format" and then prompt you further "Your separator is a
comma ?" - might just do the trick.

Why didn't the importers/exporters hit on an obscure character like the tilde (~)
as the universal separator ?

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CSV Files

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> 
> Ed Guy wrote:
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
> comma ?" - might just do the trick.

It would be nice if they were a fixed record length, EXCEPT that then you have a much 
bigger file since you have to size each field for the longest values. Some systems will 
let you export as fixed length, of course - but not all of them.

> Why didn't the importers/exporters hit on an obscure character like the tilde (~)
> as the universal separator ?

I've seen it used (I've even used it myself).

I, of course, usually run these things through my ParseRat program 
(http://www.parserat.com/) to clean them up and put them in a format I like. (The 
example C code I posted is a bit taken from it.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CSV Files

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-05-08T08:24:19+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CD8D2A3.17B7F0C5@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<ude2mijsl0od06@corp.supernews.com> <aSGB8.9109$vT1.775341@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3CD7E604.4AB1F423@Azonic.co.nz> <znOB8.22$Sh.16@nwrddc01.gnilink.net>`

```
Michael Mattias wrote:
> 
> Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> UNSTRING CSV-RECORD ......

I think that you failed to understand what is meant by "to allow them to
have commas embedded".

eg:

123,"Mr A J Davis","22, Main St","Ackton",420.56,56,...

Apart from misstepping the fields you would also wind up with a leading
space on those fields where you replaced the quote.

I usually find that it is best to step along each character setting and
unsetting an 'In-Quote' flag field and skipping after a comma until
first non-space.
```

#### ↳ Re: CSV Files

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-07T02:27:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CD73B48.367DA030@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<ude2mijsl0od06@corp.supernews.com>`

```


jasman@theoffice.net wrote:

> I've been tasked with processing a CSV format file after it has been uploaded to
> an IBM Mainframe. I'm very familiar with this format and would prefer to
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks

I'm not sure you can come up with a 'logic set' to cover this topic.

By no means definitive, but my particular approach on a PC. (I need CSVs to convert
old RM data files to MF data files, plus from M/F output CSVs going into oil/gas
company DBs. Additionally I need CSVs to create data sets as imports to MS Access
tables).

1 - Some propose, but I dislike, using STRING and UNSTRING

2 - I go with a template, fixed format :-

01 CSV-RECORD.
    05 CustomerNumber            pic x(05).
    05                                        pic x value ",".
    05 CustomerBalance            pic -999,999.99.
    05                                        pic x value ",".
    05 etc......

2a) It used to be that you designated text fields by surrounding them with quotes(")
as well as the commas. I think 'importing' systems are less stringent now and will
accept just commas - I'm just in the process of trying this out.

2b) Fixed format - easier to read taking the CSV into an editor that handles really
*long* lines, allowing you global replace etc.

2c) Make the CSV record a copyfile, then you can include it any relevant program

2d) Take advantage of the MOVE CORRESPONDING by keeping the field names generic,
(thanks Thane <G>)  :-

Input record :-

01 CUS-record.
    05 CustomerNumber            pic x(05).
    05 CustomerBalance            pic s9(06)v9(02) comp-3.
    05 etc.....

    MOVE CORR(ESPONDING) Cus-record to CSV-record

*BUT* carefully check out the syntax for MOVE CORRESPONDING so that you get it
right, for your particular record.

3) If you've dabbled with anything like MS Access, you'll know that it will quite
happily prompt you that you appear to be importing fixed format with the comma
separator.

4) Given that you take the above sort of standard approach, you write one program
with necessasry read/write logic, take copies of this program, re-identify the
Program ID and use copyfiles for both input and output.

Backtracking to the MOVE CORRESPONDING and copyfiles :-

FD IN-file
01 IN-record.
copy "cusrecord.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==IN==.
FD OUT-file
01 OUT-record.
copy "cusCSVrec.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==OUT==.

read IN-file next record etc......
move corresponding IN-record to OUT-record
write OUT-record

Obviously just switch the "INs" and "OUTs" around when you want the CSV to be the
input file.

(I can do it slightly 'neater' in OO - but oh dear, you are on a mainframe <G>).

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: CSV Files

- **From:** jasman@theoffice.net
- **Date:** 2002-05-07T11:08:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<udfdckprd08pef@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<ude2mijsl0od06@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Thank you for your comments. I agree since I am taking the CSV file apart, the
UNSTRING verb is a possibility. The problem, however, can be very simple or very
complex. For example, a variable number of fields separated by commas that may
or may not be enclosed by double quotes and quoted fields may contain commas and
double double qotes (which need to resolve to a single character value of a
quote). e.g.

John,"Smith",Smith, John","123 Some St.",Atlanta,GA,37918,123.45,-25.00

As you can imagine the first and last fields, for a generic routine could start
and end with any character. So I can't count on looking for the double quote, I
would have to handle the case of double quote, a comma (indicating the first
field missing, or other character being the first position of the data. And
based on what I started with will change what terminates the field.

I can write routine in COBOL, OS/390 Assembler, SQR, or several other languages,
but I'm still hoping that someone has already got the code working and tested
and could save me some time.

Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: CSV Files

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-07T14:18:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lnRB8.100144$v7.8268362@bin6.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<ude2mijsl0od06@corp.supernews.com>`

```

<jasman@theoffice.net> wrote in message
news:ude2mijsl0od06@corp.supernews.com...
> I've been tasked with processing a CSV format file after it has been
uploaded to
> an IBM Mainframe. I'm very familiar with this format and would prefer to
> use/write a general purpose routine as I see more of these kind of files
on the
> way.
>
> Before I start writing, has anyone already created this logic and would
you
> share?

One trick is to not tamper with commas imbedded within quotes. We convert
non-imbedded commas to low-values first, then UNSTRING the result based on
the low-values tag. We also remove the quotes. Here's a snippet (hint: SW is
either 1 or 2):

000078     ENTRY 'DECSV' USING LINK-LEN LINK-RECORD.
000079
000080     MOVE 1 TO SW    *>Looking for quote 1 or 2
000081     MOVE 0 TO J.
000082     PERFORM VARYING I FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL I > LINK-LEN
000083         EVALUATE LINK-RECORD(I:1) ALSO SW
000084             WHEN '"' ALSO 1
000085                 COMPUTE SW = 3 - SW   *>Found 1st quote
000086             WHEN '"' ALSO 2
000087                 COMPUTE SW = 3 - SW   *>Found 2nd quote, new
000088             WHEN ',' ALSO 1           *>delimit external comma
000089                 ADD 1 TO J
000090                 MOVE LOW-VALUES TO LINK-RECORD(J:1)
000091             WHEN ',' ALSO 2           *>Copy internal comma
000092                 ADD 1 TO J
000093                 MOVE LINK-RECORD(I:1) TO LINK-RECORD(J:1)
000094             WHEN OTHER                *>Copy all else
000095                 ADD 1 TO J
000096                 MOVE LINK-RECORD(I:1) TO LINK-RECORD(J:1)
000097             END-EVALUATE
000098         END-PERFORM.
000099     PERFORM VARYING I FROM J BY 1 UNTIL I > LINK-LEN
000100         MOVE SPACE TO LINK-RECORD(I:1)
000101         END-PERFORM.

The above transforms:

"AB,CD","123","XYZ",456   to   AB,CD^123^XYZ^456bbbb....

where ^ represents low-values.

Hope this helps.
```

## Thread: What next?? — 16 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### What next??

- **From:** "noname" <noname@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-07T00:47:31+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<BCEB8.29704$%1.3390215@news-binary.blueyonder.co.uk>`

```
Hope you dont mind but I have posted this message at alt.certification.mcse
as well, under the subject "Degree".
I got a City & Guilds (thats a degree equivalent) pass in COBOL here in the
UK last year. What i want to know now is what would be my next step if I
wanted to get a personnel manager to say "come on in lets talk...". What do
I need in my skill-set, in lieiu of experience to trigger an initial
interest in myself from a company. I would like to do COBOL programming, I
am at this very moment also reading up on MVS JCL (Doug Lowe), but fear that
I *may* be too slow to cut it as a programmer. My next best interest is
Networking hence my post in the .mcse NG. Is the MCSE certification worth it
from my point of view or does it only open doors for current IT
professionals. Perhaps the CISCO CNA may help, but I dont want to throw good
money around for the courses if none are going to work in my favour. Any
help would be greatly appreciated. T.I.A.

N.
```

#### ↳ Re: What next??

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2002-05-07T01:01:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c1f562$b56cefa0$61caf943@chottel>`
- **References:** `<BCEB8.29704$%1.3390215@news-binary.blueyonder.co.uk>`

```
Don't worry about being slow.  Programming involves lots of details and
most people can absorb 7+-2 details per pass through the material.  With
experience you pick up things faster because your experience allows you to
see the similiarities with languages you already know so you can
concentrate on learning the differences.  Persistance is more important
than intelligence.  As long as you can learn you can learn to program.
```

```
noname wrote:

> Hope you dont mind but I have posted this message at alt.certification.mcse
> as well, under the subject "Degree".
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

As you honestly said, "......but fear that I *may* be too slow to cut it as a
programmer. My next best interest is Networking.....". As regards the
programming part, you initially will have to be the judge of that,
self-analysing. (Be true unto thyself).

As to your second choice Networking. Don't know a damn thing about it. But I
*do" know that my cousin's daughter up in Carlisle, with *no knowledge* of
computing, made a bundle in the Manchester area tied into Networking. Similarly,
a friend's son here, interested in computers, but not from a programming aspect,
also got interested in Networking, and now about 23-26 years of age is out
contracting on his own !

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: What next??

- **From:** "noname" <noname@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-07T15:28:05+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2wRB8.32245$%1.3633018@news-binary.blueyonder.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<BCEB8.29704$%1.3390215@news-binary.blueyonder.co.uk> <3CD72FDA.C0FF88C0@shaw.ca>`

```
Jimmy,
        I note, interestedly, that you made no mention of MCSE
certification. Is MCSE so unimportant? The reason I ask is because a
training company recently phoned me offering a 'seat' on their on-line
Technical MCSE course citing "...everybody [employers] is looking for
MCSE's", but looking at job adverts for IT (and there arent very many at the
moment) *nobody* even mentions MCSE as a desired skill. I dont suppose that
many employers would take on a COBOL programmer *only* if s/he had an MCSE,
in this instance an MCSE would be irrelevant. What I meant to say is that
newtorking job adverts are not asking for MCSE.
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3CD72FDA.C0FF88C0@shaw.ca...

alt.certification.mcse

the

do
> > I need in my skill-set, in lieiu of experience to trigger an initial
> > interest in myself from a company. I would like to do COBOL programming,
I
> > am at this very moment also reading up on MVS JCL (Doug Lowe), but fear
that
> > I *may* be too slow to cut it as a programmer. My next best interest is
> > Networking hence my post in the .mcse NG. Is the MCSE certification
worth it
> > from my point of view or does it only open doors for current IT
> > professionals. Perhaps the CISCO CNA may help, but I dont want to throw
good
> > money around for the courses if none are going to work in my favour. Any
> > help would be greatly appreciated. T.I.A.
> >
>
> As you honestly said, "......but fear that I *may* be too slow to cut it
as a
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What next??

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-07T16:36:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CD80241.4EA79190@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<BCEB8.29704$%1.3390215@news-binary.blueyonder.co.uk> <3CD72FDA.C0FF88C0@shaw.ca> <2wRB8.32245$%1.3633018@news-binary.blueyonder.co.uk>`

```


noname wrote:

> Jimmy,
>         I note, interestedly, that you made no mention of MCSE
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> newtorking job adverts are not asking for MCSE.

Honestly can't answer you on that one - it *may* or *may not* be important. With
some cynicism I do tend to view all those shelves of books, (MSCE
qualification),  I see in our big box bookstores as of questionable merit. Just
good 'ole MS marketing ?

Now if the guy interviewing you for the job, has himself been the MSCE route
????

When I left the RAF (trained in shorthand/typing), had I wanted to, I could have
got a job as a Hansard recorder in the H. of Commons. Useful then, but shorthand
now ?

Assume you wanted to get into OO COBOL, I can recommend TWO book as a *must
have*. I can also recommend FOUR books *don't bother* - they are sheer crap.
(Not your immediate interest, but Young Kim just posted a reference here to his
on-line course for OO - excellent).

Remember people are successfully using C++, Java, VB etc., without formal
training, and earning a nice buck. That applies, (Networking),  to the two young
people I mentioned. Obviously both bright and used their savvy.

You will have to make your own evaluations - and watch that you don't shell out
hard earned cash on something of questionable merit, including some on-line
courses, now going big time in the US - interestingly article in yesterday's
local paper - Canada is following suit with a projected $4.4 million market..

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What next??

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-05-08T08:42:08+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CD8D6D0.E3DFF1D@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<BCEB8.29704$%1.3390215@news-binary.blueyonder.co.uk> <3CD72FDA.C0FF88C0@shaw.ca> <2wRB8.32245$%1.3633018@news-binary.blueyonder.co.uk> <3CD80241.4EA79190@shaw.ca>`

```
> noname wrote:
> 
> >         I note, interestedly, that you made no mention of MCSE
> > certification. Is MCSE so unimportant? 

Minesweeper Consultant, Solitaire Expert.

Recently here a 12 year old (I think it was) recently passed some MCSE
certification, which shows that all that is required to pass is knowing
how to navigate the MS dialog boxes.

> The reason I ask is because a
> training company recently phoned me offering a 'seat' on their on-line
> Technical MCSE course citing "...everybody [employers] is looking for
> MCSE's", 

Actually this just shows that the training company wants your fees.

> What I meant to say is that
> newtorking job adverts are not asking for MCSE.

Exactly.  MCSE is orientated entirely to networking Windows machines
using MS products, it ignores others. Any fool do that.  What most
'networking jobs' are is integrating several different systems into one
network.  An MCSE would just say "replace that with Windows", as they
have been trained to.
```

#### ↳ Re: What next??

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2002-05-07T20:10:59-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0205071910.49a9cc5c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<BCEB8.29704$%1.3390215@news-binary.blueyonder.co.uk>`

```
"noname" <noname@nospam.com> wrote in message news:<BCEB8.29704$%1.3390215@news-binary.blueyonder.co.uk>...
> Hope you dont mind but I have posted this message at alt.certification.mcse
> as well, under the subject "Degree".
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> am at this very moment also reading up on MVS JCL (Doug Lowe), 

----------
If you are reading up on JCL, it would appear that you are interested
in a career (or a job, to start!) as a mainframe IBM programmer.

You will need COBOL, JCL, CICS, VSAM and probably DB2.  More and more
mainframe jobs are also requiring MQ Series.  For Client/Server and
multi-tier, as well as internet applications, IBM seems to be pushing
COBOL-Java interoperbility.

Good luck.

Business knowledge is often important; you should be familiar at least
with the fundamentals of accounting.

Stephen J Spiro
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: What next??

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-08T03:55:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CD8A166.E058C005@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<BCEB8.29704$%1.3390215@news-binary.blueyonder.co.uk> <4145699b.0205071910.49a9cc5c@posting.google.com>`

```


Stephen J Spiro wrote:

> If you are reading up on JCL, it would appear that you are interested
> in a career (or a job, to start!) as a mainframe IBM programmer.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> with the fundamentals of accounting.

Just in case you've got your City and Guilds as a bean counter, translate what Stephen is saying as :-

     "Don't go printing cheques from Accounts Payable/Bought Ledger, where the amount is followed by CR".

Believe it or not - it happened at the first installation I was at in Canada ! The programmer was a 'sweetie'
but she hadn't a bloody clue about bookkeeping. - even when I explained that if she took such a cheque into her
bank, they would have asked her for the money, not given it to her. <G>

Jimmy
```

```
looking for. I've got the COBOL qualification now, and I am reading the MVS
JCL book by Doug Lowe. Can you tell me more about the MQ series? <snip>IBM
seems to be pushing
COBOL-Java interoperbility.</snip> would you recommend a delve into JAVA
then? The whole hog or just javascript?

Thanks anyway to everyone who has 'chipped-in' on this subject which is
v.important to me. Within the next 18 months I am going to be made redundant
from my current non-IT job, so I want to use the intervening months
learning, reading and learning some more. Thanks.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What next??

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2002-05-09T21:11:18-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0205092011.123972f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<BCEB8.29704$%1.3390215@news-binary.blueyonder.co.uk> <4145699b.0205071910.49a9cc5c@posting.google.com> <2fnC8.4123$m46.306910@news-binary.blueyonder.co.uk>`

```
"noname" <noname@nospam.com> wrote in message news:<2fnC8.4123$m46.306910@news-binary.blueyonder.co.uk>...
> Thanks for that one Stephen, this answer is more like the answer I was
> looking for. I've got the COBOL qualification now, and I am reading the MVS
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Full Java, not Javascript.  

But do not spread yourself too thin.  If you are going to go the
mainframe route, get the JCL, get famiar with ISPF, you have to know
VSAM cold, including IDCAMS, you need solid CICS, and good DB2.

Programming is a PROFESSION, not a trade.  A couple of courses no
longer prepares you...  More and more, employers are requiring real
academic degrees, or LENGTHY programs in computer schools.

Stephen J Spiro
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What next??

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@paralynx.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-09T22:51:06-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CDB5FCA.5613@paralynx.com>`
- **References:** `<BCEB8.29704$%1.3390215@news-binary.blueyonder.co.uk> <4145699b.0205071910.49a9cc5c@posting.google.com> <2fnC8.4123$m46.306910@news-binary.blueyonder.co.uk> <4145699b.0205092011.123972f@posting.google.com>`

```
Stephen J Spiro wrote:

> Programming is a PROFESSION, not a trade.  A couple of courses no
> longer prepares you...  More and more, employers are requiring real
> academic degrees, or LENGTHY programs in computer schools.

That was one of the things which surprised me when I came from England to North America 
in the 60s.  Programmers here were considered UNION LABOUR.  In the UK they were 
considered managerial/professional.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What next??

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-05-10T14:25:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<abgkvh$71c$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<BCEB8.29704$%1.3390215@news-binary.blueyonder.co.uk> <4145699b.0205071910.49a9cc5c@posting.google.com> <2fnC8.4123$m46.306910@news-binary.blueyonder.co.uk> <4145699b.0205092011.123972f@posting.google.com> <3CDB5FCA.5613@paralynx.com>`

```

On  9-May-2002, Ed Guy <ed_guy@paralynx.com> wrote:

> That was one of the things which surprised me when I came from England to
> North America
> in the 60s.  Programmers here were considered UNION LABOUR.  In the UK
> they were considered managerial/professional.

Odd.  I have always heard that Europe had programmers as union labour.  I
haven't worked in a city where I knew of programmers who were unionized, but
I have read of cases where programmers discovered their pay checks were
smaller - after the company made a deal with the union to unionize the
programmers.

What city/cities did you find the converse?

But I dislike the categorization in general.  Often professionals are
"rewarded" by management by being offered to become one of them.  Obviously
anybody with any kind of ambition at all would jump at that chance. 
Alternative paths should be available to reward someone with a career path
that doesn't involve management.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What next??

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@paralynx.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-10T12:48:17-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CDC2401.6D8F@paralynx.com>`
- **References:** `<BCEB8.29704$%1.3390215@news-binary.blueyonder.co.uk> <4145699b.0205071910.49a9cc5c@posting.google.com> <2fnC8.4123$m46.306910@news-binary.blueyonder.co.uk> <4145699b.0205092011.123972f@posting.google.com> <3CDB5FCA.5613@paralynx.com> <abgkvh$71c$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> On  9-May-2002, Ed Guy <ed_guy@paralynx.com> wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> What city/cities did you find the converse?

London & Birmingham from personal experience (in the early & mid 60s - I went to 
Saskatchewan in 1967) but what applies in those two generally applies to the rest of the 
UK.
```

#### ↳ Re: What next??

- **From:** "Last Boy Scout" <Crazy-Joe@whitehouse.gov>
- **Date:** 2002-05-14T00:18:37-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<abq6le01g47@enews2.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<BCEB8.29704$%1.3390215@news-binary.blueyonder.co.uk>`

```
Try applying for positions for smaller companies.  Small companies often
require workers to Design new programs, do lots of maintenence programming,
run and print reports, attend meetings, Go to obligatory boring training,
surf the net, Write boring documantation, back up files, computer system
maintenence, etc, etc.

Before they let you do anything it usually requires you to learn the entire
file system and write a couple programs on the test system.

Anyway smaller companies often let you learn more than you can imagine.  At
a large company you may never even run a program as programmer.  They
probably would make you start as a job scheduler or on the help desk for 2
years before you ever worked on a program.

Hang in there and learn more about finding the job.  Find someone that will
help you rewrite your resume and how to sell your existing skills.  A person
often has more useable skills than they are aware of.

It doesnt hurt to learn new skills.  Database skills are often in demand.
JCL is a little different on every job.  If you want to study JCL find some
place where they teach it on an acutal mainframe.  Nothing like doing it for
real.  Another option is applying for an internship.  If they have programs
like that in your area.
```

## Thread: COBOL FAQ — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### COBOL FAQ

- **From:** wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
- **Date:** 2002-05-07T12:02:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,news.answers,comp.answers
- **Message-ID:** `<computer-lang/cobol-faq_1020772894@rtfm.mit.edu>`

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2002/05/06
Version: 3.03
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Spring 2002

The full COBOL FAQ can be viewed at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send
private email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

Toward the top of the FAQ is a pointer to the appendix listing the most
recent significant updates and changes.

This FAQ answers such questions as (and addresses topics such as):

 - Where can I get a COBOL compiler? 
 - Is there a free COBOL compiler? 
 - Where can I contact... ?
 - What is happening with the draft of the next COBOL Standard and what
is in it? 
 - What about OO COBOL? 
 - Books about COBOL
 - COBOL Tools
 - What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
 - What about USAGE? COMP? Storage for data in xyz format? etc?
 - How do I get started with COBOL? Where can I get education?
Tutorials? Etc

and much, MUCH more
```

<a id="t-9aba43bc12"></a>
## Thread: URGENT - Error 114 (Signal 11) — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### URGENT - Error 114 (Signal 11)

- **From:** cstrm@yahoo.com.br (Wueislly)
- **Date:** 2002-05-07T12:24:55-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1717d0ac.0205071124.64f0f0ff@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,

I need some help with an error that I'm receiving in cobol.

Well, first some information about the system:

Cobol/HP-UX HP35326 B.13.26
V4.1 revision 10 build 10/10/2 G; 33697. Run Time System
OXUPR/BMB/013260

I'm running a C program that calls a Cobol program and this cobol
calls many other cobol programs and c programs ...
The error is:

Execution error: file 'blbn_olnrc'
error code: 114, pc=0, call=1, seg=0
114 Attempt to access item beyond bounds of memory (Signal 11)

This error is not occuring every time, sometimes when it occurs and we
try to run again with the same data it finish successfully.

Anyone knows what can cause this type of error ?

Thankx
Wueislly
```

#### ↳ Re: URGENT - Error 114 (Signal 11)

- **From:** "kurimao" <kelpogs@singnet.com.sg>
- **Date:** 2002-05-10T23:23:52+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<abgomm$8g1$1@dahlia.singnet.com.sg>`
- **References:** `<1717d0ac.0205071124.64f0f0ff@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,
In the OS/390 environment, we use to get the same error but flagged as
U4038. This is usually
caused by tables being accessed beyond it's max occurence. Try checking some
file data
being loaded into the tables without bounds-checking.

Hope this helped you a little.

"Wueislly" <cstrm@yahoo.com.br> wrote in message
news:1717d0ac.0205071124.64f0f0ff@posting.google.com...
> Hi,
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> Wueislly
```

#### ↳ Re: URGENT - Error 114 (Signal 11)

- **From:** "Reason" <steveno@nocfs.com.au>
- **Date:** 2002-05-13T14:08:22+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<x%GD8.15$t06.2080@nsw.nnrp.telstra.net>`
- **References:** `<1717d0ac.0205071124.64f0f0ff@posting.google.com>`

```
Possible causes from my experience:
- A runaway index in Cobol.
- A bug in the C program.
- Wrong parameter or parm sequence.
```

## Thread: Cobol and C — 8 msgs

### Cobol and C

- **From:** cstrm@yahoo.com.br (Wueislly)
- **Date:** 2002-05-07T12:36:52-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1717d0ac.0205071136.159bcfea@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,

I'm using HP-UX and MF Cobol and I have a doubt about C and cobol.

I have a cobol program that calls a C program.
The cobol is like this:

01 WS01-NAME-REC OCCURS 100.
   03 WS01-FIRST PIC X(20)
   03 WS01-LAST  PIC X(20)


CALL 'FunctioncalledbyCobolprogram' USING WS01-NAME-REC.


An the C is:


typedef struct t_names
{
 char firstname[20];
 char lastname[20];
} t_name_t;


int FunctioncalledbyCobolprogram( t_name_t *nameslist )
{

 sprintf( nameslist[0].firstname, "%s", "First Name" );
 sprintf( nameslist[1].firstname, "%s", "Second Name" );
 sprintf( nameslist[2].firstname, "%s", "Third Name" );

 return(1);
}


Note that in the cobol we have an array of 100 and the C is receiving
as a pointer but filling the data as an array (nameslist[0]...) is
this correct or I should do the declaration as below ?!

int FunctioncalledbyCobolprogram( t_name_t *nameslist[100] )

What is the difference ? Can the first one any cause problem ?


Thankx
Wueislly
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol and C

- **From:** ctimmer@gci.net (Curt Timmerman)
- **Date:** 2002-05-07T20:03:12
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Xns92077A9E2388Actimmermanacsalaskac@10.136.100.32>`
- **References:** `<1717d0ac.0205071136.159bcfea@posting.google.com>`

```
cstrm@yahoo.com.br (Wueislly) wrote in 
<1717d0ac.0205071136.159bcfea@posting.google.com>:

>Hi,
>
…[45 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I believe 't_name_t *namelist[100]' will give you a array of pointers which 
is definately not what you want.

Try:
int FunctioncalledbyCobolprogram( char *nameslist )
and print the raw data at *nameslist to see what you are actually getting 
from the cobol call.

The Cobol call may be doing something wierd because you are not using a 
subscript when it's expected.

Curt
```

```
Wueislly
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol and C

- **From:** "JJ" <jj@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-08T00:19:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Da_B8.102178$Ii2.8928951@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<1717d0ac.0205071136.159bcfea@posting.google.com>`

```
The C sprintf function will expect that nameslist[n].firstname will be null
terminated.  The Cobol that I use will not give the C subroutine a null
terminated string - they will be space padded.  You would first have to scan
backwards from the end of namelist[n].firstname for the last non-space
character and stick a '\0' after it if you want to use sprintf or any C
library function that deals with strings.


"Wueislly" <cstrm@yahoo.com.br> wrote in message
news:1717d0ac.0205071136.159bcfea@posting.google.com...
> Hi,
>
…[45 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol and C

- **From:** cstrm@yahoo.com.br (Wueislly)
- **Date:** 2002-05-08T07:25:33-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1717d0ac.0205080625.12ff8196@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<1717d0ac.0205071136.159bcfea@posting.google.com> <Da_B8.102178$Ii2.8928951@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
JJ,

As Richard had wrote the OCCURS can not be on 01 level, this was my
mistake I skipped this when writing the message, the correct cobol
code is:

01 WS01-NAME-REC.
  03 WS01-NAMES OCCURS 100.
    05 WS01-FIRST PIC X(20)
    05 WS01-LAST  PIC X(20)

CALL 'FunctioncalledbyCobolprogram' USING WS01-NAME-REC.

This code and the C part are working fine, with or without the null
terminator, my problem or my doubt is if it's ok in the long run,
let's suppose I use this concept on a huge program that process a lot
of data is it ok to declare the receiving parameters in C as 't_name_t
*nameslist' (NOT an array) and then populate the data in nameslist
using 'nameslist[0].firstname, nameslist[1].firstname, ... ) ?


Thankx
Wueislly







"JJ" <jj@nospam.com> wrote in message news:<Da_B8.102178$Ii2.8928951@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>...
> The C sprintf function will expect that nameslist[n].firstname will be null
> terminated.  The Cobol that I use will not give the C subroutine a null
…[55 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol and C

- **From:** "Jude Robert" <juderobert@cox.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-10T01:45:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<YCFC8.12889$km2.595223@news1.east.cox.net>`
- **References:** `<1717d0ac.0205071136.159bcfea@posting.google.com> <Da_B8.102178$Ii2.8928951@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <1717d0ac.0205080625.12ff8196@posting.google.com>`

```
The sprintf will add a '\0' to the end of the string and this could be a
problem
depending on what the COBOL program is doing with the data. Also, if the C
program uses
a string variable in the sprintf statement and that variable contains a
string longer
than 19 characters (one needed for the null terminator) it will flow over
into the
next element. This is not what you want, this is not how COBOL acts.

I would use memcpy to copy up to a max of 20 characters and if there are
fewer than 20 characters use a second memcpy to pad with spaces.


"Wueislly" <cstrm@yahoo.com.br> wrote in message
news:1717d0ac.0205080625.12ff8196@posting.google.com...
> JJ,
>
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> "JJ" <jj@nospam.com> wrote in message
news:<Da_B8.102178$Ii2.8928951@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>...
> > The C sprintf function will expect that nameslist[n].firstname will be
null
> > terminated.  The Cobol that I use will not give the C subroutine a null
> > terminated string - they will be space padded.  You would first have to
scan
> > backwards from the end of namelist[n].firstname for the last non-space
> > character and stick a '\0' after it if you want to use sprintf or any C
…[52 more quoted lines elided]…
> > >
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol and C

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-05-08T08:53:58+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CD8D996.ACDEB701@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<1717d0ac.0205071136.159bcfea@posting.google.com>`

```
Wueislly wrote:
> The cobol is like this:
> 
> 01 WS01-NAME-REC OCCURS 100.
>    03 WS01-FIRST PIC X(20)
>    03 WS01-LAST  PIC X(20)

OCCURS is not allowed on an 01 level.

"The OCCURS clause should not be specified ... at the 01 level or as a
77 level-number."

> CALL 'FunctioncalledbyCobolprogram' USING WS01-NAME-REC.
 
There is no subscript on the OCCURing item.  Move the OCCURS to a new 02
level and try again.
```

---

```
102800     03  FILLER          PICTURE  X(01)  VALUE @1C@.
103000     03  MAXI-CITY       PICTURE  X(16).
103100     03  FILLER          PICTURE  X(01)  VALUE @1D@.
103300     03  MAXI-STATE      PICTURE  X(02).
103400     03  FILLER          PICTURE  X(01)  VALUE @1E@.
103600     03  FILLER          PICTURE  X(03)  VALUE EOT.
103700**   03  FILLER          PICTURE  X(01)  VALUE @04@.
103900**   03  FILLER          PICTURE  X(01)  VALUE "4".

then do a Write inv-line from maxi-line.

in the cobol application.

I get this:

&%1B$&a495h1665V?$?????9660506?840?003?1Z11829885?UPSN?567933?126??01/01?002
?N?

in monitor mode which looks right but the printer doesn't seem to see the
EOT and then prints Maxicode Data too long. I get some text for some other
barcodes.

Jim Rutz
```

## Thread: Aborting Programs - C++, Java etc. — 54 msgs

### Aborting Programs - C++, Java etc.

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-08T17:49:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CD964CE.D0EC5318@shaw.ca>`

```
My question is related to OO COBOL, but the same must prevail with those
of you using C++, Java etc., where you have classes which are more than
just called sub-routines.

As with procedural COBOL, there are many instances where an OO  program
can go wrong, using COBOL files - an error on file-status, SQL tables -
SQLSTATE/SQLCODE <> zeroes, attempting to create droplists or listboxes
where the records required are missing - the list is not exhaustive, but
you get the idea.

With a procedural COBOL program, the above is handled fairly concisely -
hit an error condition and instruct the program to GO TO EXIT-PROGRAM or
PERFORM EXIT-PROGRAM.

I can't help but feel that I'm making a 'big meal' out of  this, but
this is how I'm currently handling it :-

method-id. "mainLine"
----------------------
do some housekeeping
set ContinueProgram to true
invoke self "openFiles"

if ContinueProgram
    invoke self "makeListboxCollection"

    if ContinueProgram
        invoke self "processRecords"

        if etc...
-------------------------------------
method-id. "openfiles".
-------------------------------------

*> not exactly how I do it, but conveys possible errors

Local-storage section.
copy "sqlreslt.cpy: replacing ==(tag)== by ==01 ls==.

invoke os-CustomerFile "openIO" returning ls-SqlResult

if ResultOK
   invoke os-InputTransactions "openInput" returning ls-SqlResult

    if ResultOK etc.....
    .

 if FileError ( OR TableError)
    invoke self  "abortProgram" - and the method "abortProgram" merely
does this :-
                    (set CancelProgram to true)
End-if
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------

By their very nature, methods in OO (parallel to a PERFORM PARAGRAPH  if
you like), start at the beginning of the method, proceed through the set
of code, possibly invoking further methods from within the same class or
another class, returning back to the 'active' method and continue
processing through to the end of the method, indicated by either EXIT
METHOD or END METHOD. (This is clearly apparent when viewed through the
debug Animator - going step-by-step, you see the program 'closing out'
on each method which has been invoked in a particular cycle. Same of
course applies to performing PARAGRAPHS).

It seems (at least to me), that the trick is to signal an error, which I
test with 'if ContinueProgram.....", to ensure that the 'active' method
doesn't continue to function as though everything is still kosher - if
you allow it to do that, inevitably the program/class logic will crash.
I can invoke the "abortProgram" to 'set CancelProgram to true' from many
methods, including those resulting from invokes to other classes." This
tends to result in numerous tests throughout many methods to ensure that
CancelProgram hasn't yet been triggered, and also to ensure that these
methods 'jump' to EXIT/END METHOD, rather than attempting further code..

Recall, procedural code, just tell the program to either go to or
perform EXIT-PROGRAM.

Does the question make sense to you - and if you are doing something
parallel with other OO languages - exactly how do you handle this ?

Qualifier to above : I'm am totally using OO classes. You can invoke
'utility' classes from a procedural program - and that gives you the
ability to do the standard EXIT-PROGRAM.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```


Howard Brazee wrote:

> We have a system where programs call an external program to do an abort.  I
> suppose there must be some advantage to this - possibly in operations, but
> having a dump of the called program is absolutely useless to programmers.

Using suck-it-and-see, I did try a method "exitProgram" :-

Method-id. "doSomeStuff"
--------------------------
Local-storage (or even Global W/S)
01 ls-Customer-info.
 copy "sqlreslt.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==05 ls==.
 copy 'cusrecrd.cpy" replacing ==(tag1)== by ==05 ls==,
                                               ==(tag)== by == ls==.

invoke os-CustomerFile "readPrimeKey"
    using ws-key returning Customer-info

if FileError (that's the sqlresult above)
    invoke self "exitProgram"
    *>  see changed text below
End-if

move ls-Name to ws-whatever
move ls-balance to ws-balance etc.

End Method "doSomeStuff"
-----------------------------
Method-id. "exitProgram".
-----------------------------
 EXIT PROGRAM
End Method "exitProgram".
----------------------------

In OO it doesn't do a damn thing for you. "doSomeStuff" certainly invokes the
"exitProgram" method, but after that returns to the last 'active' statement and
attempts to move those fields, even if you have a crashed index.

At the moment, and I consider it to be not a very attractive approach,
(although one could argue this is XP(eXtreme Programming), where I commented
above, substitute the following :-

if FileError (that's the sqlresult above)
    invoke self "abortProgram" - ( at least this is setting a flag to

CancelProgram)
    EXIT METHOD
End-if

That takes care of getting out of "doSomeStuff", but that may in turn, with a
domino effect, have been invoked as the nth method in this cycle. As it is, I
have to ensure that the 'earlier' methods are checking to see whether
CancelProgram was set, (i.e. if ContinueProgram.......).

It's a bummer.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
Sorry for not being more explicit. The flag is in Global W/S accessible by all
methods.

WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01 FlowFlag                            pic 9 value 0.
    88 ContinueProgram            value 0.
    88 CancelProgram               value 1.

I'm not sure where you are going with this one, but I would certainly
appreciate any input that indicates I was traipsing down the wrong path
unnecessarily.

Just one point - those individual methods you referred to, for the most part
they are invoking classes containing either COBOL files or SQL Tables. Now just
in case it helps in your thinking, this is that sqlreslt.cpy :-

------------------ sqlreslt.cpy -----------------------

   (tag)-SqlResult                  pic 9(03).
    88  ResultOK                   value 0.
    88  FileResultOK              value 0.
    88  RecNotFound             value 1.
    88  NoMoreRows             value 2.
    88  FileFinis                       value 2.
    88  DuplicateKey               value 3.
    88  DataError                    value 97.
    88  CursorError                 value 98.
    88  FileError                      value 99.
    88  TableError                   value 97 thru 99.

*>---------------------------------------
```
