###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Aborting Programs - C++, Java etc.

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-09T12:50:37-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<abectn0shj@enews4.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<3CD964CE.D0EC5318@shaw.ca> <abbso302lp1@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CD97EE4.B5E1D4CC@shaw.ca>`

```
> Sorry for not being more explicit. The flag is in Global W/S accessible by
all
> methods.

Thanks for the extra info.

> I'm not sure where you are going with this one, but I would certainly
> appreciate any input that indicates I was traipsing down the wrong path
> unnecessarily.

There are at least four styles of error handling, but I wouldn't say that
any one of them is wrong.  I do have my preference, though, backed up by a
rational argument.  I hope it is of use to you, but I'm unsure which of
these options are available to you in OO COBOL.

Option 1:  Error flags

This is the approach you're asking about.  I certainly do this (for exit
code, for example) but will use Inline errors if they're available to me.

Option 2:  Inline errors

This is a popular mechanism for libraries to use.  The snag for COBOL is
that it requires a functional form.  Just for illustration, please allow me
to make "openFiles" into functions that return True on success, False on
failure.

Your code would look something like this:

method-id. "mainLine"
----------------------
do some housekeeping

if invoke self "openFiles"
    if  invoke self "makeListboxCollection"
        if invoke self "processRecords"
            ...

        end-if

        invoke self "destroyListboxCollection"
    end-if
    invoke self "closeFiles"
end-if

This may only seem marginally different that your original code, but it has
an advantage.  The information you need is important at the instant you
return from your call.  By using a temporary variable (the return value of
the function,) you can tightly scope that information to the part of your
code that needs it.

When you, or someone else, comes back to the code, you don't have to seek
out the dependencies (who writes and who reads) upon your working-storage
flag, because that logic is now in the syntax.

Option 3:  Error handlers

If the language permits it, you can set an error handler.  On a macroscopic
scale, this is similar to establishing a debugger for your operating system
to run when there's a system fault.  In code it would look something like:
(Please forgive the syntax, I'm making it up as I go along:


method-id. "mainLine"
----------------------

    on error goto MainExit:

    do some housekeeping


    invoke self "openFiles"
    invoke self "makeListboxCollection"
    invoke self "processRecords"

MainExit:
    invoke self "destroyListboxCollection"
    invoke self "closeFiles"

This is simpler code, but in part because of some different assumptions
about the calls.  "closeFiles", for instance, will have to be smart enough
to not generate additional errors in attempting to close files that are
already closed.

I like this better than inline errors because it's a bit cleaner to read.
"openFiles" will generally not fail, so it's error-trapping is physically
(if not algorithmically) distanced from it.  For cases where success/failure
is an expectation rather than an exception, a inline errors can be combined
with this approach:

method-id. "mainLine"
----------------------

    on error goto MainExit:

    do some housekeeping


    if invoke self "checkForNewData"
        invoke self "openFiles"
        invoke self "makeListboxCollection"
        invoke self "processRecords"
    end-if

MainExit:
    invoke self "destroyListboxCollection"
    invoke self "closeFiles"


Option 4:  Exception blocks

This is my personally preference.  It's a good comprimise between the tight
localization of inline errors and the "as-needed" application of
error-handlers.  There are a couple of forms, but they basically look like:

    invoke self "openFiles"
    try
       ...
    finally
        invoke self "closeFiles"
    end-try

I've only included one call in this example.  After entering the try block,
the finally block will be executed in any event -- error or not.  After the
finally block is executed, any error will be re-raised for containing blocks
to handle.  This is a concise way to indicate that the files must be closed
if they've been opened.  You can rack up multiple blocks:

    invoke self "openFiles"
    try
        invoke self "makeListboxCollection"
        try
           ...
        finally
            invoke self "destroyListboxCollection"
        end-try
    finally
        invoke self "closeFiles"
    end-try

Or, you can use an except block.  Except blocks will be executed in the
event of an error, but the error will not be re-raised.  So, the
error-handling routine can look like:

method-id. "mainLine"
----------------------
    do some housekeeping

    try
        invoke self "openFiles"
        invoke self "makeListboxCollection"
        invoke self "processRecords"
        ...
    except
        // report error
    end-try

    invoke self "destroyListboxCollection"
    invoke self "closeFiles"

Try ... Finally/Except/Catch blocks are a fixture of OO languages, so I
would expect there to be an analog in OO COBOL.  Even if they cannot be
teamed with inline errors, they're a descriptive way of marking exceptional
blocks and their means of recovery.

________

I hope that this is of some use to you -- please don't hesitate to ask for
elaboration on any point.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Aborting Programs - C++, Java etc.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-09T16:38:32-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<abeq9201jj9@enews4.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<3CD964CE.D0EC5318@shaw.ca> <abbso302lp1@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CD97EE4.B5E1D4CC@shaw.ca> <abectn0shj@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CDADFE5.567BCE67@shaw.ca>`

```
> Just for interest - you will see Bill pointed me at three new aspects in
COBOL
> 2002, plus it will also contain a series of EC (Exception Conditions),
somewhat
> like Declaratives. As it stands Net Express does have an Exception Handler
class.

This sounds like Option 3, but I'm unsure of the scope.  Do you makes these
declarations for each class, or for the application as a whole?

> - I read it some while back - it didn't appear to fit. (From memory, it
was
> allowing you to build up a series of messages for errors). Maybe I should
go
> back and give it a more thorough read in view of what you have written.

Sometimes the error-handling mechanisms offered by the development platform
have a logging system hooked into them.  Although this can be of use, it's
not of much worth for ground-level error-handling.  Your programs reaction
to a specific error is highly contextual to the location of the error.  Any
global error-handler cannot know how to react to this in a meaningful way,
other than to report that it happened.

It sound like I could stand to read something as well.  Can you direct me to
a specification (or as near as you can get) to the strain of OO COBOL you're
using?

> Reassuring to know it wasn't just me 'creating a problem' - your examples
> indicate the thought you've got to give this. Dear 'ole COBOL does appear
to
> beat the OO crowd when it comes to using the good ole EXIT PROGRAM <G>

It's really the same issue in any program, COBOL or Java, OO or
procedural -- you have to be careful to trap errors.  In any domain, you'll
need to know if you should continue, and if not, you should be able to clean
up after yourself.

> Now a quick, dirty solution would be to CALL procedural Business Logic
programs
> from a Master Menu, and then each procedural program merely INVOKES
support
> classes as necessary.

> The OO COBOL syntax allows you to create object reference
> handles for the classes you would continuously be using.

It has "factories?"

> But my first steps into OO, based on Net Express, (and Ed Arranga's book
(Editor
> of cobolreport.com)) was totally using classes. As you don't need
reminding,
> given I had classes A through H, (what I in-elegantly refer to as 'Ring a
ring
> of roses'), pass the handles of all the others to a particular class, and
all of
> them A through H have the ability to invoke one another.

I've missed an important point of your question.  What I've said still
holds, but it doesn't draw out any difference between procedural and
objective error-handling.  Fortunately, there isn't much.

The rules of thumb for error trapping/handling that I use:

(1)  Trap any error that you can handle.
(2)  Let those that you can't, pass up to "someone" who can.
(3)  Raise errors for situations you can't handle.

If you do this, your code will read like a description of its normal
operation, rather than a laundry list of exception conditions.

Your strategy concerning objects will be affected by the platform.  Some
systems have garbage collection, such that you do not have to explicitly
destroy objects.  If that's the case, you can haul off and use them like
your working storage.  All of the aforementioned "rules" apply.

If you don't have garbage collection, try ... finally blocks are
particularily useful.  (Sorry about the Pascal.)

// attempt to instantiate an object
// if an exception occurs, execution will jump
// to the next exception block (not shown.)
SomeObject := TSomeClass.Create;

// do what you need to with the object
try
  SomeObject.SomeMethod;

  if SomeObject.SomeFunction then
  begin

  end;

finally
  // if the object is created, it will always be
  // destroyed here
  SomeObject.Destroy;
end;
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Aborting Programs - C++, Java etc.

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CDAFF6E.F77AB8BF@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3CD964CE.D0EC5318@shaw.ca> <abbso302lp1@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CD97EE4.B5E1D4CC@shaw.ca> <abectn0shj@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CDADFE5.567BCE67@shaw.ca> <abeq9201jj9@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```


Grinder wrote:

> This sounds like Option 3, but I'm unsure of the scope.  Do you makes these
> declarations for each class, or for the application as a whole?

I haven't absorbed this, purely extracted it from on-line help for your info :-
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
RASING AN EXCEPTION :
An object raises an exception when it has trapped an error. The objects in the
supplied class library define approximately ninety exception conditions
altogether. You can define your own exception conditions for objects you create.

ExceptionManager class provides most of the logic for actually handling
exceptions, including a default exception handler. The default exception handler
displays an error message and shuts down the application when an object raises
an exception, but an application can define its own exception handlers and
attach them to individual classes or objects to redefiine their behavior.

An object raises an exception by sending itself the "raiseException" message,
passing an error number as a parameter. The Base class implements the
"raiseException" method, so it is inherited by all objects.
We will use the Bank application introduced in the previous section as an
example of how to raise an exception. When an account object traps an error, it
responds by raising an exception. For example, attempting to open a high rate
account with an initial balance of less than $1000 is an error condition.

REGISTERING AN EXCEPTION HANDLER
In the previous session, you saw how to raise an exception. This session shows
you how you can register an exception handler for an object. An exception
handler is a method which gets the exception number and the object raising the
exception as parameters.
You can register an exception handler for a class or an instance object. If you
register an exception handler against a class, then it is invoked for exceptions
raised by all instances of the class as well as by the class itself.

Exception handler registration is a two step process:

Create a CallBack for the exception method.
 Register the CallBack against an object with the ExceptionHandler.

Next you will animate through the code which sets exception handlers for the
Account classes in the Bank application.

Animating exception registration code

At this point the BankApplication has finished its initalization and is ready to
run. In the next session, you will look at the exception handler method, and see
how it deals with an exception raised by the account classes.

WRITING AN EXCEPTION HANDLER
This section of the tutorial shows you how to write an exception handler method
to deal with exceptions raised by an object. We will look at the exception
handler the Bank application uses for dealing with exceptions from the account
classes. In the case of a failure to open a High Rate Account, the exception
handler returns a null object handle to the "openAccount" method.
The "openAccount" method code in the HighRateAccount class then returns the null
object handle from the exception handler in place of a valid handle to an
account object. The exception handler could be designed to do something else;
for example, failure to open a High Rate Acount because of an insufficient
initial investment could return a Savings Account instead.
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

> It sound like I could stand to read something as well.  Can you direct me to
> a specification (or as near as you can get) to the strain of OO COBOL you're
> using?

That one's a bit difficult, primarily because it is inclusive in the N/E
package. However, there are On-line Books, Language Reference Manuals, Books 1
and 2. Unfortunately Micro Focus, very recently, switched from the old Merant
title back to Micro Focus - but my current URL to the books doesn't appear to
work.

Let's try this one, Bill or Jean in Quebec - what is the latest URL that will
get us into the books ?.

Another one for BIll - for Grinder's info - can you re-quote that URL to take
him to the ISO draft dated 2001 Dec 4 ? (Grinder - the ISO draft is
comprehensive for OO with the exception of collections/dictionaries and
'Finalizer' to include automatic garbage collection). Using N/E at the moment I
use their own specific 'finalizer' methods to destroy objects - and YES :-

    if thisObject <> null
       invoke thisObject "finalize/deepfinalize/depfinalizewithdepth"
        returning thisObject
    End-if

As regards "lists" collections/dictionaries there are three compilers that have
them, Fujitsu, Micro Focus and Hitachi - the latter two are certainly based on
Smalltalk collection hierarchy.

I could suggest go to the M/F site to look at examples, but I don't think that
would help too much. Being demos, for the most part, they are stand alone
procedural programs doing invoking.

>
> It has "factories?"

Yes :-
--------------------------------------------------------------
Class-id.            ThisClass inherits from .....

Class-Control    CharacterArray is class "chararry"
                         SortedCollection is class "srtdcolln"
                         .
*>----------
FACTORY
*>----------
Method-id "new".
*>---------------
Linkage section.
01 lnk-self                            object reference.
Procedure Divison returning lnk-self.
 invoke super "new" returning lnk-self
End Method "new".
*>------------------
other Factory methods
END FACTORY.
*>------------------
OBJECT.
*>---------------
++OBJECT/@@WORKING-STORAGE  SECTION
++ = Micro Focus, and @@ = December 2002, J4 Standard
*>--------
instance methods
END OBJECT
END CLASS ThisClass.
*>-----------------------

>
> (1)  Trap any error that you can handle.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>

As my comment above - as a substitute for garbage collection currently, use the
M/F "finalizer" methods

Still in the process of building, but Young Kim at U of Iowa is starting some OO
COBOL on-line tutorials - see http://www.kimsoft.com/api-cobol/oo-cobol.htm

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Aborting Programs - C++, Java etc.

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-10T01:26:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CDB2185.9BCC43C4@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3CD964CE.D0EC5318@shaw.ca> <abbso302lp1@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CD97EE4.B5E1D4CC@shaw.ca> <abectn0shj@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CDADFE5.567BCE67@shaw.ca> <abeq9201jj9@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CDAFF6E.F77AB8BF@shaw.ca>`

```


"James J. Gavan" wrote:

> Grinder wrote:
>
> > This sounds like Option 3, but I'm unsure of the scope.  Do you makes these
> > declarations for each class, or for the application as a whole?
>

Grinder,

Further to my previous, posted the messages here in CLC to a discussion group. Got a real
quick response from Calin in Toronto :-
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
The try-catch thing that C# is using looks perfect.  More than that, you
can"throw" exception at your own free-will!  Maybe we can get the J4 (or the
vendors) to implement it?  DREAM ON!

The exception manager is not that bad. This is how I do it in every object
I'm worried about (MicroFocus NetExpress):
1. create callback to a method (ie onMyErrors) in an exception class (plain
class, inherits from base);
2. register the object with the exception manager
|            invoke ExcManager "register"
|                    using theObject
|                             theCallBackMethod     -> onMyErrors
3. the method onMyErrors has the parameters:
|            - errorObject [IN]
|            - errorNo [IN]
|            - errorExtraINfo [IN]
|            - errorReturn [OUT]

What you do in it is check the error number:
- if it's one of yours, you jump on it;
- if it's not one of yours, but you want to process it (ie exit the app),
you do it;
- if it's not one of yours and you don't know what to do, you pass it to the
defaultHandler.

Not fault proof, but it works fine so far.
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
The reference to C# prompted Bill to ask for an explanation. Like me he knows about as
much about C# as we both do about Hindi <G> His question was - won't the new COBOL
feature RAISE an exception (object) do the same thing ?

Calin's example I understand. Seems that using Exception Handler class with the
callbacks, I can save myself a lot of aggro. More importantly, his example works TODAY,
not wait for something available in x years time.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Aborting Programs - C++, Java etc.

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-10T12:55:58-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<abh1jp01437@enews2.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<3CD964CE.D0EC5318@shaw.ca> <abbso302lp1@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CD97EE4.B5E1D4CC@shaw.ca> <abectn0shj@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CDADFE5.567BCE67@shaw.ca> <abeq9201jj9@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CDAFF6E.F77AB8BF@shaw.ca> <3CDB2185.9BCC43C4@shaw.ca>`

```
> The try-catch thing that C# is using looks perfect.  More than that, you
> can"throw" exception at your own free-will!  Maybe we can get the J4 (or
the
> vendors) to implement it?  DREAM ON!

From what I've seen, the strategy of your code throwing/raising errors is
underutilized.  Some programmers are so focussed on "fixing" errors that
they overlook the benefit of catching the problem upstream.

> What you do in it is check the error number:
> - if it's one of yours, you jump on it;
> - if it's not one of yours, but you want to process it (ie exit the app),
> you do it;
> - if it's not one of yours and you don't know what to do, you pass it to
the
> defaultHandler.

This is very compatible to my philosophy.
```

```
 - go to

 - Select
     Draft COBOL standard
 - Get a ZIPped copy by selecting
     Latest DRAFT of ISO/IEC 1989 - COBOL (2 MB zipped)

FDIS ("Final Draft International Standard") should be "available" soon - but
exactly where and from whom and by whom isn't quite finalized.  Thane, or
Chuck, or Stephen, or I will certainly post a URL when/if it is generally
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Aborting Programs - C++, Java etc.

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-10T20:47:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CDC31A2.D691C14A@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3CD964CE.D0EC5318@shaw.ca> <abbso302lp1@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CD97EE4.B5E1D4CC@shaw.ca> <abectn0shj@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CDADFE5.567BCE67@shaw.ca> <abeq9201jj9@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CDAFF6E.F77AB8BF@shaw.ca> <abh1ba013pf@enews2.newsguy.com>`

```


Grinder wrote:


> > > This sounds like Option 3, but I'm unsure of the scope.  Do you makes
> these
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> to.....

see my other message which will take you to the books (On Line Dox)

>
> I can't admit diligence in the search, but I've had difficulty reading up on
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Can you spell that one out, because I'm not sure I follow you.

Some possible comments as to what you might be alluding to. On the PC-front, as
Bill pointed out quite some while ago, most vendors just didn't want to know,
didn't want to get into expensive development, if the end result (cash) was
iffy.

Micro Focus - certainly their Ray Obin with two others was one of the initial
evangelists for OO COBOL. M/F published a paperback which Ray wrote on behalf of
the OCTG ( I think ?), sub-group to J4, in 1993. Based on the earliest, or one
of the earliest drafts, M/F set to with an OO compiler, product 'X' (don't know
its name), preceded VISOC and Net Express. The latter two came with a complete
set of support classes including GUI classes where literally, given the time, I
could replicate Intuit's Quickbook screens with draw and paint. (Sure the
support classes eventually finish up calling APIs - but to the developer you are
writing 'semi-readable' COBOL - by that I mean, it's geared to those Windows
functions)
As I've indicated for collections/dictionaries, they followed the Smalltalk
hierarchy.
Net Express now also includes classes to communicate with Java.

Don't know start date, but then Fujitsu got involved. Their version more closely
followed the draft standard at the point in time they got involved. I believe
their colleciton/dictionary approach is somewhat different.

Meanwhile, on the other side of the planet - Hitachi are doing their own thing.
We don't know, because they don't market in the West. (But they are very
*aactive* members of J4). Again their collections/dictionaries were based on
Smalltalk.

Then there is Big Blue. Oh dear, don't ask me for details. They did produce an
OO - VisualAge for OS/2. Mainframe wise, they've danced around, at one stage
SOM, and their very latest offering, while it does have OO syntax, appears to be
little more than an opening paragraph to getting into Java. (Loving 'Billy G.',
I think this is tied into the triumvirate - IBM/HP/Sun (i.e. Java). Now that may
not be a totally accurate summary of Big Blue, but enough to indicate that they
are still saying to themselves, "Which way is up ?

One of the PC vendors claimed they asked their user base and there was no
interest in OO. Supposing M/F had asked this dummy :-

M - "We're thinking of introducing an OO compiler"
J - 'What's that ?.
M - "Let's you create objects for Windows".
J - Thinks 'objects ?', and then 'Windows - I don't want to touch that with a
barge pole. I'll stick with dear old DOS and the Screen Section''.
J - "Can you explain 'objects' ?" (Well we *know* to the uninitiated, that is
virtually impossible - you just have to suck it and see)
M - They try and explain 'objects' and follow with "Like writing in C or
Quick-C, or Pascal".
J - BONG ! Bell goes off in J's mind. He took a short Quick-C course, locally,
and the instructor, who earned his daily bread by coding in Pascal, confused the
hell out of the class by expressing examples on the black-board in Pascal, until
somebody gently pointed out we were supposed to be learning Quick-C !
J "Pascal. Forget it !".

Ridiculous vendors asking the unwashed about a new product/philosphy - they have
to be the leaders on this, and take a gamble, and introduce OO just like F/J and
M/F did.

With the exception of Garbage Collection(Finalizer) and collections/dictionaries
the OO COBOL standard is CONCRETE. Seems at this stage even the 'Finalizer' is
now at least semi concrete, as a final PDF has been issued. This month, J4 gets
down to a discussion of collections/dictionaries. It they handle it correctly,
it should be a trade-off between F/J, Hitachi and M/F approaches, plus any
ensuing techniques which have developed since '96 from other OO languages.

Although late off to the races, I'm gambling we couldl finish up with a basic
set of support classes which will be superior to anything in other OO
languages.,

Does that answer your comments, 'in considerable dispute' or 'what the standard
should be' - I'm not being partisan, but I don't think either observation
applies. History is history, but would have been nice if the OO Standard had
come out much, much earlier.

If there is any dispute at all, then it must be occurring in the corridoors of
power amongst the 'suits'at Head Office. It is most certainly not apparent at J4
meetings - one of which I partially attended in 2000.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Aborting Programs - C++, Java etc.

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-10T20:05:18-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<abhqoo02kkg@enews1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<3CD964CE.D0EC5318@shaw.ca> <abbso302lp1@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CD97EE4.B5E1D4CC@shaw.ca> <abectn0shj@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CDADFE5.567BCE67@shaw.ca> <abeq9201jj9@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CDAFF6E.F77AB8BF@shaw.ca> <abh1ba013pf@enews2.newsguy.com> <3CDC31A2.D691C14A@shaw.ca>`

```
> Does that answer your comments, 'in considerable dispute' or 'what the
standard
> should be' - I'm not being partisan, but I don't think either observation
> applies. History is history, but would have been nice if the OO Standard
had
> come out much, much earlier.
>
> If there is any dispute at all, then it must be occurring in the
corridoors of
> power amongst the 'suits'at Head Office. It is most certainly not apparent
at J4
> meetings - one of which I partially attended in 2000.

You're got a lot more industry awareness than I do on the point, but (from
your description as well) there are multiple approaches to making an OO
COBOL.  I envy COBOL's history of standards, but in the end, a vendor has to
implement it for it to be of use.

I'll showcase my ignorance a bit more, if you'll entertain some more
questions?

All of my experience with COBOL has been in refactoring a DOS-based
application to utilize SP2, so I've not seen any OO features in real live
code.  So, if you'll indulge me, I'm curious.  (Again, I apologize for
syntax.)

(A)  Outside of a class, can you define local procedures?

Each program has a main-procedure that has access to a working storage area,
but can you write subprocedures to take care of repetitive tasks?  I'm not
talking about a perform of a paragraph, but rather a distinct unit of code
with its own _local_ working storage that cannot be accessed by the main
line of execution?

Something like this:

// working storage available to all code in this "unit"
declare TotalBalance 999999V99
declare PathToInput PIC(500)
declare PathToOutput PIC(500)

procedure Main
  // call a procedure to get to path to the date
  GetDataPaths

end-procedure

procedure GetDataPaths
  // local storage -- used by DataPath, but no other code in this unit
  declare TempPath PIC(500)

  // perhaps you do some stuff to get a temp path thats
  // used to construct the other paths
  move something into TempPath
  add somethingelse to TempPath

  // finally move the prepared data into unit-level storage
  move TempPath to PathToInput
  add "/input" to PathToInput

  move TempPath to PathToOutput
  add "/output" to PathToOutput
end-procedure

It's my understanding that you can do this as seperate methods within a
class, but I'm unclear if this is a language extension, or if it's "compiler
magic" that will make this happen.

(B)  Along similar lines -- if you have local procedures, do you have local
functions as well?

Could GetDataPaths be form so as to return a status (inline errors) as to
it's ability to get the paths?  Specifically I mean by passing that return
through temporary storage without having to declare working storage for the
result to go into?  Something like this:

if GetDataPaths then ...

(C)  Outside of classes, again, can you dynamically create in-memory
storage?

Can I, at runtime, say that TempPath should be a PIC(1000), or do I have to
decide this before I compile?

This was the largest distinction I saw between COBOL and other languages
I've used.  I'm sure I would howl about it in some circumstances, but it
intrigues me that you can learn so much about the program from a static
analysis.

___________

At any rate, I've blathered on quite about now.  Thanks for your remarks to
date, I'm picking up an education.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Aborting Programs - C++, Java etc.

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2002-05-11T03:33:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-pjTrUcygDZc1@thishost>`
- **References:** `<3CD964CE.D0EC5318@shaw.ca> <abbso302lp1@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CD97EE4.B5E1D4CC@shaw.ca> <abectn0shj@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CDADFE5.567BCE67@shaw.ca> <abeq9201jj9@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CDAFF6E.F77AB8BF@shaw.ca> <abh1ba013pf@enews2.newsguy.com> <3CDC31A2.D691C14A@shaw.ca> <abhqoo02kkg@enews1.newsguy.com>`

```
On Sat, 11 May 2002 01:05:18 UTC, "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
wrote:

> > Does that answer your comments, 'in considerable dispute' or 'what the
> standard
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

The above would be in "Class" working storage and would be available 
to all the code in the "methods" for the class

> procedure Main
>   // call a procedure to get to path to the date
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

The call to the "procedure" which would be a method in the class would
be

invoke SELF "GetDataPaths"

> procedure GetDataPaths
>   // local storage -- used by DataPath, but no other code in this unit
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

This is true. The methods within a class are not a language extension,
but are part of the OO specifications of the COBOL lanquage

Class level "working storage" is available to all methods within the 
instantiation of the class, method level "local storage" is available 
only to the particular instantiation of the mehod. There is/was also 
method level "working storage" that was available across all 
instatiations of a method (I believe this was removed from the 
standard).

> (B)  Along similar lines -- if you have local procedures, do you have local
> functions as well?
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

The specification of "functions" is not the same as definition of 
methods. They are separate parts of the language. The "methods" can 
return a value but it is not the same as the "value" returned by a 
function.

> (C)  Outside of classes, again, can you dynamically create in-memory
> storage?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

You can allocate storage through operating system API calls and assign
the address of the allocated storage to a COBOL linkage section 

With Visualage COBOL on OS/2 I can use the "OMMAllocate" routine to 
allocate some storage and then use the "OMMFree" routine to free the 
storage when I no longer need it.

This can be compiler/runtime specific (like OMMAllocate/OMMFree), 
although  you can usually use the OS API calls for private and shared 
storage management.

> This was the largest distinction I saw between COBOL and other languages
> I've used.  I'm sure I would howl about it in some circumstances, but it
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
```

```
> Class level "working storage" is available to all methods within the
> instantiation of the class, method level "local storage" is available
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> standard).

That makes sense (for it NOT to be in the standard, that is.)  Class methods
are reasonable, but Class data storage works against the division between
class defintion | object instance.  I can't say I wouldn't use it if it's
available to me, but if you need to relate data to a group of objects, it
should be part of your data model, not tucked away into class storage.
(Hope I'm not sounding preachy.)

Can you utilize methods outside of an object?  (I guess they would be called
procedures, in that case.)  That's the one feature I would have retroactivly
included in the original design of COBOL.  I hope I'm missing something, but
if there were procedures in COBOL, there would be a lot less dispute about
Structured COBOL.

> The specification of "functions" is not the same as definition of
> methods. They are separate parts of the language. The "methods" can
> return a value but it is not the same as the "value" returned by a
> function.

I'm not fully understanding this.  Can you give me a quick blurb for each?

Also, this is something new with OO COBOL -- Gad, I hope I've not missed
something so useful.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Aborting Programs - C++, Java etc.

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2002-05-11T07:29:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-MTKD7gt4RMsx@thishost>`
- **References:** `<3CD964CE.D0EC5318@shaw.ca> <abbso302lp1@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CD97EE4.B5E1D4CC@shaw.ca> <abectn0shj@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CDADFE5.567BCE67@shaw.ca> <abeq9201jj9@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CDAFF6E.F77AB8BF@shaw.ca> <abh1ba013pf@enews2.newsguy.com> <3CDC31A2.D691C14A@shaw.ca> <abhqoo02kkg@enews1.newsguy.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-pjTrUcygDZc1@thishost> <abiar10gda@enews1.newsguy.com>`

```
On Sat, 11 May 2002 05:39:34 UTC, "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
wrote:

> > Class level "working storage" is available to all methods within the
> > instantiation of the class, method level "local storage" is available
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

The removal of "Method working storage" that is available across 
instantiations is a GOOD thing. 

Class working storage is unique to the class instance.

Class attributes are unique to the class instance and are visible to 
other classes.

Class methods are visible to other classes and act like the "messages"
in Smalltalk.

> Can you utilize methods outside of an object?  (I guess they would be called
> procedures, in that case.)  That's the one feature I would have retroactivly
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
If a class is an object.... Methods are only available with the 
class... (part of the language definition)

> > The specification of "functions" is not the same as definition of
> > methods. They are separate parts of the language. The "methods" can
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

By function, I meant the same thing as an "Intrinsic Function" (which 
is part of the language since COBOL-92 IIRC) where the syntax looks 
like

move Function current-date to my-date

current-date is a function that returns the current date. It has a 
"type" of character 

There is a long list of intrinsic functions available.

Functions are "in line" in the code and act as if they are a data item
as far as the language syntax goes. They also accept arguments like 
Function numval(a-data-field) which returns a floating point number.

Methods are invoked/called by the "Invoke" verb. An method can have a 
list of input parameters and a list of output parameters.

invoke SELF "MyMethod" using arg-a arg-b returning arg-c


I believe that there were requests for "user defined" functions that 
would act like the "Intrinsic functions" but I do not think these made
it into the standard, although I would have liked to have them 
available.

> Also, this is something new with OO COBOL -- Gad, I hope I've not missed
> something so useful.
> 

OO COBOL has been around for a while. The Microfocus Workbench product
had it back in the mid 90's and so has IBM Visualage COBOL. These two 
vendors were implementing bits of the "proposed standards" as 
extensions.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Aborting Programs - C++, Java etc.

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-11T20:21:52-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<abkg3l$9v4$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3CD964CE.D0EC5318@shaw.ca> <abbso302lp1@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CD97EE4.B5E1D4CC@shaw.ca> <abectn0shj@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CDADFE5.567BCE67@shaw.ca> <abeq9201jj9@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CDAFF6E.F77AB8BF@shaw.ca> <abh1ba013pf@enews2.newsguy.com> <3CDC31A2.D691C14A@shaw.ca> <abhqoo02kkg@enews1.newsguy.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-pjTrUcygDZc1@thishost> <abiar10gda@enews1.newsguy.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-MTKD7gt4RMsx@thishost> <abj3ma$3e2$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <abkd6g0tf9@enews2.newsguy.com>`

```
Well ...

Speaking of what is in the draft of the next Standard (not what is in the
current one - or some existing implementations) and TRYING to make
distinctions that I *think* match what you are talking about.

The draft Standard has "several" types of things that appear where
traditionally one "just" had Program-Id paragraphs.  I think speaking a
little about these may help you understand what is in the draft.

1) Program-Id defines a "program".  These programs may be nested in each
other (with Global data or "local" data).  Such programs can have parameters
passed to them (as they always have) but they now can also "return" a value
to the accessing entity.  Programs *may* be CALLed by other programs or by
functions (see below) or by object methods (see below).  Another "new" thing
is that they may be recursive (if explicitly identified as such) - in which
case "local-storage" items are allocated for each instance - while
working-storage is available to every instance (and EXTERNAL data is
available to everything in the run-unit).

2) INTRINSIC functions are a list of "standard" functions that return
values.  These do lots of "numeric" things (Standard deviation, PI, etc) -
data manipulation (To/From Alphanumeric/National, To/From Upper-case) -
comparisons (MAX, MIN, ORD-MAX, etc) - system/environment things
(CURRENT-DATE, DATE-COMPILED, etc) and a variety of other things. Again,
these may be accessed by programs, user-defined functions, or object methods

3) User defined functions are very similar to "programs" - in fact, other
than a "new" Function-ID paragraph, there is almost no difference between a
user-defined function and a program with a RETURNING phrase.  The difference
is that *ALL* user-defined functions MUST have a RETURNING phrase. Again,
such functions may be accessed by other user-defined functions, by programs,
or by object methods.  Because these user-defined functions are ALWAYS
"pre-defined" (by the "user"), there is "complete" conformance checking at
compile time that parameters passed and returned to user-defined functions
match what they are supposed to be.  (Conformance checking via "program
prototypes" is now available, but not REQUIRED for programs)

4) Objects - as indicated elsewhere have both "FACTORY" (one per class) and
INSTANCE definitions (one definition per class, but the actual "code" exists
separately for each instance of an object of that class).  Within both
FACTORY definitions and INSTANCE definitions, there are "methods" which are
the actual procedural code.  These may include inline (via :: operator) or
out-of-line (via INVOKE) references to other object.  However, they may all
use CALLs to programs and references to both intrinsic and user-defined
functions. Like user-defined functions, "conformance" checking is done (or
can be done) at compile-time (when possible), at run-time, or be
"turned-off" by the programmer.

  ***

Bottom-Line:
  The new COBOL "world" is a "mix-and-match" one where you can use as many
(or as few) of these types of entities as you want.  Furthermore, there are
"definitions" which make COBOL "procedures" and "functions" and "objects"
available to non-COBOL entities and allow COBOL to access such entities in
other languages as well.

This is (obviously) somewhat of a "simplification" - but does it help?
 Looking thru the CONCEPTS annex in the draft Standard gives examples and
"over-view" explanations of much of this.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Aborting Programs - C++, Java etc.

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-11T20:27:18-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<abkgdo$h14$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3CD964CE.D0EC5318@shaw.ca> <abbso302lp1@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CD97EE4.B5E1D4CC@shaw.ca> <abectn0shj@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CDADFE5.567BCE67@shaw.ca> <abeq9201jj9@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CDAFF6E.F77AB8BF@shaw.ca> <abh1ba013pf@enews2.newsguy.com> <3CDC31A2.D691C14A@shaw.ca> <abhqoo02kkg@enews1.newsguy.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-pjTrUcygDZc1@thishost> <abiar10gda@enews1.newsguy.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-MTKD7gt4RMsx@thishost> <abj3ma$3e2$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <abkd6g0tf9@enews2.newsguy.com> <abkg3l$9v4$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I should have also mentioned that SOMETIMES when people are talking about
COBOL and used to other "terms" what is meant by "procedure" is what COBOL
defines as
 - a paragraph
    or
 - a section
    or
 - an "inline" PERFORM

The biggest difference (besides the fact that these have to do with the
PERFORM statement rather than CALL or INVOKE or function) is that these have
no way of defining "local" data to the procedural code.  All 3 of them are
available to PROGRAMs, FUNCTIONs (user-defined), or object METHODs.  I don't
think this is what the original confusion/question was about, but it does
sometimes come into the question of "COBOL procedures".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Aborting Programs - C++, Java etc.

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-12T03:12:25+00:00

- **References:** `<3CD964CE.D0EC5318@shaw.ca> <abbso302lp1@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CD97EE4.B5E1D4CC@shaw.ca> <abectn0shj@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CDADFE5.567BCE67@shaw.ca> <abeq9201jj9@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CDAFF6E.F77AB8BF@shaw.ca> <abh1ba013pf@enews2.newsguy.com> <3CDC31A2.D691C14A@shaw.ca> <abhqoo02kkg@enews1.newsguy.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-pjTrUcygDZc1@thishost> <abiar10gda@enews1.newsguy.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-MTKD7gt4RMsx@thishost>`

```
lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca wrote in message news:<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-MTKD7gt4RMsx@thishost>...

> The removal of "Method working storage" that is available across 
> instantiations is a GOOD thing. 
> 

I agree.  This very issue is what finally got me motivated to join the
committee.  There is one thing "missing" from the specification in the
new standard, and that is STATIC data (we have what we call static
data, but it's not the same thing that one might know of as static
data when using one's vocabulary from other languages).  What we do
have - which is Unique, is a Factory object.  (Not unique in name, or
invention, but in implementation).  Each class has a single factory
object which has the NEW method for creating object instances.  There
can be methods in this object and data defined in the object, and
properties (exposed data).  This data is not accessible DIRECTLY by
instance objects but is accessible via factory methods and properties.

What we DO NOT have - is data that is defined within a method that
persists for the life of the object instance and is accessible only to
that method of that instance.  The change I wanted - which was not
made - was to allow working-storage in methods, but to make it
"private" data - accessible only in that method, for the duration of
the object's life.  Local-Storage is allowed in methods but its
re-initialized for each method invocation.

I finally relented on this request - knowing that it's easy to add,
where a change in the "then" existing specification of working-storage
items in methods being like "static" data - accessible via all
instances - was a nightmare and would not be easy to remove from an
approved standard.

The work around?  Allocate some storage and reference it using a based
entry in the linkage section of the method.  HOWEVER - this is
dangerous in that if you kill the object - the storage will not be
reclaimed.  (no way for anyone to find it!) - However, once we have
Finalizer, a Finalizer could issue the FREE statement and release this
storage.

> > > The specification of "functions" is not the same as definition of
> > > methods. They are separate parts of the language. The "methods" can
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> available.

These *ARE* in the next standard.
```

```
"Thane Hubbell" <thaneh@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:bfdfc3e8.0205110539.4dab64d7@posting.google.com...
> lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca wrote in message
news:<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-MTKD7gt4RMsx@thishost>...
>
> > The removal of "Method working storage" that is available across
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Thane, could you please elaborate a bit more?  I have not actually coded my
own factory yet, and have been using the "new" invocation as implimented.
If I understand what you are saying above, I could code my own factory, and
when I code:

    INVOKE CLASS-NAME "new" RETURNING object-reference.

the code I write for the factory method is executed as part of the creation
of the object?

Lets say that I created a factory method that opened an Isam file, and read
it sequentially until it found an unlocked record.  Once it found an
unlocked record, it locked the record, and initialized it's properties to
the record value.  When it could not find an unlocked record, it locked the
file, and returned an error.

I then create a second class of objects that inherits the first.  When it is
created, it wakes up with either the "ERROR-FLAG OF SELF" set to an
error-condition, or the "ISAM-RECORD OF SELF" set, as it has inherited those
properties from it's inherited class.

If there is an error, returns it.  If there is not, it stores the object
reference in an array, and returns after incrementing ISAM-RECORD-COUNT.

I write a test program:

        It says something like:

                PERFORM LOCK-ISAM-FILE until
                    ERROR-FLAG of ISAM-OBJECT IS EQUAL TO "Y".

    LOCK-ISAM-FILE.
            INVOKE ISAM-OBJECT-CLASS "NEW" RETURNING ISAM-OBJECT.

As I understand it, I have now created a number of objects equal to the
record-count of the isam file, and the isam file is locked.  The object
reference ISAM-OBJECT now points to the last instance created.  That last
instance also has a complete array of object pointers, each loaded with a
record. I could have methods in that object that treat the data as a table,
by using the object references in the array as pointers, and the following
code could produce the correct answer.

        DISPLAY
            "Number of records in the isam file is "
             ISAM-RECORD-COUNT OF ISAM-OBJECT.
        DISPLAY "They are:"
        PERFORM DISPLAY-RECORDS
            VARYING LOCAL-TEMP-NUMBER FROM 1 BY 1
        UNTIL
            LOCAL-TEMP-NUMBER IS GREATER THAN
                ISAM-RECORD-COUNT OF ISAM-OBJECT.

DISPLAY-RECORDS.
        MOVE LOCAL-TEMP-NUMBER TO ARRAY-SUBSCRIPT OF ISAM-OBJECT.
        INVOKE ISAM-OBJECT "DISPLAY-RECORD-FOR-SUBSCRIPT".

Is that the general idea?

Donald
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CDD4F87.DE9B5FAC@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3CD964CE.D0EC5318@shaw.ca> <abbso302lp1@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CD97EE4.B5E1D4CC@shaw.ca> <abectn0shj@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CDADFE5.567BCE67@shaw.ca> <abeq9201jj9@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CDAFF6E.F77AB8BF@shaw.ca> <abh1ba013pf@enews2.newsguy.com> <3CDC31A2.D691C14A@shaw.ca> <abhqoo02kkg@enews1.newsguy.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-pjTrUcygDZc1@thishost> <abiar10gda@enews1.newsguy.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-MTKD7gt4RMsx@thishost> <bfdfc3e8.0205110539.4dab64d7@posting.google.com> <h9bD8.16482$q76.2106310@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```


Donald Tees wrote:

>
> Thane, could you please elaborate a bit more?  I have not actually coded my
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Don,

This is one I didn't initially get a handle on - the significance of INSTANCES.
Like you are indicating, I religiously started each class with invoke "new" -
which of course, automatically gives me an Instance by returning an object
reference to the class.

Instancing is only applicable when you want 'more than one copy' - just like I
have a generic ISAM class, (that's based on your idea of having the PrimeKey in
the first 20 characters), and within one Business class may want to access five
different ISAMs, so I have an instance for each. (Just as an aside - don't
forget this ties in with Thane's *changes* - limiting what can be in instance
methods).

Will Price, to avoid confusing students, does most of his *work* solely in
Factory.
 Both you and I can work the same way. Obviously if you invoke "new" you are
going to get handles (object references) that you can use in the caller.

But similarly, if you only need ONE instance, i.e., just Factory, you invoke the
class WITHOUT an object reference. Two examples :-

1. CharacterArray class in Net Express - (used for turning text or numerics into
objects, either for collections or to be displayable in a Window). You DON'T do
following :-

    invoke CharacterArray "new" returning os-MyCharacterArray

instead taking one of the appropriate Factory methods it becomes :-

  invoke CharacterArray "withLengthValue"
         using lnk-length, lnk-text returning ls-string

2. Similarly I set myself up with a class Converter (Imp to Met and vice versa),
and used invoke "new" - but no justification for creating an instance. Again I
could have, (haven't changed the program yet), accessed that class as follows :-

    invoke Converter "ImperialToMetric" using InputValue
                                returning OutputValue

HTH

Jimmy
```

```
instance
> methods).
>

Would it not also apply if you wanted to return to the object later?  As I
understand it, the examples below create AND destroy the instances in one
step.  That means that if you return later, it is a new instance, not the
one that was called before. I can see where the shortcut below is usefull at
the very bottom of the inheritance chain, but at higher levels within the
chain, you want to collect the objects into groups.  At that level, you are
ALWAYS going to have more than one instance.  Am I missing something?

> Factory.
>  Both you and I can work the same way. Obviously if you invoke "new" you
are
> going to get handles (object references) that you can use in the caller.
>
> But similarly, if you only need ONE instance, i.e., just Factory, you
invoke the
> class WITHOUT an object reference. Two examples :-
>

What if I need just one instance, but I need it to persist for the duration
of an entire program, because I am going to return later.  Can I do that
without an object reference handle?

example:

        invoke com-port-handler "new" returning object-reference-one.
        move "com1:" to comport-port of object-reference-one.
        move "barcode-reader" to function-code of object-reference-one.
        set device-object (number-of-devices) to object-reference-one.
******number-of-devices initialized to zero in ws.
        add 1 to number-of-devices.
****** initialize other devices here.
        perform cash-register
            until
        "1" is equal to "2".

     cash-register.
        perform scan-devices
            varying local-subscript from 1 by 1
        until
            local-subscript is greater than number-of-devices.

    scan-devices.
        invoke device-object (local-subscript) "check-for-product-code"
        if product-code of device-object is not equal to space
            perform ring-up-sale.

I guess the real question is, why have a factory if you are not going to
need more than one instance, at some level or another?

If I understand the object methodlogy correctly, you start with factory
objects, and build them into collections.  You then write common methods for
the collections, and package them into new factories.  The new factories
become your new tool set ... that is the bottom up portion of the
developement.

If you are developing top down, I do not see the point in trying to develop
factories. From a top down standpoint, making a method a factory method
seems to be just a way of packaging it.

> 1. CharacterArray class in Net Express - (used for turning text or
numerics into
> objects, either for collections or to be displayable in a Window). You
DON'T do
> following :-
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 2. Similarly I set myself up with a class Converter (Imp to Met and vice
versa),
> and used invoke "new" - but no justification for creating an instance.
Again I
> could have, (haven't changed the program yet), accessed that class as
follows :-
>
>     invoke Converter "ImperialToMetric" using InputValue
>                                 returning OutputValue
>

I there a reason beyond getting rid of the statement that creates the object
before and the statement that destroys the object after?  Or is this just
neat way of packaging a completed object?

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Aborting Programs - C++, Java etc.

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-11T21:10:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CDD8878.7B8E40EF@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3CD964CE.D0EC5318@shaw.ca> <abbso302lp1@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CD97EE4.B5E1D4CC@shaw.ca> <abectn0shj@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CDADFE5.567BCE67@shaw.ca> <abeq9201jj9@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CDAFF6E.F77AB8BF@shaw.ca> <abh1ba013pf@enews2.newsguy.com> <3CDC31A2.D691C14A@shaw.ca> <abhqoo02kkg@enews1.newsguy.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-pjTrUcygDZc1@thishost> <abiar10gda@enews1.newsguy.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-MTKD7gt4RMsx@thishost> <bfdfc3e8.0205110539.4dab64d7@posting.google.com> <h9bD8.16482$q76.2106310@news20.bellglobal.com> <3CDD4F87.DE9B5FAC@shaw.ca> <cedD8.16654$q76.2162922@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Good points Don,

First let's recap on an INSTANCE - check that example Line Sequential I just did
for Grinder. I think it's fairly clear from there, having "setFileInfo" with
full pathname and record-size for this INSTANCE - they are accesible each time
you use an object reference to invoke the File class. Very important, same
applies on getting the  current file-status which is held per instance.

Firstly you are right - if you invoke a class (Factory methods) without an
object reference :-

    invoke myParticularClass "do this" using x returning y

each time you come back from that class it is 'dead'; each new invoke starts
from scratch - you've lost any values it contains that you might be after. Now
PROVIDING, (and I haven't tiried it, but logically it has to be right), you
invoke that same class with method "begin" which in turn references a whole
series of methods within the FACTORY, and at the final exit returns one set of
values, then you are OK.

If you want the ability to get back at a support class then you have to
establish a handle, (just as I do with files), allowing you to invoke instance
(OBJECT SECTION) methods :-

    invoke MyObjectReference "dothis" using x returning y.

Now there is a twist - I do this at the Instance level but haven't tried it with
Factory. It's what I call a "two-way handle".

Program A - invoke ProgramB "new" using self returning os-ProgramB

Note the "self" above.

ProgramB -
Factory
-------
Method-id. "new".
Linkage section.
01 lnk-ProgramA                            object reference.
01 lnk-self                                       object reference.
Procedure Divsioin using lnk-ProgramA returning lnk-self
    invoke super "new" redturning lnk-self
    invoke lnk-self 'setCallerHandle" using lnk-ProgramA

OBJECT SECTION (instance methods)

WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01 os-Caller                                            object reference.
Mehod-id."setCallerHandle".
Linkage section.
01 lnk-caller                                object reference.
Procedure Divsion using lnk-caller.
 set os-Caller to lnk-caller (i.e. ProgramA)

- Now elsewhere we have a instance method that does something :-

Method-id. "doSomething".
Local-storage section.
01 Myresult                                pic 9(06)v9(02).
Procedure Division.
    compute this giving that
    compute that giving MyResult
    invoke os-Caller "thisIsTheAnswer" using MyResult.

Obviously ProgramA *has* to have a method ""thisIsTheAnswer".

ONE INSTANCE - which is what you are interested in. Absloutely no reason why you
can't take that approach, but your "active"methods are the ones in the OBJECT
SECTION. That's how I constructed my Converter "ImpToMet" invoke "new" then
invoke the Instance/Object Section methods directly, and if I store values in
Converter's Global W/S then that info is available from any individual method
invoked..

This might help a bit, historicaly how I progressed. Back to the Radio Shack and
its 8 or 10 inch platters - bugger, you could fold the bloody things in half !
They were notorious for CRCs. So, eventually I built in, as the applicaiton
started up, check that the set of files I was currently interested in didn't
give me either 'crashed index' or files "not present"

I carrried that over to OO - in the application start-up, invoke 'common' files
and the file set applicable to a particular oil plant to ensure they were
present and correct. I'm now into SQL - but was using the CBL_FILE routines to
check for existence.
Didn't have a GenericISAM when I started, so for each file :-

    invoke ReadingsFile ":new" using xxxx returning os-ReadingsFile

Then I do an open and close to check it has a clean index.

    invoke os-ReadingsFile "openfile" returning ls-result
    invoke os-ReadingsFile "closefile" returning ls-result

*** The APP contains a complete set of Object References for ALL files required
- see below.

I then realised I could keep my Menu Selection simple with an :-

    invoke ReadingsEdit "new" using self, ParentWindow

Note the "self" again - I can get back to the App as the os-Caller.

When I get into ReadingsEdit, I need a set of files for this program (see the
*** above).:-

        set ReadingsType to true
        invoke os-Caller "getfileobject" using mySelection returning
os-ReadingsFile
        set ItemsType to true
        invoke os-Callter "getfileobject" using mySelection returning
os-ItemsFile

Yet one more neat twist in the flexibility of OO !!! (Don't you love it).

It's importaznt to note here - the 'common' files and set of plant files above
are ALL 'ONE INSTANCE" programs. I created them with 'new' to get a handle, but
each is (was) independtly formatted - no application of generic file classes
originally. (From memory, I think this is the approach you have taken to files).

When from the Master Menu the user wants to switch from Jumping Pound Creek to
Waterton Gas Plant, I DON'T need to create fresh instances.While using Jumping
Pound Creek the file class contains that full filepathaname. When he selects
Waterton, using the identical 'ONE ONLY INSTACE" I pass the new full
filepathname for Waterton Gas Plant.

Hopefully, I've clarified some of what you've asked below, plus I've also put
some comments within your text - if it is still murky, just come back with your
begging bowl <G>

Jimmy

Donald Tees wrote:

> "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
> news:3CDD4F87.DE9B5FAC@shaw.ca...
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> one that was called before.

Correct - see above - it's "dead" when you exit

> I can see where the shortcut below is usefull at
> the very bottom of the inheritance chain, but at higher levels within the
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>

As indicated you can have ONE "dummy" instance, triggered with "new" which
persists for the duration. Can you do it without an orbject reference - see my
comments about Factory.

>
> example:
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> need more than one instance, at some level or another?

The Factory "new" is the only way of triggering an instance, but subject to your
design, don't invoke "new" and all your methods can be in Factory and discount
the 'instance'.

>
> If I understand the object methodlogy correctly, you start with factory
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

As much as I admired Will's book I did get thrown by his examples of instance
objects - rather like I think you are talking, he was returning specfic file
records from instances as individual objects. It was Bill who triggered the
significance of GenericFiles and 50 instances created from the one template.

>
> If you are developing top down, I do not see the point in trying to develop
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
> Donald
```
