- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<abkd030t5f@enews2.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<3CD964CE.D0EC5318@shaw.ca> <abbso302lp1@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CD97EE4.B5E1D4CC@shaw.ca> <abectn0shj@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CDADFE5.567BCE67@shaw.ca> <abeq9201jj9@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CDAFF6E.F77AB8BF@shaw.ca> <abh1ba013pf@enews2.newsguy.com> <3CDC31A2.D691C14A@shaw.ca> <abhqoo02kkg@enews1.newsguy.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-pjTrUcygDZc1@thishost> <abiar10gda@enews1.newsguy.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-MTKD7gt4RMsx@thishost>`

```
> > Can you utilize methods outside of an object?  (I guess they would be
called
> > procedures, in that case.)  That's the one feature I would have
retroactivly
> > included in the original design of COBOL.  I hope I'm missing something,
but
> > if there were procedures in COBOL, there would be a lot less dispute
about
> > Structured COBOL.
> >
> If a class is an object.... Methods are only available with the
> class... (part of the language definition)

So there's been no deepening of the scope in straight (not object-related)
code?

> I believe that there were requests for "user defined" functions that
> would act like the "Intrinsic functions" but I do not think these made
> it into the standard, although I would have liked to have them
> available.

This fits with my understanding of what you've said out procedures
(method-like structures outside of the scope of an object.)  If OO COBOL
does not have procedures, it would be bizarre for it to allow you to define
funcitons.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Aborting Programs - C++, Java etc.

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-11T03:48:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CDC946E.A3FF27F8@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3CD964CE.D0EC5318@shaw.ca> <abbso302lp1@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CD97EE4.B5E1D4CC@shaw.ca> <abectn0shj@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CDADFE5.567BCE67@shaw.ca> <abeq9201jj9@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CDAFF6E.F77AB8BF@shaw.ca> <abh1ba013pf@enews2.newsguy.com> <3CDC31A2.D691C14A@shaw.ca> <abhqoo02kkg@enews1.newsguy.com>`

```


Grinder wrote:

> You're got a lot more industry awareness than I do on the point, but (from
> your description as well) there are multiple approaches to making an OO
> COBOL.  I envy COBOL's history of standards, but in the end, a vendor has to
> implement it for it to be of use.
>

I still say NO to that one. There was a progression, based on the versions of
the draft. Some of the old time J4 folks here Bill and Stephen would have to
confirm, but I don't think the 'shape' of OO has changed dramatically through
the progression.
Conceivably REPOSITORY syntax is new. I'm not up on that because N/E doesn't
contain it, but essentially, when compiling it allows you to 'forward check' to
ensure methods you are invoking in other classes from your current program are
available.
So the invoked class *must* exist and if necessary you can setup dummy methods.
Certainly within Net Express no more than two lines required :-

Method-id. "thisIsAdummy".
End Method "thisIsAdummy".

If I've screwed that up, be assured Bill will jump in <G>

One significant change, within the past year was a recommendation from Thane to
J4 that Working-storage in a method be disallowed. M/F allows both W/S which
means it can contain constant values or Local-storage. Plus within M/F I had the
ability to include File Definitions and Declaratives within methods. I didn't
care for it at the time, but Thane was dead-on. Allowing for W/S, Files etc.,
within methods conflicted with the concept of a FACTORY and the OBJECT being
instances of the class for umpteen files, not just one.

I don't want to bitch - but don't have envy. The last major change was COBOL 85
and now we are talking COBOL 2002.
You are well aware how the sexy PC languages have jumped ahead by leaps and
bounds, and without question we have lost COBOL programmers to the new
languages. ('Nuff said !)

>
> I'll showcase my ignorance a bit more, if you'll entertain some more
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You mentioned the magic letters "S-P-2" - Thane will absolutely L-U-V you and if
you show up at COBOL 2002 in Chicago I'm sure Bob from Flexus will give you a
big hug as well <G>

Before I attempt to answer the following - there are 'features' associated with
invoking that can be set in current Net Express, but  (1) NOT mentioned in their
examples and (2) NOT mentioned in the on-line help. But as a result of a query -
surprise, surprise they are *covered* in those language books I referred you to.
My guess most N/E programmers like me haven't incorporated this into their code,
not being aware of the existence.

Probably most comprehensive way to get an answer is look at that ISO Document,
searching on METHOD and INVOKE.

At this point I will stop,. I could go on forever ! So he can see what I'm doing
with GUIs I'm about to post some source to Young Kim - it's a complete module
invoked from a Master Menu editing a file, (SQL Table) and displaying a dialog.
Suggestion, there's a lot of source to wade through, but perhaps many of the
questions you pose below can be answered from this. Let me know if you would
like a zipfile. You can then always use that as a basis to ask me further
questions ?????

Just e-mail me privately with a 'Yes' or 'No'. (If and when you look at it,
remember it is my way and not necessarily the right way - interestingly it will
demonstrate my lousy "If ContinueProgram.....).

Jimmy

> (A)  Outside of a class, can you define local procedures?
>
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
> magic" that will make this happen.

> (B)  Along similar lines -- if you have local procedures, do you have local
> functions as well?
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> date, I'm picking up an education.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Aborting Programs - C++, Java etc.

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-11T01:12:27-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<abicom0l9s@enews1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<3CD964CE.D0EC5318@shaw.ca> <abbso302lp1@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CD97EE4.B5E1D4CC@shaw.ca> <abectn0shj@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CDADFE5.567BCE67@shaw.ca> <abeq9201jj9@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CDAFF6E.F77AB8BF@shaw.ca> <abh1ba013pf@enews2.newsguy.com> <3CDC31A2.D691C14A@shaw.ca> <abhqoo02kkg@enews1.newsguy.com> <3CDC946E.A3FF27F8@shaw.ca>`

```
> > You're got a lot more industry awareness than I do on the point, but
(from
> > your description as well) there are multiple approaches to making an OO
> > COBOL.  I envy COBOL's history of standards, but in the end, a vendor
has to
> > implement it for it to be of use.

> I still say NO to that one. There was a progression, based on the versions
of
> the draft. Some of the old time J4 folks here Bill and Stephen would have
to
> confirm, but I don't think the 'shape' of OO has changed dramatically
through
> the progression.

So vendors have mostly been implementing subsets of an existing standard?

When I looked at AcuCobol about a year ago, it looks as if they were
sneaking upon on an OO-ish implementation, much in the way VB pretends to be
OO.  I saw similar intentions (though not syntaxes) from PowerCOBOL and
NetExpress.  There's no reason to suspect their OO platforms would be "in
the wild" as well, but I apparently did.

> Conceivably REPOSITORY syntax is new. I'm not up on that because N/E
doesn't
> contain it, but essentially, when compiling it allows you to 'forward
check' to
> ensure methods you are invoking in other classes from your current program
are
> available.

> So the invoked class *must* exist and if necessary you can setup dummy
methods.
> Certainly within Net Express no more than two lines required :-

> Method-id. "thisIsAdummy".
> End Method "thisIsAdummy".

You would add these dummies (presumably with some sort of error throw in it)
to meet run-time considerations with complementary code, or compile-time
considerations?  (Under some sort of interface or class hierarchy
enforcement?)

>
> If I've screwed that up, be assured Bill will jump in <G>
>
> One significant change, within the past year was a recommendation from
Thane to
> J4 that Working-storage in a method be disallowed. M/F allows both W/S
which
> means it can contain constant values or Local-storage. Plus within M/F I
had the
> ability to include File Definitions and Declaratives within methods. I
didn't
> care for it at the time, but Thane was dead-on. Allowing for W/S, Files
etc.,
> within methods conflicted with the concept of a FACTORY and the OBJECT
being
> instances of the class for umpteen files, not just one.

I'll have to let that soak in a bit.  I've used factories, but no so
exclusively that I would want to give up local storage.  Are all (or almost
all) objects in OO COBOL instanced by a factory?

> I don't want to bitch - but don't have envy. The last major change was
COBOL 85
> and now we are talking COBOL 2002.

> You are well aware how the sexy PC languages have jumped ahead by leaps
and
> bounds, and without question we have lost COBOL programmers to the new
> languages. ('Nuff said !)

<Tangent>

When I was doing my undergrad, the [other] nerds in my department abstractly
railed against the "weaknesses" of liberal arts students -- English major
specifically.  Soft ideas vs. hard facts -- you've probably heard the bit.

The quickest way to dispense with it was to ask them the name of the
literature that had changed their lives.  Only a couple came up with decent
lies, but the argument was out of play.

</Tangent>

> You mentioned the magic letters "S-P-2" - Thane will absolutely L-U-V you
and if
> you show up at COBOL 2002 in Chicago I'm sure Bob from Flexus will give
you a
> big hug as well <G>

Bob is a gregarious guy -- a "gladhander" in my vocabulary.  I've not met
him in person, but he and a couple of guys from AcuCorp were the only decent
ones amongst an army of numbnuts that I had to fight through to get product
information.  (We were shifting to a new compiler, and I didn't like
Merant's disregard, so I met the competition.)

Thane's been great -- professional and academically.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Aborting Programs - C++, Java etc.

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-11T19:03:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CDD6AB6.BE2ED961@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3CD964CE.D0EC5318@shaw.ca> <abbso302lp1@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CD97EE4.B5E1D4CC@shaw.ca> <abectn0shj@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CDADFE5.567BCE67@shaw.ca> <abeq9201jj9@enews4.newsguy.com> <3CDAFF6E.F77AB8BF@shaw.ca> <abh1ba013pf@enews2.newsguy.com> <3CDC31A2.D691C14A@shaw.ca> <abhqoo02kkg@enews1.newsguy.com> <3CDC946E.A3FF27F8@shaw.ca> <abicom0l9s@enews1.newsguy.com>`

```


Grinder wrote:

>
> So vendors have mostly been implementing subsets of an existing standard?

Well not subsets, but the draft Standard as it was at that point in time. I'd
forgotten, Hitachi were fairly early starters along with M/F. (Ed Arranga's book
- he's Editor of cobolreport.com, does include as appendices the class
hierarchies for Hitachi, IBM and M/F - from memory, published in '96). Fujitsu
followed some time later.

I mentioned Ray Obin's handbook ('93), and don't see any dramatic change in
structure of a class or method since then, (subject to those restrictions
proposed by Thane - prohibiting some elements in methods which conflict with the
INSTANCE concept) . Really the draft enhancements, are the up from bits, like
Repository, giving you the ability of 'forward checking'. The latest draft does
include Multiple Inheritance, but I don't think anybody has implemented, as yet.

>
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I don't know AcuCOBOL but from messages posted here in the past their Screen
Section has a *dual* function, can be used for a DOS text Window or true GUI
display. Their approach does appear to be a 'sub-set' for Windowing. From a
recent announcement they've taken it a step further with their latest offerings
- but I don't think you could consider it as being OO-ish.

As regards the three that matter, at the current time, Hitachi (have no idea
what they may have done with GUIs), but both F/J and M/F have taken different
approaches to COBOL GUIs - there wasn't/isn't a standard. Similarly, again no
Standard, all three have their different Standard Classes, (includes
collections/dictoinaries). Obviously they differ but the invoking syntax is the
same.

>
> > So the invoked class *must* exist and if necessary you can setup dummy
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Assuming I've correctly spelled out Repository - (Bill hasn't jumped in yet <G>)
- you are wanting to get a clean compile of ProgramA - if it invokes ProgramB,
then B *must* exist. For purely compilation then the two dummy lines for a
method would be all that is required. When it comes to testing, yes, then either
you access the dummy method and it does absolutely nothing, or as you suggest
you throw an error.

>
> I'll have to let that soak in a bit.  I've used factories, but no so
> exclusively that I would want to give up local storage.  Are all (or almost
> all) objects in OO COBOL instanced by a factory?
>

First take a look at my response to Bill about CONSTANT use in methods. I also
did a "thumbnail" for Don on using FACTORY

As to using an INSTANCE with the file data "at the top", I'll repeat this :-
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
*>-----------------lsqfile.cbl-----------------------------------

Class-id.          LineSequentialFile
                   inherits from FileErrors.

Class-control.     LineSequentialFile      is class "lsqfile"
                   FileErrors              is class "filerror"
                   .

*> Note : All lines marked +++ MUST HAVE the same value as
*> the variable MaxRecordSize

*>---------------------------------------------------------------
FACTORY.
*>---------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "new".
*>---------------------------------------------------------------
Linkage section.
01 lnk-self                object reference.
Procedure Division returning lnk-self.
   invoke super "new" returning lnk-self
End Method "new".
*>------------------------------------------------------------
End FACTORY.
*>------------------------------------------------------------
OBJECT.
*>------------------------------------------------------------
FILE-CONTROL.

     Select               Data-File
     assign               Data-filename
     organization         line sequential
     access               sequential
     file status          ws-FileStatus.

*>---------------------------------------------------------------
FILE SECTION.

FD  Data-File
    record varying from 1 to 1500 characters        *> +++
       depending on ws-RecordSize.
01  Data-record           pic x(1500).              *> +++

*>---------------------------------------------------------------
OBJECT-STORAGE SECTION.
copy "LinkErr.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==ws==.
01 Data-filename                      pic x(100).
78 MaxRecordSize                      value 1500.
01 ws-RecordSize                      pic 9(04) value 1.

For brevity, I'll just list one "access" plus the method passing the parameters
for this particular instance.
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "readNext".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Linkage section.
01 lnk-ReturnValues.
copy "sqlreslt.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==05 lnk==.
05 lnk-record.
   10 occurs 1 to MaxRecordSize
      depending on ws-RecordSize     pic x.

Procedure Division returning lnk-ReturnValues.

  initialize lnk-ReturnValues
  Read Data-File next record
    At End
      Set fileFinis to True
    Not at End
      Move  Data-record (1:ws-RecordSize)
      to    lnk-record  (1:ws-RecordSize)
  End-Read

  if   ws-fileStatus = "00" or "10"
       continue

  else set FileError to true
       invoke super "begin" using ws-ErrorParams
  End-if

End Method "readNext".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "setFileInfo".
*>---------------------------------------------------------------
Linkage section.
01 lnk-Filename                  pic x(100).
01 lnk-Parent                    object reference.
01 lnk-recordSize                pic 9(04).
copy "sqlreslt.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==01 lnk==.

Procedure Division using     lnk-filename
                             lnk-Parent
                             lnk-RecordSize
                   returning lnk-SqlResult.

  set ResultOK          to true
  initialize               ws-ErrorParams
  move lnk-filename     to Data-filename
                           ws-filename
  set ws-Parent         to lnk-Parent
  move lnk-recordSize   to ws-RecordSize

  if   ws-RecordSize > MaxRecordSize
       move "RS" to ws-fileStatus
       set FileError to true
       invoke super "begin" using ws-ErrorParams
  End-if

End Method "setFileInfo".
*>---------------------------------------------------------------

Regarding private message, making a minor modificatin before I send zipfile.
It's where I use FACTORY methods for colleciton/dictionary creation, rather than
invoke "new". Probably early next week.

Jimmy
```

```
Mr. Grinder:

You do make me very curious as to your identity....

I was fairly sure as to the definition of "gladhander" but decided to
look it up anyway.  According to Meriam-Webster's:

To "glad-hand" is to:

transitive senses : to extend a glad hand to (candidates glad-handing
everyone they meet)

intransitive senses : to extend a glad hand (glad-handing as if he
were running for mayor)

I take it as a high complement.....although I am not currently running
for politcal office.  Perhaps I will seek the Mayoral seat in
Calgary....then I can watch Jimmy like a hawk!!

;-)

>Thane's been great -- professional and academically.

Everyone who has ever worked with Thane will agree with this
wholeheartedly!

Now here's a plug for my friends Ed Arranga, Will Price, et. al.
.......

Meeting Thane in person in Chicago on June 21-23 is the "icing on the
cake" for your attendance at COBOL Expo.  Lots of great speakers and
the opportunity to speak one-on-one with your COBOL compiler vendor or
3rd party tool vendor of choice.  

DISCLAIMER:  I received no compensation for this plug, despite the
fact that it was both shameless and opportunistic.  Ed should at the
very least buy me a cup of coffee in Chicago....or perhaps a
beer....for my plug of COBOL Expo....but I'll let him off the hook and
refer to this as a "public service announcement!"

;-)



Bob Wolfe
```

#### ↳ Re: Aborting Programs - C++, Java etc.

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-05-10T03:02:12+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3cdbbd42_2@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<3CD964CE.D0EC5318@shaw.ca>`

```
I read the posts in the thread, and Grinder has given a pretty comprehensive
solution. Unfortunately, OO COBOL does not support the preferred solution he
offered (yet), and his syntax is also invalid in OO COBOL. Nevertheless, his
ideas are spot on. It is my intention to look at implementing the Exception
Classes and Methods described below, in OO COBOL. For more on this, see
below.

As you have asked for comparison with how it is handled in C++, Java, etc.,
I'll expand a little on Grinder's excellent post and hope you can catch the
drift, Jimmy...

The following is based on my own experience which, in turn, is based on the
writings of John Zukowsky, who is (in my opinion, at least) one of the
World's best educators on Java. (Nearly everything I know about Java I
learned initially from his writings...)

Talking about the Basic Exception Handling model in Java he says:

"Java follows the basic C++ syntax for exception handling. First you TRY to
execute a block of statements. If an abnormal condition occurs, something
will THROW an exception that you CATCH with a handler. In addition, FINALLY,
there may be a block of statements that you always want executed, no matter
whether an exception occurred and no matter whether it is handled if it does
occur.

Throwing an exception is friendlier than terminating the program, because it
provides the programmer with the option of writing a handler to deal with
the exception."

So what we end up with is an event driven model. The main program invokes
some other Class. It raises an exception. Rather than coding a bunch of IFs
to test various possible error flags (which was one of the things I think
you are trying to avoid), you can simply let the "event" of raising the
exception, trigger the User written (or, in Java), System Provided handler
for it.

There are intimations of this in CICS where you can specify ON... EXCEPTION,
and SQL where you can specify (at least in DB2 you can...):

WHENEVER
  [NOT FOUND/ SQLERROR/ SQLWARNING]
  [CONTINUE/ GOTO host-label/ GO TO host-label]

         EXAMPLES

The following statements can be embedded in a COBOL program.

Example 1:  Go to the label HANDLER for any statement that produces an
error.

     EXEC SQL WHENEVER SQLERROR GOTO HANDLER END-EXEC.

Example 2:  Continue processing for any statement that produces a warning.

   EXEC SQL WHENEVER SQLWARNING CONTINUE END-EXEC.

Example 3: Go to the label ENDDATA for any statement that does not return.

     EXEC SQL WHENEVER NOT FOUND GO TO ENDDATA END-EXEC.

However the Object Oriented Languages take this much further, to the point
where you can write virtually ANYTHING as an event that can be caught by any
Method code that is interested in dealing with it.

Two Java Classes which may be of interest to you are:

1. SQLException

defined as:

public class java.sql.SQLException
extends java.lang.Exception
extends java.lang.Throwable
extends java.lang.Object

and

2. SQLWarning

defined as:

public class java.sql.SQLWarning
extends java.sql.SQLException
extends java.lang.Exception
extends java.lang.Throwable
extends java.lang.Object

Both of these Classes have their own Constructors and Methods, the second
one simply extending the first by adding 2 new Methods, as OO Classes are
wont to do...

Both of them are provided as Classes in the java.sql package. But suppose I
want to extend this even further? Say, for instance I want to know if an
INSERT failed.(This is a contrived example because the standard SQLException
can be made to return the SQLSTATE by invoking one of its methods, but it
will serve for the purpose of discussion...)
...
//InsertFailedException is a general exception for SQL INSERT problems...
//
class InsertFailedException extends SQLException{
 public  InsertFailedException () {
 }
 public InsertFailedException (String  whyitfailed) {
       super (whyitfailed);
 }
}

The Method that invokes this Class must specify that it THROWS
InsertFailedException. Let's say I have a Method in the Customer Class
called "insertNewCustomer"...

public String insertNewCustomer (...parms for this Method...)
          throws InsertFailedException, SQLException {
          ...includes code to set up the  INSERT statement and invoke a
stored procedure on the database to get a new
         Customer ID....
         ....
         if ((newID = getNewID ()) == null) {
             throw new InsertFailedException ('Could not obtain Customer
ID');
         }

(Note the difference between THROW and THROWS... a common source of error in
early Java programs).

Now I can insert a new customer and have the system automatically invoke my
INSERT Failure handler or my general SQL failure handler, without the need
for any flags or ifs...

try {
      insertNewCustomer = ...various stuff required for the Method, that we
are not concerned with here...
      }
      catch (InsertFailedException e){
        system.err.println (e);
      }
      catch (SQLException e) {
         system.err.println ('Database failure when creating Customer=' +
e);
      }

So, from this very brief exploration you can see that the OO paradigm allows
us to handle exceptions as events, allows us to extend existing exception
classes to tailor our own exception events, and allows us to write our own
exception handlers.

Now the question is: How could we implement all of this in COBOL
(specifically, OO COBOL)?

After looking carefully at the underlying Java Classes and how they are
built up from basics, I believe it IS possible. Certainly with PowerCOBOL it
is possible to define your own custom Events and Methods for an Object. (In
fact, Events are defined just like any other custom property.)

The TRY construct in Java kind of alerts the system to the fact that
Exceptions can be expected. I can think of no equivalent in COBOL at the
moment, but it may be possible to write a Class that provides the same
service.
(It might not even be necessary if the right Event processing can be
implemented...)

I would like to thoroughly investigate this and come up with some OO Classes
that could be invoked from OO COBOL to achieve the same effects as you see
above. Unfortunately, I simply don't have time at the moment. I have written
this response in the middle of the night...<G>

Nevertheless, I hope this gives some insight into how exceptions CAN be
handled in OO, and may stimulate you to do some more investigation of your
own, Jimmy.

HTH,

Pete.

news:3CD964CE.D0EC5318@shaw.ca...

> of you using C++, Java etc., where you have classes which are more than
…[50 more quoted lines elided]…
> --------------------------------------------------------------------------
--------
>
> By their very nature, methods in OO (parallel to a PERFORM PARAGRAPH  if
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
Good post Pete. You may recall I mentioned this same topic elsewhere some while
back - got no response. Well as a result of Grinder's messages which I repeated,
they've all opened up - at least the Net Express boys <G>.

You'll have seen my quote from Calin, and using the existing Exception Handler,
looks like this could be useful. However, as you said, look at all the possible
options and try and arrive at a suitable solution which suits your particular
programming style. Looking at Simon's comments, essentially don't necessarily
try to handle the complete gamut in one class, perhaps sub-classing, with a lot
of emphasis on objects.

Long term, as Bill indicated - OO COBOL with those added features may do the
trick, plus revamped Declaratives and the host of Exception Conditions to be
built in. (Both F/J and M/F - we may not be too far away from them actually
implementing those new features - they will both go at it neck and neck to stay
competitive)

However, we are interested in the here and now, and your thoughts are turn to
Java. You've probably already looked, but is there anything in the existing
POWERCOBOL classes that might assist you ? (What's behind that - If I stick with
a COBOL approach currently, ensuring any classes are flexible enough, it may be
possible to address the new features as they become available in either F/J or
N/E - let's be blunt - at the present time they are the only two that matter
when it comes to OO).

I *knew*, although I was handling it, (with a lot of emphasis on the developer
coding to trap errors),  there had to be a better way - now to sort out all the
possible options in my own mind, from the input here and elsewhere..

Jimmy
```

```
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The books I was talking about are a two volume jobbie, published with the
blessing of Sun Microsystems Press. The authors aren't shy about noting some
of Java's early deficiencies, plus they point out, to get around some of the
shortcomings of what we call cllections/dictionaries, they show how the
language had to introduce 'an how to' to fix the shortcomings. Each volume
comes with a CD.

Core Java 2 Vol 1 - Fundamentals - Cay S. Horstman & Gary Cornell - ISBN
0-13-081933-6
Vol 2 - Advanced Features - same authors - ISBN 0-13-081934-4.
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Aborting Programs - C++, Java etc.

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-05-13T11:01:49+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3cdf8d1c_7@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<3CD964CE.D0EC5318@shaw.ca> <3cdbbd42_2@Usenet.com> <01c1f90c$c8192760$f9c2f943@chottel>`

```
Charles,

The books that Jimmy has recommended, while being very good, are far too
heavy for someone starting Java (at least, in my opinion).

Also, Zukowski is really for advanced Java, after you have mastered the
basics.

I recommend "SAMS Teach yourself Java 2 in 24 hours" by Rogers Cadenhead.

Here's the URL to find out more about the book and to order it:
http://www.cadenhead.org/book/java2412/

This book is beautifully written and the man is not only extremely
knowledgeable, but also has a wicked sense of humour. In fact teaching
yourself Java with this book is an entertaining (as well as enlightening)
experience.

(I have no connection with the author and am not on commission, I just found
this book to be the most useful when I was learning Java <G>)

Once you have mastered the basics and got a few Java programs and Applets
working you will want to explore more interesting (read "advanced")
things... This is where  Zukowski comes in.

Try "Mastering Java 2" by John Zukowski. (ISBN: 00-7821-2180-2) published by
Sybex.

He has recently published another book on Java Collections which I haven't
had a chance to get yet but will soon.

You probably couldn't find him because I mis-spelled his name...sorry.

Pete.
```

<a id="t-1862916edf"></a>
## Thread: VA COBOL and IMS emulator — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### VA COBOL and IMS emulator

- **From:** COBOLMAN <member@mainframeforum.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-08T14:02:52-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3cd9765c$1_6@news.teranews.com>`

```
cross posted at ibm.software.cobol

We are looking at using VA COBOL for mainframe development. We have
heavy use of IMS and DB2 both online and batch. Many years ago we were
using MicroFocus COBOL workbench with the IMSVS86 IMS emulator and the
Stingray XDB DB2 emulator. We would like to use thsi same concept with
VA COBOL but are not aware of compatable IMS emulators like IMSVS86. We
know that we probably could use UDB for the DB2 part.

We are aware that VACOBOL can interface with a mainframe and IMS and
DB2. The idea here is to offload development and unit testing as much as
possible in order to reduce mainframe costs.

Does anyone have any suggestions?
```

#### ↳ Re: VA COBOL and IMS emulator

- **From:** "Brian" <sorry@no.email>
- **Date:** 2002-05-08T21:17:31+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wJfC8.1341$xb4.252753@news6-win.server.ntlworld.com>`
- **References:** `<3cd9765c$1_6@news.teranews.com>`

```
I also once used Micro Focus Workbench with XDB and IMSVS86 at a UK bank, in
a department with 250 programmers.

Later on we upgraded to their Mainframe Express product which is far
superior. As well as IMS and DB2, it emulates CICS, Assembler (now includes
the new z/OS instructions), JCL, mainframe catalog, REXX, CLIST, and it can
be used with MQ Series. It also lets you view or download mainframe source
code and data from Windows, with access to various SCM systems (in our case,
Endevor).

We evaluated VisualAge COBOL a couple of years ago but it relied too heavily
on the mainframe for it to be of benefit to us. I don't know of any other
IMS emulation I'm afraid.

Brian.
UK
```

```
Hi,

My question is with respect to indexed files. We have a client who
needs integration help. They have a system in COBOL which is compiled
and running on HP-UX. It uses indexed files that are stored on this
HP-UX machine. I want to be able to write a COBOL program under the
windows environment that can access these indexed files that reside on
the HP-UX machine over the LAN.

Can someone help me with this please? Thanks for your time in advance.

Thanks,
Chacko
```

```
Chacko wrote:
> 
> My question is with respect to indexed files. We have a client who
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> the HP-UX machine over the LAN.

You should be able to get Samba to run on HP-UX and then Windows will
see that drive as just another network disk.  However I would be very
careful to ensure that the record and file locking sematics are
compatible, especially for locking the index files on updating. 
Incompatible locking could result in corrupted files.
```

```
Chacko.
```

```
> Hi,
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

Before you go to the trouble, determine whether the Windows version of the
compiler can read index files generated by the same compiler running under a
different OS. I'll wager the two file systems are incompatible. Maybe not,
but at least get some assurance in advance.
```

<a id="t-218fa93a4c"></a>
## Thread: IBM VisualAge Cobol in Windows 2000. — 1 this month / 7 total

_Thread started [2002-03](2002-03.md#t-218fa93a4c)._

#### ↳ Re: IBM VisualAge Cobol in Windows 2000.

- **From:** ronnie <member@mainframeforum.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-08T16:32:50-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3cd99982$1_5@news.teranews.com>`
- **References:** `<dc662413.0203271043.5a0f6d76@posting.google.com>`

```
We are presently using VA Cobol version 3.0.4.

We have successfully compiled and executed 0S/390 batch programs.

Using the compiler cob2 compiler which comes with VA cobol. Note there
are some setup parm which must be enter. It depends on what you are
trying to do
```

- **References:** `<abdpos$1e5$1@news.urz.uni-heidelberg.de> <mtuldusm7vgangq6io6jgcgq43qo40kv33@4ax.com>`

```
"Paul Knudsen" <pknudsen@charter.net> wrote in message
news:mtuldusm7vgangq6io6jgcgq43qo40kv33@4ax.com...
> Hmm maybe you mean Bob the Dinosaur, who hasn't been in the strip for
> a while.  I don't think he was a programmer though.  His function was
> to give Catbert a fur-nougie when he (Catbert) got too far out of
> line.

He wasn't a programmer, but:

Panel 1:
PHB: "We need COBOL programmers for our mainframe millennium problem."

Panel 2:
PHB: "If you see anyone who looks like a COBOL programmer, let me know."
Dilbert: "Turn around"

Panel 3:
PHB (to Bob): "Are you a COBOL programmer?"
Bob: "No, but I'm often told I look like one."
PHB: "You're hired."

The next strip has the PHB assigning the intern ASOK to learn from Bob's
vast experience. The strip ends up:

Asok: "So, you recommend waiting for a meteor to kill us all."
Bob: "The glaciers are way too slow."

And the third strip in the series:

Bob: "Most problems go away if you wait long enough, Asok."

Bob: "It might look like I'm standing motionless, but I'm actively waiting
for our problems to go away."

PHB: "There's been a reorganization."
Bob (to Asok): "I don't know why this works, but it does."
```

## Thread: search problem — 6 msgs

### search problem

- **From:** sb_sweets@hotmail.com (sweets)
- **Date:** 2002-05-09T09:53:49-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e65a2eca.0205090853.787d9fff@posting.google.com>`

```
I am pretty new with cobol and am having problems with searching in a
lookup table.  Below is what I have written.  Any help would be
greatly appreciated.

The table I have prepared in notepad is as follows:

1  January
2  February
3  March ....etc

================================================================================

       Identification Division.
       Program-id. Datetabl.
       Environment Division.
       Input-Output Section.
       File-Control.

           Select  table-file
                   assign to disk 'month.txt'.


           Select  print-file
                   assign to  disk 'date.txt'.

      /
      *------------------------------------------------------------
      *   DATA DIVISION - FILE SECTION
      *------------------------------------------------------------
       Data Division.
       File Section.

       FD  table-file.
       01  table-rec.
           05  month-num   pic x(02).
           05  month-wrd   pic x(09).

       FD  print-file.
       01  pr-line         pic x(50).
      /
      *------------------------------------------------------------
      *    DATA DIVISION - WORKING-STORAGE SECTION
      *------------------------------------------------------------
       Working-Storage Section.

       01  table-of-months.
           05  table-entries occurs 12 times indexed by x1.
               10  ws-month-num    pic x(02).
               10  ws-month-wrd    pic x(09).

      *------------------------------------------------------------
      *    FLAGS AND SWITCHES
      *------------------------------------------------------------

       01  eof-flag-in         pic 9.
           88  eof-in          value 1.
           88  not-eof-in      value 0.

      *------------------------------------------------------------
      *    VARIABLES
      *------------------------------------------------------------

       01  v1-table.
           05 v-month     pic 9.

      *------------------------------------------------------------
      *    DETAIL-LINES
      *------------------------------------------------------------

       01 dl-date.
           05                      pic x(10) value spaces.
           05      dl-month        pic x(09).
           05                      pic x(10) value spaces.
           05      dl-day          pic x(02).

      *------------------------------------------------------------
      *    PROCEDURE DIVISION
      *------------------------------------------------------------
       Procedure Division.

       0000-main.

       open input table-file
            output print-file
       set not-eof-in to true
       move zero to v-month

       perform until eof-in
           read table-file
               at end
                   set eof-in to true
               not at end
                   perform 1000-table-entry
           end-read
       end-perform

       perform 3000-print
              varying x1 from 1 by 1 until x1 > 12
       close table-file
             print-file
       stop run
       .

       1000-table-entry.

       set x1 to 1

       display 'enter month '
       accept v-month

       search table-entries
           at end display 'end of table reading'
           stop run
           when ws-month-num (x1) = v-month
           display 'found'
           move dl-month to ws-month-wrd (x1)
           when ws-month-num (x1) not = v-month
           display 'not found'
       end-search
       .

       3000-print.

       move ws-month-wrd (x1) to dl-month
       move ws-month-num (x1) to dl-day
       write pr-line from dl-date after advancing 2 lines
       .
```

#### ↳ Re: search problem

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-09T12:34:30-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<abec59$re7$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<e65a2eca.0205090853.787d9fff@posting.google.com>`

```
Can you help us understand what  you THINK the program is doing? My guess
(and I could be wrong) is that you plan on reading the entire file to fill
the table - and then you plan on allowing the user to enter (Accept ) a
random month "name" and see if it is in the table.

Alternatively, you could be trying to get the names of the month from the
ACCEPT statement and fill the table from that.

Either way - you have "confused" your READ and ACCEPT logic.

One other thing to "look at" is the difference between SEARCH ALL and
"sequential" SEARCH as to how they handle the starting place of the index.

 ***

This looks (to me) to be homework - and most of us (in this NG) don't "do
homework" for you - but I hope these "hints" will get you on the right
track.
```

#### ↳ Re: search problem

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-09T18:02:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CDAB976.D622E10E@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<e65a2eca.0205090853.787d9fff@posting.google.com>`

```
For somebody new to COBOL - full marks for an elegantly written program.  What was
your objective, get a handle on file handling, or are you really wanting to search a
Date table. Just so happens, it's the easiest table to search, as follows (plus
variations) :-

01 MonthsTable.
     05 MonthsA    pic x(48) value
                "Jan,Feb'Mar,Apr,May,Jun,Jul,Aug,Sep,Oct,Nov,Dec,".
    *> Note : My preference, I've included commas for readability

     05 MonthsB redefines MonthsA.
         10 MonthRecord occurs 12 .
    *> Subject to compiler above line could also be :- 10 occurs 12.
                15 MonthName                    pic x(03).
                15                                        pic x. *> this is the
comma

Either you read a record in from file or you prompt user to enter a date :-

01 Date8.
     05 ws-Year              pic 9(04). *> ccyy
     05 ws-Month            pic 9(02).
     05 ws-Day               pic 9(02).

Obviously do some format checks on the date, e.g. Year is between 19... and 20....,
Month is 1 thru 12, Days are 1 thru 31, (days - allowing for shorter months and leap
years). Have you got the Intrinsic Functions ?- check out the syntax for those
handling dates. (If you have Thane Hubbell's 'Teach Yourself COBOL in 24 Hours", you
will get some good tips from there).

You've got your date correct :-

    move MonthName (ws-Month) to ..........

If however your particular intent is to get a handle on SEARCHING, then go to your
Language Reference Manual or text book and check out SEARCHING TABLES, with indices,
as you've indicated. The SEARCH ALL construct is very powerful..

Just remembered - go to cobolreport.com and check out Bill Klein's COBOL FAQ - his
Appendix A has some date examples. U of Limerick is a good source for code
(http://www.csis.ul.ie/COBOL/examples/ )

Jimmy, Calgary AB

sweets wrote:

> I am pretty new with cobol and am having problems with searching in a
> lookup table.  Below is what I have written.  Any help would be
…[123 more quoted lines elided]…
>        .
```

#### ↳ Re: search problem

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-05-10T00:21:45+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3cdb043c_7@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<e65a2eca.0205090853.787d9fff@posting.google.com>`

```
If this is code from a newbie I am very impressed...

So much so that I'll even help you out  <G>...

As Bill has pointed out we don't do Homework here, but as you have posted
the source you have, and it is very good code, well, just this once...

See below...

sweets <sb_sweets@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:e65a2eca.0205090853.787d9fff@posting.google.com...
> I am pretty new with cobol and am having problems with searching in a
> lookup table.  Below is what I have written.  Any help would be
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
============================================================================
====
>
>        Identification Division.
…[96 more quoted lines elided]…
>
****************** Here's your problem... V-month is a single digit. Make it
'99' or, better still, 'xx'.
Remember to enter '01' etc. for months up to October.

Think about ways to overcome this necessity by validating the code the user
enters... What if they enter '�$' ?

>        search table-entries
>            at end display 'end of table reading'
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Currently, if you enter '1' (or any other month number) the program will
immediately stop with no output (other than 'end of table reading'), right?
You should be able to see why this is now...

Congratulations on your style of code...I like truth values for flags of '1'
and '0' (with 88 levels, of course...), lower case (there's no need to SHOUT
<G>), full stops on a separate line (and only where required)...after
37years of writing COBOL what you have above is very close to what I code
myself. (I spread constructs like 'perform...varying' over lines, and I do
use SECTIONs, but these are personal preferences and not necessities.)

HTH and Good luck with your study.

Pete.



 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

#### ↳ Re: search problem

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-05-10T07:49:43+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CDB6D87.D5943165@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<e65a2eca.0205090853.787d9fff@posting.google.com>`

```
sweets wrote:
> 
> The table I have prepared in notepad is as follows:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 3  March ....etc
 
>            Select  table-file
>                    assign to disk 'month.txt'.

The file prepared in notepad will be a text file with each record
variable in length and a CR/LF terminator.  CR=Carriage Return, LF=Line
Feed.  The deafault ORGANISATION of a file is SEQUENTIAL where the
records are fixed length.  You need to specify that this file is
ORGANISATION LINE SEQUENTIAL so that it reads each record up to its
CR/LF terminator.
 
>        FD  table-file.
>        01  table-rec.
>            05  month-num   pic x(02).
>            05  month-wrd   pic x(09).

This doesn't define exactly how the records are.  You have the file
(above) as '1  January' (with 2 spaces).  You need to define this as:

             05  Month-Num   PIC XX.
             05  FILLER      PIC X.
             05  Month-Wrd   PIC X(9).


>        open input table-file
>             output print-file

You should add a FILE STATUS in each SELECT and check this after each
OPEN and other operation. 

>        perform until eof-in
>            read table-file
>                at end
>                    set eof-in to true

>                not at end
>                    perform 1000-table-entry
>            end-read
>        end-perform

I expected '1000-table-entry' to store the record just read into the WS
area.  It doesn't.  The PERFORM will loop reading one line from the file
each time.  You will be performing 1000-table entry 12 times.

What you probably should be doing is:


	 SET X1      TO 1
>        perform until eof-in
>            read table-file
>                at end
>                    set eof-in to true
>                not at end
                     MOVE Table-Num  TO WS-Table-Num(X1)
                     MOVE Table-Wrd  TO WS-Table-Wed(X1)
                     SET X1 UP BY 1
>            end-read
>        end-perform

At this point the 12 (I assume) entries from the file are now in the
table.

>        perform 1000-table-entry
 
>        perform 3000-print
>               varying x1 from 1 by 1 until x1 > 12
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>        .

This bit (above) is fine, and so is 3000-print.
 
>        1000-table-entry.
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>                   stop run

Why the STOP RUN ?  It is in fact at this point that you should be
saying 'not found'.  

>            when ws-month-num (x1) = v-month
>                 display 'found'
>                 move dl-month to ws-month-wrd (x1)
>            when ws-month-num (x1) not = v-month
>                 display 'not found'

Saying 'not found' here will result in several 'not found' messages.

>        end-search
>        .

Your current logic is that after each line read you will search an empty
WS table prucing 12 'not found' and then do a stop run.  The 2nd line
(february) is never read.
```

#### ↳ Re: search problem

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2002-05-10T11:31:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020510073107.22359.00001462@mb-fv.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<e65a2eca.0205090853.787d9fff@posting.google.com>`

```
In article <e65a2eca.0205090853.787d9fff@posting.google.com>,
sb_sweets@hotmail.com (sweets) writes:

>I am pretty new with cobol and am having problems with searching in a
>lookup table.  Below is what I have written.  Any help would be
>greatly appreciated.
>

check your field sizes and types on the variables that you are comparing.
when are you loading your table-of-months ?
check your sequence of events as to what should happen when.
```

<a id="t-1f9b14ad91"></a>
## Thread: COBOL copybook to DFSORT symbols? — 2 msgs

### Re: COBOL copybook to DFSORT symbols?

```
comp.lang.cobol (too).
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL copybook to DFSORT symbols?

- **From:** Frank Yaeger <fyaeger@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-10T16:03:26-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CDC51BD.6D7F6994@attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<200205092018.g49KHxC48236@northrelay01.pok.ibm.com> <abena7$m5m$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William,
   Thanks for posting that here.  (I think I posted information about the tool
back when it first became available, but I don't remember for sure.)

For an overview of the DFSORT Symbols feature, see:

http://www.storage.ibm.com/software/sort/mvs/professor_sort/srtmasym.html

For complete information on the DFSORT Symbols feature, see Chapter 7 of
"DFSORT Application Programming Guide":

http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com:80/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/ICECA109/7.0?DT=20020318091649
```

## Thread: Easytrieve question but no group... — 5 msgs

### Easytrieve question but no group...

- **From:** hanakj@ameritech.net (jim)
- **Date:** 2002-05-09T13:49:31-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9f857340.0205091249.1023a016@posting.google.com>`

```
Anyway, I need to read a sequential file and report how many records
of each type there is in the file.  Each record has a type; type 10,
type 20, etc.  The file is already sorted in type order.  I know this
is a COBOL group, but I'm being forced to do Easytrieve+, and I
thought this would be the best place to ask.  Thanks!
```

```

> type 20, etc.  The file is already sorted in type order.  I know this
> is a COBOL group, but I'm being forced to do Easytrieve+, and I
> thought this would be the best place to ask.  Thanks!

I do this the same way as I did CoBOL 30 years ago.  I find a similar
program and crib from it.

Are your file formats in macros (copy libraries)?  What do you know how to
do with EasyTrieve?  It is a pretty intuitive language for a task like
yours.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Easytrieve question but no group...

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-05-10T18:10:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<abh269$efv$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<9f857340.0205091249.1023a016@posting.google.com> <abenq2$4u2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <9f857340.0205100901.7f53620b@posting.google.com>`

```

On 10-May-2002, hanakj@ameritech.net (jim) wrote:

> I was pretty good in EZT about 23 years ago.  I have forgotten most of
> it, however.  It seem to me that what I need to do could probably be
> accomlished in about 3 lines of actual code.  Can you point me to any
> resources?

It is real easy.  Your hardest task will be to create your JCL to run this.
You only need to do this once and then crib for all other EasyTrieve
programs.  (Of course, if this is already available, then you have
EasyTrieve programs available to crib as well, and don't need to be asking
us).

Here's a program:   (Sorted reports are almost as easy)
//Z46ACCT    JOB (12651,'EZT'),'U37   JONES,
//             CLASS=N,MSGCLASS=S
/*ROUTE PRINT R0010
//EASY#1     EXEC PGM=EZTPA00
//STEPLIB    DD DSN=EASY.LOADLIB,DISP=SHR
//EZTVFM     DD UNIT=SYSDA,SPACE=(4096,(100,100))
//ACCTFD     DD DSN=BRS.ACCTDT,DISP=SHR
//SYSPRINT   DD SYSOUT=*
//OUT1       DD DSN=UMS.D92747.ACCTF4,DISP=(NEW,CATLG),UNIT=DEVEDISK,
//             SPACE=(CYL,(5,1),RLSE)
//SYSSNAP    DD SYSOUT=*
//SYSOUT     DD SYSOUT=*
//SYSIN      DD *
FILE OUT1 FB (270 27000)
  OUT-REC       1     270   A
  OUT-AMT    55        11   A
  OUT-R2     66       142   A
FILE ACCTFD
ACCTFD-REC      1     396   A
  ACCT-FILL     1     129   A
  ACCT-SID    130       9   A
  ACCT-REST   139     258   A
  ACCT-AMT  ACCT-REST +54   8 P 2
  ACCT-R2   ACCT-REST +62 142 A
WK-AMT   W   11  N  2
JOB INPUT ACCTFD
IF ACCT-SID = '524794886'
   OUT-REC  = ACCT-REST
   WK-AMT   = ACCT-AMT
   OUT-AMT  = WK-AMT
   OUT-R2   = ACCT-R2
   PUT OUT1
END-IF
```

## Thread: Call CSP program from COBOL program — 2 msgs

### Call CSP program from COBOL program

- **From:** starrara@gruppocoin.it (simone)
- **Date:** 2002-05-10T01:31:44-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<feecca73.0205100031.4e3c3dd4@posting.google.com>`

```
I would like call CSP program from COBOL program.
I use COBOL II.
Please help me.
Thank you.
```

#### ↳ Re: Call CSP program from COBOL program

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-10T12:53:43-03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CDBED07.9C06476B@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<feecca73.0205100031.4e3c3dd4@posting.google.com>`

```
My first recommendation is don't.  CSP is a disaster and due to die.  If
you must use CSP try to get to Release 4.1 which generates COBOL code
(by default COBOL II) and read the manuals.  CSP was designed so that it
could call COBOL but not so that it could be called by COBOL.

Be aware that your organization should be moving to Enterprise COBOL
since COBOL II and its immediate successor are or will shortly be out of
service.  You also so be moving to LE.  The CSP has been superseded by
Visual Age Generator so I don't know how long CSP will be in service.  I
would start a project to kill CSP.

simone wrote:
> 
> I would like call CSP program from COBOL program.
> I use COBOL II.
> Please help me.
> Thank you.
```

<a id="t-bf879926a6"></a>
## Thread: Working-Storage and Objects — 11 msgs

### Working-Storage and Objects

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-11T12:11:05-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<abjjba$p1m$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
(Follow-up on another thread),

It has been so long since the "final resolution" on this issue, that I had
to look this up.  I think there is still some "confusion" related to
Working-Storage and Local-Storage for object (in the final draft).

1) You *may* define Working-Storage in an "instance definition" (this is the
data division "at the top" of the object instance definition - AFTER the
factory definition and before any methods).  Such data is "by definition"
GLOBAL and available to ALL methods of that class.  Such data is
"individual" for each instance (each gets its own copy). Such data
"persists" for the life of the object instance.

2) Local-Storage may exist inside a method definition.  Such data does NOT
persist for the life of the object instance.  However, if you "recurse" into
the same method, it is "pushed" and "popped" with the recursion).  (The same
is true of Local-Storage in PROGRAM definitions that use recursion - even if
you never "touch" OO.)

    ***

The only thing "missing" is a type of data that is local to a specific
method and that persists for the life of the object instance.
```

#### ↳ Re: Working-Storage and Objects

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-11T18:09:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CDD5E25.BD9C37CB@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<abjjba$p1m$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:

> (Follow-up on another thread),
>
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Regarding last sentence - hence my *plug* for the syntax of the new CONSTANT to
be changed :-

Local-storage section.
01 MessageTable CONSTANT.            *> No can do in current draft
 05 MessageB.
    10 pic x(xx) value "Don't be bloody stupid".
     10 pic x(xx) value "You gotta be kidding !".
     10 pic x(xx etc....
 05 MessageA redefines MessageB.
    10 ThisMessage occurs ??? pic x(xx).

Linkage section.
01 n                                                    pic 9(02).
Procedure Divsion using n.
 move ThisIsMessage(n) to ErrorMessageText
etc.....

Above can be referenced from a table in global W/S - my contention, much easier
if it is concentrated in the method you are currently coding.

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: Working-Storage and Objects

- **From:** "Steven Lalewicz" <strl@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2002-05-14T12:32:42+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<abqug3$3fq$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com>`
- **References:** `<abjjba$p1m$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote in message ...
>(Follow-up on another thread),
>

Hi Bill

Sorry I've not read this thread so I hope I'm not going over old ground.

>It has been so long since the "final resolution" on this issue, that I had
>to look this up.  I think there is still some "confusion" related to
>Working-Storage and Local-Storage for object (in the final draft).
>
>1) You *may* define Working-Storage in an "instance definition" (this is
the
>data division "at the top" of the object instance definition - AFTER the
>factory definition and before any methods).  Such data is "by definition"
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I did not think that one could use items defined in a working-storage
section
of a factory definition in methods in the object definition. Where does it
say this?

Example:
 class-id. example.
 factory.
 working-storage section.
 1 num1 pic 9.
 end factory.
 object.
 method-id. meth1.
 procedure division.
* num1 out of scope!
 move 0 to num1.
 exit method.
 end method meth1.
 end object.
 end class example.

I've always thought if I wanted to access num1 from the object definition
part
I need to invoke a factory method in the factory definition.

>2) Local-Storage may exist inside a method definition.  Such data does NOT
>persist for the life of the object instance.  However, if you "recurse"
into
>the same method, it is "pushed" and "popped" with the recursion).  (The
same
>is true of Local-Storage in PROGRAM definitions that use recursion - even
if
>you never "touch" OO.)
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I'm not sure this fits in with the model of OO. Every time a method is
invoked, it may be subject to different objects of that class. If you want
data to be avaliable every time in your method for a particular instance,
then it seems to me that this is equivelant to just defining more fields in
the working-storage of the object definition.

Example:
 class-id. example.
 object.
 working-storage section.
 1 num1 pic 9.
 1 meth-count-per-instance pic 9999.

 method-id. meth1.
 procedure division.
     add 1 to meth-count-per-instance
     exit method.
 end method meth1.
 end object.
 end class example.

Regards,
Steven Lalewicz.

>--
>Bill Klein
> wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Working-Storage and Objects

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Working-Storage and Objects

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:

> Steven,
>   You are missing a point from my original note where I said
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> Property syntax

I go along with your definition until I get to the PS. I now recall I mentioned
this once before. "Property" does not appear in the Net Express on-line help,
other than with reference to the Form Design Module/HTML/Activex etc. It is only
when I get into the Language Reference Manual (on-line book), that I find
reference to PROPERTY associated with Get/Set. What N/E doesn't clarify is your
suggestion it can be done from FACTORY - as I read it there's no distinction,
(in their current version), between Factory or Object.

Given that this Property syntax is hidden away in the On-line book only -  it's
doubtful that many, if any, N/E developers are using it, so at the moment in the
'real world' its' a bit conjectural..

On the basis that I keep Factory minimal and use Object Section to create
instances, that's where I look for my global W/S in the Object(Instance area).

Want a challenge - just like you came up with a neat case for Multiple
Inheritance, can you think of where one might want to access Factory global W/S
as opposed to the Instance W/S - given  of course, that you have an Instance ?
Other than that the feature is provided, I can't figure it gives you any edge.

*** ALERT ***

Anybody from Micro Focus reading this ? To check the on-line books I accessed
via Start Programs - so I'm pulling the info off my hard disk. Following up on
something BIll wrote earlier, I also have this new bookmark :-

http://supportline.microfocus.com/documentation/books/nx31sp1/nx31indx.htm

which takes me directly to the books. Anyway, there appears to be a SCREW UP ! I
get a 'Table of Contents' listing the books, but when I select, say Language
Reference Book(s),  I get a screen with :-

    "(Arrow pointing NW) - Start by clicking Introduction in the Contents",

and the left part of the screen just contains one word 'UNDEFINED" - can't even
use the 'Back' to return to the 'Table of Contents'.

Jimmy
```

```
Yes there is a little mess here. It was intended the property syntax be in
NX3.1
and the docs were updated in preparation, but the work was never completed
in time.

I don't think Bill was talking about any specific compiler just the features
avaliable in the standard.

I think this useful to do. You may have a class which wants to keep track of
things over the life time of all the instance object created. For example
you
may wish to count the number of instance objects created.

My grumble about the standard is one can't access the working-storage
items in the factory definition in object methods but have to go via
the factory methods. One can do the equivelant in other OO langauges.
```

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3CE29F0B.B9BC70D3@shaw.ca...
<snip>
> > My grumble about the standard is one can't access the working-storage
> > items in the factory definition in object methods but have to go via
> > the factory methods. One can do the equivelant in other OO langauges.
>
> If that is true with regards to OO, I'm surprised somebody didn't plug for
this
> feature to be in OO COBOL. In a similar context Steve Biggs submitted a
paper on
> 'Finalizer' pointing out that like other OO languages, our 'Finalizer'
should be
> parallel.
>

As already indicatred, you CAN access FACTORY Data from Instance Object's
methods "directly" (not via explicit methods) *IF* you define them as
properties.  See the topic "Object property" in the FCD (or FDIS) for the
syntax to do so.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Working-Storage and Objects

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Steven Lalewicz" <steven.lalewicz@microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-15T21:26:09+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<abui64$guj$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com>`
- **References:** `<abjjba$p1m$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <abqug3$3fq$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com> <abrajr$oiu$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3CE16B00.7286E61@shaw.ca> <abtf2s$d0m$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com> <3CE29F0B.B9BC70D3@shaw.ca> <abucib$d2o$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

William M. Klein wrote in message ...
>"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
>news:3CE29F0B.B9BC70D3@shaw.ca...
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>> If that is true with regards to OO, I'm surprised somebody didn't plug
for
>this
>> feature to be in OO COBOL. In a similar context Steve Biggs submitted a
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Hi Bill,

You mean like this:

       class-id. class1.

       repository.
           class class1
           property num2
           .

       factory.
       data division.
       working-storage section.
       1 num1 binary-short.
       1 num2 binary-short property.
       end factory.

       object.
       data division.
       working-storage section.
       method-id. "method1".
       procedure division.
      * would like to be able to do this
      *    add 1 to num1
           add 1 to num2 of class1
           exit method
       end method "method1".
        end object.
       end class class1.


Well I suppose it is better than having to manually create
factory methods to do the same thing. But I suppose I'm
used to being able to do it more directly than that in other
languages (JAVA and C++).

The down side of using properties and also creating factory
methods is then they are now public to any program using
the class. So if you are trying to keep track of something
internal to the class, someone else could invoke your
factory methods and alter your data. The standard does not
have the concept of private methods which other OO programming
languages have.

Regards,
Steven Lalewicz.
>--
>Bill Klein
> wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com
>
>
```

<a id="t-2cea0cf5b5"></a>
## Thread: Standard, Revision, Process, et al — 13 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### Standard, Revision, Process, et al

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-12T20:25:15-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<abn4lv$58p$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
(Posted to comp.lang.cobol and distributed to J4 and WG4)

I thought the following note that I sent to another COBOL "list" might be of
interest in this NG.

    **

Replying to several recent posts in this group on a topic that I did *not*
bring up, I thought I would make some comments on this area. Many (if not
most) of this group know my views of the current standardization process,
but I think that there are "fair" and "unfair" (or accurate and less than
accurate) things one can say about it. I hope that others may correct any
"factual" errors in the following.

1) Both the selection of and the prioritization of "new" features and
enhancements in this (2002) revision were done at the WG4 (international)
not J4 (US) level. It has always been MY impression that WG4 has a FAR
greater representation by "real end users" than J4 does. In fact, looking at
some of the countries that have been involved (not all still involved)

    Netherlands - no vendors
    Austria - no vendors
    France - no vendors
    Canada - no vendors
    New Zealand - no vendors
    England - only vendor also in J4, others not vendors
    Germany - 1 or 2 vendors, but usually MORE non-vendors
    US - varies - but traditionally about "half and half" (at WG4 meetings)
    Japan - all (???) vendors
    X/Open (liaison) - All vendors

Now as the 90's progressed and travel budgets were cut and various "war"
issues made travel difficult, there have been SOME WG4 meetings with heavier
vendor participation, but I don't think it has ever been as much as half.

2) In J4 (where the actual "drafting" of Standard's text occurred), I think
there have always been at least 1/3 non-vendors on the voting lists. I also
think that the majority (but definitely NOT ALL) of the vendor
representatives have "regular" (at least once a week) direct contact with
end users. I do think that it is COMMON (but again not universal) that most
of the vendor representatives have NOT written a "production" program *in*
COBOL within the last decade, but most of them DO see "real world" COBOL
code as part of their jobs.

3) If you were to chart the actual papers that "added text" to the draft
Standard, I think you would find that at least 75% (possibly more) of the
text was added by papers written by vendor representatives (and if you took
out Report-Writer/VALIDATE and Standard-Arithmetic "stuff" - both primarily
written by non-J4 members, it would be even HIGHER). I think that this is
due to
 - expertise
 - exactness in style
 - paid part of their jobs
 - knowledge of the "historical" document they were adding to
 - personalities involved

4) I think that the "best" year/date for discussing the last major
"addition" to COBOL was 1989 (at least in the US). The Intrinsic Function
Amendment REALLY did add "functionality" - while the "Correction Amendment"
(almost by definition) did not. If I were to GUESS, I think that both
many/most in J4 and WG4 *today* would say that doing the "Correction
Amendment" was a mistake. (I have mixed feelings about it). But it is
interesting to think about what MIGHT have been done as an amendment
instead:
 - FIMS (Forms Interface Management System)
which is "basically" a dead-end, unless its inclusion in COBOL might have
resulted in a GUI as well as character based definition)
 - VALIDATE (very popular in Europe, but still NOT implemented by any
compiler vendor)
 - MOCS (possibly the most important, but the one that has actually been the
MOST volatile/subject to change during the current revision, so would an
amendment have gotten it right?)

5) When first "seriously" working on the current revision, two possible
"time-tables" were looked at (for different "revision scopes"). Plan A would
have been a "larger" revision with the HORRIBLE ending date of 2002 <G>.
Plan B (which was "limited" - and was selected) was intended to result in an
"official full revision" in 1996 or 1997. I think (but could be mistaken)
that both J4 and WG4 view that their "biggest" failure in this revision was
in NOT "strictly" following the initial Plan B approach and getting an
official revision DONE by 1997 (or 2000 - at least). I have my personal
opinions on how/why this slipped, but I would think that this group (in
particular) would be "amused" that if there had been an official '96
Standard it MIGHT not have included ANY OO (as this was categorized as
"important" not "critical" - with the statement that it should be moved to
"critical" IF it was ready in time.)

6) I personally think that AT LEAST three years of delay in this revision
was due to a problem that many/most outside the committee don't know about.
That was getting a "valid" electronic base document. At least 3 major
"efforts" were used in doing this:
 - converting from NROFF/TROFF to WordPerfect
 - Demodularizing the old text
 - Converting from WordPerfect to FrameMaker

Much of the "early" years of the revision work was almost "wasted" in trying
to write papers against a moving (and often just "virtual") document.
Planning and debate was possible, but true "revision work" was limited.

***

I hope that this helps answer some of the "issues" raised in this group (and
elsewhere). I do NOT want to imply that I am happy with either how things
have worked out or the structure in place for the future, but I did think
that some "historical" perspective was needed.
```

<a id="t-72059209e2"></a>
## Thread: [OT] Dynamic Timestamp in Dynamic SQL? — 28 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases) · [`Date and calendar processing`](topics.md#dates)_

### [OT] Dynamic Timestamp in Dynamic SQL?

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2002-05-13T10:10:20-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<abohgc$onc$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```

All righty... this'un's ugly.

There's a process I'm putting together which needs to get all the 
transactions entered since the previous time the process was run.  Data 
are in DB2 tables and are checked for, among other criteria, timestamp.

Since the data are being pulled from the tables in ways that the tables 
weren't designed for (isn't that *always* the case?) I've set things up to 
use an IKJEFT01 and extract data by SQL to flatfiles... and then use 
FileAid and SyncSort to fold, spindle and mutilate these files rather 
quickly... and then a simple batch COBOL program to (what else?) print 
some reports.

Now... it would be easy, at the start of the run, to grab a timestamp and 
toss it off to a file so that (if I were working in COBOL) I could read 
this file and get rows WHERE DATE_TIME_STAMP BETWEEN :WS-YESTERDAYS-TS AND 
CURRENT TIMESTAMP... or to write the control statements from 
WORKING-STORAGE:

01  WS-SQL-CTL-CD05.
    05  FILLER PIC X(04) VALUE SPACES.
    05  FILLER PIC X(30) VALUE
        'WHERE DATE_TIME_STAMP BETWEEN '.
    05  FILLER PIC X VALUE QUOTE.
    05  WS-YESTERDAYS-TS PIC X(26) VALUE SPACES.
    05  FILLER PIC X VALUE QUOTE.
    05  FILLER PIC X(05) VALUE ' AND '.
    05  FILLER PIC X VALUE QUOTE.
    05  WS-CURRENT-TS PIC X(26) VALUE SPACES.
    05  FILLER PIC X VALUE QUOTE.

... to a file which is then used as the SYSIN for the IKJEFT01 step... but 
I am not working this in COBOL and would dearly love not to have to 
implement a program in Prod to do just that.

So... given the tools at hand - SQL, FileAid and SyncSort - does anyone 
know of a way to create a control statement to do this?

Gah.  Thanks much.

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: [OT] Dynamic Timestamp in Dynamic SQL?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-13T09:27:25-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aboigi$blj$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<abohgc$onc$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
I may be "off-base" here, but if your SQL is running under IKJEFT01, can't
you add a REXX or CLIST at the top (or bottom) of your input to do this?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Dynamic Timestamp in Dynamic SQL?

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2002-05-13T10:49:43-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<abojq7$403$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<abohgc$onc$1@panix1.panix.com> <aboigi$blj$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

