```
William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:
>I may be "off-base" here, but if your SQL is running under IKJEFT01, can't
>you add a REXX or CLIST at the top (or bottom) of your input to do this?

Could be... but I'm not about to propose a solution that's beyond my 
skills and writing such a thing would be.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Dynamic Timestamp in Dynamic SQL?

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-13T16:00:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yqRD8.101$8M5.15@nwrddc01.gnilink.net>`
- **References:** `<abohgc$onc$1@panix1.panix.com> <aboigi$blj$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <abojq7$403$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```

1. Syncsort or DFSORT:

"BETWEEN"  = INCLUDE COND=(recordtime, GE, Starttime,
AND,recordTime,LE,endtime)

2. Or is the problem getting the "now" time?

If so, you just use the SQL part of your system to  SELECT CURRENT-TIMESTAMP
and use that for comparisons

3. Or if you want to get "since the last time I did this" you select
CURRENT-TIMESTAMP at the end of each run, save it, then modify your SELECT
to select where source_table.timestamp_column  >
last_timestamp_as_saved_on_last_run.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Dynamic Timestamp in Dynamic SQL?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2002-05-13T12:55:16-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<abor5k$2la$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<abohgc$onc$1@panix1.panix.com> <aboigi$blj$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <abojq7$403$1@panix1.panix.com> <yqRD8.101$8M5.15@nwrddc01.gnilink.net>`

```
In article <yqRD8.101$8M5.15@nwrddc01.gnilink.net>,
Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:
>
>1. Syncsort or DFSORT:
>
>"BETWEEN"  = INCLUDE COND=(recordtime, GE, Starttime,
>AND,recordTime,LE,endtime)

The difficulty is in getting Starttime = time of last run.

>
>2. Or is the problem getting the "now" time?

No, that one I have... but thanks.

[snip]

>3. Or if you want to get "since the last time I did this" you select
>CURRENT-TIMESTAMP at the end of each run, save it, then modify your SELECT
>to select where source_table.timestamp_column  >
>last_timestamp_as_saved_on_last_run.

This is what I want to do, the 'since the loast time I did this'... are 
you suggesting that I do something like this?

SELECT SOMESTUFF
      ,MORESTUFF
FROM TABLE01
WHERE TIMESTAMP BETWEEN (SELECT DISTINCT LAST_RUN_TS 
                         FROM TABLE01 
                         WHERE USER_ID_COL = 'DUMMYROW')
                    AND CURRENT TIMESTAMP
;
...
(other steps)
...
UPDATE TABLE01
SET LAST_RUN_TS = CURRENT TIMESTAMP
WHERE USER_ID_COL = 'DUMMYROW'
;

This might be made to work... if, of course, I can set up the various 
entries in other tables to synchronise these things (key dependencies and 
such)...

... and if I can get auth to update a Prod table during my testing... gah.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Dynamic Timestamp in Dynamic SQL?

- **From:** tmjones@aol.com (Thomas Jones)
- **Date:** 2002-05-13T11:01:24-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b8189eaf.0205131001.5edf8e64@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<abohgc$onc$1@panix1.panix.com> <aboigi$blj$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <abojq7$403$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
Assuming you were going to get the last timestamp from a column
function you could use IKJEFT01 to "extract" something like this SQL:

SELECT 'WHERE DATE_TIME_STAMP BETWEEN ''' CONCAT MAX(DATE_TIME_STAMP)
''' AND CURRENT TIMESTAMP' FROM THE.TABLE



docdwarf@panix.com wrote in message news:<abojq7$403$1@panix1.panix.com>...
> In article <aboigi$blj$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>,
> William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Dynamic Timestamp in Dynamic SQL?

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2002-05-13T12:28:45-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0205131128.1e3b32bc@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<abohgc$onc$1@panix1.panix.com> <aboigi$blj$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <abojq7$403$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote in message news:<abojq7$403$1@panix1.panix.com>...
> In article <aboigi$blj$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>,
> William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

Why not just declare and use one or more cursors in your simple COBOL
program.  The SELECT statements can have ORDER BY clauses to sort as
required.  You would only need multiple cursors where you were
producing several reports in different sequences.  You could
alternatively even use COBOL's internal sorts, which would probably
just be invocations of SYNCSORT depending on how your installation has
set up the default system SORT specifications.

The COBOL program could even read and write the small control file
updating it appropriately when the report processing is complete.  If
the small single record control file had an 80 character record in
which all fields were usage display, it would be easy to edit it
separately betweeen executions if so desired, which might be necessary
in the event of program malfunction or SQL system difficulties
necessitating a rerun.

Hope this is useful, Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Dynamic Timestamp in Dynamic SQL?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2002-05-13T20:50:57-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<abpn1h$c1n$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<abohgc$onc$1@panix1.panix.com> <aboigi$blj$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <abojq7$403$1@panix1.panix.com> <fcd09c56.0205131128.1e3b32bc@posting.google.com>`

```
In article <fcd09c56.0205131128.1e3b32bc@posting.google.com>,
Robert Jones <robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote in message news:<abojq7$403$1@panix1.panix.com>...
>> In article <aboigi$blj$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>,
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>program.

Because I am writing this for as much speed as possible and IKJEFT01 
executing SQL is faster than COBOL and cursors.

>The SELECT statements can have ORDER BY clauses to sort as
>required.

SyncSort is, in this case, not only used for ordering but for de-duping.

Here's an exercise for you: you have two tables of similar format across 
which rows may be duplicated.  The selection criteria you need to use are 
across non-key columns, as is the de-duping column. The tables are not 
large... one is about 300K rows, the other 3M.  Oh, and there's a third 
table which has to be joined against either table for additional selection 
criteria.

Also... each day a year-to-date position must be established (to be used 
for the next day's Opening Balances) and each table has to be read twice, 
once for RECORD_STATUS = 'A' and once for RECORD_STATUS = 'B'.  And then 
there's an additional set of reads for items which have same-day billing 
status... they're to be totalled differently.  Oh yes, and one *more* set 
of reads to get all rows with a date of current day - (1 year - 1 day), to 
reconcile anything that went into yesterday's year-to-date balance that 
might have rolled off.

Now that you have all your cursors coded to do this... remember that the 
system has to be closed to users while you are extracting your data so you 
have maybe a half-hour window.

>You would only need multiple cursors where you were
>producing several reports in different sequences.  You could
>alternatively even use COBOL's internal sorts, which would probably
>just be invocations of SYNCSORT depending on how your installation has
>set up the default system SORT specifications.

This is a possibility, true... but see my comment below.

>
>The COBOL program could even read and write the small control file
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>necessitating a rerun.

With all due respect, Mr Jones, what you recommend here seems, to my limit 
abilities and expertise, to be a one-size-fits-all nightmare of a program 
guaranteed to be a headache for the next set of hands that touches it.  
Diferent cursors, half-a-dozen reports, control files for checkpoints... I 
have dealt with monsters like this in the past and this is why I am taking 
the approach of:

//STEP01 - IKJEFT01 to slice and dice the data by nine SELECTs into:
a) year-to-date unbilled rows from 300K table
b) complete set of department names (in case nothing unbilled in a))
c) current date billed rows (not same day billing) from 300K table
d) current date billed rows (not same day billing) from 3M table
e) current date unbilled rows from 300K table (no unbilled on 3M table)
f) current date billed rows (same day billing) from 300K table
g) current date billed rows (same day billing) from 3M table
h) current date rolloff rows from 300K table
i) current date rolloff rows from 3M table

I then use FileAid to format all records similarly and put an alpha code 
in each... A for year-to-date balances, B for billed rows (not same day), 
C for unbilled rows, D for same-day billing and E for roll-off rows.

I then use SyncSort to create total records, a maximum of five for each 
department...

... and then I use a COBOL program, simplest of the simple, to read the 
file and generate the report.  No cursor-drag, no DRBMs to install in 
Prod... just plain batch.

>
>Hope this is useful, Robert

Actually, yes... it has caused me to explain, at length, the approach I 
took and the reasons for it.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Dynamic Timestamp in Dynamic SQL?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-14T11:28:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<abohgc$onc$1@panix1.panix.com> <aboigi$blj$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <abojq7$403$1@panix1.panix.com> <fcd09c56.0205131128.1e3b32bc@posting.google.com> <abpn1h$c1n$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:abpn1h$c1n$1@panix1.panix.com...
> In article <fcd09c56.0205131128.1e3b32bc@posting.google.com>,

> executing SQL is faster than COBOL and cursors.

Oh, come on. With as much I-O as you are doing, the difference between what
program (COBOL or IKJEFT01 ) is calling for the I-O is moot.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Dynamic Timestamp in Dynamic SQL?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2002-05-14T08:58:49-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<abr1m9$4at$1@panix1.panix.com>`

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Dynamic Timestamp in Dynamic SQL?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2002-05-14T14:45:26-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0205141345.6383fb5b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<abohgc$onc$1@panix1.panix.com> <aboigi$blj$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <abojq7$403$1@panix1.panix.com> <fcd09c56.0205131128.1e3b32bc@posting.google.com> <abpn1h$c1n$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
Hello again,

I should have thought about your question more carefully, though what
I wrote was reasonable, in the light of your greater explanation it
needed review.

Thinking about your initial timestamp selection question, why not just
select everything from midnight or some other fixed convenient and
consistent time when system load is low and working back one day,
year, etc for the various selects. Putting all the selects and cursors
in a COBOL program would allow you to enforce table locking to ensure
that all the selects are working with the same data, if that matters!

Syncsort when used internally as a COBOL program's sort still allows
you to use its independent control statements, or did a few years ago,
when I last used it.

DISTINCT is a phrase that can be used with SELECT statements to remove
duplicates.

COBOL is quite good at reformatting, but the SELECT statements can be
used to produce a common set of columns from different selects by
using literals instead of column names where appropriate.  Such
SELECTs could even be combined with UNION using the ALL phrase to
remove duplicates, but this might upset your sequencing requirements
as the ORDER BY would apply to them all in common.

Organising a COBOL program in a "structured" fashion can be quite
effective in handling even quite complex tasks and isn't necessarily
more complex than the JCL for invoking several different invocations
of SYNCSORT, FILEAID and the already required COBOL report program,
though your approach can provide greater flexibility, especially where
the requirement frequently changes.

Hope this keeps you amused for a while, Robert

docdwarf@panix.com wrote in message news:<abpn1h$c1n$1@panix1.panix.com>...
> In article <fcd09c56.0205131128.1e3b32bc@posting.google.com>,
> Robert Jones <robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk> wrote:
…[94 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Dynamic Timestamp in Dynamic SQL?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com

```
In article <fcd09c56.0205141345.6383fb5b@posting.google.com>,
Robert Jones <robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk> wrote:
>Hello again,
>
>I should have thought about your question more carefully, though what
>I wrote was reasonable, in the light of your greater explanation it
>needed review.

I agree with... some of this, aye.

>
>Thinking about your initial timestamp selection question, why not just
>select everything from midnight or some other fixed convenient and
>consistent time when system load is low and working back one day,
>year, etc for the various selects.

This cannot be done because the job needs to be run at a particular point 
in the billing cycle.  Without getting *too* specific... a charge from 
Tues, 14 May, 7:pm may or may not be a same-day charge for Tuesday, a 
charge from Tues, 14 May 9:pm *cannot* be a same-day charge for Tuesday 
(if the billing cycle has been run already) but may or may not be a 
same-day charge for Wednesday.  The business day, as it were, is 
predicated not by the clock but by the billing run... which can occur 
more-or-less at any time, give or take a little.

>Putting all the selects and cursors
>in a COBOL program would allow you to enforce table locking to ensure
>that all the selects are working with the same data, if that matters!

Putting all the SELECTs and CURSORs in a COBOL program would require... a 
COBOL program.  Do you recall what I mentioned a few posts back about 
'given SQL, FileAid and SyncSort'?

>
>Syncsort when used internally as a COBOL program's sort still allows
>you to use its independent control statements, or did a few years ago,
>when I last used it.

Using SyncSort internally as a COBOL program's sort still requires... a 
COBOL program.  I seem to recall mentioning something about that at the 
very beginning of the thread... and please excuse my testiness but 
repeating myself does not make me jolly.

>
>DISTINCT is a phrase that can be used with SELECT statements to remove
>duplicates.

Thank you for this information.

>
>COBOL is quite good at reformatting, but the SELECT statements can be
>used to produce a common set of columns from different selects by
>using literals instead of column names where appropriate.

I have a small awareness of the capabilities of COBOL; than you for trying 
to expand upon it.  However... at the beginning of the thread I said 
something about 'SQL, FileAid and SyncSort'.

>Such
>SELECTs could even be combined with UNION using the ALL phrase to
>remove duplicates, but this might upset your sequencing requirements
>as the ORDER BY would apply to them all in common.

Thank you.

>
>Organising a COBOL program in a "structured" fashion can be quite
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>the requirement frequently changes.

Organising a COBOL program in *any* fashion requires... a COBOL program.

My memory is, admittedly, porous but... I recall having said something 
about the tools I wanted to use to address this task.

>
>Hope this keeps you amused for a while, Robert

Honestly... I appreciate your efforts but you seem to be rather... eager 
to ignore one of the premises of the situation.

DD

>
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote in message news:<abpn1h$c1n$1@panix1.panix.com>...
…[96 more quoted lines elided]…
>> DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Dynamic Timestamp in Dynamic SQL?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2002-05-14T15:03:48-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0205141403.1d56329f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<abohgc$onc$1@panix1.panix.com> <aboigi$blj$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <abojq7$403$1@panix1.panix.com> <fcd09c56.0205131128.1e3b32bc@posting.google.com> <abpn1h$c1n$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
Minor additional point.  The timestamp used as the cut off point for
selection does not have to be that of the time that the
program/procedure is run, but could be several hours earlier, or even
for historical investigations, any time in the past.  This means that
the actual timing of the execution of the program/procedure does not
affect the results.  I should also emphasize that the approach that I
outlined also permits each set of reports to consistently apply to the
same duration and not vary according to the time of day that it runs. 
As specified, it would seem that the procedure you are planning would
sometimes provide a bit more than a day's data and sometimes a bit
less, depending upon the variations between daily execution times.
```

```
>Minor additional point.  The timestamp used as the cut off point for
>selection does not have to be that of the time that the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>affect the results.

Major point: certain conditions can change at any time during the business 
day and thereby have an effect on the program's results; this is the 
rationale behind running the job at a particular point during the billing 
cycle and shutting all users out of the system while it is being run.

>I should also emphasize that the approach that I
>outlined also permits each set of reports to consistently apply to the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>less, depending upon the variations between daily execution times.

I should be very happy that you didn't make any more suggestions about how 
I could write a COBOL program for this.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Dynamic Timestamp in Dynamic SQL?

- **From:** "Larry Kahm" <lkahm@bellatlantic.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-14T16:07:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eDaE8.584$Ou5.558@nwrddc03.gnilink.net>`
- **References:** `<abohgc$onc$1@panix1.panix.com> <aboigi$blj$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <abojq7$403$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
DD,

OK, rather than using REXX or CLIST, could you create a COBOL program to
generate your SYSIN control cards?

First step reads the 0 generation of a GDG (which you would need to prime
with some value) and builds your input control cards, used in the second
step to extract your data.
Additional processing.
Last step, as part of your COBOL report program, you could write the "time
stamp of last run" in the +1 generation.

I would think that creating a small production file (and loading it) would
be easier/better than "mucking" with the production DB2 tables.  It also
lends itself to step/job restart as well as repeated runs.

Just a thought...

Larry
```

#### ↳ Re: [OT] Dynamic Timestamp in Dynamic SQL?

- **From:** Susan A Allen <susan.a.allen@boeing.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-17T21:54:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CE57C26.427C3CEB@boeing.com>`
- **References:** `<abohgc$onc$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```


docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> 
> ... to a file which is then used as the SYSIN for the IKJEFT01 step... but
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> know of a way to create a control statement to do this?

you may have what you need already -- but I run a daily job to drop all
records with this JCL stream (longish)
all in JCL

//* THIS JCL WILL DISPLAY, DELETE AGED ROWS, THEN RE-DISPLAY          
//*        T1  BEST_REPORTS  TABLE                                    
//*        T2  BEST_STDS     TABLE                                    
//*        T3  BEST_CHG-HIST TABLE                                    
//*        T4  BEST_IPL      TABLE                                    
//*                                                                   
//* * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * 
//*==> T1 - DISPLAY THE OBJECTS                   - BEST_REPORTS      
//* * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * 
//*                                                                   
//T1DIS1   EXEC PGM=IKJEFT1B,DYNAMNBR=99,REGION=0K                    
//STEPLIB  DD DSN=DB21.DSNLOAD,DISP=SHR  COMMENT:DB2LDLIB             
//*                                                                   
//SYSTSIN  DD *                                                       
   DSN SYSTEM(DB2P)                                                   
   -DIS  DB(MISS012P) SPACE(BERE*) LOCKS LIMIT(*)                     
   END                                                                
//*                                                                   
//SYSTSPRT DD SYSOUT=*                                                
//SYSTERM  DD SYSOUT=*                                                
//SYSPRINT DD SYSOUT=*                                                
//SYSOUT   DD SYSOUT=*                                                
//*                                                                   
//SYSOUT   DD SYSOUT=*                                                
//*                                                                   
//* * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * 
//*==> T1 - ABEND IF T1DIS1 STEP MORE THAN WARNS  - BEST_REPORTS      
//* * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * 
//         IF T1DIS1.RC > 4 THEN                                      
//T1DIS1AB EXEC PGM=ABEND806,ACCT=EXCP                                
//SYSPRINT DD SYSOUT=*                                                
//SYSUDUMP DD SYSOUT=*                                                
//         ENDIF                                                      
//*                                                                   
//* * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * 
//*==> T1 - DELETE AGED ROWS FROM THE TABLE       - BEST_REPORTS      
//* * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * 
//*                                                                   
//T1RDS    EXEC PGM=IKJEFT1A,REGION=0K                                
//STEPLIB  DD DSN=DB21.RUNLIB.LOAD,DISP=SHR  COMMENT:DB2RNLIB         
//SYSTSPRT DD SYSOUT=*                                                
//SYSPRINT DD SYSOUT=*                                                
//SYSTSIN  DD *                                                       
   DSN SYSTEM(DB2P)                                                   
   RUN PROGRAM(DSNTIAD) PLAN(DSNTIAD)                                 
   END                                                                
//SYSIN    DD *                                                       
   LOCK  TABLE DBMADB2P.BEST_REPORTS  IN EXCLUSIVE MODE;              
   DELETE FROM DBMADB2P.BEST_REPORTS                                  
     WHERE ROW_INSERT_DT < (CURRENT DATE - 3 DAYS);                   
//*                                                                   
//* * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * 
//*==> T1 - THIS STEP PURPOSELY ABENDS THE JOB WHEN A NON-FATAL,      
//*    BUT SIGNIFICANT DB2 ERROR HAS OCCURED IN THE DSNTIAD STEP      
//* * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * 
//         IF T1RDS.RC > 4 THEN                                       
//T1RDSAB  EXEC PGM=ABEND806                                          
//SYSPRINT DD SYSOUT=*                                                
//         ENDIF                                                      
//*                                                                   
//* * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * 
//*==> T1 - RE-DISPLAY THE OBJECTS                - BEST_REPORTS      
//* * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * 
//*                                                                   
//T1DIS2   EXEC PGM=IKJEFT1B,DYNAMNBR=99,REGION=0K                    
//STEPLIB  DD DSN=DB21.DSNLOAD,DISP=SHR  COMMENT:DB2LDLIB             
//*                                                                   
//SYSTSIN  DD *                                                       
   DSN SYSTEM(DB2P)                                                   
   -DIS  DB(MISS012P) SPACE(BERE*) LOCKS LIMIT(*)                     
   END                                                                
//*                                                                   
//SYSTSPRT DD SYSOUT=*                                                
//SYSTERM  DD SYSOUT=*                                                
//SYSPRINT DD SYSOUT=*                                                
//SYSOUT   DD SYSOUT=*                                                
//*                                                                   
//* * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * 
//*==> T1 - ABEND IF T1DIS2 STEP MORE THAN WARNS  - BEST_REPORTS      
//* * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * 
//         IF T1DIS2.RC > 4 THEN                                      
//T1DIS2AB EXEC PGM=ABEND806,ACCT=EXCP                                
//SYSPRINT DD SYSOUT=*                                                
//SYSUDUMP DD SYSOUT=*                                                
//         ENDIF                                                      
//*                                                                   
//* * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * *


                                    
// etc. and so forth
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Dynamic Timestamp in Dynamic SQL?

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2002-05-17T22:47:39-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ac4fcb$kni$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<abohgc$onc$1@panix1.panix.com> <3CE57C26.427C3CEB@boeing.com>`

```
In article <3CE57C26.427C3CEB@boeing.com>,
Susan A Allen  <susan.a.allen@boeing.com> wrote:
>
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>all in JCL

[snippette]

>//SYSIN    DD *                                                       
>   LOCK  TABLE DBMADB2P.BEST_REPORTS  IN EXCLUSIVE MODE;              
>   DELETE FROM DBMADB2P.BEST_REPORTS                                  
>     WHERE ROW_INSERT_DT < (CURRENT DATE - 3 DAYS);                   

... and there's the rub.  The past couple of days have been a fine 
example; on Tuesday the Billing Run completed at 7:14pm and the job I'm 
working on at 7:15pm, on Wednesday there was a bit of a problem and the 
Billing Run didn't complete until 10:21pm so the job fired off at 
10:22pm... and yesterday the Billing Run ended at 8:22pm and the next job 
ran at 8:23.

Each run of the job I am working on should include all charges for the 
preceding 'Billing Day', said 'Billing Day' defined as the period between 
two completions of the Billing Run.

DATE(TBL_TIME_STAMP) = CURRENT DATE - 1 DAY won't deal with a Billing 
Day's spanning midnight.  TBL_TIME_STAMP = CURRENT TIMESTAMP - 24 HOURS 
won't take into account a job that runs a 7:15pm one night and 10:22 the 
next.

I have asked 'If people in their right minds realise that 'a chain is 
only as strong as its weakest link' then what would be the reason 
for anyone in their right mind think that one job can be run with a degree 
of precision which is not met by the rest of the system?'... and the 
response has been 'That's a *good* question, we'll get back to you on 
that'.

Thanks for your time, though... greatly appreciated.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Dynamic Timestamp in Dynamic SQL?

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2002-05-18T20:13:25-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0205181913.19763cc8@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<abohgc$onc$1@panix1.panix.com> <3CE57C26.427C3CEB@boeing.com> <ac4fcb$kni$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
While I don't know a way of doing what you request in exactly the way
that you wish, there is another possible approach, which is to have a
new small table created for your purpose and to add an additional step
to the end of the billing run to insert a row containing the current
timestamp every night.  Then your procedure could select the
timestamps from the last two rows of this new table to construct your
selection criteria.  This table would also provide an historical
record of the timestamps when each billing run completed, which might
be of use for other purposes.

As a possible "refinement" to make the selection criteria easier to
specify in your current SQL, the rows on this table could each contain
two timestamps, the first would be the timestamp on which the previous
billing run completed and the second would be that on which the
current billing run completed.  Each night, the last row on the table
would be updated to add the current timestamp in the second position
and to insert a new row with the current timestamp in the first
position, leaving the second position null in the new row, ready for
update the following night.

Hope this helps, Robert
```

```
Almost what I've decided upon, based on what I've read here... I'm *not*

make this more headache than it is worth); I am going to generate two 
dummy rows in one of the tables I use and update the timestamps in those.

Thanks much for your time... and, once again, my apologies for my 
surliness.

DD
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<acc3a2$l25$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
In article <3ce71845_3@anonymous>, Anonymous  <Nobody> wrote:
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:abohgc$onc$1@panix1.panix.com...
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>the process, then I believe I can show you the missing piece.

Greatly appreciate the offer, old boy, but it won't be needed.  A kludge 
has been cobbed together (using SQL to SELECT and then UPDATE a TIMESTAMP 
in a dummy row in one of the tables I am querying, a row set up just for 
this purpose) and will be implemented for the Prod runs of the job... 
thanks much, anyhow!

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Dynamic Timestamp in Dynamic SQL?

- **From:** Susan A Allen <susan.a.allen@boeing.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-21T23:25:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CEAD752.51F8FDC2@boeing.com>`
- **References:** `<abohgc$onc$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ce71845_3@anonymous> <acc3a2$l25$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```


docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>  A kludge has been cobbed together (using SQL to SELECT and then UPDATE a TIMESTAMP
> in a dummy row in one of the tables I am querying, a row set up just for
> this purpose) and will be implemented for the Prod runs of the job...
> thanks much, anyhow!
> 
Actually that was going to be my next suggestion (now that I have a few
minutes to read the news.
I have a column on my tables called ROW_INSERT_DATE - it does what you
think it does; time stamps when the row was added to the table; there is
no reason at all for an application to not also add other useful date
fields; pertinent to the application.

It seems here you need a column (one or two) that are applied when the
record is created (or updated) by the billing cycle and then when the
next step grabs it for further processing.  Testing the time stamps lets
you know if it has processed through all the steps or not.

	Best wishes

		Susan A
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Dynamic Timestamp in Dynamic SQL?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-22T03:51:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<iBEG8.115302$Q42.6044254@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<abohgc$onc$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ce71845_3@anonymous> <acc3a2$l25$1@panix1.panix.com> <3CEAD752.51F8FDC2@boeing.com>`

```
I've kinda missed this whole discussion - can someone point me to a copy of it please?

A few points that may help, just ignore if they are not relevant please. :)

 (1) If you just want a current timestamp when you create a record, set the default value of the record to
be current-timestamp.

 (2) If you want to update a record with the current timestamp, I don't really see the problem either,
       just set the column value to current-timestamp.

 (3) If you need to select a current timestamp, there are several dummy tables you can select it from.
      No real need to have a column in a record to select from.

Really, timestamps are pretty efficient and cool under DB/2. :)

-Paul


"Susan A Allen" <susan.a.allen@boeing.com> wrote in message news:3CEAD752.51F8FDC2@boeing.com...

>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> Susan A
```

<a id="t-07ab6ed60d"></a>
## Thread: USA IT workers a thing of the past — 10 this month / 11 total

### USA IT workers a thing of the past

- **From:** "BR-549" <frodo@iwon.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-13T16:13:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ICRD8.49234$eV5.3961587@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
First they shipped our blue collar jobs overseas, now they are shipping our white collar jobs overseas or bringing in "scabs". Whatever happen to America first? When this is all said and done, the American Middle Class will be shrank to the size of Kenneth Lay's organ. There will a small group of very rich, and the rest of us cleaning their pools and polishing their Rolls.

This boils down to American Korporations not wanting to invest in their employees. This boils down to American Korporations viewing employees as disposable. What would be wrong with training American workers to close the skill's gap? Why do we need to bring in more foreign workers when there American workers willing, able and available? Especially, in IT, most computer types know the value of keeping current and enjoy trainings. However, the current philosophy seems to be to use an employee until they are outdated and then bring in new, younger, foreign trained worker at much less money. Or take those same jobs and ship them overseas.

This same attitude is what has sparked the current climate of disposable staff...read contractors and consultants. Hire workers with no commitment to them and with a third party taking fairly significant cut of the money paid for their services. Is this really the America way? How does the supported the "Family Values" the our current Republican Administration espouses as their guiding light?

Does the following story bother you as much as it has bothered me? Write your Congressional representatives (links at the bottom), and pass on this email.

Information Technology Association of America's Story

First, the bad news: The U.S. IT work force shrank by an aggregate of 528,496 workers in 2001, with companies hiring 2.1 million IT workers while laying off about 2.6 million, according to the Information Technology Association of America's http://www.itaa.org third annual IT work force study released Monday. Next, the hopeful news: IT hiring managers are forecasting happy days around the corner, according to the report, with aggregate demand for 1.1 million IT workers in 2002.

If hiring managers live up to the expectations expressed in the report-based on phone interviews with 532 hiring managers--it will mean the IT job market will have risen from the dead-or at least the comatose-with demand for IT professionals increasing 27 percent this year over last. But even that rebound wouldn't put the IT work force back where it was in 2000: It's only 71 percent of the number of IT jobs that needed to be filled just two years ago. 

While demand for IT workers may be on the upswing, hiring managers--as they have in the past--said a significant number of open positions will go unfilled because of a chronic gap between the skills required by hiring organizations and the skills possessed by most applications. In fact, half of those 1.1 million jobs that could be added will go unfilled due to the skills gap, the report said. 

The ITAA, an Arlington, Va., trade group representing IT product and service providers, has historically used this purported gap as ammunition in lobbying for increases in the quota of H-1B visas, which allow hiring organizations to use IT professionals from outside the United States. With the H-1B quota due for consideration before Congress again next year, the ITAA plans to ask for another increase in the quota despite the rough 2001 experienced by many laid-off IT workers. 

"There's lots of individual workers and companies showing pain," granted ITAA President Harris Miller. "That's what the data show. But we don't want to make a mistake and repeat what occurred 12 years ago when this country went through a recession. … The conclusion reached-and it was false-by education and by companies was that we didn't need people with technical skills." 

Besides planning to increase hiring, managers are also turning to other sources for their IT needs. Indeed, the report cited a 17 percent increase in outsourcing in 2001 compared to 2000. And, according to experts, increasingly that outsourcing is coming from offshore providers. According to a separate study by Meta Group Research Fellow Howard Rubin, the United States' share of the estimated 5 million global IT jobs is shrinking. (Meta's numbers don't jibe with the ITAA's because of varying definitions for what constitutes an IT worker. Narrow definitions used by the government, for example, have traditionally not counted positions such as technology support or Web development in counting IT workers, noted a spokeswoman for the ITAA.) 

This country now has about half of the worldwide IT work force, and that work force is growing at a rate of about 10 percent annually. Worldwide IT work force expansion rates, meanwhile, have been growing by up to 20 percent yearly, with countries such as Canada, India and the Philippines experiencing a growth rate two to four times that of the United States. 

As for the type of worker IT managers are looking for, technical support workers won't gain much ground. Companies are predicting they'll hire 281,406 help desk types in 2002. That's up from 218,238 last year, but less than half of the 616,055 that hiring managers said were needed in 2000. Network design and administration skills, meanwhile, will be in big demand with 199,348 hires forecast in the coming year. Programmers and software engineers are the next most sought-after position, with 161,487 anticipated hirings in 2002. 

The ITAA report was based on interviews with hiring managers at 155 IT firms and 377 non-IT firms between Feb. 11 and March 14. It also found that workers at IT firms have suffered disproportionately, with total IT jobs in that sector dropping by 15 percent. IT jobs at non-IT firms dropped by 4 percent. The South, which the largest percentage (3,406,519) of IT workers call home, also shed the largest percentage of workers, at 34 percent, or 181,928 workers. 

Link to original story:

http://eletters1.ziffdavis.com/cgi-bin10/flo?y=eP4x0Bf2oL0DSr0mry0AX

or here:

http://www.eweek.com/article/0,3658,s=25210&a=26432,00.asp

Congressional Addresses:

http://www.house.gov/house/MemberWWW_by_State.htm

http://www.webslingerz.com/jhoffman/congress-email.html
```

#### ↳ Re: USA IT workers a thing of the past

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-05-14T00:54:22+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ce051af_1@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<ICRD8.49234$eV5.3961587@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
Very interesting Statistics.

Sadly, this trend is being reflected around the World. 

It isn't just that cheap overseas labour is taking the jobs.

The fact is that the whole Industry is being re-shuffled.

More and more Companies are saying: "Why are we paying a fortune for in-house IT when we are NOT an IT Company?".

The days when the ONLY way to get Corporate IT systems was to write them, are gone.(OK, there are a few remaining outposts of fortress COBOL but, believe me, their days are numbered....)

Whether we, as IT workers, like it or not, the fact remains that most Companies will eventually vote with their feet and go for a "standard" solution like SAP, Chordiant, Siebel or whatever...

Some of the more astute Managers are starting to realise that it is cheaper to Re-Engineer the Business Processes (BPR) so as they conform to a standard solution, than it is to employ IT people to amend the package or write bespoke software to make it comply with the Company's existing processes.

A history of failed delivery and unmet expectations by IT departments has also not helped the perception of IT as a function that simply soaks up money.

The reason that "Web Enablement" solutions are still a lively area of the IT market is because there are no  current "standard" solutions that fit this area. (They are on the way, and it is only a matter of time...)

I would not personally recommend young people to get into IT at the moment. I know that sounds like sacrilege and the perceived wisdom is that IT is the future... it is, but NOT IT as we understand it. I would encourage youngsters to go the Business Administration or Accountancy route. (IT is a part of this, but it is not necessary to make a living from IT).

Management and Business will always be there (there cannot be a Market Place without it, and the Market Place is what drives everything...), IT specialists will be limited to the field of hardware, Networks, and configuring and tuning standard packages.

(Employees of IT Companies will still do a bit of software development, but there will be a very limited demand (compared to the historical demand where EVERYONE needed programmers) for these people, and the remuneration will therefore be lower than has been the case. Especially when they must compete with offshore sweat shops. (We are already seeing the emergence of this trend and it is one of the things that has prompted your post...))

It is ironic to me that I wrote in the last years of the last Century (1994 to be precise):

"History will show that Computer Programming was a phenomenon of the latter half of the 20th Century. By the early part of the 21st Century it was no longer required."

I didn't expect this to become reality before 2010 at the earliest and more likely 2020. And yet the trends are already in evidence and gathering momentum.

I believe a combination of demand from the Market Place, smarter Operating Systems, and more general computer literacy in the population, along with the development of standard businesss solutions (already well under way and growing exponentially every year) will bring this prophecy to fruition.

Write to your Congressman by all means. It won't make a jot of difference.

You cannot buck the Market.

The Market has decided it was paying too much for IT and is making an "adjustment".

It is reminiscent of the situation in 19th Century England when Lace Makers were replaced by machines clever enough to do it (the Lace Makers smugly thought it could never happen, as the knowledge they held was too complex for any machine to ever be able to implement it), or the situation with the Coal industry in 20th Century Britain where it just became uneconomic to mine it, and industry no longer had the same demands for it as had driven the Industrial Revolution. There are many examples...The Steel industry in the USA in the late 20th Century... Why should we ever think that IT is/was immune?

For 60 odd years now computer technology has been infiltrating and changing the way we live. For the most part, it has made our lives better. I long for the day when ANYBODY will be able to sit at a computer and make it do what they want it to do. If that means the end of the Technocracy (including my own career, which has been dedicated to IT for the past 37 years) then I shall figure we did a good job and fade out gracefully... probably still writing OO COBOL components and Java Beans because they fascinate me...<G>)

I arrived back in Europe 5 weeks ago. I have a track record of successful IT Project Management. And yet I found that every job I applied for there were at least 100 applicants. I have been a freelance for the past 25 years and have never been unemployed for more than 2 weeks when I didn't want to be (until now...<G>) The only way I can get work is by calling on people I know (which I have never done in the past...). I have NEVER seen the market so depressed. 

"USA IT workers a thing of the past"?  It ain't just the USA....<G>

Pete.
  BR-549 <frodo@iwon.com> wrote in message news:ICRD8.49234$eV5.3961587@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com...
  First they shipped our blue collar jobs overseas, now they are shipping our white collar jobs overseas or bringing in "scabs". Whatever happen to America first? When this is all said and done, the American Middle Class will be shrank to the size of Kenneth Lay's organ. There will a small group of very rich, and the rest of us cleaning their pools and polishing their Rolls.

  This boils down to American Korporations not wanting to invest in their employees. This boils down to American Korporations viewing employees as disposable. What would be wrong with training American workers to close the skill's gap? Why do we need to bring in more foreign workers when there American workers willing, able and available? Especially, in IT, most computer types know the value of keeping current and enjoy trainings. However, the current philosophy seems to be to use an employee until they are outdated and then bring in new, younger, foreign trained worker at much less money. Or take those same jobs and ship them overseas.

  This same attitude is what has sparked the current climate of disposable staff...read contractors and consultants. Hire workers with no commitment to them and with a third party taking fairly significant cut of the money paid for their services. Is this really the America way? How does the supported the "Family Values" the our current Republican Administration espouses as their guiding light?

  If hiring managers live up to the expectations expressed in the report-based on phone interviews with 532 hiring managers--it will mean the IT job market will have risen from the dead-or at least the comatose-with demand for IT professionals increasing 27 percent this year over last.   While demand for IT workers may be on the upswing, hiring managers--as they have in the past--said a significant number of open positions will go unfilled because of a chronic gap between the skills required by hiring organizations and the skills possessed by most applications. ##### ↳ ↳ Re: USA IT workers a thing of the past
```

- **From:** Joseph Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2002-05-14T20:19:48+08:00

```
In article <3ce051af_1@Usenet.com>, Peter E. C. Dashwood
<dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

> 
> I would not personally recommend young people to get into IT at the =
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Silly Boy!
If young people want to get into IT, then they should start to look for
jobs with the companies that are creating software. i.e. SAP, IBM,
Microsoft, etc. There are plenty around and more on the way as you
yourself have said. For if standardized components are on the way, then
someone is going to have to create, maintain and extend the components.

It's just that in-house programmers may only be used for stuff that is
not available off the shelf.

In fact I would put it to you that the number of IT
(programmers/developers) staff will increase after componentization.
How many people write operating systems these days? This used to be
done in a big company. Now a small number of us write and extend the
OS, but a vast number of us use their work to create solutions. 

I see no difference. If I can buy an off the shelf General Ledger and
plug it together with other components to build a solution, then there
will be a greater need for programmers as more and more of these
combinations are thought of and implemented.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: USA IT workers a thing of the past

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-05-14T23:36:50+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ce19417_3@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<ICRD8.49234$eV5.3961587@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3ce051af_1@Usenet.com> <140520022019482124%josephk@iinet.net.au>`

```

Joseph Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au> wrote in message
news:140520022019482124%josephk@iinet.net.au...
> In article <3ce051af_1@Usenet.com>, Peter E. C. Dashwood
> <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > that IT is the future... it is, but NOT IT as we understand it. I would
=

> > route. (IT is a part of this, but it is not necessary to make a living =

> Silly Boy!
Thanks. It is a long time since anyone addressed me as "boy". Or "silly" for
that matter...<G>

> If young people want to get into IT, then they should start to look for
> jobs with the companies that are creating software. i.e. SAP, IBM,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
This is exactly what I said.

(Quoting from my response to Jimmy's accusation of "Doom and Gloom"....)

<start quote>
"It is important that young
people looking at computer programming as a future career should be aware of
the true situation. It isn't that you CANNOT do it, it is just that the
demand for it is in decline and looks like continuing to be so. In other
words, unless you REALLY want to program computers, you could probably make
more money doing Accountancy or Business Management."
<end quote>

How you can be agreeing with me Joe, if you
think what I wrote is silly?

> It's just that in-house programmers may only be used for stuff that is
> not available off the shelf.
>
> In fact I would put it to you that the number of IT
> (programmers/developers) staff will increase after componentization.

Not in comparison to the number (estimated 3,000,000 for COBOL alone) who
have been historically required to maintain in-house software.

Yes, there will be a living to be made writing software for "software
companies" but it will NOT be the living that WAS to be made writing and
maintaining in-house software, and most of it WON'T be COBOL. (At least
within 15 years it won't...)

> How many people write operating systems these days? This used to be
> done in a big company. Now a small number of us write and extend the
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Not if standard software becomes "smart" enough to do it for you, by
interacting with you to establish what you want... Think in terms of
"Wizards" only they will be "Application Wizards" rather than system
software wizards, and they will be many times "smarter" than the wizards we
have today...

Pete.





 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: USA IT workers a thing of the past

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Joseph Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2002-05-15T18:37:18+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<150520021837180735%josephk@iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<ICRD8.49234$eV5.3961587@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3ce051af_1@Usenet.com> <140520022019482124%josephk@iinet.net.au> <3ce19417_3@Usenet.com>`

```
I generally agree with you Pete.
I was just being a bit flipant!

However, Just as the number of "COBOL" developers decrease, a
proportianate increase in the number of component pluggers will be
required (if not more).

Most users can create a spreadsheet, but usually defer to an expert -
accountant, etc - to assist them when creating a complicated
spreadsheet.

Components will make it easier to connect one thing to another, but it
will also make it easier to incorrectly connect one thing to another.

Expertise in the domain of the problem will be not be required, it's
just that programmers will become experts in programming and not
necessarily require the extensive knowledge of accounting/ops research
etc that I got in my education. They will have the experts in these
fields on hand for consultation in the component construction
companies.

The user organizations would also have experts in accounting/ops
research etc (or hire them). These experts will be expected to be
familiar with the components from the component companies and will do
the connecting of various components together from their  knowledge of
the users requirements and their domain of expertise.

So I see a shift of programmers from goal based developement for a
customer to domain based development for a component house. A shift of
programmers from programming to re-education in a domain so that they
can become component pluggers for customers.

If we re-define programming as both the developers of components (which
of themselves can do nothing) and the pluggers of components (who
create a working application for the user) then the net number of
programmers will increase.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: USA IT workers a thing of the past

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-14T22:42:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CE192AA.451E01EA@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<ICRD8.49234$eV5.3961587@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <abq51r01dpb@enews2.newsguy.com>`

```


Last Boy Scout wrote:

> If you go to www.tilj.com, you will see that 3 people from China were
> indicted for stealing server hardware and software from Lucent
> Technology and trying to set up their own company in China. Too bad
> Lucent didn't hire someone from the USA.

Just using this as a link to get back to the original topic. Pete
sensibly answered, the world is changing, outsourcing, lower demand for
in-house staff etc. I can understand, because the topic of 'green card
people from India' has come up before. US citizens are feeling concerned
about their jobs, and rightly so.

Indirectly the originator of the thread was talking 'protectionism' - a
very dangerous area. Go back through your history; around about 1830 the
Monroe doctrine came into play, you didn't want those damned Europeans
creating empires in S. America - the Americas for Americans. Good
policy, but did tend to make USA isolationist, and in fact reluctantly
you got dragged into both World Wars 1 and 2.

Subsequent to WW 2, the USA took a greater interest in international
politics, such as putting post-War 2 Europe back on its feet with the
Marshall Plan. Ignore traumas like Korea, Vietnam, but international
trade became the big thing through the fifties to current date, and the
US economic might made it a big player. As international trade became
more competitive, (and boy are you folks in the US aggressive <G>),
protectionism started to creep in, protect the home market and
industries.

Are you aware of NAFTA - that's supposed to be about Mexico and the
gringos from the US and Canada playing 'happy families'. In fact it's
rather more, the US government being an arm of US commerce. You may not
even be aware, but you are about to royally screw your biggest trading
partner Canada., (and the figure is 75%), with legislation just passed
protecting your own agricultural community. The impact is going to cost
us $Billions.

Not too much we can do effectively, other than our government trying to
negotiate a fairer deal - but when the elephant sneezes or flatulates -
we get a big whiff  up here.

I think it was Pete who mentioned the coal industry in the UK - I have
very, very great sympathy for coal workers, who faced a very dangerous
existence - but you just can't keep a non-viable industry going just to
feed folks you feel sorry for. Similarly your own steel industry has
taken a nose dive.

Above may seem off topic but is inter-related. You can no more stop
progression in our field than you can the demise of the coal industry or
the decline of the US steel industry. I mentioned in an earlier message,
large Canadian company had outsourced IT to IBM, and the staff were
switched over to IBM. Follow that up with the big smooth marketing
outfits like SAP - they are going to erode the programming base. I'm
willing to bet you might have to shell out between $200,000 to possibly
$500,000 for their 'product' but as the recipient CEO, that makes more
sense to you than an internal IT department with an annual budge of say
$3 - $5 million, or perhaps more.

So where does that put us - you and me. To quote an old British labour
union expression "F.... You Jack. I'm all right !". I'm into my sunset
years, and although I will regret a decline in a profession I've loved,
it it not going to cripple my remaining existence on Mother Earth.

In your case a deep concern for your working future, and quite rightly.
But a projectionist mindset isn't going to get you anywhere.
International commerce is making a grab - there are many, but two
immediate examples Microsoft, (they've just bought some outfit in Canada
for around $? million), and Wallmart. The later has challenged the
status quo both here in Canada and the UK. (Surprisingly the concept was
not accepted in Germany, where they fell flat on their faces).

For youngsters Pete suggested Admin or Accounting. Certainly a B.Admin
seems to be an attractive route to go, giving you a generalist skill
that you can bend to different commercial needs. Accounting - hmmmm not
so sure. Spent some three years in general accounting before I got into
what we then called EDP, (even ADP !). Interesting but a bit of a drudge
with its monthly timelines to be repetitively met. It can only be
effective for you, the individual, if you become very, very much of a
specialist in a unique area of accounting - say Treasury, where you play
the market, with zillions of dollars as though it was your own version
of Monopoly.

The same pattern applies to IT. Given that you are of reasonable
intelligence, and with some forethought, you can become a proficient
COBOL programmer, (obviously with different levels of competency
depending upon you individual makeup). Working on a mainframe you could
be a whiz at setting up silky smooth JCL sets. In to-day's world, from
your personal angle, that just isn't good enough. Without being cocky we
would all be in agreement that COBOL is the clearest language in which
to fathom business rules. Even if you stick solely with COBOL, you shoud
have an objective of  specializing, getting into a very unique area.
(Example, my next door neighbour, trained as a lab technician, works
very successfully from home with a whole daffy of equipment writing in C
for a company head-quarterd in LA. Specialty graphics associated with
lab results. His HQ is constantly pestering  him to relocate to LA,
which he refuses to do. He's considered an important enough employee to
continue working from Calgary, although he goes down to LA roughly every
4 to 6 weeks, and they pick up the tab for travel and accommodation).

Sometimes specialties are difficult to find, some happily trip over them
by accident, and get into a unique world they would never have gotten
into, following the traditional Human Resources recruiting route. Bu you
can lean towards becoming a specialist - components.

Ahhh ! Says Joe down in Aussieland, more bullshit about OO !  With a
broad statement, "I can do it in structured COBOL". Well, I threw out a
challenge to him - doesn't really matter, but he never responded.
Without question, if you want to survive in IT, you are going to have to
become a specialist and components is a lucrative path to follow, so
that you become a survivalist.

Joe's comments, (paraphrased),  to Pete's latest, "So you buy umpteen
components off the shelf, and now you've got a 100 or a 150 people
customizing them - big deal.". Give him currently the benefit of the
doubt. I would wager, 80% WILL be off the shelf, with possibly some 20%
written in house. We are only at the BEGINNING of components and the 20%
while not totally disappearing, will reduce. Example - you produce
components which create an invoice- does all the good standard stuff,
addressing, product/service information, dollar extensions, totaling
etc. Even takes care of federal, 'local' sales taxes, providing you fit
to a pattern.

Ahhh ! says somebody in Canada, wont work for us, because we have (a)
Federal (b) Provincial and (c) in the case of the have-not provinces on
the East Coast - HST (Harmonized Sales Tax) - where the feds collect a
'total' tax and return a portion to the have-nots. Agreed - it wont work
- so somebody up in the Great White North writes a component module to
cover this specific instance. Now they sell it to other Canadian
companies.

A further aspect of componentizing is specialization to an industry -
this is *currently happening* in the Lower 48, where the various States
have similar but different vehicle licence/registration systems. They
are working on swapping components that fit all, where appropriate.
Carry that same concept through to airline reservation systems, hospital
administration, tutorial administration etc., to name a few.

Before you ask - I haven't a bloody clue as yet what componentizing is
about. I understand the abstract concept and where it can lead, but
being a meat and potatoes man, I just have to see it backed up by hard
solid code - and please, in COBOL for preference. I know I can reference
VB and Java texts about components, but I'm sure that much of it can be
done in COBOL - which is exactly what Pete Dashwood is doing with F/J
POWERCOBOL. Having triggered this as a topic elsewhere, I've had some
initial responses - they haven't helped me much. I really want to
doggedly take a crack at this one, starting out with a very clear
definition of what a component is. One person responded, and I'm being
facetious about his reply, "A component is whatever you want it to be,
providing it is loose".  Mr. Meat and Potatoes doesn't like that one - I
want to get down to the real nitty gritty.

In the US you are a vary nationalistic people, very oftern tending to
look inwards, (don't believe all you see on TV, the media condition you
like Pavlov's dogs), but if you want to be a survivalist :-

(1) Follow through, by your own determined efforts. Do your bit for
Uncle Sam, and "The Stars and Stripes Forever". Think of JFK. He said
it, but I actually doubt he wrote it, "Don't ask what your country can
do for you.(protectionism). Ask what you can do for your country".

(2) Want to give yourself a shot at being a survivalist in IT, and still
using COBOL as the backup, ( because of it's superb handling of business
problems)  - COMPONENTS and specialize, specialize,
specialize...........

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: USA IT workers a thing of the past

- **From:** Edward Reid <edward@paleo.org>
- **Date:** 2002-05-15T11:13:06-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.B907F34200289A6D0DFF1E70@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<ICRD8.49234$eV5.3961587@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <abq51r01dpb@enews2.newsguy.com> <3CE192AA.451E01EA@shaw.ca>`

```
On Tue, 14 May 2002 18:42:26 -0400, James J. Gavan wrote
> Think of JFK. He said
> it, but I actually doubt he wrote it, 

I always assumed this too, but went looking just now. Found a page at 
http://www.school-for-champions.com/speeches/kennedy_inaugural.htm that 
says "Kennedy was a Pulitzer Prize-winning author and well-read, but he 
also depended on Ted Sorenson and an able staff of speech writers. 
Sorenson stated in his book on the Kennedy years that the President was 
the sole architect of the speech."

> "Don't ask what your country can
> do for you.(protectionism). Ask what you can do for your country".

In this context the longer quote is interesting, because it's much more 
globally oriented:

"As so, my fellow Americans: ask not what your country can do for you 
-- ask what you can do for your country.

"My fellow citizens of the world: ask not what America will do for you, 
but what together we can do for the freedom of man.

"Finally, whether you are citizens of America or citizens of the world, 
ask of us here the same high standards of strength and sacrifice that 
we ask of you."

Sentiments seldom heard today, sadly. Twenty years later the new 
president was better known for "what's your sign".

Edward Reid
```

<a id="t-f257945ce7"></a>
## Thread: Rebuilding DBMS — 5 msgs

### Rebuilding DBMS

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-14T05:14:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CE09CB1.196246AD@shaw.ca>`

```
"Rebuilding ?", probably wrong terminology, but bear with me. Plus I'm
talking small datasets on PCs.

- COBOL files - for either crashed indices or to get rid of waste space,
(deleted records) - I can use Micro Focus REBUILD.

- MS Access Tables -  I'm guessing I will have wasted space due to
deleted rows (?),  and although haven't  hit it *yet*,  for sure,
Murphy's Law will prevail and inevitably I will get a glitch in the MS
myapp.mdb.

Wasted space is not that critical, given the small datasets, and any
prior reason for doing this was associated with RM/COBOL using Radio
Shack 8/10 inch platters with very limited storage.

I should add, and applies to both Files/Tables, datasets are  active for
no more than a six-week cycle while data is being setup, inspection
occurs at oil plant, reports produced - finito. The ms.mdb can then be
zipped to a diskette. Small users, so no fancy backup systems which
would just cost them more than they can afford.

As yet, haven't addressed the 'rebuild/cleanup" features of MS Access,
which in theory, a reasonably computer literate user should be able to
handle.

Any guidelines on addressing the problem - particularly with reference
to where the DBMS becomes inaccessible through a hard disk glitch ?.

Jimmy
```

```
worked well.

FF
```

#### ↳ Re: Spell Number

- **From:** "Ron" <Ron@NoSpamForMe.Com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-14T14:01:49-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<abrmv0$371$1@suaar1aa.prod.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<2e7bb509.0205141033.a883f45@posting.google.com>`

```
05 nbr pic 9(5)v99
05 filler redefines nbr.
    10 nbrdigit occurs 5 times pic 9(5).
    10 filler pic xx.
05 sub  pic 9.
05 ptr  pic 99

05 output-area pic x(80).

move 1 to ptr
perform varying sub from 1 by 1 until sub > 5
  evaluate nbrdigit (sub)
     when 0
        continue
     when 1
        move 'one' to output-area (ptr:)
        add 4 to ptr
     when 2
        ....
  end-evaluate
end-perform

There's more to it than that. But that's all you get
to help you get started. You have to consider place
value and where to put "hundred" or "dollars" ...,
capitalization at the beginning. etc.  Show us what
you have done so far.

=================================================

someone help?
> Thanks In Advance
> Tom
```

## Thread: comp-x — 4 msgs

### comp-x

- **From:** "Tomba" <tombatore@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-14T23:49:49+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ce18684$0$6951$ba620e4c@news.skynet.be>`

```
hy,

making some screens with microfocus cobol, I wondered what in a pic clause
that COMP-X is standing for.
Does anyone have an idea?

thanks,

 Steven DG
```

#### ↳ Re: comp-x

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2002-05-15T00:55:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-eL0wcoMyhYSp@thishost>`
- **References:** `<3ce18684$0$6951$ba620e4c@news.skynet.be>`

```
On Tue, 14 May 2002 21:49:49 UTC, "Tomba" <tombatore@hotmail.com> 
wrote:

> hy,
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

It is used to specify "native binary" 
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: comp-x

- **From:** cobol@dudleysworld.co.uk (John Dudley)
- **Date:** 2002-05-15T03:49:59-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ab988303.0205150249.32c15e9b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3ce18684$0$6951$ba620e4c@news.skynet.be> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-eL0wcoMyhYSp@thishost>`

```
COMP-X is not native binary, COMP-5 is.
Byte swapping can occur with COMP-X data items depending on you
processor type, where COMP-5 items are stored in the native format.

lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca wrote in message news:<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-eL0wcoMyhYSp@thishost>...
> On Tue, 14 May 2002 21:49:49 UTC, "Tomba" <tombatore@hotmail.com> 
> wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> It is used to specify "native binary"
```

#### ↳ Re: comp-x

- **From:** "Mike Kinasz" <mkinasz@cbspayroll.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-15T01:01:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vriE8.38281$Po6.16256@rwcrnsc52.ops.asp.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3ce18684$0$6951$ba620e4c@news.skynet.be>`

```
If you define a field like this

01  YER-NEW-FIELD   PIC X(1) COMP-X.

Then you access it like it's a byte.... 0-255.


Mike



"Tomba" <tombatore@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:3ce18684$0$6951$ba620e4c@news.skynet.be...
> hy,
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

## Thread: Date Arithmetic — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Date and calendar processing`](topics.md#dates)_

### Date Arithmetic

- **From:** "Brian Caddell" <briancad@nospam.netspace.net.au>
- **Date:** 2002-05-15T10:25:57+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<abs9uj$al$1@otis.netspace.net.au>`

```
Does anyone know a good routine for subtracting two dates to get the number
of days between? I seem to recall that Fujitsu had one, but I can't find it
any more.

Thanks,
Brian Caddell
```

#### ↳ Re: Date Arithmetic

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-15T01:24:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ANiE8.13817$v81.2029921@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<abs9uj$al$1@otis.netspace.net.au>`

```

"Brian Caddell" <briancad@nospam.netspace.net.au> wrote in message
news:abs9uj$al$1@otis.netspace.net.au...
> Does anyone know a good routine for subtracting two dates to get the
number
> of days between? I seem to recall that Fujitsu had one, but I can't find
it
> any more.

It's a COBOL Function, viz:

COMPUTE NUM-DAYS =
    FUNCTION INTEGER-OF-DATE(END-DATE) -
    FUNCTION INTEGER-OF-DATE(BEG-DATE)

Where END-DATE and BEG-DATE are PIC 9(8) fields of containing YYYYMMDD
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Date Arithmetic

- **From:** "Brian Caddell" <briancad@nospam.netspace.net.au>
- **Date:** 2002-05-16T08:08:44+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<abum9j$2ugf$1@otis.netspace.net.au>`
- **References:** `<abs9uj$al$1@otis.netspace.net.au> <ANiE8.13817$v81.2029921@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
Thanks. It's terrific, isn't it.
Brian

…[15 more quoted lines elided]…

```

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-16T12:51:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uXNE8.156037$M7.15449567@bin7.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<abs9uj$al$1@otis.netspace.net.au> <ANiE8.13817$v81.2029921@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <abum9j$2ugf$1@otis.netspace.net.au>`

```

"Brian Caddell" <briancad@nospam.netspace.net.au> wrote in message
news:abum9j$2ugf$1@otis.netspace.net.au...
> Thanks. It's terrific, isn't it.
> Brian

Then you'll like:

COMPUTE LEN = FUNCTION STORED-CHAR-LENGTH(DATA-NAME)

LEN now contains the number of leading characters in DATA-NAME until
trailing blanks.
```

```
Frank,

a global decline in the way IT is done does NOT mean that America is "on its
way out".

It is only computer programming, for Chrissake!

Having recently been in America, I can assure you that, seen with the eyes
of a visitor, the USA continues to function as it always has.

A great society with potential for Greatness and potential for
Insignificance.

(Is anybody else concerned about the proliferation of Starbuck's...<G> While
I'm all in favour of small business this seems to me like overkill in a
meaningless industry... I say this as a coffee lover <G>)

I believe there is still very much that the US will contribute to the World,
and the full Kennedy quote moves me as much today as it did when I watched
him say it on Black and white TV in a tiny little country far removed from
America.

In the words of John Paul Jones (an American Hero if I remember correctly
<G>):
"Don't give up the ship!"

Pete.
```

```
> Yes, but this time 'bigness' is by individual choice rather than imposed
by
> the sword.
>
Huh?  Are you saying that the G8 countries have no armies and never use
force? What world are you living in?  Show me a G8 conference in the last
five years that has not REQUIRED force to keep the serfs out, even inside
the countries that control it.  They are still dismantling the barbed wire
in Montreal from two years back.

Besides which, medieval power elites only used force occasionally, in the
same way.  They kept control by passing laws that gave them control of the
land and the food supply, the same way it is being done today.  You have to
have happy, working serfs somewhere to keep the elite in luxury.

Every human act is ultimately by some individual's choice.  The key is
what individual made the choice, not that it was made by a human.They are
all that.

> >
> > The idea that the G8 is for the people is ridiculous.  There is not a
> single
> > elected representative, only reps for countries and companies.  The
entire
> > structure is designed to help companies move freely between countries,
so
> > that they do not have to be citizens of a specific country like ordinary
> > people.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> argument in the 18th century. Nationalism should not get in the way of
free
> trade.
>

The fact that it is not "of the people" or "by the people" does not
automatically make it "for the people" either.  What you seem to be saying
is that we are all a bit thick, and should just take our medicine like good
little boys and girls.  That was what the king said to Adam Smith.  If I
recall correctly, Adam Smith told the king to fuck off.

As well, the church settled that the earth was the center of the unniverse a
few hundred years before that.  The scientists settled that velocities could
be added without having to consider that they were relative, just about the
time of Smith.

In fact, Adam Smith was arguing that countries should act as individuals in
the world market, to provide balance. Just as individuals within the country
should not form power elites, so countries within the world should not.
Free trade requires more than one person or one country.  A powerfull group
at the top carving the world up for their own purposes is not free trade. A
powerfull group at the top carving the world up for their own purposes is
tyrany.

Times are a bit different than in the 18th century, just in case you have
not noticed. Economics is  social theory.  Economic theories have to take
into account the society they exist in.  They do not stand outside a social
structure like a law of physics.

Donald


> >
> > The reasons are even the same.  They are immortal, the same as a title
was
> > designed to be.
> >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```
