Huh!  I'm not sure if I understand you, but AFAIK serfdom wasn't imposed by
the sword but was caused by the arms race (Military Industrial Complex?) of
the time.  It was also sometimes entered into voluntarily for safety.

It was caused by the cost of swords, armour etc, the amount of time that was
required to be skilled in their use, and fear of invaders (terrorists?).
Farmers in a group could no longer just work a farm, and afford to buy
weapons from the proceeds nor find time to train to use them, therefore the
farmers supported a warrior class.  As armour and weapons got better
(heavier?) warriors had to train from childhood.  Enormous skill was needed
to be a mounted knight.  The support costs were phenominal.

When people could no longer afford to pay their support dues (taxes) they
were made debt serfs.  As the warriors got better, more people tried to get
under their protection so the warriors became lords and they could set more
arduous terms.  Some families went into voluntary serfdom just to get
safety.

Serfdom tended not to be imposed by conquest.  Even the Norman conquest of
England just took over an existing system.

Does all this sound familiar?

This is all a bit simplistic and I apologise if I've got it wrong.  I've
been a long time out of school. :-)

Rick

>
> >
> > The idea that the G8 is for the people is ridiculous.  There is not a
> single
> > elected representative, only reps for countries and companies.  The
entire
> > structure is designed to help companies move freely between countries,
so
> > that they do not have to be citizens of a specific country like ordinary
> > people.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> argument in the 18th century. Nationalism should not get in the way of
free
> trade.
>
> >
> > The reasons are even the same.  They are immortal, the same as a title
was
> > designed to be.
> >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Bottom Line

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** stephenjspiro@mail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2002-05-16T22:00:56-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<928495c6.0205162100.760f5859@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3ce1c931.2489078@nntp> <3ce3a176_5@Usenet.com> <hwNE8.18610$w04.2175361@news20.bellglobal.com> <HUNE8.5665$Yg7.970305@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <ac0o36$m49kh$1@ID-39799.news.dfncis.de>`

```
"Rick Price" <rick@hpdsoftware.com> wrote 
> Huh!  I'm not sure if I understand you, but AFAIK serfdom wasn't imposed by
> the sword but was caused by the arms race (Military Industrial Complex?) of
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>
You might want to review your history of 19th century Russia, where
the rights of serfs (and even formerly free peasants) were constantly
eroded. And where they were routinely drafted into the army.

Additionally, even going back to medieval times, the idea of a warrior
class protecting the peasantry is a myth.  In case of war, peasants
(including serfs bonded to either Land or Lord), were drafted in large
numbers and expected to bear the brunt of the casualties.  This was
not merely in defensive situations, but in foreign adventuring as
well.  The Kings of France and England would routinely establish
levels of manpower for each vassal lord to provide from his own serfs.

Stephen J Spiro
```

'In fact, we are all becoming serfs again, as the entire world is ruled by
the multi-nationals much the same as England was ruled by the nobility 500
years back.  They own more and more of the land, control all the jobs, and
are even taking over "small bussiness" down to the coffee shop level'
to which the reponse was
'Yes, but this time "bigness" is by individual choice rather than imposed by
the sword.'.

if 'we are all becoming serfs again' does '"bigness" is by individual
choice' make it any safer for our descendants?

Rick

To be trite,  in so far as we buy things from, or supply things to
multi-nationals, we're all working for them.  In the main it suits us, but a
lot of the time, we can only get something cheap because someone else is
having to work cheap.

>
> The enemies of the pharmacist multi-nationals are the insurance
> multi-nationals, as they lobby the states to limit the other's power so
they
> can make more money.

Except where the insurance multi-nationals also provide pension schemes, and
they have a lot of  equity in the pharmaceutical mult-nationals.

>
> Choice is good.  Having powerful influences on the State is better than
> having no influences on the State.

Until the powerful influences become the state.  Until they get together to
decide what is best for the state and maybe pick a suitable person to head
the state.  You can vote for the person I'm supplying money to or a
(different) person I'm supplying money to.

BTW I'm mainly taking a Devil's Advocate position here but not entirely <G>.

Rick

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Bottom Line

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-05-17T16:05:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ac39eo$39$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3ce1c931.2489078@nntp> <3ce3a176_5@Usenet.com> <hwNE8.18610$w04.2175361@news20.bellglobal.com> <HUNE8.5665$Yg7.970305@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <ac0o36$m49kh$1@ID-39799.news.dfncis.de> <928495c6.0205162100.760f5859@posting.google.com> <ac2c2t$kv45j$1@ID-39799.news.dfncis.de> <ac33i9$pr1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <ac373k$mddta$1@ID-39799.news.dfncis.de>`

```

On 17-May-2002, "Rick Price" <rick@hpdsoftware.com> wrote:

> > Except we have perfect freedom to pick which multi-national to work for
> > -
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> multi-nationals, we're all working for them.

And they're working for us.

> In the main it suits us, but a
> lot of the time, we can only get something cheap because someone else is
> having to work cheap.

Or they're getting to work cheap.  When Nike opens up a sweat shop in some
poor country, it doesn't force people to work for it.  If we protest and
they close down the factory, we are sending the workers back to slash and
burn economies, starving and destroying our jungles.  I can understand why
union workers don't like those factories - but I don't understand why
ecologists and people who say they care about the poor object.  (programmers
aren't union, but we object to companies hiring foreign programmers - we
would rather have others support us being comfortable than foreigners being
fed)

Also.  those factories (unlike mining), have a history of being the best and
quickest way of bringing the poor people in poor countries into wealth
within a couple of generations.  I wonder if mining might do this in the
future as it becomes more high-tech.


> > Choice is good.  Having powerful influences on the State is better than
> > having no influences on the State.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> (different) person I'm supplying money to.

That tends to happen in one industry states.   Except the industry (or
religion) supports the dictator who abuses the people.   When there are
multiple companies producing wealth (power), the politicians have to satisfy
a bunch of powerful wealth producers.  And the wealth producers have to
satisfy their skilled workers and consumers.    The ideal would be if the
politicians simply tried to satisfy us, but when that gets tried seriously
it tends to be to tax us for circuses - or in the case of Hawaii, benefits.
 I wish it weren't so.   But it is better to support what you see DOES work
than to support what you wish WOULD work.

On the other hand - we need to keep bitching - just as long as we don't
always win!


> BTW I'm mainly taking a Devil's Advocate position here but not entirely
> <G>.

Understood.

It's interesting - my biggest complaint about systems that pull down the
powerful is that in real life this means we pull down the powerful we can
reach - who are those who are powerful to influence the really big guys.  I
hate lobbyists, but don't see a better alternative.

When communism devalued individuals - it produced vacuums that people such
as Stalin could dominate.
```

- **References:** `<3ce1c931.2489078@nntp> <3ce3a176_5@Usenet.com> <hwNE8.18610$w04.2175361@news20.bellglobal.com> <HUNE8.5665$Yg7.970305@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <ac0o36$m49kh$1@ID-39799.news.dfncis.de> <928495c6.0205162100.760f5859@posting.google.com> <ac2c2t$kv45j$1@ID-39799.news.dfncis.de> <ac33i9$pr1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <ac373k$mddta$1@ID-39799.news.dfncis.de> <ac39eo$39$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
>
> It's interesting - my biggest complaint about systems that pull down the
> powerful is that in real life this means we pull down the powerful we can
> reach - who are those who are powerful to influence the really big guys.
I
> hate lobbyists, but don't see a better alternative.

There's a difference between a lobbyist and an interest group. Lobbyists
are, in the main, experts. Do you really want a legislator to decide whether
hydrogenated yak-fat should require a prescription when he knows absolutely
nothing about yaks? Lobbyists inform and educate lawmakers and even,
sometimes, write the bills that become the laws. Nothing wrong with that.

I like the concept of interest groups. They act as a foil to the herd
mentality of mass democracy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Bottom Line

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-18T17:12:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nYvF8.174282$M7.17837329@bin7.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3ce1c931.2489078@nntp> <3ce3a176_5@Usenet.com> <hwNE8.18610$w04.2175361@news20.bellglobal.com> <HUNE8.5665$Yg7.970305@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <ac0o36$m49kh$1@ID-39799.news.dfncis.de> <928495c6.0205162100.760f5859@posting.google.com> <ac2c2t$kv45j$1@ID-39799.news.dfncis.de> <ac33i9$pr1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <ac373k$mddta$1@ID-39799.news.dfncis.de> <ac39eo$39$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <h9aF8.12268$th.1104538@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```

"JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com>

Based on my limited tenure on the hill (nine months as a legislative aide to
a U.S. Senator), I'll try to respond to all at once.

Yukon Mama - Legislators are for sale:

It doesn't work that way. Almost always groups contribute to a politican for
what the pol has DONE, not what he WILL DO. No matter what position a pol
takes on an issue, some group will agree and reward him. If a pol will get
$10K from either Handgun Control or the NRA, he's free to vote his
conscience.

Liam Devlin - Lobbyists present only one side:

Usually that's true. But lobbyists from both sides visit with the
Legislative Aides of a congress-critter. He, the LA, gets info from both,
often back and forth, before presenting a position paper to his boss. The
boss almost NEVER visits with a lobbyist.

Donald Tees - Representative rights suppressed by special interests:

I certainly hope so. Currently, EVERYBODY is opposed to spam and they flood
their congress-critter's office accordingly. On the other side of the spam
issue is a well-financed lobbying group from the Direct Marketers
Association. The two contrary views balance out and paralyze the congress on
the issue of spam (a Good Thing(tm)).

You can have freedom or you can have democracy: pick one.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Bottom Line

> > Not the same - there still are powers that will influence Bush.  That

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> reactions is controled?  That makes it better?

That's not what I'm afraid of.  I want the Constitution to be in control.  I
want privacy, I want freedom of speech, and I want even the parts of the
Constitution I don't care for still controlling what the state can do.  
It's not that I am in love with what the Constitution says - it's just that
if the state doesn't accept the authority of the Constitution, it won't
accept any authority at all.
```

```
In article <ac373k$mddta$1@ID-39799.news.dfncis.de>,
Rick Price <rick@hpdsoftware.com> wrote:
>
>"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>multi-nationals, we're all working for them.

Ummmmm... my dictionary may be different, true, but unless you are 
equating 'customer' and 'employee' then it seems that you are equating 
'trite' with 'wrong'.  Consider:

'The problem here in South Florida is that the temperature has been too 
cold.'

'Really?  Somewhere in Nebraska a man is teaching his son how to tie his 
shoes.'

'Exactly... and since that man is not part of the solution he is part of 
the problem!'

>In the main it suits us, but a
>lot of the time, we can only get something cheap because someone else is
>having to work cheap.

Eh?  When I studied economics I was taught that (absent a command economy) 
the only reason that someone can get something cheaply is because someone 
else is willing to *sell* it cheaply.  Consider the phenomenon of 'loss 
leaders' in retail markets.

DD
```

<a id="t-bf34ffc578"></a>
## Thread: Drive Volume Information — 7 msgs

### Drive Volume Information

- **From:** "Rob" <rng519@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-16T16:54:02+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<abvl4f$263d$1@arachne.labyrinth.net.au>`

```
Hi,
Anybody know a way in Cobol to retrieve a computer drive volume information
ie. Volume name, serial number etc.
We have a routine in VB to do this, so another possibility is to find a way
to call a VB program from Cobol and have the parameter pass back to Cobol.

Can anybody offer any suggestion?
Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: Drive Volume Information

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-16T16:49:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CE3E2F6.685AA775@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<abvl4f$263d$1@arachne.labyrinth.net.au>`

```


Rob wrote:

> Hi,
> Anybody know a way in Cobol to retrieve a computer drive volume information
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks

You haven't specified which compiler, but with your reference to VB, guess it
has to be a PC. Check out any library routines available - if you compiler has
them. Another alternative there *may* be a an API routine by Young Kim - see
his :-

http://www.kimsoft.com/api-cobol/api-cobol.htm

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Drive Volume Information

- **From:** "Rob" <rng519@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-17T09:11:37+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ac1e90$dqp$1@arachne.labyrinth.net.au>`

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Drive Volume Information

- **From:** "Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-17T01:19:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dUYE8.2358$iw7.126821623@newssvr16.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<abvl4f$263d$1@arachne.labyrinth.net.au> <3CE3E2F6.685AA775@shaw.ca> <ac1e90$dqp$1@arachne.labyrinth.net.au>`

```
Rob,

I've never used this particular API, but I have used:

     CALL "GetWindowsDirectoryA" WITH STDCALL USING
                         BY REFERENCE WINDOWS-DIRECTORY
                         BY VALUE 240
                         RETURNING Returnvalue.

so that I know whether I'm running under Windows NT or 95/98/ME, and I
believe Windows 2000 still installs under WINNT (I'm not sure tonight,
however, I can check it out tomorrow at work).  I don't run W2K at home
since XP is the latest flavor of MS.  At work, I have a "triple boot" PC
that runs Win98/Win NT 4.0/Win 2000, but I honestly never paid any attention
to where Windows 2000 installed.

I've also used:

     CALL "GetDriveTypeA" WITH STDCALL USING
                         BY REFERENCE ROOTPATH-NAME
                         RETURNING Returnvalue.

to make sure that Users don't try to map a drive to a CD-ROM or floppy.

Although I'm using Fujitsu 6.1, I think the same API calls will work for
5.0.  You need to link with "kernel32.lib" and within that lib is
"GetVolumeInformationA", which should give you the information that you
need.  Unfortunately, I don't have the additional parameters to add to the
call, but I think others in this group can help you, or possibly a search of
"GetVolumeInformation" at the MS support site will give you the parameters.
You'll need to translate the "C" parameters to COBOL, as was done above, but
hopefully, this will give you a place to start.

Best of Luck,

Denny

"A Unisys Mainframe guy trying to become "semi" Fujitsu PC COBOL literate"

"Rob" <rng519@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:ac1e90$dqp$1@arachne.labyrinth.net.au...

PC.

…[8 more quoted lines elided]…

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Drive Volume Information

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rob" <rng519@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-17T14:07:59+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ac1vl2$ovr$1@arachne.labyrinth.net.au>`
- **References:** `<abvl4f$263d$1@arachne.labyrinth.net.au> <3CE3E2F6.685AA775@shaw.ca> <ac1e90$dqp$1@arachne.labyrinth.net.au> <dUYE8.2358$iw7.126821623@newssvr16.news.prodigy.com> <viZE8.1077$pa.236711@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
Thanks for everyone help.
I've solved the problem using
     CALL "GetVolumeInformationA" WITH STDCALL USING
                                  By Value RootPathName
                                  By Value VolumeNameBuffer
                                  By Value VolumeNameSize
                                  By Reference VolumeSerialNumber
                                  By Reference MaximumComponentLength
                                  By Reference FileSystemFlags
                                  By Value FileSystemNameBuffer
                                  By Value FileSystemNameSize.

## Thread: COBOL FAQ — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### COBOL FAQ

- **From:** wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
- **Date:** 2002-05-16T11:56:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,news.answers,comp.answers
- **Message-ID:** `<computer-lang/cobol-faq_1021550161@rtfm.mit.edu>`

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2002/05/06
Version: 3.03
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Spring 2002

The full COBOL FAQ can be viewed at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send
private email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

Toward the top of the FAQ is a pointer to the appendix listing the most
recent significant updates and changes.

This FAQ answers such questions as (and addresses topics such as):

 - Where can I get a COBOL compiler? 
 - Is there a free COBOL compiler? 
 - Where can I contact... ?
 - What is happening with the draft of the next COBOL Standard and what
is in it? 
 - What about OO COBOL? 
 - Books about COBOL
 - COBOL Tools
 - What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
 - What about USAGE? COMP? Storage for data in xyz format? etc?
 - How do I get started with COBOL? Where can I get education?
Tutorials? Etc

and much, MUCH more
```

Hello all,

my program must send a text ( Pic X(20) ) to the clipboard. After this send
I will use Strg+c, Strg+v in other Applicationï¿½s.

I found a demo-prog at www.kimsoft.com/api-cobol/api-cobol.hmt  but I canï¿½t
animate/compile the demo-file
(error 114, attempt to access item beyond bounds of memory (Signal 11))

Sp6a.

Ralph Leiherr

#### ↳ Re: Clipboard

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-16T09:37:28-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

Just off the top of my head, I suspect that your routine is failing because
the string is not null terminated, and that a move is trying to move a lot
more than X(20).  Try executing

        MOVE X'00' TO THE-FIELD (20:1) before placing it into the clipboard.

Donald

#### ↳ Re: Clipboard

- **From:** "Panos Zotos" <pzotos@syntax.gr>
- **Date:** 2002-05-22T16:50:45+03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<acg7ks$ppk$1@usenet.otenet.gr>`
- **References:** `<ac0aav$rej$01$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
You can use the CLIPBOARD class available in the GUI class library that will
give you access to Window's clipboard.

It offers two class methods, read and write that do this. Write, which is
the one you need, takes a CharArray as a parameter so make sure you create
this chararray object from your pic x(20) string, before using it there.

regards

Panos

<a id="t-03a01e86b5"></a>
## Thread: Correct Length of a string — 89 this month / 93 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Correct Length of a string

- **From:** "Ralph Leiherr" <leiherr@vwda.de>
- **Date:** 2002-05-16T16:10:52+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ac0ej0$vg1$01$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
Hi all,

I have an string with Pic X(1000). Only the text "Das ist ein Test" is in
it. I want now, how is the length of this string (without last spaces !!).
The correct length is 16.

With the Inspect - Command I can count the Spaces but I need the space
between the words.

OK, I can redefines the string with Pic X(01), Occurs 1000 and check byte by
byte from 1000 by -1 until the string <> Space. The Length is (1000 - 984) =
16, but I donï¿½t want check the string 984 times.

With the Inspect - Command I can count the Spaces but I need the space
between the words.

Has anyone a better  idea  ???

Thanks

Ralph
```

#### ↳ Re: Correct Length of a string

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-16T15:30:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<JgQE8.7664$PP6.1822886018@newssvr30.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<ac0ej0$vg1$01$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
You did not specify which compiler you are using.  This would have some
impact on the suggested code.

The quickest, assuming you have intrinsic functions, is to use FUNCTION
REVERSE.  Then you may INSPECT tallying leading spaces.

If you do not have FUNCTION REVERSE, or if it causes too much overhead, try
something like the following:

PERFORM VARYING I FROM 1 BY 25 UNTIL I > 1000
    IF THE-STRING (I:) = SPACES
        SUBTRACT 1 FROM I
        PERFORM VARYING I FROM I BY -1
                UNTIL I = 0 OR THE-STRING (I:1) NOT = SPACE
            CONTINUE
    END-IF
END-PERFORM
* At this point I is the string length = the displacement of the last
non-space character
* Note that the order of the tests on the UNTIL clause on the second PERFORM
is quite important

This code fragment assumes that most of your strings are actually much
shorter than 1000.  If this is not the situation, then a 'binary chop' would
actually do fewer tests.  If most of your strings are quite long, then a
variant of the code fragment which tests from the right would be faster.

Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation
```

#### ↳ Re: Correct Length of a string

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-05-16T15:34:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ac0j92$bvl$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<ac0ej0$vg1$01$1@news.t-online.com>`

```

On 16-May-2002, "Ralph Leiherr" <leiherr@vwda.de> wrote:

> I have an string with Pic X(1000). Only the text "Das ist ein Test" is in
> it. I want now, how is the length of this string (without last spaces !!).
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Has anyone a better  idea  ???

First - a loop counting as you said can be very efficient - don't discard
that idea unless testing shows otherwise.   (I bet it is more efficient than
the INSPECT verb)
Second - if you use a loop, reference modification may be easier than
subscripting.  YMMV.
Third - you can make your INSPECT delimiter a string of 10 spaces if you
like.
```

#### ↳ Re: Correct Length of a string

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-16T15:59:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CE3D754.C21DEE2@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<ac0ej0$vg1$01$1@news.t-online.com>`

```


Ralph Leiherr wrote:

> Hi all,
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Has anyone a better  idea  ???

This is one that always annoys me, and I haven't found an easy answer. INSPECT
wont do it for you. So, ham-fisted as it is this is what I do :-

    perform varying n from MaxLength by -1 until MyText(n:1) <> spaces
                if MyText(n:1) <> spaces
                    EXIT PERFORM
                End-if
    End-perform

        *> n then gives you the 'true' length

Now an alternative to the above, if you *know* the likelihood is that there will
be more white space than text, (your reference to 984), then do a standard
'forwards' PERFORM with an increment until you get 'two spaces"

**  See Mr. Stephen Spiro - a justified reason for using EXIT PERFORM  <G>.

I doubt you are into OO, using N/E classes  - but there is a trick where you can
get rid of the 'white space' - but still a reasonable amount of messing around.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Correct Length of a string

- **From:** "Ron" <Ron@NoSpamForMe.Com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-16T14:39:01-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ac11sp$9u0$1@suaar1aa.prod.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<ac0ej0$vg1$01$1@news.t-online.com> <3CE3D754.C21DEE2@shaw.ca>`

```
05 tally-count pic 9(7).

move zero to tally-count.
inspect function reverse (data-field)
 tallying tally-count for leading spaces.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Correct Length of a string

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-16T20:29:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CE41680.1A2E9331@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<ac0ej0$vg1$01$1@news.t-online.com> <3CE3D754.C21DEE2@shaw.ca> <ac11sp$9u0$1@suaar1aa.prod.compuserve.com>`

```


Ron wrote:

> 05 tally-count pic 9(7).
>
> move zero to tally-count.
> inspect function reverse (data-field)
>  tallying tally-count for leading spaces.

El neato ! I like that one, but you've got to admit looks a bit obscure until
you think on it and suck it and see. And to be effective, so that you don't
screw up the original data-field, you would be well advised to copy it to
MyField to do your INSPECT.. That then allows you to take data-field, and if
necessary put an x'00' into the position
'truelength' + 1.

What the helll, just submitted this one to J4, to add to the 'goodies' for COBOL
200X after 2002 has been issued. (OK Pete, mea culpa, mea culpa, mea maxima
culpa <G>) :-

Jimmy
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FUNCTION - TRUELENGTH

There is a requirement, whether procedural or OO code to establish the
'true' length of an item :-

Working-Storage Section.
01 ws-Length                                        pic 9(04).
01 ws-MyText                                      pic x(1000) value
                                                            "This is the actual
text".

compute ws-Length = function TrueLength (ws-MyText).

Definition : Function TrueLength returns the 'true' length of the
appropriate field for all characters, plus intervening white space,
until it meets the first trailing space character.

In above example ws-Length would = 23

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------

PS: If they 'buy' it - wonder what the resulting 'legalese' will look like <G>




>
>
…[55 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Correct Length of a string

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-17T01:42:44+00:00

```
    Ron's INSPECT FUNCTION REVERSE does not modify the original data-field;
it only inspects a copy of it where all the bytes appear in reverse order.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Correct Length of a string

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Ron" <NoSoy@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-16T21:54:17-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ac1rcr$fe5$1@suaar1ab.prod.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<ac0ej0$vg1$01$1@news.t-online.com> <3CE3D754.C21DEE2@shaw.ca> <ac11sp$9u0$1@suaar1aa.prod.compuserve.com> <3CE41680.1A2E9331@shaw.ca>`

```
You don't need to move it to separate area. The action
happens in a copy created by function reverse. The original field
is not modified.

To move low-values to the end of field you can do:

05 tally-count pic 9(7).

move zero to tally-count.
inspect function reverse (data-field)
  tallying tally-count for leading spaces.
move low-values to data-field (length of data-field - tally-count + 1 : tally-count).

=======================================================
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message news:3CE41680.1A2E9331@shaw.ca...
>
>
…[106 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Correct Length of a string

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-05-17T14:27:55+00:00

> > inspect function reverse (data-field)
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Correct Length of a string

- **From:** stephenjspiro@mail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2002-05-16T21:43:31-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<928495c6.0205162043.677d0d76@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<ac0ej0$vg1$01$1@news.t-online.com> <3CE3D754.C21DEE2@shaw.ca>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote 
> 
> This is one that always annoys me, and I haven't found an easy answer. INSPECT
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Jimmy, you disappoint me. That is an AWFUL reason for using EXIT
PERFORM.
First of all, since the PERFORM VARYING ... UNTIL contains the test,
you can
code it this way, and it will fall thru:

     perform varying n from MaxLength by -1 until MyText(n:1) <>
spaces
                 if MyText(n:1) <> spaces
                     CONTINUE
                 End-if
     End-perform

But wait! Since the PERFORM VARYING ... UNTIL contains the test, why
do you test again inside? You can code it this way, and it is MUCH
simpler, and much more efficient.

     perform varying n from MaxLength by -1 until MyText(n:1) <>
spaces
            CONTINUE
     End-perform

The PERFORM statement does all the work.
Apology accepted. <G>

Stephen J Spiro
Wizard Systems
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Correct Length of a string

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-05-18T00:39:05+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ce5997c_2@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<ac0ej0$vg1$01$1@news.t-online.com> <3CE3D754.C21DEE2@shaw.ca> <928495c6.0205162043.677d0d76@posting.google.com>`

```
Interesting....

In 1968 on an NCR 315 running a Tape based COBOL Compiler I first saw the
PERFORM construct used as you describe, Stephen.

Of course in those days there was no EXIT PERFORM or CONTINUE or Reference
Modification, so a rather ingenious programmer had got round the problem by
doing the following:

Perform dummy
   varying n
       from MaxLen
          by -1
        until char (n) <> space.
move n to TrueLen.

(Yes, we put full stops after every statement (well, sentence,
actually...<G>) as there were no scope delimiters...)

[Somewhere else in the program...]
dummy.
    exit.

I remember being stunned by the magnificence of this patent abuse of the
EXIT Statement, and yet it was so simple and elegant and worst of all...it
worked!

I lost no time in trying it on IBM architecture and it worked there too...
(I understand now WHY it will "work" on ANY COBOL platform, but I didn't in
those days...)

I see your refinement of Jimmy's solution (which I'm sorry to say I find
ugly in the extreme, no offense, Jimmy) as a simple variation on the above.

There is no doubt that what Stephen has posted is "better" but, personally,
I still find it ugly...<G>

Why bother with CONTINUE at all? As long as you are removing unnecessary
constructs, take it out. That's what scope delimiters are for! (Well, it is
one of the reasons...<G>) If the scope is already delimited and happens to
have nothing inside it, then the compiler writer doesn't need to be Einstein
to figure that you have incorporated what you want done within the overheads
of the PERFORM...VARYING. Throw out a warning and let  me get on with
it...(That's what Fujitsu does; I realise there are other compilers that
will fall over, and any time now, Bill will probably post to say that the
STANDARD requires SOME kind of construct within the scope of a
PERFORM....<G>. I don't care. I STILL think it is ugly! And that includes
even AFTER you remove that GodAwful CONTINUE!

The most elegant solution is the one using the Fuction Reversed. Thane
showed it to me about 3 years ago and I have used it ever since.

Fujitsu Users (who are extremely well served by their vendor...I hope they
stay in the COBOL game...) can use the elegant function that JerryMouse
mentioned. (Did you see the scathing comments from Fortress COBOL, Jerry?
<G> Let 'em eat their hearts out, I reckon...<G>)

As this is now about the fourth time we have visited ways to find the "True
Length" of a string (Whatever the Hell that means...<G> I suspect different
things to different people and depending on different strings...<G>) I
really didn't intend to post this, but I was so amazed to see the
discussion, and the fact that Jimmy has seen fit to apply to J4 for a
"solution" (Good Luck, Jimmy [tua culpa et al <G>] ),that I just had to add
some more worthless foam to this pointless conversation (Yes, my life is
descending into sadness...<G>).  If you EVER get the "TrueLength" function
you have requested, Jimmy, incorporated into (and published as) a COBOL
Standard, BEFORE 2015, under the auspices of  Alphabet Soup, I would agree
that THAT would be the best solution.

(Not only that... I shall personally present you with the new desktop
computer of your choice, complete with all  the software you want, AND I'll
admit that your above solution is the most beautiful piece of code, in ANY
Programming Language, since "Hello World!". <G>)

" 'Tis a consummation devoutly to be wished..."  - MacBeth





Stephen J Spiro <stephenjspiro@mail.com> wrote in message
news:928495c6.0205162043.677d0d76@posting.google.com...
> "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote
> >
> > This is one that always annoys me, and I haven't found an easy answer.
INSPECT
> > wont do it for you. So, ham-fisted as it is this is what I do :-
> >
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Now an alternative to the above, if you *know* the likelihood is that
there will
> > be more white space than text, (your reference to 984), then do a
standard
> > 'forwards' PERFORM with an increment until you get 'two spaces"
> >

<G>.
> >
>
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…

PS: Some of these challenges can come back to haunt you - Dakota KJ 994 - Huh ?

During latter part of WW2 cocky Montgomery told his American counterparts, Ike,
Bradley etc., that he could, "Achieve this objective by .........". They
politely laughed, to which he responded, "OK If I'm right, you give me my own
aeroplane". Ike gently agreed. Monty won. Being the modest creature that he was,
he asked for his aeroplane. They were bloody FURIOUS - but a bet's a bet. They
gave him above said Dakota.

KJ 994 was its RAF registration number. Sat at RAF Northolt (north of Heathrow)
for years - that's where I saw it. Regularly maintained and serviced by the RAF.
He used it very little, but certainly for trips when he was Supreme Commander
(?) NATO  and when he was British CIGS.

PPS: Any of you American guys thinking Monty was an absolute idiot - you've been
reading too much Stephen Ambrose - recall that it was his own academic peers who
brought Ambrose down with an accusation of plagiarism.

Jimmy

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Correct Length of a string

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-05-20T15:07:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<acb36j$dtf$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<ac0ej0$vg1$01$1@news.t-online.com> <3CE3D754.C21DEE2@shaw.ca> <928495c6.0205162043.677d0d76@posting.google.com> <3ce5997c_2@Usenet.com>`

```

On 17-May-2002, "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
wrote:

> Why bother with CONTINUE at all? As long as you are removing unnecessary
> constructs, take it out. That's what scope delimiters are for! (Well, it
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> even AFTER you remove that GodAwful CONTINUE!

I like being obvious in my code.  The CONTINUE statement makes my intention
obvious.
IF A=B
ELSE
    PERFORM NOT-EQUAL
END-IF

Is still understandable, but an indented CONTINUE is more obvious.
IF A=B
    CONTINUE
ELSE
    PERFORM NOT-EQUAL
END-IF
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Correct Length of a string

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-05-21T12:59:18+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3cea3d34_3@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<ac0ej0$vg1$01$1@news.t-online.com> <3CE3D754.C21DEE2@shaw.ca> <928495c6.0205162043.677d0d76@posting.google.com> <3ce5997c_2@Usenet.com> <acb36j$dtf$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Very good and clear example of what you are saying, Howard.

Comments below,

Pete.
Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:acb36j$dtf$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On 17-May-2002, "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> > one of the reasons...<G>) If the scope is already delimited and happens
to
> > have nothing inside it, then the compiler writer doesn't need to be
> > Einstein
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > will fall over, and any time now, Bill will probably post to say that
the
> > STANDARD requires SOME kind of construct within the scope of a
> > PERFORM....<G>. I don't care. I STILL think it is ugly! And that
includes
> > even AFTER you remove that GodAwful CONTINUE!
>
> I like being obvious in my code.  The CONTINUE statement makes my
intention
> obvious.
> IF A=B
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> END-IF

This is NOT the same thing at all!

There is no IMPLICIT action in your IF statement; there IS in my
PERFORM...VARYING.

If I say:    perform
                   varying J
                       from 1
                          by 1
                     until  some-entry (J) = somedata
               end-perform

There is an action implied, even though the PERFORM scope is vacant. (The
action is to increment J and compare table entries...SOMETHING is being done
because of the intrinsic nature of the PERFORM ...VARYING construct. In
fact, I have used this "overhead" to accomplish my purpose and therefore
need nothing else in the PERFORM scope. So a CONTINUE is superfluous.)

BECAUSE there is an implied action most compilers will compile this and
simply give a warning. I don't know of ANY compilers that will compile your
IF construct without giving an ERROR. (To be fair, I would never code such a
construct so it is possible I just haven't encountered this, and there are
compilers that will compile it correctly and simply generate a NOOP).
Anyway, I agree with you that THIS needs a CONTINUE or, better yet, a
reconstruct:

IF NOT A = B
   PERFORM NOT-EQUAL
END-IF

(Note that, unlike the above, there is no way that I can reconstruct the
PERFORM...VARYING so as to obviate the CONTINUE. This tells me that it
doesn't need CONTINUE, irrespective of how it is constructed.)

I therefore contend that using CONTINUE with the PERFORM does NOT make your
intention any more "obvious". On the contrary, it simply muddies the water.
(think Occam's Razor...less is more <G>).

I would not contend this if there was no scope delimiter for the PERFORM. It
is the job of the scope delimiter to delimit the scope; that scope can be
empty with perfect validity, PROVIDED SOMETHING is being done by the
PERFORM.

For example, I can't imagine you would code:

PERFORM
     CONTINUE
END-PERFORM

because it is every bit as pointless as:

PERFORM
END-PERFORM


I realise that this exchange is unlikely to change either of our minds,
Howard, and that's OK. <G>

At least the opinions have been explored and others can draw their own
conclusions.

Pete.







 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Correct Length of a string

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-05-21T15:13:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<acdnt4$5b$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<ac0ej0$vg1$01$1@news.t-online.com> <3CE3D754.C21DEE2@shaw.ca> <928495c6.0205162043.677d0d76@posting.google.com> <3ce5997c_2@Usenet.com> <acb36j$dtf$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3cea3d34_3@Usenet.com>`

```

On 21-May-2002, "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
wrote:

> There is no IMPLICIT action in your IF statement; there IS in my
> PERFORM...VARYING.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> need nothing else in the PERFORM scope. So a CONTINUE is superfluous.)

Come to think of it, I have done the same thing - but changed it to avoid
the warning.  In this instance CONTINUE is superfluous.  But we agree that
it isn't superfluous in all instances.   So a statement saying it is
superfluous should be qualified.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Correct Length of a string

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-18T06:47:53-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uecd8p3o353a60@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<ac0ej0$vg1$01$1@news.t-online.com> <3CE3D754.C21DEE2@shaw.ca> <928495c6.0205162043.677d0d76@posting.google.com>`

```

Stephen J Spiro <stephenjspiro@mail.com> wrote in message
news:928495c6.0205162043.677d0d76@posting.google.com...
[...]
> But wait! Since the PERFORM VARYING ... UNTIL contains the test, why
> do you test again inside? You can code it this way, and it is MUCH
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> The PERFORM statement does all the work.

The PERFORM fails when MyText is all spaces.

It is far better to write

perform varying n from MaxLength by -1 until n = 0
    if MyText (n:1) not = spaces
        exit perform
    end-if
end-perform

I say "far better" because it is my "sense" from my study of
Formal Methods and "proofs of program correctness", that
limiting the use of constructs in languages may be important
for the purpose of communicating program correctness. In the
above, I limit the use of PERFORM to guarantee that all tests
are within the bounds of the data item, "MyText". I then use
a separate test to terminate the PERFORM early.

While it is possible to achieve the same result by overloading
the termination condition in the PERFORM; it is not necessarily
"better" to do so.
---------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Correct Length of a string

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-05-19T09:48:43+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CE766EB.EDABABB@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<ac0ej0$vg1$01$1@news.t-online.com> <3CE3D754.C21DEE2@shaw.ca> <928495c6.0205162043.677d0d76@posting.google.com> <uecd8p3o353a60@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:
> >
> >      perform varying n from MaxLength by -1 until MyText(n:1) <>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> The PERFORM fails when MyText is all spaces.

     IF ( MyText = SPACES )
         MOVE ZERO       TO n
     ELSE
         PERFORM 
             VARYING n FROM MaxLength BY -1 
               UNTIL MyText(n:1) NOT = SPACE

             CONTINUE
         END-PERFORM
     END-IF

> I say "far better" because it is my "sense" from my study of
> Formal Methods and "proofs of program correctness", that
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> a separate test to terminate the PERFORM early.

Whatever. Just shows what too much book reading does to ya.

> While it is possible to achieve the same result by overloading
> the termination condition in the PERFORM; it is not necessarily
> "better" to do so.

Whatever.  I just make it work 100% correctly using current tools at
hand. I say mine is 'far better' because it doesn't need all that
'Formal Methods' and "proof of program correctness" jargon to see why it
_is_ better.   :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Correct Length of a string

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Edward Reid <edward@paleo.org>
- **Date:** 2002-05-18T21:37:27-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.B90C7A1700491EF510D31090@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<ac0ej0$vg1$01$1@news.t-online.com> <3CE3D754.C21DEE2@shaw.ca> <928495c6.0205162043.677d0d76@posting.google.com> <uecd8p3o353a60@corp.supernews.com> <3CE766EB.EDABABB@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
On Sun, 19 May 2002 4:48:43 -0400, Richard Plinston wrote
> Whatever.  I just make it work 100% correctly using current tools at
> hand. I say mine is 'far better' because it doesn't need all that
> 'Formal Methods' and "proof of program correctness" jargon to see why it
> _is_ better.   :-)

Neither did Rick's. He simply used that as additional supporting 
evidence. He "limited the use of constructs"; so did you (to 
alternation and repetition). He "guaranteed that all tests are within 
the bounds"; so did you, in a different way. Noticing these 
similarities helps us understand why both of these code snippets are 
better than some others.

One programmer accusing another of using jargon? Isn't that the pot 
calling the kettle black?

Formal methods and proofs of correctness indeed have not reached into 
real world programming beyond some projects with extremely high 
reliability requirements. That doesn't mean those studies had no effect 
on your programming. Indeed, the whole concept that one should write in 
a clear, simple, direct style is an integral part of that movement. 
Those attributes are important to formal methods because it's so 
difficult to treat programming languages with formal methods. But then 
people said hmm, if it's so hard to describe with formal methods, maybe 
it's kind of hard for the human brain too ...

Indeed, what we now agree are easily understood constructs are exactly 
those selected for formal treatment because of their manageability. 
Your example uses alternation and repetition, which are manageable 
formally, and -- funny thing -- also relatively easy for the human mind 
to grasp.

Additional supporting evidence is ALWAYS welcome in trying to determine 
if a program works correctly. Or should be -- those who don't welcome 
it don't make good programmers. Certainly formal methods can't do it 
all. Neither can testing: as Dijkstra eloquently pointed out, testing 
can only show the presence of errors, not the absence. Those two 
together aren't enough either, so we add all the psychological methods 
we can, to enable us to connect the source code with the problem 
description. And it still isn't enough, obviously, since we still end 
up with bugs in production due to a variety of failings.

To make matters even more complicated, people aren't all alike -- our 
minds differ. Duh? Yet some people seem to forget this, and think that 
one way of writing a program is an absolute best. There's no such 
thing, because it's critically important that PEOPLE be able to 
understand programs, and people differ.

It's difficult for us now to remember what the entire psychological 
mindset of programming was 30 to 40 years ago. Indeed, for those who 
learned programming since 1980, I think it's very difficult to 
understand that what they learned in classes simply wasn't known 20 
years previously. I started in 1966, and I wrote plenty of GO TOs in 
FORTRAN and branches in assembler. By the time I started COBOL in 1972, 
I had a bit of a glimmer of where structured programming was leading, 
and my code was better. But on the occasions when I see code I wrote 25 
years ago, I'm still often appalled. Sometimes appalled by code I wrote 
yesterday too, but not as often.

The world of programming will continue changing. And both academic 
study and real world experience will continue to shape it.

Edward Reid
```

It is not clear, to me, whether your code snippet was designed
to be correct or was patched because it failed, in production,
when an occurence of "MyText" contained all spaces.

If it is the former, how does the code communicate that

If it was the latter, why should I trust any code you have ever

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Correct Length of a string

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-05-20T08:08:18+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CE8A0E2.BFB457E0@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<ac0ej0$vg1$01$1@news.t-online.com> <3CE3D754.C21DEE2@shaw.ca> <928495c6.0205162043.677d0d76@posting.google.com> <uecd8p3o353a60@corp.supernews.com> <3CE766EB.EDABABB@Azonic.co.nz> <uefpa49jkjve31@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:
> 
> It is not clear, to me, whether your code snippet was designed
> to be correct or was patched because it failed, in production,
> when an occurence of "MyText" contained all spaces.

No. And nor would any code unless it contained comments to the effect
that it has patched until it works (mostly).

> If it is the former, how does the code communicate that
> "correctness" was intentional?

Any code can be the result of several bodged efforts and then be
prettied up in such a way that the final code _looks_ like it was
designed that way from the start.

One of the ways that correctness is communicated is through trusted
idioms.  Most languages are idiomatic in that there are standard ways of
constructing frequently used mechanisms.  Cobol suffers in this respect
because it started with assembler/spagetti/flow chart idioms and these
are still engrained in many developers as 'site standards'.  As the
language has developed newer idioms have been added: first 'structured
programming' using Ans74 then later with Ans85 allowing different
constructs.  'Book readers' (not a derogitory term) and those taught in
other languages have added even more idiomatic ways of coding, some of
which are quite inappropriate.

This history has led to a confusing array of code, most of which cannot
be trusted by glancing at it, because it is not recognised by the reader
as a familiar idiom.

In other languages such as C, though a Cobol programmers may find it to
be unreadable, other C programmers can usually work out quickly what the
code is doing by recognising standard ways of coding.  This of course
assumes it was written by a C coder.  A Cobol programmer trying to write
in C may use Cobol idioms and then no-one would be able to understand
it.

>> I say "far better" because it is my "sense" from my study of
>> Formal Methods and "proofs of program correctness", that
>> limiting the use of constructs in languages may be important
>> for the purpose of communicating program correctness.

It is.  But each Cobol programmer or site has chosen a different
'limited use', some did so several decades ago, some are archaic, most
of them I would hate. This 'limited use' only communicates correctness
if it is the +same+ limited use that the readers uses.  ie I trust what
I recognise.

I dropped SECTIONs, THRU and GOTO ~-Exit style many years ago because it
was extremely difficult to viually 'prove' correctness, and hard to
recognise non-correctness.  ie it was too labour intensive.  Some have
written special checkers and imposed even more restrictions to
compensate.

And then there is Foodman. He clearly recognises his own code as being
correct directly because of his own 'limited use'.  Unfortunately is it
not an idiom that is used by anyone else* so it is just a confusing mess
to most.  He also has difficulty recognising what others see as normal
code, due primarily to his isolation from it.

Idiomatic 'Limited use of constructs' is _vital_ to reognition as 'proof
of correctness', but they must be _my_ idioms.

> If it was the latter, why should I trust any code you have ever
> written? <G>

Actually I never trust _any_ code that I have not written until I have
verified that it behaves correctly, and then I don't trust it.  I would
expect the same courtesy for my code.
 
> All my "book reading" raises questions; not answers!

Yes, but everyone needs to read the same books, and then draw the same
conclusions.



Footnote *: Actually an associate of mine wrote code similar to
Foodman's for many years.  He had been on the first outsider's Leo
programming course and had worked with many odd machines in the 50 and
60s in several assembler and macro languages.  He moved to Cobol but
used constructs developed over a couple of decades that he knew worked. 
Some of that code is still in productive use and I work on it from time
to time but I have developed techniques for converting it to my 'better'
idioms as I go.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Correct Length of a string

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Edward Reid <edward@paleo.org>
- **Date:** 2002-05-19T19:00:10-04:00

- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.B90DA6BA008FBC9210D4F580@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<ac0ej0$vg1$01$1@news.t-online.com> <3CE3D754.C21DEE2@shaw.ca> <928495c6.0205162043.677d0d76@posting.google.com> <uecd8p3o353a60@corp.supernews.com> <3CE766EB.EDABABB@Azonic.co.nz> <uefpa49jkjve31@corp.supernews.com> <3CE8A0E2.BFB457E0@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
On Mon, 20 May 2002 3:08:18 -0400, Richard Plinston wrote
> Actually I never trust _any_ code that I have not written until I have
> verified that it behaves correctly, and then I don't trust it.  I would
> expect the same courtesy for my code.

I don't trust code I've written either, until I've verified that it 
behaves correctly, and then I don't trust it.

In other words, I *apply* the same courtesy to my own code.

Reliable programming requires a lot of self-doubt and self-examination.

Edward Reid
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Correct Length of a string

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Edward Reid <edward@paleo.org>
- **Date:** 2002-05-20T20:28:54-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.B90F0D0600E3E76710D31090@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<ac0ej0$vg1$01$1@news.t-online.com> <3CE3D754.C21DEE2@shaw.ca> <928495c6.0205162043.677d0d76@posting.google.com> <uecd8p3o353a60@corp.supernews.com> <3CE766EB.EDABABB@Azonic.co.nz> <uefpa49jkjve31@corp.supernews.com> <3CE8A0E2.BFB457E0@Azonic.co.nz> <01HW.B90DA6BA008FBC9210D4F580@news-east.usenetserver.com> <3ce83b5e_3@Usenet.com>`

```
On Sun, 19 May 2002 19:52:12 -0400, Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote
> Er... I doubt it, that is, I don't think so... or maybe...perhaps it
> does...could it?...um...that is to say....not sure... <G>

;-)

>> Reliable programming requires a lot of self-doubt and self-examination.
> PS.  Edward, could we make the above apply only to program code and not to
> people?

Sure, Pete ... total agreement here.

> Self-doubt will never improve the quality of anyone's code one iota.

By self-doubt, I mean doubting one's own code. No need to doubt your 
programming ability (much less anything more personal), but always 
doubt your code. (Same for self-examination: it's the code.)

Doubting your own code *will* improve its quality, for the same reasons 
that having others doubt your code will improve it.

As well, doubting your programming ability will lead to improvements in 
your programming ability. Indirectly this will eventually improve your 
code. But that's not what I was referring to, and Pete picked up my 
point accurately: doubt your own code.

On Mon, 20 May 2002 7:27:33 -0400, Michael Mattias wrote
> Me, once I have tested code, it goes in the same category as "the compiler"-
> that is, previously tested code is in the "last place I would look" for any
> aberrations.

I have found many bugs in well-tested code -- surely you have too. This 
means even code I've tested myself. (I don't really have the patience 
to be a top-notch tester.)

I've found many bugs in compilers, though certainly not nearly as often 
as in custom code.

No, these aren't the first places I look, not by a long shot. But I 
maintain a healthy skepticism toward all code.

Many years ago I developed this guiding principle:

: When you've looked everywhere the problem can possibly be,
: then start looking for it where it cannot possibly be,
: because that's where it must be.

This has served me well.

Edward Reid
```

Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3CE8A0E2.BFB457E0@Azonic.co.nz...
> Rick Smith wrote:
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Dammit Richard! You rumbled me...<G>

I believe your quote should be placed on the wall as an inspiration to all
cowboys...Yippeekiiiyayyy!!

>
> In other languages such as C, though a Cobol programmers may find it to
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

While I believe your point regarding recognition of idioms engendering
trust, I do believe that SOME languages are SO counter intuitive that they
contain no recognisable idiom...<G>

APL comes to mind.

I have watched with dismay an APL expert sit down and write 7 lines of code
in fewer minutes, that did EVERYTHING my laborious 3 hour COBOL effort took
400 lines to do...<G> It is truly frightening... Just as I had decided that
I had better start learning APL, another APL guy came in, looked at the
code, shook his head, and walked out... This restored my confidence that if
code is going to be maintained by OTHERS you had damn well better make it
intelligible...<G>

I believe you have hit an important point here, Richard, in that the
RECOGNITION of patterns (idioms, if you like) is extremely important in
deciding how much confidence people have in the code. It may well be that
this is what Programmers mean when we talk about "good" and "bad" code. (It
has always amused me when people talk about code as good or bad; "good" and
"bad" are moral terms, code is either correct and easily maintainable or it
isn't...<G>)

>
> I dropped SECTIONs, THRU and GOTO ~-Exit style many years ago because it
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Yes, this one has been thrashed to death. I do use SECTIONS but I use them
carefully and within the spirit of the COBOL Language:
Division>Section>Paragraph>Sentence>Statement. This has always made sense to
me, but I totally respect the right of others to do it differently. I have
maintained all kinds of code in all kinds of idioms in all kinds of
languages. It is not a problem. That's why I call myself a programmer <G>.

> And then there is Foodman. He clearly recognises his own code as being
> correct directly because of his own 'limited use'.  Unfortunately is it
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Well, you waved the red rag again so I have to respond...<G> (Besides, you
are letting him off far too easily...)

He has difficulty with other people's code not just because he recognises
only his own idiom, but also because he is a reactionary jerk who hasn't got
the imagination or intelligence to even see merit in other ways of doing
things, or to be stimulated or interested by new stuff.

A mean-spirited, mean little man, living in a mean little bubble, enforcing
his own mean little laws on the poor sods who work for him.

Yeah, I know... I should let it go and get over it...<G> I am trying...<G>

> Idiomatic 'Limited use of constructs' is _vital_ to reognition as 'proof
> of correctness', but they must be _my_ idioms.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> expect the same courtesy for my code.

This last is excellent. There IS only code that "hasn't crashed yet".

Pete.

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>

Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3CE8A0E2.BFB457E0@Azonic.co.nz...
[...]
> Idiomatic 'Limited use of constructs' is _vital_ to reognition as 'proof
> of correctness', but they must be _my_ idioms.

Wow! Your response was so good I forgot, for several hours,
my earlier comments and why I made them. Thank you!

One of the areas, about which I have given a great deal
of thought, is adherrence to specifications; that is, how to
write code that matches, as closely as possible, a
specification. It seems a natural extension to write the code
in such a way as to show, in some fashion, that the coder
has considered "correctness" in the writing of the code.

In the earlier example, the solution required traversal across
a bounded array of characters; therefore, the coder should
show that boundaries will not be passed during the traversal.

After considering your comments, your earlier comment,
"Whatever," is clear to me, now. I had become fixated on
something that is probably not achievable and may not be
worthwhile.
```

```

Rick Smith <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
news:uejgkn29ttpf5@corp.supernews.com...
>
> One of the areas, about which I have given a great deal
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Ha! This is one of the major flaws in "Waterfall" development.

Someone "translates" their perception of someone else's requirement into a
Specification, which is then implemented in code by someone else again, over
a period that is often months or even years, and both these guys are then
surprised when the original person says: "Er...not quite like that..." Or,
"Good, but it just needs this one small change.." <G>

You can remove the need for a development program specification altogether,
if you simply use an iterative prototyping workshop. Here the User sits with
the programmer and they both produce a screen or form (prototype) that
BECOMES (through iteration), exactly what the Business requires. Everybody
is happy and the kudos are equally shared. And it took one third of the time
it would have done if you had investigated it, specced it, programmed it,
tested it, then UATed it... It's called RAD (Rapid Application Development)
EVERYBODY who has a stake in the end product (IT and the Business) gets to
work together on the production of it.

"Fine for input screens and displays and reports", you say (well, some of
you do...<G>) "But who specs the actual system flows? what about the updates
and stuff?".

This is also built by iteration  (but this time with Operations involved if
it requires mainframe or batch processing) or maybe just within the IT
department. Yes, an overall Design must be produced by the people qualified
to do it, but it doesn't have to be PERFECT on day one, and it doesn't mean
that no other work can proceed until it is formally signed off! In fact, the
design process is also iterated, with a better design emerging from each
round.

This is a dynamic, flexible approach to EVOLVING a system, rather than just
building it.

And when it is complete you can use automation to write the specs...

Then you NEVER have to sweat how close your code matched the spec. because
it is ALWAYS 100% <G>

Pete.



 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Correct Length of a string

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-21T11:03:54-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ueko6qlqbtm68@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<ac0ej0$vg1$01$1@news.t-online.com> <3CE3D754.C21DEE2@shaw.ca> <928495c6.0205162043.677d0d76@posting.google.com> <uecd8p3o353a60@corp.supernews.com> <3CE766EB.EDABABB@Azonic.co.nz> <uefpa49jkjve31@corp.supernews.com> <3CE8A0E2.BFB457E0@Azonic.co.nz> <uejgkn29ttpf5@corp.supernews.com> <3cea3d37_3@Usenet.com>`

```

Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3cea3d37_3@Usenet.com...
>
> Rick Smith <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Ha! This is one of the major flaws in "Waterfall" development.
[...]
> And when it is complete you can use automation to write the specs...
>
> Then you NEVER have to sweat how close your code matched the spec. because
> it is ALWAYS 100% <G>

Most systems must comply with law, standards, de facto
standards, and both external and internal requirements.
One takes the documents which specify compliance and
translates the content of those documents into specifications.
The specifications are then translated into code which matches,
as closely as possible, those specifications. The end result is
code which complies with law, standards, de facto standards,
and both external and internal requirements.

RAD is of no help in setting the specifications for sales tax,
income tax, government payroll reporting, EFT, EDI, etc.
These are set external to the system and, often, external to
the organization.

Furthermore, having specifications which reference the
appropriate parts of documents, with which the system
must comply, makes it easier to amend the specifications
when those other documents change. The closer the code
matches the specifications; the easier the update to the code.

Nothing I wrote should have been construed to infer any
particular development methodology; nor did I imply, by any
reference, complete, system-wide specifications.
```
