###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Correct Length of a string

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2002-05-21T15:30:39-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ace78v$nib$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<ac0ej0$vg1$01$1@news.t-online.com> <3CE8A0E2.BFB457E0@Azonic.co.nz> <uejgkn29ttpf5@corp.supernews.com> <3cea3d37_3@Usenet.com>`

```
In article <3cea3d37_3@Usenet.com>,
Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
>Rick Smith <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>"Good, but it just needs this one small change.." <G>

The ever-popular 'I know it's what I told you but it's not what I WANT'... 
and wants have a nasty habit of changing.  Consider:

Coder: 'Here's what you asked for.'

User: 'Not bad... but can you include (feature)?'

(note that this is a Very Polite user... not the ones who whine 'But it 
doesn't do *(function)*, how can you expect me to do anything with a 
program that doesn't do *(function)*?')

Coder: 'All right... here's what you asked for, with (feature).'

User: 'That's... all right, but it'd be really nice if it had (feature1).'

Coder: 'All right, here's what you asked for, with (feature) and 
(feature1).'

User: 'Ummmmm... can you get it to do (feature2a)?'

Coder: 'All right... here's what you asked for, with (feature) and 
(feature1) and (feature2a).'

User: 'Wow, that's *really* cramped and complex... can't you make it, you 
know, a little easier to use?'

The textbooks call this 'feature bloat'.

>
>You can remove the need for a development program specification altogether,
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>work together on the production of it.

With all due respect, Mr Dashwood... this assumes a workplace populated by 
motivated workers who have the same goal of Actually Getting Work Done.  
In my experience such places are rare; they violate Rule #0 of 'Nobody 
Wants To Do Any Work'.

A spec document can be seen as a contract between the requesting party and 
the coder: 'I give you a program that does *this* and then we both move on 
with out jobs.'

[snip]

>This is a dynamic, flexible approach to EVOLVING a system, rather than just
>building it.

... and if people become more enamored of the process - and some folks 
actually prefer developing a system to maintaining or using it, imagine 
that! - than they are of using the result of the process to actually 
Do Their Jobs... then the process is never Done.

How can this be bad?  Consider the process which results in printing the 
bills which are sent to the customers who pay the company money; if the 
process is never Done then the bills are never sent.

('Y'know, after we got done yesterday I got to thinking... maybe it'd be 
better to have the company logo left-justified, instead of centered... and 
can you put the motto in italics?  And that detail-line layout we did, 
that just isn't working for me... maybe you oughta bring back the one we 
did two weeks ago and see how that works...')

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Correct Length of a string

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-05-22T00:28:12+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3cead7c5_3@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<ac0ej0$vg1$01$1@news.t-online.com> <3CE8A0E2.BFB457E0@Azonic.co.nz> <uejgkn29ttpf5@corp.supernews.com> <3cea3d37_3@Usenet.com> <ace78v$nib$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
Good post, Doc!

Comments below...
<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:ace78v$nib$1@panix1.panix.com...
> >
> >Someone "translates" their perception of someone else's requirement into
a
> >Specification, which is then implemented in code by someone else again,
over
> >a period that is often months or even years, and both these guys are then
> >surprised when the original person says: "Er...not quite like that..."
Or,
> >"Good, but it just needs this one small change.." <G>
>
> The ever-popular 'I know it's what I told you but it's not what I WANT'...
> and wants have a nasty habit of changing.  Consider:
>
There are a number of very complex Human problems at work here, and I cannot
pretend that RAD solves them all.

IMO, it simply solves them better than the traditional Waterfall.

Yes Doc, Users DO change their minds. Sometimes it is because they are slow
on the uptake, sometimes because the Business itself is in a state of flux,
sometimes because they have been pushed into the front line by their
Management (just as Programmers often are...) without having had time to
think about it.

But sometimes programmers are surly and hostile. Sometimes they are
supercilious, despising the fact that the User really doesn't grasp the
simplest technology.  This kind of behaviour will not bring out the best in
a User (or anybody for that matter) and the result is that a nervous User
forgets points to include in the spec. Then he is faced with having to
request a change and sometimes they'll just fudge the Business Process
rather than do that... (I know a certain Dwarf who did exactly this
recently...rather than deal with the Bureaucracy required to get new tables
into Production...no names but I'm sure you'll sympathise with the short
person concerned (as indeed, do I <G>)).

And it can also be argued that if programmers made more effort to understand
the Business Process they could HELP the User to predict what is likely to
be required beyond the exact statement of the spec. (If Programmers had
imagination, that is...<G>.)

So there are faults on both sides.

However the Waterfall simply allows the Programmer to be "right".

"I did what it said."

OK, you did what it said. You didn't think about it, consider it, wonder
what was trying to be achieved and maybe put a few "hooks" in there for
future reference...just did what it said. Certainly can't be wrong. Arse is
completely covered.

But has it helped the Business?

Isn't there a ring of "I vas only followink orders..." about this?

When Users and Programmers work together in a Workshop (at least in MY
workshops...) there is an atmosphere of "can do" and everyone is intent on
ensuring that the Business requirements are met. It is a "blameless"
culture. Try it out, get it wrong, fix it, Ah! that's better! Now what else
do we need this bit to do...

I have taken certain liberties with your scenario below. I agree that it is
accurate and all-too-often reflects what really happens. However, I believe
it doesn't HAVE to be like that...

Here's another Reality...<G> [my stuff added in square brackets]

> Coder: 'Here's what you asked for.' [BTW, I was thinking about the way we
handle wugglepuffs in the Business Process and I can't get a feel for where
the floggletoggle gets input. Can you 'splain me, please?]

[User explains then looks at Coder's solution]
>
> User: 'Not bad... but can you include (feature)?' [See, there is an
exception condition that I forgot about where the floggletoggle can be
candied... and we need to make sure the Client isn't Diabetic]
>
> (note that this is a Very Polite user... not the ones who whine 'But it
> doesn't do *(function)*, how can you expect me to do anything with a
> program that doesn't do *(function)*?') [Absolutely! There is no place for
bad manners on either side; after all, we're on the same Team, right <G>? I
would have a quiet chat to Users who behaved like this and if it didn't
improve they'd be replaced on the Project Team...]
>
> Coder: 'All right... here's what you asked for, with (feature).' [Now it
caters for Diabetics. Are there any other blood groups or medical conditions
we might need to be aware of?]

At this point MY Reality diverges from the DOC's... Those of you who are
into Quantum Mechanics will realise that this is a perfectly natural and
acceptable way for the Universe to behave. As we are now in a different
Reality branch I'll drop the square brackets...

User:  "Oops! There's the Haemophiliacs, now you mention it..."

Coder <coding furiously>: "OK, I've included hypo and hyper glycaemics
too... in fact I've built a table of exceptions and we can just add any
others we think of..."

User: "Wow! Cool! Thanks...".

Later that day a conversation takes place between the User and his
Management...

Manager: "Hows the development of the new Wugglepuff Process going?"

User: "Well, IT seem to have a real good grasp of it and this morning we
sorted the Candied Floggletoggle problem."

Manager: "Excellent! So you're getting good support then?"

User: "Yep! We work well together and I'm even starting to pick up a bit
about computers..."

Meanwhile, back in DOC's Reality...
>
> User: 'That's... all right, but it'd be really nice if it had (feature1).'
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
The Universe can (and does) support both of these Realities (along with an
unknown number of others) simultaneously (compare with Schroedinger's
pussy).

It is entirely up to each of us to decide which one we will "live" in.

Make your choice and collapse the probability wave into solid personal
Reality (open the box and check the cat). You can do it. It just needs some
mental adjustment...<G>



> The textbooks call this 'feature bloat'.
>

Doc, you are reading the wrong textbooks...<G>  It is also referred to as
"Scope Creep". Whether we use Waterfall or RAD it has to be managed. The
different Methodologies manage it in different ways, that's all.
> >
> >You can remove the need for a development program specification
altogether,
> >if you simply use an iterative prototyping workshop. Here the User sits
with
> >the programmer and they both produce a screen or form (prototype) that
> >BECOMES (through iteration), exactly what the Business requires.
Everybody
> >is happy and the kudos are equally shared. And it took one third of the
time
> >it would have done if you had investigated it, specced it, programmed it,
> >tested it, then UATed it... It's called RAD (Rapid Application
Development)
> >EVERYBODY who has a stake in the end product (IT and the Business) gets
to
> >work together on the production of it.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
As I get older people keep giving me respect...(not that I don't like
it...<G>) it's just that I wish they did out of respect for my arguments and
positions, rather than the increasing veneration of my bones...<g>. Please
feel free to treat me with the same contempt you treat everybody else, Doc
<G>

You are right of course. My argument IS predicated on "a workplace populated
by motivated workers who have the same goal of Actually Getting Work Done. "
Not just "Actually Getting Work Done", but improving the position of "The
Company" in the (perhaps futile...<G>) desire that increased productivity
will actually trickle back to the Workers in some way (at the very least by
ensuring there is a job to go to next year...). And it should be fun,
too...<G>

While I accept that such places may be rare in your experience, Doc, is that
a reason to abandon all hope of creating them? I believe it is the job of
Management to ensure that we all get a fun-filled satisfying workplace. I
certainly do my best to achieve this.


> A spec document can be seen as a contract between the requesting party and
> the coder: 'I give you a program that does *this* and then we both move on
> with out jobs.'
>
Hahaha! I think that should be "our jobs" but it makes sense as it
stands...<G> (Freudian slip...?)

I am not minimizing the place of the contract in Business. I am saying that
the SPIRIT of the contract should be different. A contract (or spec. in this
instance) should NOT just be a document that covers one party's arse and
makes them right, at the expense of the other party. It should be general
enough to establish a direction and lay the ground for both parties to work
together in that direction.

Personally, I prefer no formal spec. at all. Spend the time in DOING it
rather than TALKING (or in this case, writing) about it.But then, I have a
high tolerance to leaving my arse uncovered; if you're making a frontal
attack, you are unlikely to be shot in the arse...<G>

> [snip]
>
> >This is a dynamic, flexible approach to EVOLVING a system, rather than
just
> >building it.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

That's why we timebox the iterations and say: "OK, that's it. Here's the
best we can do with the time and resources available...and by the way, all
the ESSENTIAL functionality is in there, but some of the Bells and Whistles
aren't."

As it has been developed in close co-operation with the User department, who
set the priorities when it became apparent that not everything could be
achieved, there is never any moaning from the Users. (Compare this with
systems developed by Waterfall...)

> How can this be bad?  Consider the process which results in printing the
> bills which are sent to the customers who pay the company money; if the
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Wicked, Doc!  I loved that last paragraph.  You will always have Users who
want change for the sake of it, just as there are certain Humans who can't
walk past a shoe shop (despite having several hundred pairs of shoes,
usually worn once...and I am not talking about the Imelda Marcoses of this
World...) These Users have to be constrained and that is all there is to
it... (waving scissors over her credit card works for me...for a while at
least...) Tell them that all "enhancements" must be paid for now the project
is wound down. If they do this while the Project is running, point out to
them that doing these changes may require some other feature to fall out of
the timebox, so they better think about it carefully and prioritize.

Personally, I have no problem with doing whatever frivoulous changes they
want. It is their money and I am their servant.

Pete.




 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services

                http://www.usenet.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Correct Length of a string

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2002-05-22T09:38:16-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<acg708$ohk$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<ac0ej0$vg1$01$1@news.t-online.com> <3cea3d37_3@Usenet.com> <ace78v$nib$1@panix1.panix.com> <3cead7c5_3@Usenet.com>`

```
In article <3cead7c5_3@Usenet.com>,
Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>Good post, Doc!

Pfoo... you'se jes' easily impressed.

>
>Comments below...
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>IMO, it simply solves them better than the traditional Waterfall.

'Better' is, of course, based on the criteria one uses to evaluate The 
Good (gotta love that Platonic terminology); if a part of The Good is 
having a readily-traceable process by which departments can be held 
accountable for budget-expenditures then RAD might fall short of more 
traditional methods.

>
>Yes Doc, Users DO change their minds. Sometimes it is because they are slow
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>think about it.

These are a few reasons, of course.

>
>But sometimes programmers are surly and hostile. Sometimes they are
>supercilious, despising the fact that the User really doesn't grasp the
>simplest technology.

These kinds of attitudes work both ways, Mr Dashwood... or have you never 
met a manager who states with pride that 'I'm not technical'?

>This kind of behaviour will not bring out the best in
>a User (or anybody for that matter) and the result is that a nervous User
>forgets points to include in the spec. Then he is faced with having to
>request a change and sometimes they'll just fudge the Business Process
>rather than do that...

This 'nervousness' is not the only reason that things are 'forgotten'... 
but whatever the cause the RAD process does not allow for accountability.  
When a user states 'Things are late because the programmer isn't giving me 
what I asked for' then a traditional spec allows a programmer to say 'This 
is what you asked for, here's what I gave you... where is the 
discrepancy?'

>(I know a certain Dwarf who did exactly this
>recently...rather than deal with the Bureaucracy required to get new tables
>into Production...no names but I'm sure you'll sympathise with the short
>person concerned (as indeed, do I <G>)).

This was not because of nervousness or forgetfulness, Mr Dashwood... this 
was because a certain Dwarf wanted to spend time Getting His Job Done 
instead of dealing with a Bureaucracy.

>
>And it can also be argued that if programmers made more effort to understand
>the Business Process they could HELP the User to predict what is likely to
>be required beyond the exact statement of the spec. (If Programmers had
>imagination, that is...<G>.)

... and it can be argued in the other direction, as well, and that if a 
user made more effort to understand the company's various systems then 
said user might be able to predict what is likely to be required to 
accomplish their requests.

>
>So there are faults on both sides.

Hence the onus is not to be placed on either.

>
>However the Waterfall simply allows the Programmer to be "right".
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>But has it helped the Business?

Insofar as it has accomplished the expressly stated desires of the users, 
yes.

>
>Isn't there a ring of "I vas only followink orders..." about this?

Not to my ears, no... there is the ring of 'I know my job, you know your 
job, we work together, each with our own knowledge'.

>
>When Users and Programmers work together in a Workshop (at least in MY
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>do we need this bit to do...

As stated before, your workshops might just possibly (note the 
subjunctive) not reflect my own experiences, limited as they may be.

>
>I have taken certain liberties with your scenario below. I agree that it is
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>improve they'd be replaced on the Project Team...]

... yea and verily, even unto the user who sits in a corner office and 
signs the checks associated with the project?  You know... the one who 
says 'Look, I'm Very Busy right now... just do what I told you to do and 
then we'll see where you made a mistake... because I, of course, *never* 
do'.

>>
>> Coder: 'All right... here's what you asked for, with (feature).' [Now it
…[46 more quoted lines elided]…
>pussy).

Mr Dashwood, if you wish to bring in such delightful constructs then it 
seems you've neglected a prior one... remember Heisenberg?

'How's the project moving along?'

'Really fast!'

'Good... where are you at in it?'

'Can't say... the ability to distinguish location precisely is inversely 
related to the ability to distinguish velocity precisely.'

>
>It is entirely up to each of us to decide which one we will "live" in.

Mr Dashwood, with all due respect... by that kind of reasoning it is easy 
to find a job in the headquarters of a multinational corporation with 
annual turnover greater than that of Proctor & Gamble where only Esperanto 
is spoken.

>
>Make your choice and collapse the probability wave into solid personal
>Reality (open the box and check the cat). You can do it. It just needs some
>mental adjustment...<G>

Pharmaceutical?

>
>
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
><G>

That is possible... note the 'due' (in the sense of 'appropriate')

>
>You are right of course. My argument IS predicated on "a workplace populated
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>too...<G>

... and hats!  Don't forget the hats, everyone gets to wear a nice hat... 
and all enjoyable things are *not* illegal, immoral or fattening... and I 
should be the King of England.

>
>While I accept that such places may be rare in your experience, Doc, is that
>a reason to abandon all hope of creating them? I believe it is the job of
>Management to ensure that we all get a fun-filled satisfying workplace. I
>certainly do my best to achieve this.

Mr Dashwood, your beliefs seem to run... in a different channel than those 
of, say, those who believe that 'The job of Management is the allocation, 
co-ordination and motivation of personnel and resources towards the 
accomplishment of a stated Executive goal.'

>
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>stands...<G> (Freudian slip...?)

Fraudulent typo, definitely!

>
>I am not minimizing the place of the contract in Business. I am saying that
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>together in that direction.

The emphasis here is on *together*... not one party assuming the 
knowledges, competences or responsibilites of another party... at least 
not without assuming the salary that goes along with exercising them, no.

>
>Personally, I prefer no formal spec. at all. Spend the time in DOING it
>rather than TALKING (or in this case, writing) about it.But then, I have a
>high tolerance to leaving my arse uncovered; if you're making a frontal
>attack, you are unlikely to be shot in the arse...<G>

Only if your opponent arrays forces incompetently, Mr Dashwood... a 
competent strategist allows for such possibilities and prepares to take 
advantage of them.

>
>> [snip]
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>aren't."

... and ESSENTIAL (caps original) is differentiated from 'Bells and 
Whistles' (caps original) by... whom?

>
>As it has been developed in close co-operation with the User department, who
>set the priorities when it became apparent that not everything could be
>achieved, there is never any moaning from the Users. (Compare this with

'... never any moaning from the Users'?  Pharmaceitucal reality, indeed!

>

…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>the timebox, so they better think about it carefully and prioritize.

... and all of this differs from telling them 'Sorry, it wasn't in the

>
>Personally, I have no problem with doing whatever frivoulous changes they
>want. It is their money and I am their servant.

There we differ, again... I am not a servant, I am a consultant; I do not 
work *for* people, I work *with* people.

Part of working *with* people is delineating the bits of 'you do that, I 
do this'... or so I've found.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Correct Length of a string

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-05-22T17:40:33+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3cebca9a_7@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<ac0ej0$vg1$01$1@news.t-online.com> <3cea3d37_3@Usenet.com> <ace78v$nib$1@panix1.panix.com> <3cead7c5_3@Usenet.com> <acg708$ohk$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
Good debate, Doc...

I guess everyone will draw their own conclusions.

I don't think you are "wrong" and I agree that what you describe is the sad
reality in many places. I just feel it shouldn't be that way and I do
everything I can to ensure it isn't, when I'm managing projects or working
with development teams.

A few quick responses below...

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:acg708$ohk$1@panix1.panix.com...
>
> 'Better' is, of course, based on the criteria one uses to evaluate The
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Unfair! RAD projects are just as accountable budget wise as any other
project. The Business Sponsor monitors expenditure carefully and ensures
that it stays within bounds. The RAD PM reports weekly expenditure against
plan.

>
> These kinds of attitudes work both ways, Mr Dashwood... or have you never
> met a manager who states with pride that 'I'm not technical'?
>
I have met such Managers and I am not irritated by them. I enjoy explaining
what we're doing to them in simple terms that relate to the Business
Process. I usually learn a lot about Business Processes from such Managers,
and I can think of at least one occasion where such a Manager made an
invaluable input that changed the technical architecture of a system. He
didn't need to be technical, all he needed to do was trigger it by asking a
"stupid" question...


> This 'nervousness' is not the only reason that things are 'forgotten'...
> but whatever the cause the RAD process does not allow for accountability.

You keep saying this, but that doesn't make it true...

There is Accountability. It goes with Empowerment. The difference is that
the Team is accountable, rather than the person who wrote the spec. or the
person who programmed it. Just because it is a "no blame" culture where
people are free to get it "wrong" before they get it "right", does NOT mean
there is no accountability.

What does YOUR Accountability buy you anyway?  So the Programmer says he did
what the spec. said. We are no further ahead. The situation is not improved
because the Programmer is blameless. Whatever was done, it is still "wrong".
Arguing about whose fault it was is a pointless waste of time. It needs
fixing.

So, are you saying that the Programmer will do a better job on the new
revised spec.? Nope, he did his best on the first one. Will the spec. writer
do a better job? Maybe, but it is a fair bet he also did his best on the
first attempt. NOTHING has been gained by assigning fault. All that has been
achieved is a "distancing" between the two parties which is extremely
counter productive in the long run.

> When a user states 'Things are late because the programmer isn't giving me
> what I asked for' then a traditional spec allows a programmer to say 'This
> is what you asked for, here's what I gave you... where is the
> discrepancy?'

Well, RAD Users don't say that because they are involved, and carry the same
responsibility as all the other project team Agents carry. This is a typical
arse cover comment, which is spawned directly by the Waterfall culture... If
the guy didn't feel under attack he wouldn't be trying to defend himself.

> >
> >And it can also be argued that if programmers made more effort to
understand
> >the Business Process they could HELP the User to predict what is likely
to
> >be required beyond the exact statement of the spec. (If Programmers had
> >imagination, that is...<G>.)
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Yes, and one of the spin offs from RAD projects is that some technophobic
Users actually come to realise that computers don't bite (byte?<G>) and can
even be fun to use...
> >
> >So there are faults on both sides.
>
> Hence the onus is not to be placed on either.

That was exactly my point. No blame.
>
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> >future reference...just did what it said. Certainly can't be wrong. Arse
is
> >completely covered.
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Absolutely NOT! All that has happened is that a lot of time (read "money")
has been wasted and we are now about to compound this error by deciding
whose fault it was... The overall target we are trying to achieve has been
lost and we will now turn round and repeat the same error by producing
another spec. that will probably be wrong (or at least imperfect) as well.

No wonder IT costs so much...

> >
> >Isn't there a ring of "I vas only followink orders..." about this?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Well, if you decide that "your job" consists of doing exactly what is
specified, without thinking or questioning it, even if you can see it is
blatantly wrong ... (I have had programmers do this on Waterfall projects.
When I questioned them, they smiled smugly and said exactly what you said:
"I did what it said". At which point I have smiled and said: "Well, you're
off this project, anyway..." I can't use people who are deliberately counter
productive. Fortunately, the situation doesn't arise on RAD projects,
because there isn't a spec to argue about; there is only a requirement to be
met and a goal to be achieved.


> >
> >I have taken certain liberties with your scenario below. I agree that it
is
> >accurate and all-too-often reflects what really happens. However, I
believe
> >it doesn't HAVE to be like that...
> >
> >Here's another Reality...<G> [my stuff added in square brackets]
> >
> >> Coder: 'Here's what you asked for.' [BTW, I was thinking about the way
we
> >handle wugglepuffs in the Business Process and I can't get a feel for
where
> >the floggletoggle gets input. Can you 'splain me, please?]
> >
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> program that doesn't do *(function)*?') [Absolutely! There is no place
for
> >bad manners on either side; after all, we're on the same Team, right <G>?
I
> >would have a quiet chat to Users who behaved like this and if it didn't
> >improve they'd be replaced on the Project Team...]
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yea, verily, even unto the said User's Boss, and his Boss's Boss, and unto
the Bosses of the third and fourth generation. The Business Sponsor signs
the checks and he should be a VERY Senior Executive. Once a team is
empowered there is no renegging... And the Business Sponsor doesn't tell
anyone what to do. He simply sees they deliver what they promised.

> >>
> >> Coder: 'All right... here's what you asked for, with (feature).' [Now
it
> >caters for Diabetics. Are there any other blood groups or medical
conditions
> >we might need to be aware of?]
> >
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> User: 'That's... all right, but it'd be really nice if it had
(feature1).'
> >>
> >> Coder: 'All right, here's what you asked for, with (feature) and
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> User: 'Wow, that's *really* cramped and complex... can't you make it,
you
> >> know, a little easier to use?'
> >>
> >The Universe can (and does) support both of these Realities (along with
an
> >unknown number of others) simultaneously (compare with Schroedinger's
> >pussy).
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Yes, the Uncertainty Principle (almost...<G>) certainly applies to Computer
projects, whether they are RAD or Waterfall.

In fact Doc, you may have unwittingly stumbled on why Waterfall Projects
move so slowly... There is such a concern over constant measurement and
evaluation that the position is known with great certainty at any given
moment. (All those time sheets and job codes and accounting that cuts into
the time it takes you to do the actual job...). Hence, by Heisenberg, the
Velocity is unknown and can lapse into motionlessness without anybody
noticing...

On a RAD project, the Velocity is high and the position is of little concern
(other than that it must be between certain constraints, defined with
uncertainty). The difference is that nobody CARES where we are in terms of a
Task Oriented plan. What matters is how many GOALS we achieved today. (For
this reason, I use Goal Oriented Planning when Managing RAD projects...) It
is a subtle, but important difference.
> >
> >It is entirely up to each of us to decide which one we will "live" in.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Yes, I know...I took some rhetorical license. There's no point in posting
here if it has to be deathly serious... <G>.
OK, seriously, it ISN'T entirely our choice which one we will work/live in.
We have to make a living where we can (this is very appropriate to my own
circumstances at the moment...the situation in Europe is the worst I have
seen it for 25 years and there are hundreds of applicants for every job.
COBOL oriented jobs are almost non-existent as I reported a couple of weeks
back. SAP is in demand everywhere...), but we CAN do something to make it
better. Whether on a Waterfall or a RAD project. Stop thinking "WHO's
Right?" and start thinking "WHAT's Right?". That alone will make a
difference.
> >
> >Make your choice and collapse the probability wave into solid personal
> >Reality (open the box and check the cat). You can do it. It just needs
some
> >mental adjustment...<G>
>
> Pharmaceutical?

Whatever...<G>
>
> >
> >You are right of course. My argument IS predicated on "a workplace
populated
> >by motivated workers who have the same goal of Actually Getting Work
Done. "
> >Not just "Actually Getting Work Done", but improving the position of "The
> >Company" in the (perhaps futile...<G>) desire that increased productivity
> >will actually trickle back to the Workers in some way (at the very least
by
> >ensuring there is a job to go to next year...). And it should be fun,
> >too...<G>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Ah! The Doc resorts to sarcasm...never thought I'd see the day <G>. As for
you being King of England, I understood you were...or was that Queen <G>?

> >
> >While I accept that such places may be rare in your experience, Doc, is
that
> >a reason to abandon all hope of creating them? I believe it is the job of
> >Management to ensure that we all get a fun-filled satisfying workplace. I
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
No they don't. I agree with each element of the above definition. In fact, I
reckon it is a pretty fair statement of the role of "Management". I just
have a distinctly individual way of achieving these goals. (Although I wish
it wasn't individual. I'd like to see more people glad to go to work in the
morning and actually finding the workplace quite a pleasant environment.)

Call me RADical if you like...<G>

> >Personally, I prefer no formal spec. at all. Spend the time in DOING it
> >rather than TALKING (or in this case, writing) about it.But then, I have
a
> >high tolerance to leaving my arse uncovered; if you're making a frontal
> >attack, you are unlikely to be shot in the arse...<G>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yes, you are right and I have the scars on my arse to prove it. Undeterred,
my arse and I go on together...

> >
> >That's why we timebox the iterations and say: "OK, that's it. Here's the
> >best we can do with the time and resources available...and by the way,
all
> >the ESSENTIAL functionality is in there, but some of the Bells and
Whistles
> >aren't."
>
> ... and ESSENTIAL (caps original) is differentiated from 'Bells and
> Whistles' (caps original) by... whom?
>

 The Users of course. It is THEIR project and THEIR money...
> >
> >As it has been developed in close co-operation with the User department,
who
> >set the priorities when it became apparent that not everything could be
> >achieved, there is never any moaning from the Users. (Compare this with
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
No Doc, this time I'm really serious. You DON'T hear Users moaning at the
completion of RAD projects (provided the projects were implemented
properly - there have been many mis-managed RAD projects and I've cleaned up
after one or two), because the Users were (forgive the buzzword)
"Stakeholders" in it. They worked on it, were consulted about it, decided
the priorities and there is no "Us" and "Them" (as we so often find on
Waterfall projects). If it is not EXACTLY what they hoped, they keep quiet
about it until funding can be obtained for another go... To moan, would be
to decry their OWN work... Besides, they decided the priorities of what
would be produced, based on what is essential to run the Business...

> >
>
> ... and all of this differs from telling them 'Sorry, it wasn't in the
> spec' by... what?
>
By Attitude, for a start.  By allowing them to decide what gets dropped,
conciously (not because it was accidentally omitted from a spec.), and by
letting them see that the very best possible use of time and resources was
made. (they were part of it, they don't need convincing.)


> >
> >Personally, I have no problem with doing whatever frivolous changes they
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Well, it sounds very Grand, Doc.

At the end of the day you're just a Cyber whore like the rest of us... You
take the money and you pay your pimp just like all contractors. If you want
to kid yourself that that ISN'T being a servant, cool.

I have no such illusions. Accepting that my role is to serve in exchange for
money, and given that personal pride makes me want to do the very best I can
at whatever I do, then I will give the very best service I can to my
clients. Sure, I'll work *with* them, that's what is needed to achieve the
best results. As for being a "Consultant"... on the occasions when people
ask for my advice I call myself a consultant, the rest of the time
"insultant" is probably more appropriate <G> I don't care what they call me
as long as they pay the invoices...

> Part of working *with* people is delineating the bits of 'you do that, I
> do this'... or so I've found.
>
Nah, do it all yourself. That way you never have to rely on anyone
else...<G>

Pete.



 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Correct Length of a string

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com

```
In article <3cebca9a_7@Usenet.com>,
Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>Good debate, Doc...

Shucks, t'ain't nothin'... jes' a bit o' back-n-forth 'twixt folks what 
done gots differin' 'speriences.

>
>I guess everyone will draw their own conclusions.

You do?  I guessed everyone fell asleep *long* ago!

>
>I don't think you are "wrong" and I agree that what you describe is the sad
>reality in many places. I just feel it shouldn't be that way and I do
>everything I can to ensure it isn't, when I'm managing projects or working
>with development teams.

As I've prefaced much of what I've stated with 'it has been my experience' 
(or a variant thereof) it would be a doubting of my veracity were you to 
be so... intolerant as to call me 'wrong' ('I've seen this happen'  'No, 
you haven't!') and not conducive to what I'd call an interchange of much 
value ('Well, you're a poopie-head!'  'No, *you're* a poopie-head... and a 
big smelly, too!')

As for That Which Is and That Which Should Be... how to balance between 
the two has been dealt with by far greater minds than mine.

>
>A few quick responses below...
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>plan.

RAD projects - at least by your presentation - are *not* accountable, 
however, by the measured, contractual goals and obligations embodied in a 
formal spec.

>
>>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>"stupid" question...

There are times one can find beautiful flowers growing in manure, true... 
and yet I avoid keeping heaps of dung around the office.  It has been my 
experience that there's never been a space on my status reports for 
'Explaining to Proudly Non-Technical Manager What I'm Doing' and such 
things eat into the time that I am allotted for doing other things... like 
my Job.

>
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>You keep saying this, but that doesn't make it true...

I do not need to make it true.

>
>There is Accountability. It goes with Empowerment.

... and Thinking Outside of the Box?  And finding Synergies in your 
Paradigms?

Mr Dashwood, when the buzzwords start to fly there is usually little that 
can be done with Logic.

>The difference is that
>the Team is accountable, rather than the person who wrote the spec. or the
>person who programmed it. Just because it is a "no blame" culture where
>people are free to get it "wrong" before they get it "right", does NOT mean
>there is no accountability.

Remember my earlier paragraph about 'Can you left-justify the logo and put 
the slogan in italics'?  Comes time for the 'All Right, Where Are We?' 
meeting the programmer can say 'I have complied with what was required by 
me in the contractual agreement codified in the specs; for some reason 
this person is unsatisfied'.

After that it can be decided, by those who have sway over allocation of 
resources, whether it is a better allocation of the programmer's time to 
endlessly shift about logos and slogans or move on to other matters.

>
>What does YOUR Accountability buy you anyway?  So the Programmer says he did
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>fixing.

Here is where *my* hopes begin... such little hopes as 'Smith's inability 
to articulate a business need has wasted time and money; perhaps it would 
be wiser to give this task to someone else.'

The project *is* further along... because at least they all know who 
*cannot* do something.

>
>So, are you saying that the Programmer will do a better job on the new
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>counter productive in the long run.

One has gained the knowledge whereby one might remove the faulty to a 
distance where their competence, or lack thereof, will not impede the 
progress of others.

[snip]

>> >
>> >And it can also be argued that if programmers made more effort to understand
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>That was exactly my point. No blame.

My error for my lack of precision; I was using 'onus' in the sense of 
'burden'... as in 'the onus is upon the programmer to learn the 
business-side' or 'the onus is upon the user to learn the programming 
side'.

>>
>> >
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>whose fault it was...

If the expressly stated desires of the users are not helpful to the 
business then it might be time to request a few resignations.

>The overall target we are trying to achieve has been
>lost and we will now turn round and repeat the same error by producing
>another spec. that will probably be wrong (or at least imperfect) as well.

As long as the same people are doing the same things, quite likely... but 
as stated above if someone does not know how to ask for what they want - 
or if getting what they ask for will have an adverse effect upon the 
business - then such folks need to be seen as such, quite quickly, and 
relegated to positions where they do not burn up the company's resources, 
either personnel or materiel.

>
>No wonder IT costs so much...

This is along the lines of 'If you think education is expensive then 
consider the alternative'.

>
>> >
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>blatantly wrong ...

My job consists of a variety of things... and one of them is making the 
person who signs the checks for the project to smile.  I know of very few 
people who continue to smile when people who are unable to artculate their 
desires are placed in positions where their actions can cost the company 
money.

>(I have had programmers do this on Waterfall projects.
>When I questioned them, they smiled smugly and said exactly what you said:
>"I did what it said". At which point I have smiled and said: "Well, you're
>off this project, anyway..." I can't use people who are deliberately counter
>productive.

Few people 'can use' folks who cannot make up their minds or tell you what 
they want... but there seem to be a lot of them out there.

>Fortunately, the situation doesn't arise on RAD projects,
>because there isn't a spec to argue about; there is only a requirement to be
>met and a goal to be achieved.

[snip]

>> >[User explains then looks at Coder's solution]
>> >>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>anyone what to do. He simply sees they deliver what they promised.

... and there is never, *ever* anything such as a Miserable, Stinkwad, 
Corner-Office Idiot who is so stupid that he needs his initials 
monogrammed on his cuffs in case who forgets who he is who insists on 
micromanagement to the poing where an ABEND in Prod will cause him to call 
into his office *anyone* who might be able to actually Fix The Problem and 
have them explain to (proudly non-technical) him exactly what is it that 
went wrong and how they can promise it will never, ever, happen again.

... and I am the King of England.

[scenario snippage]

>> >The Universe can (and does) support both of these Realities (along with an
>> >unknown number of others) simultaneously (compare with Schroedinger's
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>projects, whether they are RAD or Waterfall.

... and yet in every Status Meeting one will hear 'All right... where are 
we now, where are we going and how fast are we getting there?'... or a 
variant thereof.

>
>In fact Doc, you may have unwittingly stumbled on why Waterfall Projects
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>the time it takes you to do the actual job...).

Not when a Competent Manager includes such things in the timeline... or so 
I've been told.  I was once on a project that went into Twice-Daily Status 
Meeting Mode - always a bad sign - and the guy who signed my timesheets 
called me into his office.

'What's all this stuff on your weekly status about administrative 
paperwork and status meetings?'

'It is what it says... it is the time I spent dealing with admin paperwork 
and status meetings.'

'Well, it's too much.'

'You're telling me?  Which do you want me to skip, the paperwork or the 
meetings?'

'You can't do that... just put down that you were doing something else.'

'You want me to falsify my timesheet?!?'

'No, I don't want you to falsify it... I just want you to put down that 
you were doing something else than what you said you were doing.'

[snippage]

>> >It is entirely up to each of us to decide which one we will "live" in.
>>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>OK, seriously, it ISN'T entirely our choice which one we will work/live in.

Thanks much for clarifying that.

>We have to make a living where we can (this is very appropriate to my own
>circumstances at the moment...the situation in Europe is the worst I have
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>difference.

As my Sainted Mother told me when I was getting ready to start my first 
job: 'When it comes to work you should remember two things: you can be 
wrong about something... and be fired for it; you can be right about 
something... and be fired for it.'

Me, I tend to think 'what promise from me did this person accept regarding 
what it is that I will do?'... and then I do my job.

>> >
>> >Make your choice and collapse the probability wave into solid personal
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>you being King of England, I understood you were...or was that Queen <G>?

The King... and I should be what I am, just to be consistent.

>
>> >
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>Call me RADical if you like...<G>

No, you have called yourself 'distinctly individual' and that is as close 
enough... consider that bit about 'fun' you proposed.  Believe it or not 
there are some folks who are *not* motivated by fun and it would be a 
mistake for a manager to try to make things more fun.  I recall reading in 
a copy of Sun T'zu that one needs to appeal to different people in 
different ways... the greedy through gain, the lazy through the lash, the 
intelligent by allowing them to show their superiority and worth, etc.

Not a lick o' fun in the bunch... not that it couldn't be worked in, true, 
but there's a bunch of other motivators out there.

>
>> >Personally, I prefer no formal spec. at all. Spend the time in DOING it
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>my arse and I go on together...

Competent strategist, then, but using incompetent marksmen.

>
>> >
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> The Users of course. It is THEIR project and THEIR money...

Of course... and a spec allows an impartial verification of this 
differentiation.


>> >
>> >As it has been developed in close co-operation with the User department, who
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>"Stakeholders" in it.

Not just *any* stakeholders, Mr Dashwood... Empowered Stakeholders!  
Empowered Stakeholders who are Synergising Paradigms!

Buzzwords, Mr Dashwood... and they do not seem to have the ring of 
'Victory has many fathers but failure is an orphan'.

>They worked on it, were consulted about it, decided
>the priorities and there is no "Us" and "Them" (as we so often find on
>Waterfall projects).

Ummmm... then who were the Users you mentioned before who were 
differentiating between ESSENTIAL and Bells and Whistles (caps original)?

>If it is not EXACTLY what they hoped, they keep quiet
>about it until funding can be obtained for another go... To moan, would be
>to decry their OWN work... Besides, they decided the priorities of what
>would be produced, based on what is essential to run the Business...

... and they did so in a fashion which kept no specs to see how close 
folks came to knowing what they asked for.

[snip]

>> >Personally, I have no problem with doing whatever frivolous changes they
>> >want. It is their money and I am their servant.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Well, it sounds very Grand, Doc.

It is merely the way my work is done... no more, no less.

>
>At the end of the day you're just a Cyber whore like the rest of us... You
>take the money and you pay your pimp just like all contractors. If you want
>to kid yourself that that ISN'T being a servant, cool.

Speak for yourself, Mr Dashwood; I, for one, do not necessarily associate 
remuneration with prostitution.

>
>I have no such illusions.

You have bigger and better ones, perhaps?  Whatever the case, it is most 
gracious of you to consent to discussing such matters with those who, in 
your own words, 'kid themselves' and 'hold illusions' about what they do.

[snippage]

>> Part of working *with* people is delineating the bits of 'you do that, I
>> do this'... or so I've found.
>>
>Nah, do it all yourself. That way you never have to rely on anyone
>else...<G>

... and that way you do everyone elses work and you don't get everyone 
else's salary.  Servile, indeed... had much practise with 'Thank you, Sir, 
may I please have another?'

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Correct Length of a string

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-05-23T11:39:35+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3cecc68e_4@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<ac0ej0$vg1$01$1@news.t-online.com> <3cead7c5_3@Usenet.com> <acg708$ohk$1@panix1.panix.com> <3cebca9a_7@Usenet.com> <achjdb$21j$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:achjdb$21j$1@panix1.panix.com...
> In article <3cebca9a_7@Usenet.com>,
> Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
So all "buzzwords" are meaningless? Empowerment is an essential part of RAD
projects. Teams need to be empowered to make decisions within a specified
region. This empowerment comes delegated from Senior Management and saves
time (RAD projects are all about saving time..). It works on the basis that
if a Business Process needs to be changed, the team are empowered to change
it. They must document why it needed to be changed and what the reasons were
behind the decision that was taken. That is part of the Acccountability
which you say doesn't exist, and which I said comes from empowerment.  You
can scoff all you like at "buzzwords", but this one is an essential part of
RAD projects.
Once the process is changed, it stays changed and the system implementation
is predicated on it remaining so. It is a decision taken jointly by the
Business and IT working together, not against each other. The normal
channels and paperwork that are required to change a process in most
Organisations are simply replaced in this instance. (The Project Office and
Change Control (if they are in place) are notified, and copied on the
paperwork (which is seldom more than a few pages) and it is then up to them
to fill in any forms they may require. In companies that implement TickIT or
ISO9000, a specific Procedure is authorised to allow properly authorised RAD
teams to do this.)

It works and it works well. It wouldn't without empowerment.

> >The difference is that
> >the Team is accountable, rather than the person who wrote the spec. or
the
> >person who programmed it. Just because it is a "no blame" culture where
> >people are free to get it "wrong" before they get it "right", does NOT
mean
> >there is no accountability.
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
 You simply haven't grasped the concept. The TEAM are responsible for the
allocation of resources and the programmers on the team will discuss how
their time can best be spent, with the Users on the team. (actually they
won't; they'll just sit down together and achieve the goals they have been
set for achievemnet within this timebox). Even as the Project Manager it is
not my job to tell programmer A he should be moving the logo around (or
not). It is my job to ensure that the project Goals are identified and the
team is focussed on them. If goals fail to be achieved (it happens
sometimes, though it is rare) then the team members who were working on it
(IT and Business) will be asked to account for what happened. If it
transpires that a lot of time was spent moving logos around instead of
achieving the desired goals, then the lesson is learned that it is not a
good idea to spend excessive time on things that are not directly
goal-oriented. In extreme cases where there was blatant incompetence (this
has never happened to me so far - I choose RAD teams in careful consultation
with the existing Management and back my own assessment of people also) some
team members would be replaced.

Your post shows that you are locked into the idea of a heirarchical
Management who decide things, an IT department who work like mindless
automatons from specs prepared by stupid technophobic Users, and the whole
idea is to ensure that no blame can be attached to your good self, who only
did what was asked...It is no wonder the whole idea of RAD is anathema to
you <G>
> >
> >What does YOUR Accountability buy you anyway?  So the Programmer says he
did
> >what the spec. said. We are no further ahead. The situation is not
improved
> >because the Programmer is blameless. Whatever was done, it is still
"wrong".
> >Arguing about whose fault it was is a pointless waste of time. It needs
> >fixing.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Ah! So you not only want to be seen to be seen to be superior and blameless,
you want the poor bastard removed !!

> The project *is* further along... because at least they all know who
> *cannot* do something.
>
Cannot? Or was never given a chance to shine? Intimidated, instead of
motivated, crushed instead of supported, is it any wonder the poor sod
failed? If he CANNOT do the job, that is one thing. If he is not ALLOWED to
do the job that is quite another.


> >
> >So, are you saying that the Programmer will do a better job on the new
> >revised spec.? Nope, he did his best on the first one. Will the spec.
writer
> >do a better job? Maybe, but it is a fair bet he also did his best on the
> >first attempt. NOTHING has been gained by assigning fault. All that has
been
> >achieved is a "distancing" between the two parties which is extremely
> >counter productive in the long run.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I can see it is extremely difficult to work in an environment where you are
surrounded by idiots, Doc. If you keep on getting them removed, you will
eventually get to work on your own. Is that the actual aim here?

> >>
> >> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> >OK, you did what it said. You didn't think about it, consider it,
wonder
> >> >what was trying to be achieved and maybe put a few "hooks" in there
for
> >> >future reference...just did what it said. Certainly can't be wrong.
Arse is
> >> >completely covered.
> >> >
> >> >But has it helped the Business?
> >>

users,
> >> yes.
> >>

"money")
> >has been wasted and we are now about to compound this error by deciding

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Or, a more helpful approach could be to sit doown and find out what the real
needs of the business are (whether they can express them or not...). It is
very hard to do that unless you are prepared to actually work WITH the
Business. Before you know it, you have a RAD team....<G>

> >The overall target we are trying to achieve has been
> >lost and we will now turn round and repeat the same error by producing

well.

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
So how did these cretins come to be employed in the first place? Guess HR

> >> >
> >> >Isn't there a ring of "I vas only followink orders..." about this?
> >>
> >> Not to my ears, no... there is the ring of 'I know my job, you know
your
> >> job, we work together, each with our own knowledge'.
> >>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> person who signs the checks for the project to smile.

Ah! So your superiority does not preclude obsequiousness, then...<G>?

> I know of very few
> people who continue to smile when people who are unable to artculate their
> desires are placed in positions where their actions can cost the company
> money.
>
EVERYBODY who works in a Company, from the CEO to the Janitor can cost it
money by their actions.

Your beef is that the Business are "unable to articulate their  desires" .
But communication is a TWO way process. Why aren't you able to elicit from
them what is needed and wanted?

Because it isn't your job, right?

So, you'd rather sit back and criticise than get in and help. Fair enough,
that's your prerogative.

And that is where we basically diverge.


> >(I have had programmers do this on Waterfall projects.
> >When I questioned them, they smiled smugly and said exactly what you
said:
> >"I did what it said". At which point I have smiled and said: "Well,
you're
> >off this project, anyway..." I can't use people who are deliberately
counter
> >productive.
>
> Few people 'can use' folks who cannot make up their minds or tell you what
> they want... but there seem to be a lot of them out there.
>
I sincerely suggest to you Doc, that if you were less intimidating there
would be fewer of them "out there". I believe you when you say they can't
tell you what they want. You are an articulate, educated, intelligent,
specialist doing a high tech job. The average Business User is someone who
went into the Business many years ago because that was his best option. It
was never part of his brief to be articulate, educated, intelligent, etc.
(The fact that many Business Users ARE so, is a bonus...<G>). It is
intimidating for people who maybe just graduated High School to have to try
and provide written instructions to someone who is probably University
Educated and perceived to be of superior intellect. It is made even more
intimidating when said person evinces an attitude that does not "suffer
fools gladly", and then goes ahead and does what he knows to be incorrect
with the express purpose of "exposing" the incompetence of the person who
wrote the spec. While you may be gratified to see them squirm, it is not
helping the Business. (But you probably aren't too concerned about that
anyway, as long as the guy who writes your check is happy, right?)


> >>
> >
> >Yea, verily, even unto the said User's Boss, and his Boss's Boss, and
unto
> >the Bosses of the third and fourth generation. The Business Sponsor signs
> >the checks and he should be a VERY Senior Executive. Once a team is
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
OK, at this point you got me back...<G> I know the kind of person you are
referring to and your adjectives are appropriate. Fortunately, these little
Napoleons contain the seeds of their own destruction and seldom last very
long in positions of Authority before being moved sideways (or offered
redundancy). Don't play the game. Never promise such a person ANYTHING! Tell
him it is fixed for now, until the next time it fails, or until he is
prepared to authorise having it re-done properly. If he says that is not
acceptable, tell him it is the best you can do, and maybe he should get
someone else to look at it...

Obviously, those of this ilk do not get onto RAD teams. (That's one reason
why we have empowerment; so this kind of thing can be bypassed. And that's
another reason why the empowerment HAS to come from very Senior Management,
usually the Business Sponsor.)

> >
> >In fact Doc, you may have unwittingly stumbled on why Waterfall Projects
> >move so slowly... There is such a concern over constant measurement and
> >evaluation that the position is known with great certainty at any given
> >moment. (All those time sheets and job codes and accounting that cuts
into
> >the time it takes you to do the actual job...).
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Hahaha! Yes, I've had a similar experience also. We set up an Account for

it...<G>

> [snippage]
>
> >> >It is entirely up to each of us to decide which one we will "live" in.

weeks

Commendable.

While I have the utmost respect for "The Art of War" and I don't disagree
that different people need different motivations, I cannot imagine a World
or a Workplace without humour.

Still, I have to admit that you are probably right and there probably ARE
some poor souls who do not find anything amusing and would not be moved by
some lightness in the workplace. I hasten to add that I am advocating
"lightness" not "silliness"...(although I have seen some very silly things
in the Workplace that just happened to be funny as well...<G>)

> Not a lick o' fun in the bunch... not that it couldn't be worked in, true,

Careful. If a "motivator" becomes "demonstrating your superiority over

> >> >

be

with
> >> >systems developed by Waterfall...)

> >> '... never any moaning from the Users'?  Pharmaceitucal reality,
indeed!
> >>
> >No Doc, this time I'm really serious. You DON'T hear Users moaning at the

> >properly - there have been many mis-managed RAD projects and I've cleaned
up

> >"Stakeholders" in it.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Buzzwords have been overused to the point where their meaning becomes trite,
but that doesn't mean you can dismiss them entirely. I could "translate"
"empowered stakeholders" as:

"People who have a vested interest in seeing the successful conclusion of a
specific project, and have been delegated power, within certain constraints,
to assist in overcoming obstacles to this successful conclusion."

If I wrote that, few people would have issue with it. Yet if I put
"empowered stakeholders" you break into gales of sarcasm ... It makes me
wonder whether you are really interested in the issues being explored here
or simply the chance to have an argument...<G>

I could go on...
"People who have a vested interest in seeing the successful conclusion of a
specific project, and have been delegated power, within certain constraints,
to assist in overcoming obstacles to this successful conclusion. By
utilising a new form of Project Methodology called "RAD", these people hope
to attain a solution which will be more powerful than each of the individual
specific actions which went to achieve it. "

("Empowered Stakeholders who are synergising a paradigm.")

> >They worked on it, were consulted about it, decided
> >the priorities and there is no "Us" and "Them" (as we so often find on
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Jeez, Doc, you aren't that stupid. The Users are members of a TEAM (which
includes IT people). (A TEAM has no "Us" and "Them" It is just "Us"). Or
maybe this is a concept which is foreign to you?


> >If it is not EXACTLY what they hoped, they keep quiet
> >about it until funding can be obtained for another go... To moan, would
be
> >to decry their OWN work... Besides, they decided the priorities of what
> >would be produced, based on what is essential to run the Business...
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
OK, I give up...<G> you are either being deliberately obtuse or you simply
can't grasp this concept. "Folks" don't need to "know what they asked for"
because they can run the system and see it. Goals which were not achieved
(lower priority) are of course included in the project documentation and
will show on the Project Plan. You don't need a spec.
(Having said that, one is usually generated from the final code by software,
just so people like you can see it...<G>)
>
> >> >Personally, I have no problem with doing whatever frivolous changes
they
> >> >want. It is their money and I am their servant.

not

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…

Petulance? I touched a nerve...<G>

OK Doc. It's been real and it's been fun, but it hasn't been real fun.

I don't think I am likely to persuade you in the slightest. That's OK.
CLC wouldn't be the same without you  <G>

Pete.
```

```
In article <3cecc68e_4@Usenet.com>,
Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

Hmmmmm... no more 'great post!' or 'good debate!'... 

>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:achjdb$21j$1@panix1.panix.com...
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>So all "buzzwords" are meaningless?

No, Mr Dashwood... if you read carefully you may notice I said nothing 
about 'meaning' (a phenomenon which, as Witgenstein put it, the result of 
interpretation), I stated that 'when the buzzwords start to fly there is 
usually little that can be done with Logic'.

Please address what I have written, now what you wish me to have written; 
falsely re-stating an opposing view is often the sign of a weak argument.

[snip]

>> Remember my earlier paragraph about 'Can you left-justify the logo and put
>> the slogan in italics'?  Comes time for the 'All Right, Where Are We?'
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> You simply haven't grasped the concept.

You seem to have dodged what appears to be a valid point on my part - the 
calling of a halt to a possible excess of attention to details - by 
accusing me of a failure of comprehension.

This, Mr Dashwood, is often a tactic employed by religious zealots or 
adolescents... 'You just don't *understand!'

>The TEAM are responsible for the
>allocation of resources and the programmers on the team will discuss how
>their time can best be spent, with the Users on the team. (actually they
>won't; they'll just sit down together and achieve the goals they have been
>set for achievemnet within this timebox).

Just like that... they just 'sit down together and achieve the goals'.  
This strikes me as... exquisitely facile, Mr Dashwood, and 'facile' in the 
sense of 'specious, superficial'.

[snip]

>If it
>transpires that a lot of time was spent moving logos around instead of
>achieving the desired goals, then the lesson is learned that it is not a
>good idea to spend excessive time on things that are not directly
>goal-oriented.

A goal is to create output which satisfies the users, Mr Dashwood... and 
the user, as noted, vacillates.  A spec document, for all its flaws, 
freezes vacillation.

>In extreme cases where there was blatant incompetence (this
>has never happened to me so far - I choose RAD teams in careful consultation
>with the existing Management and back my own assessment of people also) some
>team members would be replaced.

Well, we agree on something... at times there are folks who need to go, 
aye.

>
>Your post shows that you are locked into the idea of a heirarchical
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>you <G>

And your post, Mr Dashwood, shows that you attempt to obfuscate by use of 
buzzwords and when caught doing so you issue the cry of 'You just don't 
*understand*!'... which is, quite often, the way that one who tries to 
pull a 'fast one' behaves.

What did the tailors who were making the Emperor's New Clothes say of 
those who could not see these marvelous garments?  According to 

http://www.deoxy.org/emperors.htm

--begin quoted text:

"We are two very good tailors and after many years of research we have 
invented an extraordinary method to weave a cloth so light and fine that 
it looks invisible. As a matter of fact it is invisible to anyone who is 
too stupid and incompetent to appreciate its quality." 
 
--end quoted text

... but I am certain that things appear Quite Differently to those who can 
Understand.

>> >
>> >What does YOUR Accountability buy you anyway?  So the Programmer says he did
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>you want the poor bastard removed !!

Mr Dashwood, didn't you just say a moment ago that 'In extreme cases where 
there was blatant incompetence... some team member would be replaced'?

I want people who can Do Their Jobs on the project... if this is seen as a 
desire to be 'superior, blameless and wanting the poor bastards removed' 
then my first question is... 'Would being seen this way Get The Job Done?'

>
>> The project *is* further along... because at least they all know who
>> *cannot* do something.
>>
>Cannot? Or was never given a chance to shine?

The person's ability to shine might be tested during an apprenticeship at 
a nearby bootblack's stand... the business needs someone who can do other 
things.

>Intimidated, instead of
>motivated, crushed instead of supported, is it any wonder the poor sod
>failed? If he CANNOT do the job, that is one thing. If he is not ALLOWED to
>do the job that is quite another.

It was shown that Smith was unable to articulate a business need and that 
this inability cost the project time and money.  If you want to go around 
assigning blame to circumstances rather than individuals, well and good... 
but in the meantime there are deadlines to meet so let's find someone who 
can Do This Job *now*.

>
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>eventually get to work on your own. Is that the actual aim here?

Once again, Mr Dashwood, you falsely re-state an argument.  I said nothing 
about 'idiots', I said that one might 'remove the faulty to a distance 
where their competence, or lack thereof, will not impede the progress of 
others.'

If you have found that projects are assisted by the participation of 
incompetents and faulty workers then our experiences are *very* different.  
I like to work with people who can, first and foremost, Do Their Jobs... 
after all, that is what they are getting paid for.

(A close second to the ability to Do One's Job is to have a sense of 
humor, sure... but when one is on the clock Work Comes First)

>
>> >>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>needs of the business are (whether they can express them or not...).

It would be very nice to learn what someone else's job is, sure... but if 
that learning interferes with Getting The Job Done then it must be 
relegated to a secondary position.  First and foremost, find someone who 
can Do Their Job and let them do it.

>It is
>very hard to do that unless you are prepared to actually work WITH the
>Business. Before you know it, you have a RAD team....<G>

It is even harder to explain why someone should be expected to learn 
another person's job and then do it... and *not* get paid that other 
person's salary.

>
>> >The overall target we are trying to achieve has been
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>So how did these cretins come to be employed in the first place?

Oh, I *cannot* resist... they were trying to put together a RAD team!

>Guess HR
>got it wrong and decided to put morons in the Business and Mensa in IT?

You guessed wrong, again... but that is not unusual.  For whatever reasons 
it happens that idiots get jobs that they cannot perform; when this occurs 
I find it best to get them as far away from my work as possible because 
they tend to slow me down and make me unhappy.

>
>> >> >
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>Ah! So your superiority does not preclude obsequiousness, then...<G>?

The only person who harps on such things in this discussion, Mr Dashwood, 
is you... but that is because you Grasp The Concept, I guess.

>
>> I know of very few
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>Because it isn't your job, right?

Wrong... again.  If one has mud to work with, Mr Dashwood, one is not able 
to elicit diamonds... the best one can hope for is a good brick.

>
>So, you'd rather sit back and criticise than get in and help. Fair enough,
>that's your prerogative.

And again, Mr Dashwood, you re-state my arguments falsely... I would 
rather that other people Do Their Jobs, I Do My Job and the task assigned 
Gets Done.

>
>And that is where we basically diverge.

If you diverge from wanting to get the task assigned Done there is little 
else to say.

>
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>would be fewer of them "out there".

I cannot speak of what 'would be', Mr Dashwood, as I do not have that sort 
of certainty... in the limited nature of my experience you might note that 
I spoke of what 'seems'.

>I believe you when you say they can't
>tell you what they want. You are an articulate, educated, intelligent,
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>anyway, as long as the guy who writes your check is happy, right?)

Mr Dashwood, the 'whys and wherefores' might be the subject of another 
discussion entire (and if you believe even half of what you wrote about me 
above then... shucks, I done fooled another'un!); the situation, as I see 
it, is quite simple.

As a programmer it is my job to make things easier for people by having 
the machine do things for them.

As users it is other peoples' job to know what their job is and to be able 
to tell me how they want the machine to make it easier for them to do it.

We all Do Our Own Jobs... and the business makes more money.

>
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>referring to and your adjectives are appropriate.

It wasn't easy to hold back, either.

>Fortunately, these little
>Napoleons contain the seeds of their own destruction and seldom last very
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>someone else to look at it...

Mr Dashwood, did'nt you just say a moment ago 'While you may be gratified 
to see them squirm, it is not helping the Business'?

>
>Obviously, those of this ilk do not get onto RAD teams. (That's one reason
>why we have empowerment; so this kind of thing can be bypassed. And that's
>another reason why the empowerment HAS to come from very Senior Management,
>usually the Business Sponsor.)

... but 'very Senior Management' is the result of the 'idea of a 
heirarchical Management who decide things'... something that you accused 
me of being 'locked in to'?

[snippimente]

>> >Whether on a Waterfall or a RAD project. Stop thinking "WHO's
>> >Right?" and start thinking "WHAT's Right?". That alone will make a
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>Commendable.

For some it is as easy as breathing.

[snip]

>> >No Doc, this time I'm really serious. You DON'T hear Users moaning at the
>> >completion of RAD projects (provided the projects were implemented
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>or simply the chance to have an argument...<G>

It reminds me of a fellow who used the term 'paper-based data dictionary' 
to refer to his checklist.  How about 'Folks who stand to benefit and who 
have the authority to Do Their Jobs'?

It might be that 'folks who have the authority to Do Their Jobs' points 
the absurdity of the very concept of 'empowerment'... because just about 
anyone will say that someone who has a Job to do but doesn't have the 
authority to Do Their Job is bound to fail.

[snip]

>
>> >If it is not EXACTLY what they hoped, they keep quiet
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>can't grasp this concept.

Neither, Mr Dashwood... I just have a bit of difficulty accepting things 
that 'just get done' and bring IT tasks to completion; my experience shows 
me that this is rarely the case.

>"Folks" don't need to "know what they asked for"
>because they can run the system and see it.

If this is true, Mr Dashwood, then programmers do *not* need to be able to

yourself.

>(lower priority) are of course included in the project documentation and

>(Having said that, one is usually generated from the final code by software,

I usually don't read specs for code that's already been written, Mr

>>
>> >> >Personally, I have no problem with doing whatever frivolous changes they
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…

No, Mr Dashwood, boredom... how many times can you re-state my arguments

DD
```
