###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Correct Length of RAD

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-23T00:09:57-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ueoqkktl2vp94f@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<ac0ej0$vg1$01$1@news.t-online.com> <3CE3D754.C21DEE2@shaw.ca> <928495c6.0205162043.677d0d76@posting.google.com> <uecd8p3o353a60@corp.supernews.com> <3CE766EB.EDABABB@Azonic.co.nz> <uefpa49jkjve31@corp.supernews.com> <3CE8A0E2.BFB457E0@Azonic.co.nz> <uejgkn29ttpf5@corp.supernews.com> <3cea3d37_3@Usenet.com>`

```

There are, perhaps, a few other points I should raise about
my potential to use RAD.

The work I did for more than twenty years was embedded
programming in C and software products in COBOL.

The number of distinct users number in the tens of thousands.
It was never possible for me to sit with the users to provide
solutions to fit their "peculiar" needs.

Iterative development was also out of the question. The
products had to be complete at time of shipment. While
regular updates did occur, they too had to be complete.

Furthermore, high-reliability was always a requirement.
The products with embedded programs run 24/7 365.
Any flaw in those products could bankrupt the company.

I have only done a few odds and ends for the company
during the past two years; but, at all times, I never did
anything which was not pre-specified. There was too
much risk.

I did little work during the past two years because both
my parents were ill and I looked after them. They have
both, since, passed. Should I return to work, it is unlikely
that I would ever consider RAD. I am convinced that
prespecification provides higher reliability systems and
I prefer writing software that does not break easily.

Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3cea3d37_3@Usenet.com...
[...]
> Ha! This is one of the major flaws in "Waterfall" development.
>
> Someone "translates" their perception of someone else's requirement into a
> Specification, which is then implemented in code by someone else again,
over
> a period that is often months or even years, and both these guys are then
> surprised when the original person says: "Er...not quite like that..." Or,
> "Good, but it just needs this one small change.." <G>
>
> You can remove the need for a development program specification
altogether,
> if you simply use an iterative prototyping workshop. Here the User sits
with
> the programmer and they both produce a screen or form (prototype) that
> BECOMES (through iteration), exactly what the Business requires. Everybody
> is happy and the kudos are equally shared. And it took one third of the
time
> it would have done if you had investigated it, specced it, programmed it,
> tested it, then UATed it... It's called RAD (Rapid Application
Development)
> EVERYBODY who has a stake in the end product (IT and the Business) gets to
> work together on the production of it.
>
> "Fine for input screens and displays and reports", you say (well, some of
> you do...<G>) "But who specs the actual system flows? what about the
updates
> and stuff?".
>
> This is also built by iteration  (but this time with Operations involved
if
> it requires mainframe or batch processing) or maybe just within the IT
> department. Yes, an overall Design must be produced by the people
qualified
> to do it, but it doesn't have to be PERFECT on day one, and it doesn't
mean
> that no other work can proceed until it is formally signed off! In fact,
the
> design process is also iterated, with a better design emerging from each
> round.
>
> This is a dynamic, flexible approach to EVOLVING a system, rather than
just
> building it.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> it is ALWAYS 100% <G>
```

Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3cecca4e_4@Usenet.com...
[...]
> Obviously, I disagree. The reason I disagree is based on my experience and
I
> have seen RAD deliver systems that are just as reliable as any delivered
by
> the Waterfall. If somebody can spec a problem, why can't they sit down
with
> a programmer and Prototype it?
>
> As I am not an Evangelist I do not propose to pursue this (any further
than
> I already have at length with the Doc <G>).

There is one point that has been bugging me and I felt
the need to research it, a little.

The Waterfall Model (aka, SDLC or Software Development
Life Cycle) appears to have been replaced by the Spiral
Model [Boehm]. I am not certain it is fair to contrast RAD
with the Waterfall Model, if the latter is not longer used.

I don't know whether you have looked at the Spiral Model, see
< http://www.eng.uwaterloo.ca/~msci738/clippings/00000059.pdf >
The Spiral Model and RAD might be seen as complements;
that is, use one where the other does not fit or divide the project
to use each where it is appropriate.
-------------
Barry W. Boehm, "A Sprial Model of Software Development
and Enhancement," Computer, vol. 21 (May 1988): 61-72.
```

While I am theoretically aware of the spiral model (in fact Will Price gives
an overview of it in his excellent book on OO COBOL), I have never used it
in practice and I don't know anyone who has...

That's not to say that nobody uses it ,or it is of doubtful value (from what
I've read it makes sense) just that I don't believe the SDLC model is
actually being replaced as you describe. There are still many companies
looking for PMs with SDLC; I have yet to see ONE advertising for the
Spiral...

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ The Spiral Model

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-25T13:34:24-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uevij25uqivp78@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<ac0ej0$vg1$01$1@news.t-online.com> <3CE3D754.C21DEE2@shaw.ca> <928495c6.0205162043.677d0d76@posting.google.com> <uecd8p3o353a60@corp.supernews.com> <3CE766EB.EDABABB@Azonic.co.nz> <uefpa49jkjve31@corp.supernews.com> <3CE8A0E2.BFB457E0@Azonic.co.nz> <uejgkn29ttpf5@corp.supernews.com> <3cea3d37_3@Usenet.com> <ueoqkktl2vp94f@corp.supernews.com> <3cecca4e_4@Usenet.com> <ueqdlp4d0bdva4@corp.supernews.com> <3ceebdfe_5@Usenet.com>`

```

Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3ceebdfe_5@Usenet.com...
> While I am theoretically aware of the spiral model (in fact Will Price
gives
> an overview of it in his excellent book on OO COBOL), I have never used it
> in practice and I don't know anyone who has...
>
> That's not to say that nobody uses it ,or it is of doubtful value (from
what
> I've read it makes sense) just that I don't believe the SDLC model is
> actually being replaced as you describe. There are still many companies
> looking for PMs with SDLC; I have yet to see ONE advertising for the
> Spiral...

I misinformed because I made a faulty assumption. In various
texts, where development methodology is discussed, the
older texts show SDLC, more recent texts show Spiral.
I wrongly assumed that Spiral replaced SDLC. I compounded
the error by not fully reading the article, I referenced, before
posting.

The Spiral Model actually incorporates several development
methods, including RAD and SDLC. It does so by assessing
risk. That which is to be untertaken next will depend upon the
risk.

If all risks are low, "code and fix" may be selected. This
avoids the cost of creating a formal specification, though
an informal specification may apply.

If user interface risks are high and other risks relatively low,
RAD may be selected.

If other risks are relatively high, the more formal SDLC
may be selected.

I highly recommend reading the article,
< http://www.eng.uwaterloo.ca/~msci738/clippings/00000059.pdf >.

Any job offering that mentions or implies SDLC and uses terms,
such as, risk assessment/analysis/management, prototype,
benchmark, etc., may be Spiral. This is because Spiral uses
those terms and SDLC does not.

> Rick Smith <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
> news:ueqdlp4d0bdva4@corp.supernews.com...
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> > and Enhancement," Computer, vol. 21 (May 1988): 61-72.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Correct Length of a specification

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-05-22T08:47:47+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CEB4D23.161C92C0@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<ac0ej0$vg1$01$1@news.t-online.com> <3CE3D754.C21DEE2@shaw.ca> <928495c6.0205162043.677d0d76@posting.google.com> <uecd8p3o353a60@corp.supernews.com> <3CE766EB.EDABABB@Azonic.co.nz> <uefpa49jkjve31@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:

> One of the areas, about which I have given a great deal
> of thought, is adherrence to specifications; that is, how to
> write code that matches, as closely as possible, a
> specification.

The best way to do this is to ensure that the specification is written
in an appropriate way that can be matched by the program code.  This is
much easier if the program code is written first.  In fact once the code
has been written then the specification is almost redundant and what is
actually required is a user guide.

The way I deal with this is that as I write the code I add comments
within marked blocks eg:

      *<html>
      *<hr /> <!-- --------------------------------------- -->
      *<h4><a name="pricecode">PRICE CODE</a></h4>
      *
      * The price code indicates which price is given to a 
      *     customer and it should be specified as:
      *<ul>
      *<li> R = Retail price is to be used.
      *<li> C = Cost price plus markup is to be used, 
      *         the markup is given in the Group record
      *         for the product.
      *<li> n, where n is 1,2 or 3 = Trade price for category n
      *         will apply.
      *</ul>
      * The price codes need to be setup in the 
      * <a href="azdecode.htm"> Decode File Maintenance</a>
      * under the 
      * <a href="azdecode.htm#PRICING">PRICING field</a>.
      *</html>

           [code dealing with price code here]

Comments that simply relate what the code is (supposedly) doing are a
waste of space.  These html comments explain _why_ the code is doing it
and can be easily extracted into web pages that together make up the
system documentation.

Having the specification (actually as a User Guide) within the code make
it more likely that it will be updated as the code is changed and using
make to reconstruct the web page documentation means that the latest
version should always be available.  It can also be linked together ( <a
href=...> ... </a> ) so that users can navigate through a subject.

If the system has Help pages (eg using SP2) that can be called up then
these too can be extracted from the marked blocks in a somewhat
different way.

These days I do it all from being given a half page 'requirement' by the
client to installing the hardware and laying the cabling.  In previous
incarnations I have from time to time been a 'Project Leader' supposedly
co-ordinating the workers and reporting progress.  After doing a
half-hour 'project plan' and Critical Path the Management Schedule is
discarded and the project is reorganised to put all resources onto
critical path activities.

This often meant that programming had to be started day one (because
programmers had been assigned) before the formal spec had even been
started, indeed before the customer had been met. Of course the client
wasn't told this, nor the management.  Usually I wound up writing the
User Guide (this was 20 years ago) and had the customer sign this off
while the programmers also worked from it and the trainer used it.

I have always found that it is best to start with the end products in
any system (report layouts, web page customer enquiry pages, extract
data to other systems) and work back through the processing to the
input.  For whole systems also start at the end product (User Guide,
support desk(?)) and work back from there.  If there is to be a formal
spec then do it last, that way you may not need to do it at all.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Correct Length of a specification

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-22T21:04:34-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ueog9b7h9iqq27@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<ac0ej0$vg1$01$1@news.t-online.com> <3CE3D754.C21DEE2@shaw.ca> <928495c6.0205162043.677d0d76@posting.google.com> <uecd8p3o353a60@corp.supernews.com> <3CE766EB.EDABABB@Azonic.co.nz> <uefpa49jkjve31@corp.supernews.com> <3CEB4D23.161C92C0@Azonic.co.nz>`

```

Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3CEB4D23.161C92C0@Azonic.co.nz...
> Rick Smith wrote:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> actually required is a user guide.

Program code, written without specification, is meaningless.
<G>

I will suggest that specification is used. The specification is
from the knowledge of the programmer; but is not, necessarily,
in written form. The programmer, using previously accumulated
knowledge, creates a basic specification by beginning the
system; then adds to that specification with additional code
created with knowledge gleaned from the user. The end result
is a specification, in excruciating detail, that describes a unique
system. This includes RAD, prototyping, spiral, and waterfall
development methodologies.

I have no interest in such systems. Complete specifications
of unique systems cannot be easily reused. You will also note
that, above, I presented the goal "code closely matches
specification" rather than "exactly matches".

Every proper specification encapsulates knowledge. Those with
the skills and tools to use the specification may then reproduce
that which was specified. They are also free to use the
specification, with suitable modifications, to produce similar
items.

A proper specification may be complete even though it
provides only a framework for adding detail. This is because
the specification provides the knowledge for reproducing
the framework. Adding detail, to this framework, may require
minor deviation from the specification; hence, "closely matches".
[The reader should understand that this paragraph was greatly
influenced by my study of object-orientation.]

A collection of individual specifications may then describe in
varying detail the parts necessary to create any number of
unique systems. [I will leave it to the reader to decide the value
of such a knowledge base.] It is then the collection of individual
specifications, used, together with additional detail that
specifies the complete, unique system and provides the core
of knowledge necessary to modify that system.

The ability to create code from a written specification is
crucial to this method. Therefore, given a written specification,
how might one, adherring to the specification "write code that
matches, as closely as possible, [the] specification[?]" It is this
question I sought to answer because the coder might not
fully grasp the knowledge or concepts in the specification;
but must, nonetheless, implement the specification.

> I have always found that it is best to start with the end products in
> any system (report layouts, web page customer enquiry pages, extract
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> spec then do it last, that way you may not need to do it at all.

I am familiar with the concept. Starting at the output and
working backward is a technique discussed in "Structured
Requirements Definition" by Ken Orr. Of course, the result
is a lengthy document to describe a unique system. <G>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Correct Length of a specification

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-05-25T08:17:11+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CEF3A77.BAE403EA@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<ac0ej0$vg1$01$1@news.t-online.com> <3CE3D754.C21DEE2@shaw.ca> <928495c6.0205162043.677d0d76@posting.google.com> <uecd8p3o353a60@corp.supernews.com> <3CE766EB.EDABABB@Azonic.co.nz> <uefpa49jkjve31@corp.supernews.com> <3CEB4D23.161C92C0@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
> The ability to create code from a written specification is
> crucial to this method. Therefore, given a written specification,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> but must, nonetheless, implement the specification.

I recall when I was a junior coder that a program spec was handed to me
that consisted primarily of several decision tables.  A decision table
pre-processor was available (but no one had used it) so I lifted the
spec tables directly to the source code and put a few lines of linking
code.

There was no need to understand or even care what the tables did beyond
the mechanical needs of making the DT preprocessor deal with them.  The
program worked first time, if there were any logic faults it was due to
incorrect specification rather than coding errors.  

These days decision tables can be written in the form of an evaluate.
```

Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:acb3bh$dtr$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On 18-May-2002, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> standards.

EXIT PERFORM has been in some compilers for more than
ten years.

It has been part of the draft COBOL standard for at least
five years.

It has been discussed in this group for several years. This is
but one more discussion in which EXIT PERFORM is mentioned.

It is a powerful and worthwhile addition to the language and
"shapes the ways in which we think about problems(1)."

Perhaps you could follow Bill's suggestion by requesting
"your" compiler vendor implement this feature, immediately.

Until it becomes available to you, please apply future tense to
such statements, e.g., "It will be far better to write". <G>

Howard, I mentioned one way in which EXIT PERFORM has
shaped the way in which I think about problems. How will the
availability of EXIT PERFORM shape the ways in which you
think about problems?

(1) William A. Wulf, "Trends in the Design and Implementation
      of Programming Languages," Selected Reprints in Software,
      Second Edition, IEEE Computer Society, 1982, page 21.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Correct Length of a string

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-21T17:15:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CEA80BE.56AF31F1@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<ac0ej0$vg1$01$1@news.t-online.com> <3CE3D754.C21DEE2@shaw.ca> <928495c6.0205162043.677d0d76@posting.google.com> <uecd8p3o353a60@corp.supernews.com> <acb3bh$dtr$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <ueiqqnapdsh4a3@corp.supernews.com> <acdoho$g8$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```


Howard Brazee wrote:

> On 20-May-2002, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Yes. but it isn't yet part of the standard.

Nothing to do with PERFORMING or EXITING - but your observation 'it isn't yet
part of the standard'.

I'm not aware of the implications of that comment for mainframes, but it's a
real dud when you come to the PC-world. Bill might indicate percentages, but
it's a fair bet that *most* of any future Standard(s) result(s) from vendor
extensions. The ideal is of course that J4/WG4 pick up on this stuff real pronto
- but that's another story......

Something that appealed to you at one stage, (as a debugging tool as I recall) -
Screen Section. Of course we now know you aren't going to get it from IBM, and
it is still not a part of the Standard until December 2002.

But the Screen Section has been around for years (10 or more ?), and in a DOS
environment was an absolute *must*. The alternative a screen painter tool, just
to pretty up the screen and then simplistic ANSI ACCEPT/DISPLAY. The impact of
this 'unofficial' feature on the PC-world was tremendous.

Take another extension example - should we have waited for the December 2002
Standard to be issued before getting into OO. How would COBOL programmers have
linked into other languages or the Web in a COBOL-oriented fashion ? (Just think
- Pete Dashwood wouldn't be able to get all excited about components !).

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Correct Length of screen section

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-05-22T07:35:29+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CEB3C31.E4494EDD@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<ac0ej0$vg1$01$1@news.t-online.com> <3CE3D754.C21DEE2@shaw.ca> <928495c6.0205162043.677d0d76@posting.google.com> <uecd8p3o353a60@corp.supernews.com> <acb3bh$dtr$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <ueiqqnapdsh4a3@corp.supernews.com> <acdoho$g8$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3CEA80BE.56AF31F1@shaw.ca>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> 
> I'm not aware of the implications of that comment for mainframes, but it's a
> real dud when you come to the PC-world. Bill might indicate percentages, but
> it's a fair bet that *most* of any future Standard(s) result(s) from vendor
> extensions. 

Vendor extensions are, almost by definition, unique to each vendor. 
Certainly other vendors copy their own interpretations of what others
have done, but not necessarily in an identical way.

> But the Screen Section has been around for years (10 or more ?), and in a DOS
> environment was an absolute *must*. The alternative a screen painter tool, just
> to pretty up the screen and then simplistic ANSI ACCEPT/DISPLAY. The impact of
> this 'unofficial' feature on the PC-world was tremendous.

I never liked Screen Section nor did I find a screen painter that I
liked (other than those I wrote myself).  The main issue was giving a
decent user interface rather than a pretty screen.  I always do
field-at-a-time accepts so that I can verify each field as they leave it
and take appropriate function key action at the field.

Screen section tends to be screen-at-a-time accepts like mainframe CICS
screens which (IMHO) is a very poor user interface.  

I also developed for non=IBM-PC micros, mostly Multiuser-DOS as well as
PCs (and also for MP/M and Unix) and had to cater for green screen or
colour terminals, mono and VGA PCs.  So all my base code screen handling
catered for user selection of colours (or highlighting attributes) over
several different mechanisms.

Of course my own screen generators are used to create the
working-storage and field-by-field accepts and display code from text
file layouts and field information.

But Screen Section ?  Tried it. Hated it. So did my users.


>  How would COBOL programmers have
> linked into other languages or the Web in a COBOL-oriented fashion ? 

You don't need OO to 'link into other languages' or 'the Web'.  I have
ANS85 code providing Web services for several years (without any of
those thin wrapper libraries).

All that is required is a standard way of interfacing that each language
or component can agree on. When Cobol was developed on mainframes and
then later on Unix and PCs there was only a requirement to interface to
what was around at the time.  OO gave implementors another attempt at
providing a different way of linking.  By following 'what is currently
around' such as C++, COM and CORBA, they also provide interoperability
to those.

This is not directly a result of OO, but of a re-implementation of the
way it implements linking.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Correct Length of screen section

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-21T21:36:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CEABDEB.663F203F@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<ac0ej0$vg1$01$1@news.t-online.com> <3CE3D754.C21DEE2@shaw.ca> <928495c6.0205162043.677d0d76@posting.google.com> <uecd8p3o353a60@corp.supernews.com> <acb3bh$dtr$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <ueiqqnapdsh4a3@corp.supernews.com> <acdoho$g8$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3CEA80BE.56AF31F1@shaw.ca> <3CEB3C31.E4494EDD@Azonic.co.nz>`

```


Richard Plinston wrote:

I kinda guessed you might jump in - see below regarding OO - but I tried to word it
to put  you off <G>

> James J. Gavan wrote:
> >
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

True. But given the extension has a general appeal to J4, modified or otherwise, that
specific extension, can eventually finish up as part of the Standard.

>
> > But the Screen Section has been around for years (10 or more ?), and in a DOS
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Other than that I was quite happy with Screen Section, in it's time, like you Accept
Screen just didn't appeal to me. My approach, like yours, was one field at a time and
validate - rather than a blurb at the end, "Now go back and correct field X which is
invalid".

I've carried the same approach over to GUI classes- works just fine - for desktop,
but I think it comes unglued if and when I get involved with either/both Web or
Components. Certainly for the Web I would suggest much of our design thoughts  tend
to be limited by technology currently available, (i.e. speed - response time). So
Web-wise I suspect this makes people think in terms of "Accept Screen" then hit
<SEND> button.

That's to-day's technology, which we know will increment by leaps and bounds.
Interestingly from to-day's paper, A Dr. Jim Metzler from Boston's Northeastern
University, "....says the promise of 3rd and 4th generation wireless networks that
can send information at speeds of 200 kilobits per second will transform the way
business people communicate". He's speculating today - and when it is 400 or 600
kilobits !

Given that possibility, even a desktop approach to a 10 x 24 table GUI would fit
reasonably snugly into a Web design.

>
> Screen section tends to be screen-at-a-time accepts like mainframe CICS
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Here we go <G> - Deliberately phrased it "COBOL-oriented fashion".

>
> You don't need OO to 'link into other languages' or 'the Web'.  I have
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> way it implements linking.

I was more than well aware you have followed several easy solutions - and you can
probably give me a good one, for say, getting at M/S Word. But I find it attractive
that if I can use an OLE class in COBOL to do the same, I can *still* think COBOL.
(Call it , "Give me that old time religion ....." <G>)

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Correct Length of screen section

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-05-22T13:45:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<acg74d$b80$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<ac0ej0$vg1$01$1@news.t-online.com> <3CE3D754.C21DEE2@shaw.ca> <928495c6.0205162043.677d0d76@posting.google.com> <uecd8p3o353a60@corp.supernews.com> <acb3bh$dtr$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <ueiqqnapdsh4a3@corp.supernews.com> <acdoho$g8$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3CEA80BE.56AF31F1@shaw.ca> <3CEB3C31.E4494EDD@Azonic.co.nz> <3CEABDEB.663F203F@shaw.ca>`

```
I have wished to see an environment similar to what MicroSoft is pushing for
NET - where I can buy an integrated CoBOL/Java IDE to create applications
using the strengths of both languages:

This is where I am attracted to OO.   Interdependencies scare me, even when
they're called "reusability".  But transparently using an object to use the
best tool available for screen I/O (or business rules) is very attractive.

With IBM now pushing mainframes as Web servers, maybe we will have a reason
to get OO CoBOL working successfully on the mainframe for enterprise
applications.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Correct Length of screen section

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-05-24T08:13:30+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CEDE81A.CD10A144@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<ac0ej0$vg1$01$1@news.t-online.com> <3CE3D754.C21DEE2@shaw.ca> <928495c6.0205162043.677d0d76@posting.google.com> <uecd8p3o353a60@corp.supernews.com> <acb3bh$dtr$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <ueiqqnapdsh4a3@corp.supernews.com> <acdoho$g8$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3CEA80BE.56AF31F1@shaw.ca> <3CEB3C31.E4494EDD@Azonic.co.nz> <3CEABDEB.663F203F@shaw.ca> <acg74d$b80$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> I have wished to see an environment similar to what MicroSoft is pushing for
> NET - where I can buy an integrated CoBOL/Java IDE to create applications
> using the strengths of both languages:

I don't think that MS will ever push anything that gives _Java_
integration.  Apparently it is possible with .NET to write C# programs
using some of the Java syntax and C# programs using much of the Cobol
syntax.  .NET common runtime is targetted at _killing_ Java.

Some of the grander schemes that have been hyped for .NET also seem to
be somewhat suspect.  Superficially it may seem attractive to use a
service over the net rather than developing your own or buying some
software.  But it all comes down to who has control.

MS seem to be trying to take the IT turf that in the retail industry is
occupied by the Loyalty Card companies.

The common runtime is the 'Loyalty Card', Passport is the 'card swipe',
MS hopes to sit between the sellers (and service providers) and the
buyers taking a percentage rake off and gathering all the data they can
and then selling it to whoever will pay.


As for Cobol/Jave: "But what of our children if instead they have my
brains and your looks ?".

> With IBM now pushing mainframes as Web servers, maybe we will have a reason
> to get OO CoBOL working successfully on the mainframe for enterprise
> applications.

You don't need OO for Web servers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Correct Length of a string

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-21T12:30:21-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ace07l$8qp$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Correct Length of a string

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-05-22T09:40:59+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CEB599B.D69EF84E@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<ac0ej0$vg1$01$1@news.t-online.com> <3CE3D754.C21DEE2@shaw.ca> <928495c6.0205162043.677d0d76@posting.google.com> <uecd8p3o353a60@corp.supernews.com> <acb3bh$dtr$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <ueiqqnapdsh4a3@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:
> 
> Howard, I mentioned one way in which EXIT PERFORM has
> shaped the way in which I think about problems. How will the
> availability of EXIT PERFORM shape the ways in which you
> think about problems?

The way that _I_ think about 'EXIT PERFORM' is that it is a 'GO TO' to
the end of the PERFORM range.

Actually I don't have too much problem with EXIT PERFORM, but I dislike
EXIT PARAGRAPH and EXIT SECTION, mainly because they may be misused (eg
EXIT PARAGRAPH within a PERFORMed SECTION or vv).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Correct Length of a string

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-05-22T13:52:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<acg7gd$bcq$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<ac0ej0$vg1$01$1@news.t-online.com> <3CE3D754.C21DEE2@shaw.ca> <928495c6.0205162043.677d0d76@posting.google.com> <uecd8p3o353a60@corp.supernews.com> <acb3bh$dtr$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <ueiqqnapdsh4a3@corp.supernews.com> <3CEB599B.D69EF84E@Azonic.co.nz>`

```

On 22-May-2002, Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

> The way that _I_ think about 'EXIT PERFORM' is that it is a 'GO TO' to
> the end of the PERFORM range.

Which can be useful if the program needs to be modified and we don't want to
redesign the logic.  The old way would be to set a switch.  EXIT PERFORM or
EXIT PARAGRAPH will be clearer than switches.

> Actually I don't have too much problem with EXIT PERFORM, but I dislike
> EXIT PARAGRAPH and EXIT SECTION, mainly because they may be misused (eg
> EXIT PARAGRAPH within a PERFORMed SECTION or vv).

I don't use SECTIONs, except where required copy members have them.  I also
don't write new programs with THRU clauses.  And when EXIT PARAGRAPH is
available, the last excuse to have EXIT paragraphs will go away.  So why use
SECTIONs?

What bad things are likely to happen with an EXIT PARAGRAPH within a
PERFORMed SECTION?  (or "w", whatever that is?)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Correct Length of a string

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-05-24T07:49:46+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CEDE28A.2AB94783@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<ac0ej0$vg1$01$1@news.t-online.com> <3CE3D754.C21DEE2@shaw.ca> <928495c6.0205162043.677d0d76@posting.google.com> <uecd8p3o353a60@corp.supernews.com> <acb3bh$dtr$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <ueiqqnapdsh4a3@corp.supernews.com> <3CEB599B.D69EF84E@Azonic.co.nz> <acg7gd$bcq$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> I don't use SECTIONs, except where required copy members have them.  I also
> don't write new programs with THRU clauses.  And when EXIT PARAGRAPH is
> available, the last excuse to have EXIT paragraphs will go away.  So why use
> SECTIONs?

I don't use sections either, but many do and the language caters for
them.  Any discussion of the language and its features cannot ignore a
whole group of programmers just because they use sections (tempting
though it is).
 
> What bad things are likely to happen with an EXIT PARAGRAPH within a
> PERFORMed SECTION?  (or "w", whatever that is?)

"w" was vv or vice versa.  Having an EXIT SECTION within a performed
paragraph is obviously bad, but unlikely to be detected by the compiler
or the run-time leading to program out of control.

An EXIT PARAGRAPH within a performed section has the property that it
doesn't exit the perform but merely goes to a point immediately before
the next label.  It is an unnamed GO TO.  Some programmers have
indicated that they add paragraph labels into sections as commentary
separators (a practice that is very bad in my opinion in any case).  The
point that the EXIT PARAGRAPH does its GO TO can be changed by adding or
commenting out these commentary names.

In the case of real GO TOs the label names can be searched for, or if
the label is commented out the compiler will complain.  With EXIT
PARAGRAPH the action can be silently changed by modifying code
_at_some_other_point_.

A similar problem occurs with combinations of NEXT SENTENCE and END-IF. 
One contributor here contrived a mechanism that defeated the intent of
the standard and allowed this combination.  Again with this the NEXT
SENTENCE acted as a GO TO to the unnamed point that was the next full
stop.  Some one else working in the area of this stop may remove it or
add another of their own because this stop is otherwise unrelated to
logic flow and there is no indication at that point that it is a target
for a jump.

If you are going to use a GO TO then make it explicit so that no one
misses it.  Using unnamed GO TOs such as these may lead to problems when
changes are made somewhere else.

> And when EXIT PARAGRAPH is
> available, the last excuse to have EXIT paragraphs will go away.  So why use
> SECTIONs?

I haven't used sections, thru or exit paragraphs for many years.  There
hasn't been any reasonable excuse for them since '85.  Actually '85 only
made it easier, I revised all my idioms and erased all need for exit
paragraphs when I was still using pure '74 syntax (which I was doing in
some systems until mid 90s).
```

old one that causes the errors.

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> some systems until mid 90s).

I understand the argument that maintenance is easier if you don't have to
re-structure the logic to get out of a loop.  But I think that was mainly an
excuse to keep programming in a familiar way.
```

by
> byte from 1000 by -1 until the string <> Space. The Length is (1000 - 984)
=
> 16, but I don�t want check the string 984 times.

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

Method 1:

COMPUTE LEN = FUNCTION STORED-CHAR-LENGTH(MYSTRING)

If your compiler doesn't support this function, get a compiler that does.

Method 2:

MOVE 0 TO KOUNT.
INSPECT MYSTRING TALLYING KOUNT FOR CHARACTERS BEFORE 'bb'

where 'bb' is two consecutive spaces.

How about it? The string doesn't make a lick of sense as it stands, so

delimiter.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Correct Length of a string

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-17T02:41:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CE46D8D.FF899A60@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<ac0ej0$vg1$01$1@news.t-online.com> <foXE8.44522$fU2.4156549@bin8.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3CE445B7.B3C6500C@shaw.ca> <VgZE8.19221$e66.1682242@bin6.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```


JerryMouse wrote:

> > > COMPUTE LEN = FUNCTION STORED-CHAR-LENGTH(MYSTRING)
> > >
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> delimiter.

Can you confirm would your F/J Function Stored-Char-Length in fact handle that
"Mary had....." example ?. If so,  more reason for it being part of the Standard
rather than just an extension in one compiler - and that's nothing new -
probably a major part of the enhancements to the Standard are as a result of
individual vendor extensions.

Like so many things there are always work arounds, but you aren't left guessing
with :-

compute Length = function TrueLength (my-Text)

And rather like that ditsy word in OO, 'Encapsulation', we don't really care
what goes on under the wraps, just so long as it works efficiently and gives us
the result we want.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Correct Length of a string

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-05-17T14:33:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ac3429$q52$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<ac0ej0$vg1$01$1@news.t-online.com> <foXE8.44522$fU2.4156549@bin8.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3CE445B7.B3C6500C@shaw.ca>`

```

On 16-May-2002, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:

> > Method 2:
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> the lamb, but........?

Then change it to twenty consecutive spaces.  If the answer is greater than
980, loop backwards.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Correct Length of a string

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-17T14:09:26-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ac3khj$23u$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<ac0ej0$vg1$01$1@news.t-online.com> <foXE8.44522$fU2.4156549@bin8.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3CE445B7.B3C6500C@shaw.ca> <ac3429$q52$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
In the real world of "free form" input data, there is NO "safe" number of
spaces to check for.  Especially as there are (at least) three"absolutely"
safe ways to check for this discussed elsewhere in this thread:

1) Do an INSPECT of the REVERSE of the field looking for first non-space
character

2) loop backwards thru the field finding the first non-space character

3) Loop forward with reference modification until you find the first *ALL*
spaces ending section of the code.

  ***

If this is something that you really do LOTS in your application and
performance/efficiency is essential, then PROBABLY (depending on field size,
compiler, OS, etc) #2 is most likely best.  My *guess* is that for many
programmers #3 is "best-self-documenting" (as it does what a "verbal"
description of the problem is asking for.  As far as the "simplest" (fewest
number of COBOL statements goes), then I would go with #1 - but that's
because I am "extremely" comfortable with intrinsic functions.  The only
thing that I would NEVER do is "assume" that some specific number of spaces
means that ALL the remaining characters are spaces (unless, of course, some
other part of the application has already done a compress multiple to single
space - for some other application reason).
```

Hello all,

I would appreciate your feedback on the following new product.

Turbo Reports for COBOL is a powerful application report writer for
COBOL applications. Using the power of visual design coupled with a
sophisticated code generator, now developers can design and develop
COBOL application report programs much faster and with higher quality
than using traditional development methods. The built-in Version
Control capability ensures full life-cycle change control support and
ease of maintenance.

With Turbo Reports for COBOL, companies can now:

- Achieve much improved productivity with visual report design.
- Reduce cost by automating the development of report presentation
code.
- Realize faster project delivery at lower costs
- Obtain outstanding run-time performance due to compiled code and
static database binds.
- Leverage the same tool for traditional and Web reports.
- Reduce database and file access time by running multiple reports in
a single database pass.
- Accomplish easier maintenance resulting in lower maintenance costs.
- Supports Z/OS(MVS), Win32, Unix, VM, Tandem and all other COBOL
platforms
- Realize faster report change implementation with automatic version
control.

If you or someone on your team could download the demo version from
our site at the following link, I would appreciate your feedback on
this product.

http://www.canamsoftware.com/product/tr4cobol/index.html

or to download an electronic brochure:

http://www.canamsoftware.com/product/tr4cobol/tr4cobol.pdf

I am very interested in understanding how developers like yourself can
benefit from a tool like this.

I look forward to hearing from you soon.

Regards,


Asif R. Khan
Canam Software Labs, Inc.
khan@canamsoftware.com

## Thread: Printing to file or printer with Micro Focus OCDS for Linux — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Printing to file or printer with Micro Focus OCDS for Linux

- **From:** "Panos Zotos" <pzotos@syntax.gr>
- **Date:** 2002-05-16T17:42:17+03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ac0gdr$c1j$1@usenet.otenet.gr>`

```
Hi Everybody,

We are trying to upgrade one of our customers from Microsoft Cobol for SCO
Unix to Micro Focus OCDS 4.1.40 on RedHat Linux and we have one difficulty
concerning printing to the spooler or to a text file.

The case is the following:
Using Microsoft Cobol, the customer can start a program that uses the
printer using a specific switch that redirects all print jobs to a file (for
example: ./prog -pf). Without this switch every print job goes to the
spooler.

Now that he moves to OCDS he wants to keep this functionality, but there is
no such switch in OCDS (as far as I know). Is there anything we can do to
achieve this without changing the code?

Thanks in advance for any response.

Panos
```

## Thread: COBOL, AS/400, Linux — 2 this month / 3 total

_Topics: [`AS/400, iSeries, RPG`](topics.md#as400)_

### COBOL, AS/400, Linux

- **From:** david.silber@hpdsoftware.com (David Silber)
- **Date:** 2002-05-17T08:39:58-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ad85f189.0205170739.19440de1@posting.google.com>`

```
Q.1 Has IBM or another vendor (MF? Acucorp?) announced a COBOL
compiler to work in the AS/400 Linux environment?
Q.2 If so, or if it will be forthcoming, is there any doubt that ASCII
will be the default collating sequence?
Q.3 I've read that there are tools to convert EBCDIC files to ASCII.
Does anyone know where I could get further information?
Thanks. David Silber

P.S. Has anyone heard whether IBM has YET made a commitment to provide
a COBOL 2002-compliant compiler for the AS/400 (i-Series)? This was a
question I posted some months back and William Klein, among others,
reported no commitment by IBM --- not even for the flagship S390
(z-Series).
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL, AS/400, Linux

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-17T15:29:49-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ac3p8a$1le$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<ad85f189.0205170739.19440de1@posting.google.com>`

```
Having looked at:
  http://www-1.ibm.com/servers/eserver/iseries/linux/faq.html

which says,
 "iSeries provides the support for OS/400, Linux, Java, Domino, WebSphere,
UNIX, and Windows 2000 Server applications. In a single server, you can run
your core business and e-business applications, ..."

and

 http://www-1.ibm.com/servers/eserver/iseries/linux/resc.html

and  (more generally)

 http://www-1.ibm.com/servers/eserver/iseries/linux/edition/index.html

It does *not* appear that IBM has (yet) announced any COBOL solution for
Linux on OS/400.  HOWEVER,  given the "full" Java support, I would expect
PERCobol to work there (now - or soon).  Also, if you look at:

 http://www.microfocus.com/press/releases/20020219.asp

which is the Micro Focus (and IBM) announcement that they (MF) will be
providing the (a?) COBOL for Linux on  z/OS, it wouldn't surprise me if they
also provide an OS/400 version (as they do already for a number of other
Linux environments).

 ***

My personal GUESS is that this Linux will be EBCDIC and not ASCII "as the
default" - but I could be completely wrong.  I have looked at:
  http://www-1.ibm.com/servers/eserver/zseries/os/linux/facts.html

and can't tell what Linux on z/OS is, but I would GUESS that OS/400 would be
"comparable".

I know that Micro Focus has an "intelligent" ASCII/EBCDIC data converter
(that can read COBOL copy books and convert DISPLAY data while leaving
COMP-? data alone.   I think that Reallia does as well, but I am not certain
about Fujitsu.  IBM's VA product has ways of telling whether to use an ASCII
or EBCDIC "solution" - but I don't think they have an "intelligent"
conversion tool.

  ***

No, IBM has *NOT* yet made any public statement about when/if they will
support the next COBOL Standard on *any* platform.  It should be noted that
although there is now a 99.99999 probability that there will be an approved
2002 (or at worst 2003) Standard, until it actually finishes its ISO
approval steps, there is no guarantee.  Therefore, any vendor (such as Micro
Focus) that announces support for a 2002 Standard *could* end up as
embarrassed as Fujitsu did when they announced support for a 2000 Standard.


  ***

I hope that this helps.
```

---

<a id="t-4053253a82"></a>
## Thread: Substring — 9 msgs

### Substring

- **From:** "Eddy Scheire" <fa099784@skynet.be>
- **Date:** 2002-05-17T22:33:32+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ce5694c$0$6976$ba620e4c@news.skynet.be>`

```
Hey everyone,

I encountered a very strange phenomenon recently. In a program I wrote
something like :

Move An-Element1(Counter1 + 2:Counter2 - 4) To Another-Element1
Move An-Element2(Counter3 + 5:Counter4 - 6) To Another-Element2

This code worked fine when I ran this program using a debugger (Expeditor).
When running the program without (and thus processing the production data)
it seemed like losing track of the values of this counters and by
consequence moved the wrong part of the elements.
All the counters were initialized and filled with a correct value before
they were used in the above mentioned moves.
I changed the code to :

Compute Counter5 = Counter1 + 2
Compute Counter6 = Counter2 - 4
Compute Counter7 = Counter3 + 5
Compute Counter8 = Counter4 - 6
Move An-Element1(Counter5:Counter6) To Another-Element1
Move An-Element2(Counter7:Counter8) To Another-Element2

and it worked fine.

Has anyone an idea why the first version worked with the debugger and didn't
without?
```

#### ↳ Re: Substring

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-17T16:01:18-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ac3r2u$lf4$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3ce5694c$0$6976$ba620e4c@news.skynet.be>`

```
Given your reference to "Expeditor" (which I assume you mean as "Xpeditor")
my guess is that you are running on an IBM mainframe.  If this is not
correct, let me/us know where you are running and with what compiler.
Clearly this code SHOULD work the same in all environments and with all
compiler options/directives.  I have, however, a couple of GUESSES as to
what may be happening.

1) (Most likely in my opinion),  When you tested this, you compiled with
TEST,NOOPT compiler options.  When you ran the "production" version, you had
NOTEST,OPT.  Although it is fairly unusual (now a days), there have been
cases in the past when IBM's "optimizer" has failed to produce the correct
code.  If this "problem" is recreatable, (i.e. it you can show the
production and test versions yielding different results), the I think you
should report the problem to IBM.  (Assuming the "test" version is the
expected results; if not, then report it to Compuware for a problem with
Xpeditor).

2) (Less likely)  Did you "cut-and-paste" your source code below from your
actual COBOL program?  If not, be VERY careful about where you do and do not
have spaces between "+" and "-" and following integer-literals.  The
difference between arithmetic expressions and unary operators is REAL easy
to miss.

3) (Medium-possible)  Have you checked that your run-time options are
identical in your test and non-test environments?  Check WSCLEAR (VS COBOL
II) or STORAGE (LE) particularly.  Also for SSRANGE and/or CHECK options.
```

#### ↳ Re: Substring

- **From:** Edward Reid <edward@paleo.org>
- **Date:** 2002-05-18T10:02:15-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.B90BD7270022D79410D31090@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<3ce5694c$0$6976$ba620e4c@news.skynet.be>`

```
On Fri, 17 May 2002 16:33:32 -0400, Eddy Scheire wrote
> Has anyone an idea why the first version worked with the debugger and didn't
> without?

As a platform-independent comment -- debuggers often inhibit 
optimization, and this sounds like an optimization bug.

Edward Reid
```

#### ↳ Re: Substring

- **From:** "DBuck" <dbuck@prairieinet.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-18T12:24:21-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ac62pf$n2om7$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de>`
- **References:** `<3ce5694c$0$6976$ba620e4c@news.skynet.be>`

```
It sounds like a problem with the optimizer.  I had a problem somewhat
similar to this a while back.  Try this:

MOVE AN-ELEMENT1 ((COUNTER1 + 2):(COUNTER2 - 4)) TO ANOTHER-ELEMENT1.
MOVE AN-ELEMENT2 ((COUNTER3 + 5):(COUNTER4 - 6)) TO ANOTHER-ELEMENT2.

Hope this helps....

Eddy Scheire <fa099784@skynet.be> wrote in message

didn't
> without?
>

>

Using arithmetic expressions both as the starting location and length are
part of the ANSI/ISO Standard - and have been since '85.

## Thread: Native COBOL compiler for Linux/Windows for $39.95 — 28 this month / 30 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Native COBOL compiler for Linux/Windows for $39.95

- **From:** "Shawn Gordon" <shawn@thekompany.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-17T21:36:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oJeF8.3221$eD2.82794@news2.east.cox.net>`

```
I don't like to post advertisings, but I know a lot of people have been
looking for something like this.  Our company (theKompany.com) released
today what we call KOBOL, which is an syntax highlighting IDE for COBOL
development, with support for the latest OO specification and 100% success
with the NIST COBOL85 test suites.  For one price you get Windows and Linux
and free lifetime upgrades.  The price is $39.95 for a download version and
$49.95 for the box (you can still download).  You can read details on it at
www.thekompany.com/products/kobol


Best Regards,
Shawn Gordon
President
theKompany.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Native COBOL compiler for Linux/Windows for $39.95

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-19T20:09:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ETF8.38609$th.3584349@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<oJeF8.3221$eD2.82794@news2.east.cox.net>`

```

"Shawn Gordon" <shawn@thekompany.com> wrote in message
news:oJeF8.3221$eD2.82794@news2.east.cox.net...
> I don't like to post advertisings, but I know a lot of people have been
> looking for something like this.  Our company (theKompany.com) released
> today what we call KOBOL, which is an syntax highlighting IDE for COBOL
> development, with support for the latest OO specification and 100% success
> with the NIST COBOL85 test suites.  For one price you get Windows and
Linux
> and free lifetime upgrades.  The price is $39.95 for a download version
and
> $49.95 for the box (you can still download).  You can read details on it
at
> www.thekompany.com/products/kobol

Well, I couldn't find the demo download, so I bought it.

Observations after about 20 minutes of playing:

1. It does not generate native, stand alone code. To run a program compiled
with the product, you are required to install the package on the target
machine. Further, the product doesn't generate OBJ files and does not use a
Linker, leading me to believe it generates pseudo-code requiring a runtime
system.

2. There being no documentation I can find, one wonders how to generate a
text file (CR/LF delimited). I can get a non-delimited sequential file, but
not a LINE SEQUENTIAL file. Haven't tried relative or ISAM.

3. I can't figure out how to assign a file name to a variable as in:
   SELECT MYFILE ASSIGN TO DATA-NAME

the construct:
   SELECT MYFILE ASSIGN TO 'C:\TEMP\TESTDATA.TXT'
does, however work as expected.

4. There are, of course, no windows capabilities: The product is much like
FJ V3  in that it works on/in the command line. On our XP machine, the
product works quite well.

5. The integrated editor/compiler/maker thingy works well - even has
context-sensitive colored lines. I can't figure out how to set the tabs. Oh
well.

6. So, after 20 minutes, I'd say KOBOL fits between TinyCOBOL and FJ3
(simply because FJ3 seems more robust). That opinion may change, even
dramatically, after more experimentation.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Native COBOL compiler for Linux/Windows for $39.95

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-05-20T08:35:31+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CE8A743.233904@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<oJeF8.3221$eD2.82794@news2.east.cox.net> <3ETF8.38609$th.3584349@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
JerryMouse wrote:
> 
> Well, I couldn't find the demo download, so I bought it.

There was no obvious direct link to the demo versions, but they are
there.  I went to one of the other products, found a link to that demo,
went to the ftp page for this, went up a directory and found the
koboldemo directory.

> 5. The integrated editor/compiler/maker thingy works well - even has
> context-sensitive colored lines. I can't figure out how to set the tabs. Oh
> well.

I have always disliked do-it-all IDEs, especially Fujitsu's.  They are
an anachromism developed for single-user CP/M (eg Turbo-Pascal 3) and
DOS to overcome the 'difficulty' of using command line to fire off
edits, compiles, test runs, etc. Even Windows is a poor development
environment that justifies using an IDE only because it is too difficult
and error prone to try using multiple separate tools. With a decent
multitasking OS and a decent command line with call back and editor (or
even DOS's DOSKEY) one can build much better personalised development
mechanisms (IMHO).

I mostly use a multi-user/multi-tasking DOS system with XTree Gold and
TSE (Semware) on two or more tasks and fire off make and tests on other
task consoles.

On linux I substitute Gnome Terminal, Midnight Commander and SETedit. 
YMMV.

With both TSE and SETedit I have built Cobol syntax highlighting that I
can put up with (overuse of colour is distracting) and have keywords on
keyboard macros.  I am still working on tabs for SETedit.
 
> 6. So, after 20 minutes, I'd say KOBOL fits between TinyCOBOL and FJ3
> (simply because FJ3 seems more robust). That opinion may change, even
> dramatically, after more experimentation.

As far as I could tell (on Linux) it _is_ TinyCobol bundled into an IDE
that is a version of one of their other IDEs.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Native COBOL compiler for Linux/Windows for $39.95

- **From:** "Shawn Gordon" <shawn@thekompany.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-20T13:20:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OK6G8.44502$eD2.897373@news2.east.cox.net>`
- **References:** `<oJeF8.3221$eD2.82794@news2.east.cox.net> <3ETF8.38609$th.3584349@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3CE8A743.233904@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
No this is *not* tiny Cobol, this is our own translator which generates a
native binary, it doesn't require a run time, and is not interpreted.  It
has been about 3 years in development at this stage.

Also the web site has been fixed and the demo link is now there.  We have a
manual for the IDE that somehow didn't make it in the package, but that
should be fixed today, and we are putting the compiler documentation
together now.

"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3CE8A743.233904@Azonic.co.nz...
> JerryMouse wrote:
> >
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> > context-sensitive colored lines. I can't figure out how to set the tabs.
Oh
> > well.
>
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> that is a version of one of their other IDEs.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Native COBOL compiler for Linux/Windows for $39.95

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-20T22:04:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<UpeG8.51955$th.4750831@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<oJeF8.3221$eD2.82794@news2.east.cox.net> <3ETF8.38609$th.3584349@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3CE8A743.233904@Azonic.co.nz> <OK6G8.44502$eD2.897373@news2.east.cox.net>`

```

"Shawn Gordon" <shawn@thekompany.com> wrote in message
news:OK6G8.44502$eD2.897373@news2.east.cox.net...
> No this is *not* tiny Cobol, this is our own translator which generates a
> native binary, it doesn't require a run time, and is not interpreted.  It
> has been about 3 years in development at this stage.

My mistake.

I compiled the "Hello World" program and ran it OK.

I copied the TEST1.EXE file to another machine (Win98) and attempted to run
it.

And got the message:

"A required DLL file, CYGWIN1.DLL, was not found"

So I copied TEST1.EXE to a Win2K machine and got a similar message (although
Win2K is more verbose).

I therefore concluded that more than the 'native binary' was required and I
assumed the missing stuff was either a run time or installed version of the
compiler. So, I'll amend my statement:

The resulting EXE does not work on some machines on which the product was
not installed.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Native COBOL compiler for Linux/Windows for $39.95

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "JJ" <jj@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-21T00:56:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<iXgG8.67399$e66.6214839@bin6.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<oJeF8.3221$eD2.82794@news2.east.cox.net> <3ETF8.38609$th.3584349@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3CE8A743.233904@Azonic.co.nz> <OK6G8.44502$eD2.897373@news2.east.cox.net> <UpeG8.51955$th.4750831@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
FYI -

Cygwin is a free DLL from http://www.cygwin.com/.  It's a DLL that makes it
easier to port Unix programs to Windows.

So I guess their claim that it doesn't require a runtime is true in that it
doesn't require a runtime from them, it does require an additional
third-party component.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-21T15:02:08+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<82hkeu0ndp2p52vmle1cf4lg2hktud1ok1@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<oJeF8.3221$eD2.82794@news2.east.cox.net> <3ETF8.38609$th.3584349@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3CE8A743.233904@Azonic.co.nz> <OK6G8.44502$eD2.897373@news2.east.cox.net> <UpeG8.51955$th.4750831@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
When I purchased the KOBOL thingy, I found the CYGWIN support package
as a downloadable file as well.  Cygwin provides a runtime environment
for unix/linux applications on the Windows platform.  As such it is
fairly robust - I am running a hardware emulator (Hercules) that was
developed for Linux.  I compiled it under Windows/Cygwin, and it works
perfectly (I can now run MVS on my PC)

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Native COBOL compiler for Linux/Windows for $39.95

- **From:** epc8@juno.com (E P Chandler)
- **Date:** 2002-05-23T20:15:03-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7f64e2ff.0205231915.3248f681@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<oJeF8.3221$eD2.82794@news2.east.cox.net> <3ETF8.38609$th.3584349@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com> wrote in message news:<3ETF8.38609> 
> 2. There being no documentation I can find, one wonders how to generate a
> text file (CR/LF delimited). I can get a non-delimited sequential file, but
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> dramatically, after more experimentation.

The docs supplied with the new version 1.0.1 are some help, but they
leave stuff things out which might not be obvious without a background
in Unix/Linux.

After adding a file to a project, you have to select the "main" file
which corresponds to the main function in a C program. From the
project tab, right click on the file which corresponds to "main". Then
"select as main" from the menu. Then create a makefile, then try to
compile.

Too bad the supplied docs don't cover this. Perhaps the purchased
version is better.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<7f64e2ff.0205201306.8b71500@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<oJeF8.3221$eD2.82794@news2.east.cox.net>`

```
"Shawn Gordon" <shawn@thekompany.com> wrote in message news:<oJeF8.3221$eD2.82794@news2.east.cox.net>...
> I don't like to post advertisings, but I know a lot of people have been
> looking for something like this.  Our company (theKompany.com) released
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> theKompany.com

I wish I had paid more attention to this note on their web site before
trying to install this software on Win 95:

"While we are working on support of Windows 95/98/ME for KOBOL,
unfortunately there is a serious bug in the Qt3 library from Trolltech
on these platforms that can cause problems with KOBOL. We still fully
support other Windows platforms (NT/2000/XP) and are working on a fix
for 95/98/ME. Buyers of KOBOL will naturally receive a free update
once the bug is resolved."

On Win 95, the installed program requires a missing DLL - MSVCP60.DLL,
which is a C++ runtime library file related to the program using the
STL (standard template library). Once that DLL was obtained, up came a
QT3 DLL problem related to ODBC32.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Native COBOL compiler for Linux/Windows for $39.95

- **From:** epc8@juno.com (E P Chandler)
- **Date:** 2002-05-22T00:20:19-07:00

- **Message-ID:** `<7f64e2ff.0205212320.149f00ee@posting.google.com>`

```
epc8@juno.com (E P Chandler) wrote in message news:<7f64e2ff.0205201306.8b71500@posting.google.com>...

> I wish I had paid more attention to this note on their web site before
> trying to install this software on Win 95:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> QT3 DLL problem related to ODBC32.

After rounding up some missing dlls updating others related to ODBC
and then installing the cygwin package from the vendor's website, I
was able to compile and run the supplied test program under Windows
95. The executable file for "hello, world" weighed in at 566K bytes.
This does not include the cygwin1.dll run time library which is 752K
bytes.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Native COBOL compiler for Linux/Windows for $39.95

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-22T15:39:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dZOG8.2401$L31.1418@nwrddc03.gnilink.net>`
- **References:** `<oJeF8.3221$eD2.82794@news2.east.cox.net> <7f64e2ff.0205201306.8b71500@posting.google.com> <7f64e2ff.0205212320.149f00ee@posting.google.com> <6GKG8.2860$%s1.364@nwrddc02.gnilink.net> <GoOG8.218728$M7.22139758@bin7.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```

JerryMouse <nospam@invalid.com> wrote in message
news:GoOG8.218728$M7.22139758@bin7.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com...
>
> "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> > If you are not sure, ZIP up the EXE (do not need the runtime DLL) and
send
> > to me; I'll run it through my checker and post results.
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>


What I meant was, how much of that overhead is non-code? You know, stock
images, cursors, default dialog templates, etc. included "by default" by the
compiler itself.

Here is an example. My Win32/BASIC compiler produces the equivalent to your
"Hello World" program an EXE with a file size of 6,144 bytes. No runtime
DLLs required other than the standard Windows DLLs ADVAPI32, KERNEL32,
OLE32,OLEAUT32, and USER32.

Disk image: 6144 bytes; relocation data 512 bytes, 5120 total code segment;
No resources  (My code= 192 bytes, Runtime library code embedded in exe=1868
bytes)

I have another program which is size 522,722 on disk; but the resources use
303,104 bytes of that. (code=183,296).

That's what I was interested in checking: how much code/non-code overhead
comes in your EXE file.  A half a megabyte of "code only" is a hell of a lot
of code. (Above program with 183K of code is about 8 kloc source)

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Native COBOL compiler for Linux/Windows for $39.95

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-24T12:10:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<A4qH8.10$9V.4@nwrddc03.gnilink.net>`
- **References:** `<oJeF8.3221$eD2.82794@news2.east.cox.net> <7f64e2ff.0205201306.8b71500@posting.google.com> <7f64e2ff.0205212320.149f00ee@posting.google.com> <6GKG8.2860$%s1.364@nwrddc02.gnilink.net> <GoOG8.218728$M7.22139758@bin7.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <dZOG8.2401$L31.1418@nwrddc03.gnilink.net> <7f64e2ff.0205231745.10131186@posting.google.com>`

```
E P Chandler <epc8@juno.com> wrote in message
news:7f64e2ff.0205231745.10131186@posting.google.com...
> "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:
>
> > What I meant was, how much of that overhead is non-code? You know, stock
> > images, cursors, default dialog templates, etc. included "by default" by
the
> > compiler itself.
>
> > That's what I was interested in checking: how much code/non-code
overhead
> > comes in your EXE file.  A half a megabyte of "code only" is a hell of a
lot
> > of code. (Above program with 183K of code is about 8 kloc source)
>
> After getting version 1.0.1, I've compiled the sample and am sending
> it to you via e-mail. Please let us know what you find.


Test1.exe size 566,741 (Decimal) bytes contains six sections
.txt  size x'20400'
.data size x'C00'
.bss size    x'00'
.stab         size x'7200'
.stabstr     size x'35000'

.txt is the compiled code (size 132,096 decimal). Seems like a lot for
'DISPLAY "Hello world"' even with the required Windows overhead.

.stab and .stabstr  (combined size > 240K decimal) appear to be symbol
literals, as though the stuff included when running with a debugger were
never removed in the production version. (There is no explicit
"Windows-recognized" debug data in the file). Some of the symbols are
clearly the file names of the header files included at compile time (e.g,
"/i686-pc-cygwin/include/sys/unistd.h" and "/cygnus/netrel/src/gcc-2.95")

However, many of those symbols may be used by the runtime (cygwin1.dll) for
its use, as there are a total of 3247 exported symbols. (I have never seen
sections ".stab" and ".stabstr" in PE executables before.)

The only directly imported Windows function is GetModuleHandleA from
KERNEL32.DLL; apparently all the real Windows importing is done by the
runtime DLL cygwin1.dll, from which test1.exe directly imports about 50
functions.

There are no resources (cursors, bitmaps, menus, etc) in the executable.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Native COBOL compiler for Linux/Windows for $39.95

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-28T13:10:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ClLI8.32305$R_4.20689@nwrddc02.gnilink.net>`
- **References:** `<oJeF8.3221$eD2.82794@news2.east.cox.net> <7f64e2ff.0205201306.8b71500@posting.google.com> <7f64e2ff.0205212320.149f00ee@posting.google.com> <6GKG8.2860$%s1.364@nwrddc02.gnilink.net> <GoOG8.218728$M7.22139758@bin7.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <dZOG8.2401$L31.1418@nwrddc03.gnilink.net> <7f64e2ff.0205231745.10131186@posting.google.com> <A4qH8.10$9V.4@nwrddc03.gnilink.net>`

```
Here is an update, after Mr. Chandler found a compiler switch to remove the
unneeded symbols from the compiled EXE


> ... I was able to get the size of the .exe
> down to 140K for a hello, world program by adding a -s switch to the
> command line for the link phase of g++ (the last command line needed).
This
> switch strips out un-needed symbols. I found it by reading the Cygwin FAQ
> at: http://cygwin.com/faq/faq.html
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> with the 566K version.

New program size: 141824 bytes
Sections:
.text  size   x'20400'
.data size   x'C00'
.bss size    x'00'
.idata  size x'600'


(Just as a personal note, it seems weird that 'include symbols' is the
default, and you have to do something special to get rid of them)
```

"Shawn Gordon" wrote:

> the installer has been fixed so that it includes the missing dll and the
> documentation for the IDE.  Please give it a try and you can email me
> directly with your comments.

> > development, with support for the latest OO specification and 100% success
> > with the NIST COBOL85 test suites.

Huh?

When writing a variable with USAGE COMP out to disk, whether 1, 2 or 4
bytes long, a value of 10 is translated into X'0D',X'0A' plus trailing
X'00'. Since this is a binary variable, not a character, why is LF
being translated to CR,LF on output?

(buggy program e-mailed)
```

## Thread: Wang Pace / Wang Cobol migration — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### Wang Pace / Wang Cobol migration

- **From:** "Bob Morris" <bobmorris60@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-20T22:13:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lyeG8.19144$V71.7174852@twister.socal.rr.com>`

```
American Cybernetic Corp (ACC) offers automated migration of

Wang Pace to Oracle, with Forms or PowerBuilder.

Wang Cobol to native MicroFocus or AcuCOBOL, with RDBMS database option.


WANG MIGRATION - GENERAL INFORMATION:
ACC's migration tools and support process offers a complete native
language conversion.
http://www.amcybernetic.com/legacy_wangpace.html


WANG COBOL MIGRATION:
ACC is a leader in advanced Wang Cobol migration technology. For Wang
Cobol the conversion path is to MicroFocus-Cobol, AcuCobol or AS/400
Cobol.  However for clients who wish to access their data in the Oracle
table format, ACC is the only firm who has successfully converted Wang
Cobol to native MF-Cobol including an Oracle access path and the
conversion of data to Oracle tables.
http://www.amcybernetic.com/wangcobol_features.html


WANG PACE MIGRATION:
ACC is the only firm to successfully convert Wang Pace to Oracle,
including website development, and Oracle training.

ACC's Wang Pace migration is an automated, efficient, economical process
that includes conversion and re-engineering techniques. It also allows
for advanced target platform opportunities while maintaining 1 to 1
functionality of existing Wang PACE applications. With ACC's
sophisticated migration tools, an automated conversion path can be
customized to meet the client's special requirements.
http://www.amcybernetic.com/legacy_wangpace_features.html


Homepage
http://www.amcybernetic.com
Phone:  301 961-1905
Fax:  301 765-1836
Contact:  Harry Steinberg
Location:  Bethesda, MD USA
```

### Project in Microfocus NetExpress

- **From:** Stefan Eckleder <Stefan_Eckleder@msg.de>
- **Date:** 2002-05-21T13:08:31+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CEA2AAF.B06A6E4F@msg.de>`

```
Hi,

I have created a project in Microfocus NetExpress. Now I want to compile
an link the files into the project on the command line. How can I do
this??

Thanks for your help
Stefan
```

#### ↳ Re: Project in Microfocus NetExpress

- **From:** "Lucas, Todd" <Todd.Lucas@C-Cubed.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-21T22:06:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<TxzG8.58573$Md6.1314409@news1.east.cox.net>`
- **References:** `<3CEA2AAF.B06A6E4F@msg.de>`

```
Open the NetExpress command prompt and enter "CBLLINK.EXE" to display all
options - this will compile and link your Cobol source with desired RTS
options (ie. Dynamic / Static RTS, etc.)

HTH - TL

"Stefan Eckleder" <Stefan_Eckleder@msg.de> wrote in message
news:3CEA2AAF.B06A6E4F@msg.de...

>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

```
RTFM

    You can use the command line program

"COBOL"

OR

"CBLLINK"

    "COBOL" works just like it did on 16 bit product.
    "CBLLINK" is better bet for EXE/DLL

#### ↳ Re: Project in Microfocus NetExpress

- **From:** "Panos Zotos" <pzotos@syntax.gr>
- **Date:** 2002-05-22T16:42:17+03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<acg74t$pcg$1@usenet.otenet.gr>`
- **References:** `<3CEA2AAF.B06A6E4F@msg.de>`

```
You can try the PROJMAKE.EXE utility supplied with Net Express. This builds
a project directly from the command line without having to use cobol and
cbllink, using all the build settings defined in the project.

example: projmake -build myproj.app

regards

Panos
