```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> 
> Given that you *can* build from the command line, I'm still very curious as to
> what advantage there is in doing this, when it can be done so easily from the
> IDE - even an 'application' which is a single program ?

The advantage of being able to build from a command line is that these
command lines can be included in a makefile as well as the dependancies
that will indicate when it is required to be actioned.

Quite often some shared code is enhanced while working in one program
which may, for example, change the way it interfaces.  Developing by
using whatever is best for the programmer in the way of file managers
and editors (rather than what is mandated by the IDE) and then using
make, in another task, ensures (or should do) that all affected code is
recompiled without the programmer having to sit and watch while clicking
around the system in the IDE.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Project in Microfocus NetExpress

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-24T03:35:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CEDB528.A808E642@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3CEA2AAF.B06A6E4F@msg.de> <acg74t$pcg$1@usenet.otenet.gr> <3CEBD067.13824358@shaw.ca> <3CEDEBAA.6D259413@Azonic.co.nz>`

```


Richard Plinston wrote:
> 
> James J. Gavan wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> that will indicate when it is required to be actioned.

The IDE is your 'makefile', all dependencies included in the project
tree.
(I've never used it, but you can customize your rebuilds).
> 
> Quite often some shared code is enhanced while working in one program
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> around the system in the IDE.

OK your response was informative Richard - BUT - TWO CLICKS in the IDE,
TWO CLICKS - no need to mosey around - (1) Select 'Project' drop down
menu -> (2a) Compile this program, (2b) Rebuild - immediately triggers
re-compiles of all changed programs, including changes to copyfiles, or
(2c) Rebuild All.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Project in Microfocus NetExpress

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-24T08:14:19-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<S7qH8.13222$Mn6.1607924@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<3CEA2AAF.B06A6E4F@msg.de> <acg74t$pcg$1@usenet.otenet.gr> <3CEBD067.13824358@shaw.ca> <3CEDEBAA.6D259413@Azonic.co.nz> <3CEDB528.A808E642@shaw.ca>`

```

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3CEDB528.A808E642@shaw.ca...
>
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > Given that you *can* build from the command line, I'm still very
curious as to
> > > what advantage there is in doing this, when it can be done so easily
from the
> > > IDE - even an 'application' which is a single program ?
> >
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy

If the IDE is capable of expressing the project, that is.  On mine, it is
open the one project, rebuild, close it, open a second project, rebuild
that, close it, open the third, rebuild that, then realize there was a
fourth that had to be done first, go back and do that, etc.

PSTAFF, for example, works quite well.  I can write a COBOL program in ten
or twenty seconds, then in less than fifteen minutes  or an hour I can
organize it into a project.  I can never use that project again, of course,
but I want to use the PROGRAM again, I can start all over at the begining.
It is a bit tough in that I cannot find anything, because the project
manager insists that every file on my computer be in the same directory, but
what the hell, disks are big.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Project in Microfocus NetExpress

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-24T20:49:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CEEA778.C1DC9928@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3CEA2AAF.B06A6E4F@msg.de> <acg74t$pcg$1@usenet.otenet.gr> <3CEBD067.13824358@shaw.ca> <3CEDEBAA.6D259413@Azonic.co.nz> <3CEDB528.A808E642@shaw.ca> <S7qH8.13222$Mn6.1607924@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```


Donald Tees wrote:

> "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
> news:3CEDB528.A808E642@shaw.ca...
…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
> organize it into a project.

Donald,

So can I, "display Hello World". Hey hot-shot, if you're so fast, how come your
Weighing system didn't function smoothly once they stood the building back up
again <G>

> I can never use that project again, of course,
> but I want to use the PROGRAM again, I can start all over at the begining.
> It is a bit tough in that I cannot find anything, because the project
> manager insists that every file on my computer be in the same directory, but
> what the hell, disks are big.

Donald,

Curious, and thinking this is what you might be trying to do above, gave this a
shot.
Set up Projects A, B, C and D in four separate directories(folders). Real simple
stuff - just does 'display Hello World", plus I included the same copyfile into
each program - just two values I don't use.

ProjectA - ProjectA1.cbl and ProjectA2.cbl
ProjectB - ProjectB1.cbl and ProjectB2.cbl

Realizing that copyfiles could be required by any 'sub-project',  Project C is
just a list of copyfiles - means in projects above, specific path required, e.g.
:-

    copy "\cobol\examples\projC\copy1.cpy"

Did  a Rebuild 'ALL' on Projects A and B.

Set up Project D - first program is ProjectD0.cbl which calls above four
programs
Now added the four programs above to ProjectD. Screwed it up first time around,
the four files got physically copied into the Project D directory. Created an
additional  ProjectA3.cbl  - which shows "via absolute/relative path" - i.e. it
references the directory for Project A. (There's a cautionary note the M/F
Dialog puts out about doing this). Did a Rebuild 'ALL' and runs just fine.

Perhaps a bit of a Mickey Mouse way of doing it and can be enhanced. I wont
swear to it, but about 4-5 years ago there was a thread in M/F's
Compuserve/Answerlab about a project containing sub-projects, so obviously
slicker than what I'm illustrating.

Richard - howz about NO Clicks at all if you can remember the shortcuts :-

    F7 - gives you items 1 and 2 below
    Ctrl + F7 - Compile this program - *** that's easy enough to remember
    Ctrl + Shift + F8 = Rebuild (those changed)
    Alt + Ctrl + F8 = Rebuild ALL

Graham - I don't have monster applications but - "Check All Dependencies" on 150
cbl files and 141 cpy files completed in under a minute. And the 'Rebuild ALL'
on that same group - just over 2 minutes. (For true comparison, the sources are
small as they are OO - so "Edit This File" *might* be some 20-25 pages of source
in portrait printing; probably average is around 8 - 10 printed pages).

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Project in Microfocus NetExpress

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-25T11:00:43-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0GNH8.12433$qn1.3286091@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<3CEA2AAF.B06A6E4F@msg.de> <acg74t$pcg$1@usenet.otenet.gr> <3CEBD067.13824358@shaw.ca> <3CEDEBAA.6D259413@Azonic.co.nz> <3CEDB528.A808E642@shaw.ca> <S7qH8.13222$Mn6.1607924@news20.bellglobal.com> <3CEEA778.C1DC9928@shaw.ca>`

```
To my mind, the biggest weakness of IDE's (the ones I have used) is that
they are not iterative.  If I write a batch file, I can call it in another
batch file.  If I write cobol code, I can put it in a library, and call it
from cobol.  If I design a project, I should be able to use it in my next
project as a single word.  I cannot.  I have to copy it, and if it changes,
then I have to change the copy.  If I re-use it 1000 times, then I have to
change it 1000 times.

Hell, even the Cobol copy statement can be nested, and it has been arround
for 35 years.  My biggest "rebuild all" is about 40 minutes on a 1 gig
machine.  141 dependencies would take care of the first level.

The second biggest problem is their reliance on shitty editors.  I want an
editor that can do a global data search, in hex, while I am editing a
specific source file, in ascii. Being able to efficiently search at least
two or three thousand source files for a data name in a minute or two is a
minimum requirement.  Just using the Fujitsu editor would cost me a couple
of hours a day.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Project in Microfocus NetExpress

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** graham@olympic.co.uk (graham)
- **Date:** 2002-05-24T06:00:33-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a02b0a1.0205240500.73bcae8d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3CEA2AAF.B06A6E4F@msg.de> <acg74t$pcg$1@usenet.otenet.gr> <3CEBD067.13824358@shaw.ca> <3CEDEBAA.6D259413@Azonic.co.nz> <3CEDB528.A808E642@shaw.ca>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message > 
> OK your response was informative Richard - BUT - TWO CLICKS in the IDE,
> TWO CLICKS - no need to mosey around - (1) Select 'Project' drop down
> menu -> (2a) Compile this program, (2b) Rebuild - immediately triggers
> re-compiles of all changed programs, including changes to copyfiles, or
> (2c) Rebuild All.

In all the cases where I have worked on commercial software the number
of files involved in the complete package is quite large and
invariably some batch process is used to compile and link them. In
these cases the IDE could be used, I suppose, but if you want to
perform the build as part of a process (backup sources, build, move to
release preparation area etc.) some sort of command line still seems
to be a preferred option (it may not be the *only* way but it's easy
and it works).

In addition with a couple of hundred files the time to "rebuild all"
may not be excessive but it's longer than I would choose to do staring
at a NetExpress IDE. Most developers that I've known will have a
Project(s) that reflects what they are currently working on and let
some separate task run the full system builds. I also have suspicion
that there are practical limits on Projects after having tried to get
dependencies set up for a few hundred source files a year or so ago
and giving up when it was still analysing dependencies the next
morning!

But, ultimately, it's all down to personal taste, I suspect.

graham
```

## Thread: Variable Length Records — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Variable Length Records

- **From:** "Don" <mengd@rogers.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-22T00:17:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sBG8.79417$t8_.62056@news01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>`

```
Hi, this is the first time I've posted here.  I'm using IBM COBOL II and I
have to write variable length records to a file.  I've done this before but
with a limited number of record types, say half a dozen.  In this case the
data is much more variable.  You could say that the records could be of any
length within a minimum and a maximum of course.  Is there any way to do
this in COBOL?  It would be an easy thing to do in a language like PL1.  But
COBOL doesn't support variable length fields.  Is there a way around this
COBOL constraint?  Thanks.

Don
```

#### ↳ Re: Variable Length Records

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2002-05-22T00:34:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c20128$5e0fab40$0eccf943@chottel>`
- **References:** `<7sBG8.79417$t8_.62056@news01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>`

```
How about "OCCURS DEPENDING ON"?

<7sBG8.79417$t8_.62056@news01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>...
> Hi, this is the first time I've posted here.  I'm using IBM COBOL II and
I
> have to write variable length records to a file.  I've done this before
but
> with a limited number of record types, say half a dozen.  In this case
the
> data is much more variable.  You could say that the records could be of
any
> length within a minimum and a maximum of course.  Is there any way to do
> this in COBOL?  It would be an easy thing to do in a language like PL1. 
But
> COBOL doesn't support variable length fields.  Is there a way around this
> COBOL constraint?  Thanks.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Variable Length Records

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-21T20:11:21-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<acer81$dl6$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<7sBG8.79417$t8_.62056@news01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>`

```
If you are using the (now unsupported by IBM) VS COBOL II product *and* you
are using the NOCMPR2 compiler option, then you can use the '85 Standard
syntax:

FD  file-name
   ...
  Record Varying in Size depending on WS-ITEM ...

where WS-ITEM is a working-storage item with the length of the record to be
written (without the RDW).
```

#### ↳ Re: Variable Length Records

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-05-22T13:56:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<acg7p8$bj9$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<7sBG8.79417$t8_.62056@news01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>`

```

On 21-May-2002, "Don" <mengd@rogers.com> wrote:

> Hi, this is the first time I've posted here.  I'm using IBM COBOL II and I
> have to write variable length records to a file.  I've done this before
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> COBOL constraint?  Thanks.

"Variable length fields"?   Could you give an example of what you mean?

You also say "variable length records", but those are handled natively in
all versions of CoBOL I have ever seen.  Do you have a manual?
```

#### ↳ Re: Variable Length Records

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-22T14:53:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jhOG8.55470$yl.6744018@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<7sBG8.79417$t8_.62056@news01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>`

```

"Don" <mengd@rogers.com> wrote in message
news:7sBG8.79417$t8_.62056@news01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com...
> Hi, this is the first time I've posted here.  I'm using IBM COBOL II and I
> have to write variable length records to a file.  I've done this before
but
> with a limited number of record types, say half a dozen.  In this case the
> data is much more variable.  You could say that the records could be of
any
> length within a minimum and a maximum of course.  Is there any way to do
> this in COBOL?  It would be an easy thing to do in a language like PL1.
But
> COBOL doesn't support variable length fields.  Is there a way around this
> COBOL constraint?  Thanks.

COBOL supports variable length fields and files. But it's double screwy -
for example by simply truncating trailing spaces on a text file, you'll end
up with variable length records, but this is not properly called a variable
length file.

Can you give an example of a record you want to write (and tell us who or
what is going to read the record).
```

<a id="t-44093b7779"></a>
## Thread: CICS Abend help required — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### CICS Abend help required

- **From:** sathyanr26@yahoo.com (Sathya)
- **Date:** 2002-05-21T20:29:29-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6d3501e5.0205211929.6062c51d@posting.google.com>`

```
I am getting a CICS abend of UDB4.
Does anyone know what this abend is?
I was not able to find it in native CICS CMAC and IBM site.
```

#### ↳ Re: CICS Abend help required

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-21T22:35:01-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<acf3ld$nta$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<6d3501e5.0205211929.6062c51d@posting.google.com>`

```
According to,
  http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/dfhs1p01/2.1.2

"The first thing that the transaction abend code can indicate is whether or
not this was a CICS abend. CICS transaction abend codes begin with the
letter "A". A user program or another product might also use abend codes
beginning with "A". However, if the transaction abend code begins with
anything other than "A", it is an abend code belonging to a user program or
to some other product. For the sake of convenience, all such non-CICS abend
codes are referred to in this section as user abend codes."

Therefore, this sounds like it is either an application ABEND code - or an
ABEND code from some other product.
```

#### ↳ Re: CICS Abend help required

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-22T03:37:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QnEG8.785$OV4.45643888@newssvr16.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<6d3501e5.0205211929.6062c51d@posting.google.com>`

```
    I believe ALL CICS abend codes are Axxx.  I suspect that your UDB4 is
the result of the program loading up the user abend code before calling the
abend.

"Sathya" <sathyanr26@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:6d3501e5.0205211929.6062c51d@posting.google.com...
> I am getting a CICS abend of UDB4.
> Does anyone know what this abend is?
> I was not able to find it in native CICS CMAC and IBM site.
```

- **From:** charles.godwin@ca.com (Charles Godwin)

- **References:** `<6d3501e5.0205211929.6062c51d@posting.google.com>`

```
sathyanr26@yahoo.com (Sathya) wrote in message news:<6d3501e5.0205211929.6062c51d@posting.google.com>...
> I am getting a CICS abend of UDB4.
> Does anyone know what this abend is?
> I was not able to find it in native CICS CMAC and IBM site.

Based on IBM's trend to renaming DB2 to UDB (universal Data Base) I'd
guess it's a DB2 fault. I won;t bet, just guess.
```

<a id="t-f2abbece50"></a>
## Thread: Deleting escape sequences from a string? — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Deleting escape sequences from a string?

- **From:** sjolund@iobox.com (Xedra Roland)
- **Date:** 2002-05-22T00:21:19-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4d43e3ef.0205212321.5744f5dc@posting.google.com>`

```
Can you delete all the escape sequences from a string somehow?

We have these form-templates, that include those strange escape-things
(printer commands actually, for example ^[(s1B). Now, instead of
printing, the form can be sent via e-mail. Of course, the
escape-things show up in the mail too. Are the only solutions to
either check the whole string one character at a time or to make whole
new form-templates (which is not what I'd like to do)?

- Xedra
```

#### ↳ Re: Deleting escape sequences from a string?

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <frederico_fonseca@eudoramail.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-22T09:01:12+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<avjmeu871us0ktc0be3nqekqadveocct17@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<4d43e3ef.0205212321.5744f5dc@posting.google.com>`

```
On 22 May 2002 00:21:19 -0700, sjolund@iobox.com (Xedra Roland) wrote:

>Can you delete all the escape sequences from a string somehow?
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>new form-templates (which is not what I'd like to do)?

As you do not wish to redesign, a good option would be to create a
"pdf" file and send this file instead (if doing this in windows/unix
it is easy enough)


FF
```

#### ↳ Re: Deleting escape sequences from a string?

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-22T14:48:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6dOG8.102552$fU2.10398531@bin8.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<4d43e3ef.0205212321.5744f5dc@posting.google.com>`

```

"Xedra Roland" <sjolund@iobox.com> wrote in message
news:4d43e3ef.0205212321.5744f5dc@posting.google.com...
> Can you delete all the escape sequences from a string somehow?
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> new form-templates (which is not what I'd like to do)?

You have a print file with PCL commands (to set the margins, fonts, etc.).

The character displayed as "^" is really the ESC character X'1B'.

PCL strings are of indefinite length, but almost always start with the ESC
character and USUALLY end with an upper-case letter.

Anyway, to answer your question: You'll have to do it a character at a
time - but that's not hard; it's a four or five-line perform statement.
```

#### ↳ Re: Deleting escape sequences from a string?

- **From:** "Georgi Kozinakov" <gjokok@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-24T19:13:35+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3cee7439@news.mt.net.mk>`
- **References:** `<4d43e3ef.0205212321.5744f5dc@posting.google.com>`

```
Xedra,

I had the similar problem with a LINE FEED character last month.
The procedure for removing it is the same like for ESC character:
In working-storage:
...
01 esc-ch pic 99 comp value 27.
01 esc-bug redefines esc-ch pic x.
01 space-ch pic x value " ".

in procedure division:
...
    PERFORM CHECK-LINES UNTIL ... (EOF or something)


CHECK-LINES.
    READ FILE AT END ... move 1 to eof.
    INSPECT LINE-OF-TEXT REPLACING ALL ESC-BUG BY SPACE-ch (or something
else)

HTH
Georgi

"Xedra Roland" <sjolund@iobox.com> wrote in message
news:4d43e3ef.0205212321.5744f5dc@posting.google.com...
> Can you delete all the escape sequences from a string somehow?
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> - Xedra
```

<a id="t-7737a3c61c"></a>
## Thread: Another Easytrieve Question — 3 msgs

### Another Easytrieve Question

- **From:** hanakj@ameritech.net (jim)
- **Date:** 2002-05-22T11:42:43-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9f857340.0205221042.610dc5ab@posting.google.com>`

```
Ok, here's another one.  I'm producing a report that counts the number
of records in a file for each different "producer".  Not printing a
line for each occurence of a producer, just one report line saying how
many of each producer ther is in the file.  The file is sorted by
producer already.  At the end, I get a total of how many for all
producers.  How can I put a line(-------) just above that final total
line?


SAMPLE CODE:

REPORT XALR002S NODATE LINESIZE 150 PRINTER XALR002S +
       NOSPREAD NOADJUST SPACE 5 DTLCTL EVERY SUMMARY
 SEQUENCE CI-PRODUCER
 CONTROL CI-PRODUCER
 TITLE 1 COL 2   'RUN DATE ' -4 SYSDATE +
 - - -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  - 12 Line(s) not
Displayed
 LINE 1 COL 56 CI-PRODUCER TALLY

Sample output:
   CI-PRODUCER     TALLY
    64400836          45
    64403112           6
    64403113         230
    64403142           3
    64403143          11
    64403145           4
    64403146          70
    64403156          12
    64403193           7
    64403310          41
                     429
```

#### ↳ Re: Another Easytrieve Question

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-05-22T19:43:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<acgs2l$n31$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<9f857340.0205221042.610dc5ab@posting.google.com>`

```
First:

When you're reporting needs get complex - use CoBOL, not EasyTrieve.

Second - if you don't have an appropriate language, use the following code
(from a report description) as an example:


 HEADING HOLD-LAMT ('LOAN RECEIVABLES' 'OUTSTANDING' 'AMOUNT')
 LINE 1 COL  20 FOPPS                                     +
        COL  55 DESCRIPTION                               +
 -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  4 Line(s) not Displayed

 BEFORE-BREAK. PROC
  IF LEVEL EQ 4
     DISPLAY '              '
     DISPLAY 'FINAL TOTALS '
  END-IF

   IF LEVEL EQ 3
      DISPLAY 'FOPPS TOTAL                                      ' +
       '      --------------    ------------------    --------'  +
       '  ----------------   ------'
   END-IF
 END-PROC
 AFTER-BREAK. PROC
   IF LEVEL EQ 3
      DISPLAY '              '
   END-IF
 END-PROC
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Another Easytrieve Question

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-23T11:49:30-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<88A96A00F8A26185.B764EB60CC47B4C2.E2307502315CF37D@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<9f857340.0205221042.610dc5ab@posting.google.com> <acgs2l$n31$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
On Wed, 22 May 2002 19:43:06 GMT, "Howard Brazee"
<howard.brazee@cusys.edu> enlightened us:

>First:
>
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> END-PROC

What Howard wrote will work but as I remember it, there is a Control
or CNTL statement in Easytrieve that will only print lines and fields
you specify on a control break that you define.  I think there is also
a Tally statement that you could make use of.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Ever notice that people who spend money on beer, cigarettes, 
and lottery tickets are always complaining about being broke 
and not feeling well?
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

<a id="t-f3b2f3021e"></a>
## Thread: until greater than — 18 this month / 36 total

### until greater than

- **From:** klatteross@aol.commmm (Ross Klatte)
- **Date:** 2002-05-23T00:10:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020522201039.19725.00000223@mb-ck.aol.com>`

```
108202 01  WS-MAX-ACCTS                   PIC 9(02) VALUE 99.
...
108210 01  WS-ACCT-SS                     PIC 9(02).                          
108230 01  WS-DEPT-SS                     PIC 9(02).                          
...
108300 01 WS-ROW-COLUMN-TABLE.                                            
108500    05  WS-ROW-ACCT        OCCURS 1 TO 99 TIMES                  
108520                                    DEPENDING ON                         

108550                                    WS-MAX-ACCTS.                   
108700        10  WS-ROW-DEPT      OCCURS  5 TIMES.                      
108900            15  WS-ACCT-DEPT-AMOUNT PIC 9(07).                  
...
316300     PERFORM 100-INITIALIZE-AMOUNT-TABLE
316500        THRU 100-INITIALIZE-AMOUNT-TABLE-EXIT                            
      
316700         VARYING WS-ACCT-SS FROM 1 BY 1                               
316900         UNTIL WS-ACCT-SS > WS-MAX-ACCTS                        
317100         AFTER WS-DEPT-SS FROM 1 BY 1                                 
317300         UNTIL WS-DEPT-SS > 4.                                        
...
344400 100-INITIALIZE-AMOUNT-TABLE.                                            
      
344500     MOVE ZERO TO                                                        
344700           WS-ACCT-DEPT-AMOUNT (WS-ACCT-SS WS-DEPT-SS).
344800 100-INITIALIZE-AMOUNT-TABLE-EXIT.
-------------------------------------------------------------
This code was used without incident until WS-MAX-ACCTS 
eventually reached 99, at which point it promptly blew up with 
ASSERTION FAILURE ON RANGE TEST  @ (Expression out of range 6600). 

It was salvaged by changing WS-MAX-ACCTS to a 3-digit number.  
But I found this philosophically unpleasant.  I thought that 
it should remain as a 2-digit number, especially as there 
are only two bytes in the output file's field.  
I can't say "until WS-ACCT-SS = WS-MAX-ACCTS" because then 
I lose the last iteration.  
This is quite simple using GO TO, but I would like to use 
structured 85 code. 

Any elegant suggestions? 

Ross
http://community.webshots.com/user/ross_klatte
```

#### ↳ Re: until greater than

- **From:** stringer@es.co.nz (stringer)
- **Date:** 2002-05-23T02:56:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<achlpc$98f$1@lust.ihug.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<20020522201039.19725.00000223@mb-ck.aol.com>`

```
Does your compiler support "with test after" ?

If so, try:
>316300     PERFORM 100-INITIALIZE-AMOUNT-TABLE
>316500        THRU 100-INITIALIZE-AMOUNT-TABLE-EXIT                           
 
>      
>316700         VARYING WS-ACCT-SS FROM 1 BY 1 WITH TEST AFTER                 
             
>316900         UNTIL WS-ACCT-SS IS GREATER THAN OR EQUAL TO WS-MAX-ACCTS      
                  
Strictly speaking, you don't need the greater than test, but it might save your 
backside in the event of an unforseen bug!

In article <20020522201039.19725.00000223@mb-ck.aol.com>, klatteross@aol.commmm 
says...
>
>108202 01  WS-MAX-ACCTS                   PIC 9(02) VALUE 99.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>108520                                    DEPENDING ON                        
 
>
>108550                                    WS-MAX-ACCTS.                   
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>316500        THRU 100-INITIALIZE-AMOUNT-TABLE-EXIT                           
 
>      
>316700         VARYING WS-ACCT-SS FROM 1 BY 1                               
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>344400 100-INITIALIZE-AMOUNT-TABLE.                                           
 
>      
>344500     MOVE ZERO TO                                                       
 
>344700           WS-ACCT-DEPT-AMOUNT (WS-ACCT-SS WS-DEPT-SS).
>344800 100-INITIALIZE-AMOUNT-TABLE-EXIT.
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: until greater than

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-05-23T14:25:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<acitqc$a7$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
> >316900         UNTIL WS-ACCT-SS IS GREATER THAN OR EQUAL TO WS-MAX-ACCTS
> >
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…

I always loop "UNTIL GREATER THAN", as you say it might save your backside.
But in this case it could be a contributor to a bug!   What happens when
WS-MAX-ACCTS = 99 and you increment WS-ACCT-SS from 99 to 00?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: until greater than

- **From:** stringer@es.co.nz (stringer)
- **Date:** 2002-05-24T01:20:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ack4hl$era$1@lust.ihug.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<20020522201039.19725.00000223@mb-ck.aol.com> <achlpc$98f$1@lust.ihug.co.nz> <acitqc$a7$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Point taken, but in this case I was only coding it as a back-stop retained from 
the original code.

I agree with other posters that it would be better if he used 
Pic 999 (or greater), or put in code to ensure the counter would not increment 
beyond 99 and a flag to pick up any such attempt and allow a break 
from the perform.

In article <acitqc$a7$1@peabody.colorado.edu>, howard.brazee@cusys.edu says...
>
>
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
>WS-MAX-ACCTS = 99 and you increment WS-ACCT-SS from 99 to 00?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: until greater than

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-05-23T18:26:12+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CED2634.528E8248@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<20020522201039.19725.00000223@mb-ck.aol.com> <achlpc$98f$1@lust.ihug.co.nz>`

```
>108202 01  WS-MAX-ACCTS                   PIC 9(02) VALUE 99.
>...
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>316900         UNTIL WS-ACCT-SS > WS-MAX-ACCTS

>344500     MOVE ZERO TO
> 
>344700           WS-ACCT-DEPT-AMOUNT (WS-ACCT-SS WS-DEPT-SS).

> I thought that
> it should remain as a 2-digit number, especially as there
> are only two bytes in the output file's field.

> >Any elegant suggestions?


         MOVE ZERO      TO WS-Acct-SS 
         PERFORM ..
             UNTIL WS-Acct-SS = WS-Max-Accts
              ...

         ADD 1           TO WS-Acct-SS
         MOVE ... TO WS-Acct-Dept-Amount(WS-Acct-SS ..)

This can be made to work but 'elegent' ?

Just get over it and make all these counters and limits as PIC S9(8). 
It is better to make it work rather than piss around with a few bytes.
Otherwise you will spend half your life chasing down avoidable bugs
while trying to save a byte or two. 

If it is 'elegence' you want then dump the THRU and the ~EXIT and think
about how to make it work without the crutch of having a GO TO available
if it all gets 'too hard'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: until greater than

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-05-23T14:27:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<acitvd$gc$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<20020522201039.19725.00000223@mb-ck.aol.com> <achlpc$98f$1@lust.ihug.co.nz> <3CED2634.528E8248@Azonic.co.nz>`

```

On 23-May-2002, Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

> Just get over it and make all these counters and limits as PIC S9(8).
> It is better to make it work rather than piss around with a few bytes.
> Otherwise you will spend half your life chasing down avoidable bugs
> while trying to save a byte or two.

If he wants to be efficient, he doesn't want a subscript to be DISPLAY
anyway.  The only reason to use USAGE IS DISPLAY is to make the subscript
easier to read in a dump or DISPLAY command.
```

#### ↳ Re: until greater than

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>

```
What am I missing???

Given

"Ross Klatte" <klatteross@aol.commmm> wrote in message
news:20020522201039.19725.00000223@mb-ck.aol.com...
> 108202 01  WS-MAX-ACCTS                   PIC 9(02) VALUE 99.

  ...
> 316700         VARYING WS-ACCT-SS FROM 1 BY 1
> 316900         UNTIL WS-ACCT-SS > WS-MAX-ACCTS

Please tell me what "valid" value of WS-ACCT-SS            (defined as PIC
9(02)
will EVER be greater than the value of WS-MAX-ACCTS (defined as PIC 9(02) -
with a value of "99")

Bottom-Line:
  *only* 3-digit numbers can ever be greater than the two digit number "99"
```

#### ↳ Re: until greater than

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2002-05-23T11:30:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020523073023.13422.00000167@mb-mb.aol.com>`

```
In article <20020522201039.19725.00000223@mb-ck.aol.com>, klatteross@aol.commmm
(Ross Klatte) writes:

>This code was used without incident until WS-MAX-ACCTS 
>eventually reached 99, at which point it promptly blew up with 
>ASSERTION FAILURE ON RANGE TEST  @ (Expression out of range 6600). 
>

How is your WS-ACCT-SS ever supposed to be greater than WS-MAX-ACCTS
if both counters are 2-digit numbers and MAX gets set to 99?
Your reasoning for 2-digits on WS-MAX-ACCTS would also require that

of the ACCT rows.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: until greater than

- **From:** "Alain.Feler" <Alain.Feler@wanadoo.fr>
- **Date:** 2002-05-23T22:10:31+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<acjieo$2ek$1@wanadoo.fr>`
- **References:** `<20020522201039.19725.00000223@mb-ck.aol.com> <fD4H8.1336$F_4.73336897@newssvr16.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Initialize is very convenient, but very slow, at least with my compiler
(MS-Cobol V4.5, in fact an old Microfocus Cobol for Dos sold by Microsoft).
I once got an execution time divided by 4 only replacing an initialize by
move spaces to the same array ( pic x occurs 9000)...

Speaking of 85 style, I would prefer:
77  MAX-ACCTS                   PIC 9(4) binary VALUE 99.
77  ACCT-SS                          PIC 9(4) binary value 0.
77  DEPT-SS                           PIC 9(4) binary value 0.
01  ROW-COLUMN-TABLE.
     05  ROW-ACCT        OCCURS 1 TO 99 TIMES
                                         DEPENDING ON  MAX-ACCTS.
          10  ROW-DEPT      OCCURS  5 TIMES.
                15  ACCT-DEPT-AMOUNT PIC 9(07).
...
       perform  100-INITIALIZE-AMOUNT-TABLE
...
100-INITIALIZE-AMOUNT-TABLE.
            perform varying acct-ss from 1 by 1 until acct-ss > ws-max-accts
                 perform varying dept-ss from 1 by 1 until dept-ss > 4
                      move 0 to acct-dept-amount(acct-ss dept-ss)
                 end-perform
            end-perform
            .

Terry Heinze <terryheinze@prodigy.net> a �crit dans le message :
fD4H8.1336$F_4.73336897@newssvr16.news.prodigy.com...
> INITIALIZE WS-ROW-COLUMN-TABLE.
>
…[49 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: until greater than

- **From:** klatteross@aol.commmm (Ross Klatte)
- **Date:** 2002-05-23T21:58:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020523175809.03772.00000444@mb-bk.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<acjieo$2ek$1@wanadoo.fr>`

```
Stringer's suggestion of "WITH TEST AFTER" and "UNTIL >= MAX" was 
just what the doctor ordered.  It worked perfectly, and has a cryptic
quality fully in the spirit of modern COBOL.  

Shop standards rule out some of the good ideas, such as making
subscripts binary.  Also ruled out was making the 4-row table have
a one-digit subscript; all subscripts have to be at least 2 digits.  
INITIALIZE ROW-COLUMN-TABLE gives me a syntax error.  
I think "MOVE ALL ZERO" would be acceptable, but it doesn't
look very cool.  
Most annoying of all, the standards rule out leaving out the 
table initialization code all together; it is a batch program running
on a Unisys box.  But leaving out the manual initialization frightens
all the IBM-trained programmers, and there are a lot of those.
Along the same lines, I don't really approve of the part about 
"OCCURS ... DEPENDING" either.  I don't know what IBM does,
but Unisys goes ahead and allocates 99 in memory; so I don't
see where anything is gained (other than coolness).  

>From: "Alain.Feler" Alain.Feler@wanadoo.fr 
>Date: 5/23/2002 4:10 PM Eastern Daylight Time
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>move spaces to the same array ( pic x occurs 9000)...

Personally, I like INITIALIZE for a record, where everything is all
higgledy-piggledy.  But I don't like it for a table of all zeroes, or a
table of all spaces.  I intend to use it, but I don't like it. 



Ross
http://community.webshots.com/user/ross_klatte
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: until greater than

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-24T11:01:46+00:00

```
Ross Klatte <klatteross@aol.commmm> wrote in message
news:20020523175809.03772.00000444@mb-bk.aol.com...

> Shop standards rule out some of the good ideas, such as making
> subscripts binary.  Also ruled out was making the 4-row table have
> a one-digit subscript; all subscripts have to be at least 2 digits.

Subscripts must not be binary is a shop standard? All subscripts must be at
least TWO digits is a shop standard (or is that subscript PICTURE clause)?

Whomever sets the standards needs to move forward a tad; oh, say, about
twenty-five years or so....

MCM
```

- **References:** `<acjieo$2ek$1@wanadoo.fr> <20020523175809.03772.00000444@mb-bk.aol.com> <u4pH8.11$dS.1@nwrddc04.gnilink.net>`

```
Michael Mattias wrote:

> Ross Klatte <klatteross@aol.commmm> wrote in message
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: until greater than

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2002-05-25T12:12:04-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<acod4k$398$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<acjieo$2ek$1@wanadoo.fr> <20020523175809.03772.00000444@mb-bk.aol.com> <u4pH8.11$dS.1@nwrddc04.gnilink.net> <3CEF32A8.5030508@optonline.net>`

```
In article <3CEF32A8.5030508@optonline.net>,
Liam Devlin  <LiamD-NOSPAM@optonline.nospam.net> wrote:
>Michael Mattias wrote:
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>Or out the door, as described in Docdwarf's previous post.

Here I must pause, Mr Devlin, and say...

... hmmmmmm... I dunno.

A case of ossified shop standards can be seen more than one way.  Compare:

'If it ain't broke, don't fix it'

... with ...

'If you don't change your habits of diet and exercise you're going to get 
a heart attack'

Obviously one can never be certain when another person is going to suffer 
a cardiac episode - after all, anyone can, at any time, get hit by a bus - 
but the studies which link diet, exercise and increased life expectancy 
are there for all to read.

Likewise... is it possible to say 'This company failed because the COBOL 
coding standards in their DP/IS/IT/whatever the acronym is now shop hadn't 
been changed in a quarter-century'?  I doubt that it is possible to say so 
with precision.

In an organisation there are those who have the authority and privlege to 
establish direction... in large organisations such folks are called 
Executives.  In, say, Mr Dilworth's company he is the final authority on 
coding standards; if he wants something that will compile under the rules 
for COBOL '68 then so be it... it is *his* shop.  If he is able to 
maintain a running business that meets his needs and desires while 
remaining within his restrictions, well and good and more power to him... 
it does not, of course, change the fact that he has apparently been doing 
the same thing for the past few decades and seems... resistant to any 
alternative methods.

(Consider the dispenser of gasoline/petrol... except for minor changes in 
equipment the essence of the job hasn't changed in a goodly while, one 
finds a location, sets up a shack, tank and pump and waits for motorists.  
Certainly one can earn more money by adding features, such as a mechanic 
or a shop selling packages of excessively-salted carbohydrates... but the 
guts of it, the shack, tank and pump are the same.)

On the other hand... things in a business rarely exist in vacuo and the 
Russian proveb has it 'a fish rots from the head' (which isn't really 
true... I would imagine that putrefaction would begin in the lower 
intestines, where things are turning to excrement anyhow... but I 
digress).  As was noticed by, among others, Sir Thomas Gresham: when there 
exists in circulation two kinds of money, one bad and one good, then bad 
money will drive good money out of the marketplace.  This is still debated 
to this day by financiers, economists and Other Worthy Folks but I have 
noticed a slight variation applicable to the workplace, a sort of 
Gresham's Law of Management: when there exists in a workplace two kinds of 
management, one bad and one good, then bad management will drive good 
management out of the workplace.

Given, then, the fact that there is a workplace with COBOL-coding 
standards which neglect a quarter-century of change... and that these 
standards have the support of management... and that things in a business 
rarely exist in vacuo... and that Gresham's Law of Management is a 
phenomenon which has been observed...

... then it might be, perhaps, a Good Conclusion that someone should move 
out the door, as Mr Devlin suggested.

The question then becomes, however... who?

DD
```

```

seem to exist with OBJ/GNT programs. (Microfocus 3.1).

news:acjieo$2ek$1@wanadoo.fr...

> (MS-Cobol V4.5, in fact an old Microfocus Cobol for Dos sold by
Microsoft).
> I once got an execution time divided by 4 only replacing an initialize by
> move spaces to the same array ( pic x occurs 9000)...
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>             perform varying acct-ss from 1 by 1 until acct-ss >
ws-max-accts
>                  perform varying dept-ss from 1 by 1 until dept-ss > 4
>                       move 0 to acct-dept-amount(acct-ss dept-ss)
…[59 more quoted lines elided]…
```

#### ↳ Re: until greater than

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamDD@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-25T06:39:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CEF3184.10707@optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<20020522201039.19725.00000223@mb-ck.aol.com>`

```
Ross Klatte wrote:

> 108202 01  WS-MAX-ACCTS                   PIC 9(02) VALUE 99.
> ...
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> But I found this philosophically unpleasant.


What's philosophically unpleasant about 3 bytes?

> I thought that 
> it should remain as a 2-digit number, especially as there 
> are only two bytes in the output file's field.


Poor file sizing, right?  :-)

  
> I can't say "until WS-ACCT-SS = WS-MAX-ACCTS" because then 
> I lose the last iteration.  
> This is quite simple using GO TO, but I would like to use 
> structured 85 code. 


I'm surprised no one's suggested:

	PERFORM WITH TEST AFTER
	         VARYING WS-ACCT-SS FROM 1 BY 1
	         UNTIL WS-ACCT-SS = WS-MAX-ACCTS
	         AFTER WS-DEPT-SS FROM 1 BY 1
	         UNTIL WS-DEPT-SS > 4

		MOVE ZERO TO
			WS-ACCT-DEPT-AMOUNT (WS-ACCT-SS WS-DEPT-SS)

	END-PERFORM


You can decide how elegant it is. If you've grown to 99 accounts, 

what happens when you get one more? As someone else pointed out, 

DISPLAY subscripts are inefficient, so you could make them 

Pic S9999 Binary and keep them 2 bytes.



Or, how about:

Initialize  WS-ROW-COLUMN-TABLE
```

```
Bentina Sandburg,
```

### Help! CICS WRITE is closing and disabling my file!

- **From:** randy@radiorandy.com (Randy Woods)
- **Date:** 2002-05-23T09:11:26-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e40710b8.0205230811.149f15a6@posting.google.com>`

```
I am issuing a CICS WRITE command from inside a COBOL program, which
executes perfectly.  When the program tries to execute another CICS
WRITE command, EIBRESP is 19, indicating FILE NOT OPEN.  Seems that
the WRITE command closed the file, and now nobody can use it.

Does anyone have an idea of what's going on here?  

randy at radiorandy dot com
```

- **Date:** 2002-05-23T12:39:16-05:00

- **References:** `<e40710b8.0205230811.149f15a6@posting.google.com>`

```
On 23 May 2002 09:11:26 -0700 randy@radiorandy.com (Randy Woods) wrote:

:>I am issuing a CICS WRITE command from inside a COBOL program, which
:>executes perfectly.  When the program tries to execute another CICS
:>WRITE command, EIBRESP is 19, indicating FILE NOT OPEN.  Seems that
:>the WRITE command closed the file, and now nobody can use it.

I would suggest that more likely that the filename got messed up.

```

#### ↳ Re: Help! CICS WRITE is closing and disabling my file!

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamDD@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-25T06:47:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CEF337E.5010007@optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<e40710b8.0205230811.149f15a6@posting.google.com>`

```
Randy Woods wrote:

> I am issuing a CICS WRITE command from inside a COBOL program, which
> executes perfectly.  When the program tries to execute another CICS
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Does anyone have an idea of what's going on here?  


There's nothing inherent in a Write command that will close a file. Have 
you checked the SYSOUT for the region to see if you're getting file 
errors? If you're using a file manager like CAFC, something might be 
wrong for this file's specification.

Is there now a "close on first reference" option for FCT's? <g>
```

```
hs anybody any information of whats going on there?
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu-Cobol

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-23T14:32:40-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<acjg53$8fp$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<acjh2t.v4.2@ID-20196.news.dfncis.de>`

```
Certainly NETCobol has gone "GA" since then, but I don't know if Fujitsu has
made any announcement concerning the future of its "traditional" COBOL line.
Have you contacted them directly?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu-Cobol

- **From:** "Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-24T03:01:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<42iH8.2255$hV.64987403@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<acjh2t.v4.2@ID-20196.news.dfncis.de> <acjg53$8fp$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Bill,

Actually, I have heard at least a hint of what's going on, since I recently
received an "upgrade" notice for the .NET version.  I simply asked Fujitsu
"what was up?" and was our company required to upgrade to the .NET version,
and this is the response I received:

>>> The NetCOBOL for .Net is a different compiler and rich development
environment built for Microsoft Visual Studio .NET and the .NET Framework.
If your plan is migrate your application to the web (using ASP.NET) or
developing XML Web Services, the NetCOBOL for .NET would be your choice. The
second, .NET platform supports an unprecedented level of integration between
a variety of languages and technologies.

Yes, we're still going to offer Fujitsu COBOL 6.1, and probably at end of
this year we will ship Fujitsu COBOL 7.0 out. You don't need to upgrade your
product to the .NET product, but we would recommend you to do that. <<<

I guess I wouldn't call this "official", being that it was an E-mail
response from a sales/support person, but since we use Fujitsu COBOL for
internal applications and do not plan on using it for Web based
applications, I guess we're OK for now.  However, I don't have a real warm
and fuzzy feeling about the last line:

 "You don't need to upgrade your product to the .NET product, but we would
recommend you to do that."

One of the main reasons that we moved from Micro Focus to Fujitsu was the
continued Micro Focus "forced" upgrade to newer products at substantially
higher license fees, when in fact, we really didn't need all the bells and
whistles for the COBOL PC apps that we develop.  Hopefully, Fujitsu will
hold to the above response, and continue to offer non ".NET" versions.

If they don't, I guess we'll just stick with 6.1 until such time that there
is an operating system such as "Windows XP2020_WhoCares" that will no longer
run the code.

Denny
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu-Cobol

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2002-05-25T05:26:09-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0205250426.443f814b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<acjh2t.v4.2@ID-20196.news.dfncis.de> <acjg53$8fp$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <42iH8.2255$hV.64987403@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com>`

```
I think perhaps the group perspective on this issue might be a little
skewed.

I'm not speaking here as a representative of Fujitsu - Although I do
represent the company on the COBOL Committee.

Fujitsu has shown that they are a company willing to give you current
development tools.  By that, I mean when Windows 2000 came out -
Fujitsu created a compiler compatible.  When XP came out - there were
some problems with 6.1 because of things in XP, not because of any
issue with 6.1 - and an update was released to those persons on
maintenance who had problems.  (6.1a).

Microsoft has decreed the .NET platform as the future development
platform for Windows.  They have updated all their development tools
for .NET.  Fujitsu, keeping current with development tools, has
released a .NET COBOL.  Now will .NET be the future direction of COBOL
for Fujitsu?  If you look at it from the perspective of Fujitsu
keeping tools available that support the latest MS Technology - then
yes, that's the future and they are on the path.

Does that mean the native code versions will die and not be updated? 
Well, will traditional VB die?  As goes .NET and Microsoft, so goes
Fujitsu's compilers for that platform.

What I realize is that Fujitsu is providing tools that allow the COBOL
developer - for nearly the first time - to stay CURRENT and not play
catch up all the time.  This is a good thing.

If suddenly all the highways were converted to a new scheme - where
the lanes were only 4 feet wide, and you needed a new car to drive
them, would you insist that the wider cars be converted or freely
updated for the new roads?  Would you complain against the car
manufacturers who created new cars for these new roads?  That's the
kind of situation faced here by third party tool vendors targeting the
MS platform.

I thank Fujitsu for constantly updating their product line to support
the latest available platforms and development environments.

> Bill,
> 
…[64 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

```
than company support and will not buy another software development
package without an active forum.
2.	None or poorly implemented Company forums by COBOL vendors.
3.	Use of the slow bottleneck Com objects or ODBC needed to RDBMS
http://php.weblogs.com/php_asp_7_reasons
4.	Expensive runtimes (Microfocus an AcuCOBOL).
5.	The C open source programmers thru out the world are at home
improving the similar Java-PHP-Linux and could care less for COBOL.
6.	When the middle aged COBOL programmer retired so will much of COBOL
writing.
7.	COBOL's poor Linux solutions.  Peru, China and possibly the US
department of defense are passing laws or requirements for an OSS to
customize and analyze for free public access records and security
reasons.

I want a system for the WWW that works as well on Linux as Windoze. I
probably seem like a COBOL Linux troll , however being a
non-professional programmer I wish only to use the WEB's languages
mostly for education and require the simplest solutions  and view Web
programming packages nothing more than  a World Wide Word processors
(WWW).  I am always open to COBOL solutions however the future looks
grim. Mike
```

```
the '85 (and 200x) Standard, I still question when/if they will be adding
SUPPORT for many "extensions" available in V6 - but not documented as
SUPPORTED in NetCOBOL (at least the last Beta).

For those who have looked at the Fujitsu site recently, the first "GA"
version of NetCOBOL is currently available.  HOWEVER, it comes in 3
"flavors" and one of the things that the "high-end" (i.e. most expensive)
option includes is a copy of V6 of the "traditional" product.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu-Cobol

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-05-26T16:25:05+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CF0FE51.D49425F@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<acjh2t.v4.2@ID-20196.news.dfncis.de> <acjg53$8fp$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <42iH8.2255$hV.64987403@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com> <2763b81a.0205250838.3178daff@posting.google.com> <bfdfc3e8.0205251616.3a323162@posting.google.com> <acpf9c$ib0$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> Thane,
>   Do you know if Fujitsu plans on EVER including "full" ANSI '85 support in
> NetCOBOL (e.g., support for ALTER that is *rrequired* in '85 - but as
> obsolete and removed in 200x).  

The .NET Common runtime  system is based entirely on what C# can do and
how that language works.  Other languages can only use language features
that can be matched to C#.  Consequently, although .NET markets itself
as being 'multi-language', it is only 'multi-subset'.  Or putting it
another way: you can write C# programs in any syntax.

Of course it may be argued that many of the arcane features of Cobol or
other languages are not required in a modern environment such as
provided by .NET.  But then .NET isn't about supporting existing code
and systems, it is about killing Java and promoting C#.
```

```
news:3CF0FE51.D49425F@Azonic.co.nz...
>..  But then .NET isn't about supporting existing code and systems, it is

Actually, if you believe Microsoft's stated answer to the question, "Why
.NET?" ( and I certainly have no reason to disbelieve), .NET was developed
to support Micrsosoft's (i.e., Mr. Gates') view that in the future, software
will be a subscription or pay-per-use service rather than a purchased
license to install on the desktop.

Killing Java and installing the.NET CLR and C# as the defacto standards may
well be the result of success in implementing this vision, but I rather
doubt these are goals.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu-Cobol and .NET

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-05-27T16:22:13+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CF24F25.21F6137B@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<acjh2t.v4.2@ID-20196.news.dfncis.de> <acjg53$8fp$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <42iH8.2255$hV.64987403@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com> <2763b81a.0205250838.3178daff@posting.google.com> <bfdfc3e8.0205251616.3a323162@posting.google.com> <acpf9c$ib0$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3CF0FE51.D49425F@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
> Actually, if you believe Microsoft's stated answer to the question, "Why
> .NET?" ( and I certainly have no reason to disbelieve), .NET was developed
> to support Micrsosoft's (i.e., Mr. Gates') view that in the future, software
> will be a subscription or pay-per-use service rather than a purchased
> license to install on the desktop.

I am not quite so sure of "Mr Gates view of the future".  In the first
edition of his book "The Way Ahead" there was not one mention of the
Internet.  Then, instead of fitting into the Internet that had been in
use for many years, he tried to replace it with his own 'vision of the
future' Microsoft Network.  Of course this failed so he eventually had
to work in with the Internet that everyone else used.

It isn't so much that 'software will be a subscription or pay-per-use',
but that MS wants to _make_ it a subscription or pay-per-use. At the
moment the Windows and Office upgrades are optional and voluntary.  MS
wants to make this compulsory and annual - there are _way_ too many out
there still running Win95 and Win98 (like me) and this ruins the revenue
stream and control that MS would like.

The pay-per-use over .NET, is also an important revenue stream 'vision'
for MS as they would like to take a margin on the charges as a handling
fee as it passes through Passport.

MS may well want to move to subscription and pay-per-use, I'll just keep
running my existing Windows licences and other software.
```

```
marketing number on the unsuspecting end-users that they should upgrade their OS -

I don't dispute Jerry's statement Linux is a 'cult' - I just want to know. Can
anybody offer tangible evidence as to its use not just popularity. The local paper
ran an article on Linux about a year ago. In a city saturated in oil and gas, we
aren't talking double entry bookkeeping applications as the breadwinners, and a

"Dah de dah dah !. Just wanna get the facts Ma'am. Just wanna get the facts".

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
> Thane Hubbell wrote:
>If suddenly all the highways were converted to a new scheme - where
>the lanes were only 4 feet wide, and you needed a new car to drive
>them, would you insist that the wider cars be converted or freely

Well if we went with Micro$oft instead of The Department of

person seated!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu-Cobol

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-26T18:18:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<JF9I8.24019$Kp.2810794@bin7.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<acjh2t.v4.2@ID-20196.news.dfncis.de> <acjg53$8fp$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <42iH8.2255$hV.64987403@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com> <2763b81a.0205250838.3178daff@posting.google.com> <bfdfc3e8.0205251616.3a323162@posting.google.com> <2763b81a.0205260809.52d1c36b@posting.google.com>`

```

"Mike" <maross@texoma.net> wrote in message
news:2763b81a.0205260809.52d1c36b@posting.google.com...
> > Thane Hubbell wrote:
> >If suddenly all the highways were converted to a new scheme - where
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>  http://linuxtoday.com/news_story.php3?ltsn=2002-05-06-011-26-NW-MS-LL

Does anyone doubt that Linux is a cult? To be charitable, perhaps Linux has
many supporters because it is the alternative to the hated Micros~1. But
hating Micros~1 is not evidence of objective, rational mental processes.

Emotions cloud decisions. There is no question that Michael Milkin (the junk
bond king) has done more to advance civilization and alleviate suffering
than Mother Theresa, but who's up for sainthood and who's in jail?


>
> >I am CERTAIN that we will see v7.  There is no migration path for
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Mike

Drop COBOL. You'll sleep better. Concentrate on writing free applications to
trade with other Linux users. Learn housepainting, too. You'll need to eat.
```

<a id="t-d8b3b06a85"></a>
## Thread: "enforced" structured programming — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### "enforced" structured programming

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-23T17:04:46-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<acjp2a$bpd$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
(As a follow-on to one or more other threads).

Once "your" implementation supports the new EXIT PARAGRAPH option, I would
(personally) recommend the following coding rules.  I believe that a
(relatively) easy source-code-checker could verify that these rules were
followed:

1) *always* use PARAGRAPHS, never SECTIONS (2nd choice is to always use
SECTIONS and ONLY code one paragraph-header at the top of each and never
reference it).

2) Never code a PERFORM ... THRU statement (i.e. never code the THRU phrase)

3) Never code a GO TO statement

4) Only code one STOP RUN, EXIT PROGRAM (method, whatever) *or* GoBack
statement in your program (that means only ONE of those, not one of each)

  ***

If you followed these 3 rules, I believe you would be FORCED into structured
programming (and IMHO) easily maintainable source code.

 ***

Having said this, let me say that I completely "acknowledge" two problems
with this:

A) If you PERFORM a paragraph with a STOP RUN, (EXIT PROGRAM), GoBack -
then you have a "hidden" exit.  This violates at least SOME rules for
structured programming.  This can be avoided by coding your STOP RUN
(whatever) in your "mainline" code - but that often "hides"
end-of-processing logic.

B) These rules have NOTHING to do with maintaining existing source code.  I
(personally) guess that there is SIGNIFICANTLY more COBOL "maintenance" than
"new development" going on.  Although I think that "moving toward" these
rules will *eventually* help with maintaining existing code, I strongly
doubt that the effort to change existing code to these rules can be
cost-justified.

P.S.  I have MIXED feelings about the new "RESUME" statement.  It seems to
be a GO TO in some ways, but also could be viewed as changing a DECLARATIVE
from an (implicit) GO TO - to an implicit PERFORM.  My "best guess" is that
every (new) DECLARATIVE should end EITHER with a RESUME or a (Perform of) a
STOP RUN/GoBack.
```

#### ↳ Re: "enforced" structured programming

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-05-24T14:46:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<acljei$j3o$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<acjp2a$bpd$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

On 23-May-2002, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:

> Once "your" implementation supports the new EXIT PARAGRAPH option, I would
> (personally) recommend the following coding rules.  I believe that a
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> structured programming (and IMHO) easily maintainable source code.

True, but it still seems silly to PERFORM ABORT-ROUTINE if you're not coming
back - and subject to errors if you DO come back and drop through and up to
your EXIT PROGRAM statement.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: "enforced" structured programming

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2002-05-25T15:31:32-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0205251431.523d227a@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<acjp2a$bpd$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <acljei$j3o$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message news:<acljei$j3o$1@peabody.colorado.edu>...
> On 23-May-2002, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:
> 
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> your EXIT PROGRAM statement.

Using PERFORM ABORT-ROUTINE can be preferable to using GO TO
ABORT-ROUTINE, because, when testing, ABORT-ROUTINE can be replaced by
a dummy procedure to display messages instead, so multiple errors can
be checked in the same run more easily, cutting down on the testing
time and also allowing secondary defences to be checked.

When using SECTIONs, which has been (and at present still is) my
personal preference, I don't think I have ever encountered compilers
that required a section to contain any paragraphs at all, and when I
have used paragraphs in sections more recently it has usually been to
mark the exit, except where in-house standards also required them at
the beginning.  The current version of the draft standard doesn't
require sections to contain paragraphs either.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "enforced" structured programming

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-25T23:23:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<G1VH8.1908$0v.81166468@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<acjp2a$bpd$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <acljei$j3o$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <fcd09c56.0205251431.523d227a@posting.google.com>`

```
For example:
      * .-------------------------------------------------------------.
      * |  CALLED FROM:  M9300-GET-TRACE-TABLE-IND (TWICE)            |
      * |                Z6000-FILE-ABEND                             |
      * |                Z7000-SQL-ABEND                              |
      * |  FUNCTION:  DISPLAY ABEND INFORMATION.                      |
      * |             DISPLAY TOTALS.                                 |
      * |             DISPLAY TRACE TABLE.                            |
      * |             CONDITIONALLY INVOKE THE ABEND ROUTINE. (THIS   |
      * |               ENABLES THE PROGRAMMER TO DISPLAY ABEND       |
      * |               INFORMATION AND THE TRACE TABLE WITHOUT       |
      * |               ABENDING).                                    |
      * .-------------------------------------------------------------.
       Z9000-ABEND.
           MOVE WS-ABEND-CODE              TO WS-ABEND-CODE-DISPLAY.
           DISPLAY WS-ABEND-INFO-MSG-1.
           DISPLAY WS-ABEND-INFO-MSG-2.
           DISPLAY WS-ABEND-INFO-MSG-3.
           DISPLAY WS-ABEND-INFO-MSG-4.
           PERFORM Z2000-DISPLAY-TOTALS    THRU Z2000-X.
           PERFORM M9900-DUMP-TRACE-TABLE  THRU M9900-X.
           IF WS-ABEND-CODE > ZERO
               CALL 'UTABEND0'             USING WS-ABEND-CODE
           END-IF.
           INITIALIZE                      WS-AIM4.
       Z9000-X.
           EXIT.

Note the comment beginning "Conditionally invoke."

"Robert Jones" <robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk> wrote in message
news:fcd09c56.0205251431.523d227a@posting.google.com...
> Using PERFORM ABORT-ROUTINE can be preferable to using GO TO
> ABORT-ROUTINE, because, when testing, ABORT-ROUTINE can be replaced by
> a dummy procedure to display messages instead, so multiple errors can
> be checked in the same run more easily, cutting down on the testing
> time and also allowing secondary defences to be checked.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "enforced" structured programming

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2002-05-25T21:35:48-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0205252035.21a41b8f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<acjp2a$bpd$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <acljei$j3o$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <fcd09c56.0205251431.523d227a@posting.google.com>`

```
Just an FYI - DEC VMS COBOL enforces the Ansi 85 standard rule
requiring a paragraph within a section.

I have one customer who uses sections and a common exit paragraph --
meaning the paragraph name is the SAME for each section.  A "go to
exit-x" is like an "exit section" in the draft standard.  Personally,
I use sections if that's the method coded before and I don't use them
for new development.

robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones) wrote in message news:<fcd09c56.0205251431.523d227a@posting.google.com>...
> "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message news:<acljei$j3o$1@peabody.colorado.edu>...
> > On 23-May-2002, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:
…[39 more quoted lines elided]…
> require sections to contain paragraphs either.
```

<a id="t-4eb5001528"></a>
## Thread: COBOL LE v2r2 TEST functions also working in v2r1m2? — 2 msgs

### COBOL LE v2r2 TEST functions also working in v2r1m2?

- **From:** bo774@FreeNet.Carleton.CA (Kelly Bert Manning)
- **Date:** 2002-05-24T00:50:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,bit.listserv.ibm-main
- **Message-ID:** `<ack2o8$mfq$1@freenet9.carleton.ca>`

```
I gave up on TEST when LE arrived, since we didn't get whatever the follow
on product to COBTEST was. IIRC COBTEST and PLITEST were part and parcel
of VS COBOL II and PL/I v2r3, but the follow on product was a separate
monthly charge item.

I was asked to help someone locate the source of an S0C7 abend yesterday.

Staring at the CEEDUMP I noticed an information message saying that it
hadn't tried to display variable values because it wasn't compiled with
TEST(SYM). There were also some lables indicating where statement numbers
might be found, but they were all empty.

Recompiled with TEST(NONE,SYM) and CEEDUMP started to display variables and
statement numbers. The compile listing also showed a new third TEST parm,
NOSEPARATE.

None of this is documented in the v2r1 manuals, even the web bookserver 
versions. It does seem to be fully documented in the v2r2 programming guide.

The SEPARATE option seems like a way of avoiding virtual storage headaches
in crowed regions such as IMS Message Processing Regions or CICS Regions
if TEST(SYM) bloats the load modules too much. 

They also avoided the possibility of a loop when printing variable contents
after a S0C7. The variable with bad decimal data was printed as character
data, so trying to print it didn't generate a second S0C7 for the same abend.

Looks like IBM is giving Abend-Aid a serious run for it's money. Abend-Aid
will use PL/I GOSTMT information to print statement numbers on the ABENDAID
report, but so far it doesn't seem to be picking up on this new source of
information. TEST(SYM/NOSYM) isn't on the list of recommended compile
options.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL LE v2r2 TEST functions also working in v2r1m2?

- **From:** "Larry Kahm" <lkahm@bellatlantic.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-24T11:48:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,bit.listserv.ibm-main
- **Message-ID:** `<xMpH8.248$Np5.152@nwrddc01.gnilink.net>`
- **References:** `<ack2o8$mfq$1@freenet9.carleton.ca>`

```
Debug Tool is still a separate product.

But the introduction of the additional sub-parameter came in the form of a PTF.
You can read more about it here:

http://www-1.ibm.com/support/manager.wss?rs=0&rt=0&org=SW&doc=1051316

If you want to learn how Debug Tool can be used in your site, be sure to take a
look at:

http://publib-b.boulder.ibm.com/Redbooks.nsf/RedbookAbstracts/sg246296.html?Open

(Sorry, but I could not resist the shameless plug.)

Larry Kahm
Heliotropic Systems, Inc.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: until greater than

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Anonymous <Nobody>
- **Date:** 2002-05-24T19:18:46-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3cee1f11_1@anonymous>`
- **References:** `<20020523225452.17410.00000206@mb-ft.aol.com> <gRrH8.5476$%o.469060@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3ceda6f0_3@anonymous> <AuyH8.5024$gk.790842@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
>
> There's no need to rely on the wisdom of the sages or the ages: test it
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Microseconds saved per online transaction times millions of transactions
results in runtime cost savings that eventually will pay for your next
raise, assuming of course you can convince someone you deserve one, which is
getting harder and harder to do.

I wouldn't be surprised if I have more years of online transaction
experience than the bulk of the visitors to this newsgroup have had
birthdays.  Yet rarely does a day pass but what I don't learn even more
efficient ways to do things.  I refuse to be a dinosauer stuck in the days
of punched cards and paper tape ...  I require it of myself to stay on the
leading edge of technology.

It has been my observation over the years that, with each new generation of
coder knowing less and less about what is "under the hood" when their code
is compiled, and with computers getting faster and faster, the coders tend
to get sloppier and sloppier.  Eventually you get to the point where coders
produce bloated code like ... well, Microsoft products are a good example of
needlessly bloated code.  And I attribute that to the new breed of coder who
does not see the need to code for runtime efficiency.  After all, the
computers keep getting faster and faster ...

I also have discovered over the years that coding for runtime efficiency,
and coding for efficiency in maintenance, ARE NOT mutually exclusive goals,
but in fact the two go hand and hand quite well to produce a superior
product.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: until greater than

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Anonymous <Nobody>
- **Date:** 2002-05-25T13:10:31-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3cef1a4e_1@anonymous>`
- **References:** `<20020523225452.17410.00000206@mb-ft.aol.com> <gRrH8.5476$%o.469060@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3ceda6f0_3@anonymous> <AuyH8.5024$gk.790842@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3cee1f11_1@anonymous> <FpNH8.12333$qn1.3277482@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
>
> If you think efficiency means changing PIC 99 to PIC 99 USAGE COMP, then
you
> have a lot to learn still.
>
> Donald
>

I'm sure I do, and as I previously said, I probably learn something new
every day.  One tends to do that, unless one is standing still, stagnating.

However, if you are going to do a lot of computations on the PIC 99, and you
think it is efficient to have to generate and execute extra instructions to
put it into a form that the computer can do the computation on, then perhaps
you haven't learned as much in 35 years as you think.  I have met a lot of
people with 1 year of experience, repeated 35 times.

Perhaps the reason clients pay so highly for my services is because I "still
have a lot to learn."  That's also probably what qualifies me for the
performance tuning contract I'm currently on.

And yes, I do think that changing PIC 99 to a suitable computational form
plays a small role in runtime efficiency.  Not only do I think it, I also
know it.
```
