###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: until greater than

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-25T18:01:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CEFD1A6.D35F1D0C@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<20020523225452.17410.00000206@mb-ft.aol.com> <gRrH8.5476$%o.469060@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3ceda6f0_3@anonymous> <AuyH8.5024$gk.790842@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3cee1f11_1@anonymous> <FpNH8.12333$qn1.3277482@news20.bellglobal.com> <3cef1a4e_1@anonymous>`

```


Anonymous wrote:

> >
> > If you think efficiency means changing PIC 99 to PIC 99 USAGE COMP, then
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> know it.

Ahhh. So now the above plays a SMALL ROLE in runtime efficiency, plus "I also
know it". Others have illustrated in here with timings. If you are truly
concerned about the youngsters in here using the efficiencies you deem,
illustrate with some PERFORM cycle code, plus the timings.

I would also take one step backwards. The systems design, (programming is the
second phase), has a major impact on the machine usage. An application is
perceived, then spec'ed and you finish up with a neat rectangular box containing
your design. The speed at which current systems become obsolete/inefficient is
impacted by the increasing pace of business needs/technology. Now bits start to
get added on to that rectangular box and you finish up with a octagonal shape -
still does the job, but messy. The last recourse is a completely fresh design,
meeting today's needs, plus hopefully, allowing a let-out for some of tomorrow's
needs.

Contractually you are into fine tuning, (and I'm assuming this is just 'fine
tuning', not re-design); I would suggest the installation, regardless of size, is
being a bit backward having to call in an outside resource on this one. Given
they have a Maintenance Department (team) fine tuning should be an element they
should be covering.

My own reasoning on that - EDP staff are EXPENSIVE. A project is designed, and
programmed and there is always a planned finish date. This means work allocated
to programmers is given a target date for completion. The Chief Programmer
(whatever fancy title is currently used),  will delegate tasks, having evaluated
how many man-weeks, months it should take to do. The individual may produce a
very workable program, but would *really* have liked, perhaps one or two more
weeks to make it zing by looking for efficiencies. The budget/timescale just
doesn't allow for this.

Having proven the application and got it up and running, then my contention, it
is Maintenance Department which should view source to see where they can re-group
procedures and do the fine tuning that they are obviously paying you big bucks to
do <G>

In passing, you took a swipe at Microsoft and others for bloat. I don't disagree,
but pic 9(02) versus pic 9(02) comp-3 has nothing to do with bloat.  Ironically,
I speculate the type of bloat we see in Windoze products is because of OO. Huh ?
The bright eyed and bushy-tailed, including the geeks at the U. of Bellingham,
Wa., are using OO - I have no proof - but they are probably breaking the cardinal
rule of OO - REUSABILITY. I'll wager there is a lot of 'copy and paste', rather
than, with some forethought, getting into an OO design hierarchy that makes
polymorphism sing like a sweet bird.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: until greater than

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-05-26T08:09:35+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CF08A2F.FBD69A60@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<20020523225452.17410.00000206@mb-ft.aol.com> <gRrH8.5476$%o.469060@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3ceda6f0_3@anonymous> <AuyH8.5024$gk.790842@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3cee1f11_1@anonymous> <FpNH8.12333$qn1.3277482@news20.bellglobal.com> <3cef1a4e_1@anonymous>`

```
Anonymous wrote:
> 
> However, if you are going to do a lot of computations on the PIC 99, and you
> think it is efficient to have to generate and execute extra instructions to
> put it into a form that the computer can do the computation on, then perhaps
> you haven't learned as much in 35 years as you think. 

Actually it was 35 years ago that this sort of thing was absolutely
vital. When I was doing Cobol customer support for a mainframe company
around that time I would spend hours showing customers how to meet their
run-time needs on processors slower than my watch is now.

In very many cases on modern machinery, however, this sort of detailing
is quite irrelevant. The types of applications have changed, the ratio
of processor speed to programmer cost has inverted, mult-tasking,
interrupt driven multi-threading, disk caching, have all been invented.
There are now more important aspects to consider.

> And yes, I do think that changing PIC 99 to a suitable computational form
> plays a small role in runtime efficiency.  Not only do I think it, I also
> know it.

I am sure that you can contrive suitable tests to demonstrate this, but
in real modern systems the cpu time and the disk access time are no
longer additive as they were 30 years ago and most systems spend most of
their time waiting for disk transfers to complete.

So while you claim to be 'up to date' and 'learning', you are merely
regurgitating 'wisdom' that was important several decades ago and is no
longer particularly relevant.

Yes, there are constructs that will generate slightly faster code. 
These may be INDEX or COMP or BINARY or some other depending on the
actual machine the code will run on.

Will PIC 99 COMP be the 'best' code ? - not on my machine.

Will it make any improvement to my systems ? - no.  The system
(multi-user) spends most of its time waiting for disk and has plenty of
spare CPU (around 40-80%).  I would be much better spending my time on
reducing disk transfers, such as by using extra alternate keys where
this may save searches and sorts.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: until greater than

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-25T22:39:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MoUH8.17557$Gs.1949435@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<20020523225452.17410.00000206@mb-ft.aol.com> <gRrH8.5476$%o.469060@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3ceda6f0_3@anonymous> <AuyH8.5024$gk.790842@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3cee1f11_1@anonymous>`

```

"Anonymous" <Nobody> wrote in message news:3cee1f11_1@anonymous...
> >
> > There's no need to rely on the wisdom of the sages or the ages: test it
> > yourself.
> >
> > Couple of perform loops, couple of calls to the clock - Bingo! the
answer
> > appears.
> >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> raise, assuming of course you can convince someone you deserve one, which
is
> getting harder and harder to do.

5 microseconds saved per transaction x 5 million transactions / day = 25
seconds/day saved

Or at $100/hour for an engineer, about the cost of a donut. I guess it all
comes down to how important donuts are to you.

>
> I wouldn't be surprised if I have more years of online transaction
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> leading edge of technology.

I'm glad you learn more efficient ways of doing things. What was the result
of the ten-line program I urged you to try for evaluating the actual time
difference between display and binary subscripts?

>
> It has been my observation over the years that, with each new generation
of
> coder knowing less and less about what is "under the hood" when their code
> is compiled, and with computers getting faster and faster, the coders tend
> to get sloppier and sloppier.  Eventually you get to the point where
coders
> produce bloated code like ... well, Microsoft products are a good example
of
> needlessly bloated code.  And I attribute that to the new breed of coder
who
> does not see the need to code for runtime efficiency.  After all, the
> computers keep getting faster and faster ...

There IS less and less need to code for runtime efficiency because compilers
are MUCH smarter and operating systems are more efficient than before. In
fact, I wouldn't be surprised if prior techniques for operational efficiency
don't actually interfer with the compiler's optimization techniques!

For example, my compiler does something magical with ISAM files in that
loading an ISAM file from a text file generates a file considerably SMALLER
than the original text file.

(TEXT + INDEXES + OVERFLOW < TEXT?)

The ISAM routines are obviously compacting the data. Now I COULD have spent
man-months figuring out a hand-crafted compaction routine myself....


>
> I also have discovered over the years that coding for runtime efficiency,
> and coding for efficiency in maintenance, ARE NOT mutually exclusive
goals,
> but in fact the two go hand and hand quite well to produce a superior
> product.

You're right. Execution and maintenance efficiency are not - necessarily -
mutually exclusive. But which had you rather work on? Which has more bugs?
And which requires more maintenance?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT - "Batch Window" (was: until greater than

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-26T20:45:21-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<acs33m$lec$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<20020523225452.17410.00000206@mb-ft.aol.com> <3cee1f11_1@anonymous> <MoUH8.17557$Gs.1949435@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <acritk$ln9$1@hermes.nz.eds.com> <acs25u$9k9$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:acs25u$9k9$1@panix1.panix.com...
> In article <acritk$ln9$1@hermes.nz.eds.com>,
> Tim Scrivens <tim.scrivens@nz.eds.com> wrote:
> >
 <snip>
> >
> >And then, 5 microseconds per transaction, 5 million transactions.  25
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >Not important in terms of money, but if your batch window runs long -
every
> >bit helps.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Reminds me of a story from the days of  "long ago and far away".

I had just been hired as a "medium-experienced" COBOL, CICS, IMS, ADF (don't
ask - if you never heard of this) programmer at the phone company.  (To give
you an idea of how long ago this was, it was *THE* phone company).

My new manager meet me at the elevator and while walking me to my desk - and
before introducing me to my collogues, she informed me that they had just
finished a review of the new process/application that I was hired to work
on.  The initial estimate was that the nightly batch run would take 26 hours
to run !!!

Needless to say, we did NOT get "right down" to coding that application and
did a little "re-design".

Post-Script to the humorous story.
  I stayed working for that company (thru phone company divestiture and
various other "interesting" times) and went on to many other exciting jobs
(both involving COBOL and not) - from teaching "redundant" phone-operator
mangers to be entry level programmers to being a systems programmer.
However, about 3 years after I left the company (about 7 years after that
initial walk from the elevator), I heard that the company FINALLY cancelled
the entire (still not in production) project ... and went with an entirely
different solution to the problem. <G>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: until greater than

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-27T13:45:40-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1D585A2BC416C81D.0464717689FBAC16.73B70D106A1AEA7B@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<20020523225452.17410.00000206@mb-ft.aol.com> <3cee1f11_1@anonymous> <MoUH8.17557$Gs.1949435@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <acritk$ln9$1@hermes.nz.eds.com> <acs25u$9k9$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
On 26 May 2002 21:29:34 -0400, docdwarf@panix.com enlightened us:

>In article <acritk$ln9$1@hermes.nz.eds.com>,
>Tim Scrivens <tim.scrivens@nz.eds.com> wrote:
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
>DD

While I understand what you are saying there Doc, there are instances
where the batch window is tight due to outside influences and not
because a process or a system is inefficient.  Take a large bank that
is doing central processing and has branches in many states.  If
you're central processor is based on the east coast, you've a 4 hour
delay to get all information from the west coast, hence you may not be
able to start your batch processing until 7-8 pm eastern standard. It
may be latter than that even based on when the ACH information is
transmitted from the Federal Reserve.   And you need to have the
online files refreshed and ready for tellers on the east coast by
approximately 7-8 am eastern.  Twelve hours may seem like a lot, but
it's not.  A twitch in the process or program here or there and you're
in trouble.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Blessed are the flexible for they shall not be bent out of shape.~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-27T21:20:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<20020523225452.17410.00000206@mb-ft.aol.com> <acritk$ln9$1@hermes.nz.eds.com> <acs25u$9k9$1@panix1.panix.com> <1D585A2BC416C81D.0464717689FBAC16.73B70D106A1AEA7B@lp.airnews.net> <acttpe$i41$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> (snip)
> A perfectly reasonable scenario... and one which might be resolved by,
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

Actually, I'd prefer NOT to step in here, but yes, there are certain
challenges to running 24/7/365 and processing transactions in all
time-zones.

If you go to google and search for "ZDnet Visa settlement system new
replacement", you might find an interesting article.  Unfortunately
there's no COBOL, since Visa replaced their IBM mainframe batch
settlement system, written in S/370 assembler, with a new IBM
mainframe batch system written in C and using DB2.  I believe they
processed about 109 million batch transactions in two days during a
parallel conversion.

I am aware of COBOL applications, both batch and online, that process
very high volumes of transactions, so I would argue that sometimes
every microsecond is very important. 

Hope that helps!
```

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2002-05-27T18:05:19-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<acuaiv$cl9$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<20020523225452.17410.00000206@mb-ft.aol.com> <1D585A2BC416C81D.0464717689FBAC16.73B70D106A1AEA7B@lp.airnews.net> <acttpe$i41$1@panix1.panix.com> <3CF2A32B.1745FBAE@worldnet.att.net>`

```
In article <3CF2A32B.1745FBAE@worldnet.att.net>,
Arnold Trembley  <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>> (snip)
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
>Hope that helps!

From 
http://www.eweek.com/article/0,3658,apn=3&s=703&a=19360&app=1&ap=2,00.asp

--begin quoted text:

With officials looking on anxiously, the new system processed 109 million 
transactions valued at $3.5 billion in its first two days. During the 
deployment, both the old and the new systems ran concurrently.

--end quoted text

Most enjoyable, Mr Trembley... but note that it was not argued that there 
was never a time when 'every microsecond is very important', as opposed to 
'sometimes'; what is to be remembered is that if one lives as though 
'sometimes' is 'always' then one is usually... wrong.  The ability to 
squeeze the last few ticks out of a system which is too small for its 
dataflow is a valiant one... but it is like the tailor who can let out the 
trousers of a teenager *one* last time, eventually one will have to either 
spring for a new pair of pants or be rather embarassed.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: until greater than

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2002-05-28T05:56:18-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<acvk82$psm$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<20020523225452.17410.00000206@mb-ft.aol.com> <1D585A2BC416C81D.0464717689FBAC16.73B70D106A1AEA7B@lp.airnews.net> <acttpe$i41$1@panix1.panix.com> <acuqde$clr$1@hermes.nz.eds.com>`

```
In article <acuqde$clr$1@hermes.nz.eds.com>,
Tim Scrivens <tim.scrivens@nz.eds.com> wrote:
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:acttpe$i41$1@panix1.panix.com...
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>AM) - and you better not go much past 6.

Mr Scrivens, you might not work in banking but the techniques might be 
applicable... or other techniques that might arise from a review of the 
Way Things Are Done, that is all I am pointing out.

For example: I spent time at a brokerage, long ago, and every night was a 
race to beat the window.  I took a look at some of the jobstreams and 
noticed that a goodly many of them started with an IDCAMS REPRO of the 
Customer Master File KSDS to a &&TEMP flatfile, a SORT of this flatfile in 
the same sequence and then program processing of this sorted flatfile.

(note to non-IBM mainframe folks - an indexed file was copied to a 
temporary file and this temp file was sorted for further processing)

I suggested that if this was necessary then it might save a bit of their 
precious time to consolidate jobs so that this sorting was done fewer 
times... and did folks know that SyncSort could deal with a KSDS 
directly, no REPRO needed?...

... and I was taken aside and told, in no uncertain terms:

1) I was on a contract to do online work (CICS) and was *not* to pay 
attention to what was happening on the batch side

2) The recently-arrived new CIO wanted some new hardware so these - and 
other - inefficiencies were not to be touched.

Now this second one might seem 'merely political'... until one notices 
'recently-arrived new'; these problems had been there for a while.

Now I am but a COBOL-codin' fool; there are folks who are *much* more 
skilled at the arcana of systems tuning, buffer-diddling (anyone remember 
how to RESERVE 32 ALTERNATE AREAS?) and other such things... and what I 
suggest is that they, too, get involved in jobs that run too long, if only 
to provide a fresh set of eyes.

DD
```

```
Understood, Mr Scrivens... but it might be time to recall unto Management 
the words of Thomas Jefferson:

http://odur.let.rug.nl/~usa/P/tj3/writings/brf/jefl246.htm

--begin quoted text:

But I know also, that laws and institutions must go hand in hand with the 
progress of the human mind.  As that becomes more developed, more 
enlightened, as new discoveries are made, new truths disclosed, and 
manners and opinions changewith the change of circumstances, institutions 
must advance also, and keep pace with the times.  We might as require a 
man to wear still the coat which fitted him as a boy, as civilized society 
to remain ever under the regimen of their barbarous ancestors.

--end quoted text

... and likewise with systems.  Since your organisation has bound it'sself 
to a coat of a particular size - by the tool that has been chosen, in this 
case - then it must be determined when the limits of this garment have 
been reached and the firm starts turning away new business with a 
sorrowful 'We must apologise but our DP/IT/MIS/IS systems cannot handle 
more business.'

*That*, of course, is anathema... just as it would be anathema for a 
mechanic to turn down jobs because he doesn't have a large-enough hammer.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: until greater than

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** jacksleight@hotmail.com (Jack Sleight)
- **Date:** 2002-05-26T18:05:42-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a2d426e.0205261705.101e039d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<20020523225452.17410.00000206@mb-ft.aol.com> <gRrH8.5476$%o.469060@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <7wsH8.18701$Hf4.2920005@news20.bellglobal.com> <acln84$l49$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Well, very interesting gentlemen, but the 400 lb gorilla is still
standing in the center of the room and nobody notices.

Why would anyone choose a perform varying routine with 2 levels in
preference to a move '0' or an initialize. From both perspectives
discussed here: programmer productivity and system efficiency, it
makes no sense.

Also, remember I said "efficiency" not "performance". In a mainframe
environment, where hundreds of jobs are running concurrently, choices
like the one made by Mr. Klatte, in aggregate, can have a significant
effect on CPU utilization, and by implication the cost of doing
business.

I've heard the line about per MIP and MEG costs plunging. But I
haven't heard many DP mgrs bragging about their H/W budgets plunging.
 
Because efficiency still makes sense, training yourself to code
efficiently makes sense too.

Granted in a "single transaction at a time" environment, these
efficiencies have little effect. I'd guess if you decided to perform
the process manually, it makes even less sense.

My apologies to Mr. Klatte, but that New Age B.S. just sets my teeth
on edge. "Cool", Jesus.


   

"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message news:<acln84$l49$1@peabody.colorado.edu>...
> On 24-May-2002, "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:
> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> This is costly.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: until greater than

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** klatteross@aol.commmm (Ross Klatte)
- **Date:** 2002-05-27T10:30:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020527063059.17636.00000524@mb-ms.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<8a2d426e.0205261705.101e039d@posting.google.com>`

```
>From: jacksleight@hotmail.com  (Jack Sleight)
>Date: 2002-05-26 21:05 Eastern Daylight Time

>Why would anyone choose a perform varying routine with 2 levels in
>preference to a move '0' or an initialize. 

>choices
>like the one made by Mr. Klatte, in aggregate, can have a significant
>effect on CPU utilization

>My apologies to Mr. Klatte, but that New Age B.S. just sets my teeth
>on edge. "Cool", Jesus.
>

On the program in question, I am a maintenance programmer,
not a designer.  Furthermore, I am a contractor, not an FTE. 

Typically, a Unisys shop will have a number of programmers
who learned either on IBM or on C.  These people make a
lot of mistakes on the COMP and COMP-n usages.  The
"no binary, no packed" rule is there to better utilize these
people.  
As I said earlier, my own choice would have been to eliminate
the manual initialization altogether; it is not necessary.  
As I said earlier, I cannot INITIALIZE a table.  
As I implied earlier, I would prefer MOVE ZEROES to the
table.  In looking at other programs, I observed that this
code is not used.  In every case, there is a routine
to move the zero one element at a time.  I suspect, but
do not know, that this technique was settled upon in order
to eliminate arguments and errors resulting from the 
exact behavior of MOVE ZEROES or MOVE 0 or MOVE 
LOW-VALUES.  My only legitimate interest in the code
is the fact that it undeniably blew up.  It would be 
politically and programmatically unwise to start
changing code that has proven itself in production, just
because I think that I know a better way. 

This is the first time in my life that I have ever been 
accused of being "new age."  As a point of reference,
I voted for Barry Goldwater.  I take the intended insult
as a great compliment.  This dinosaur is evolving in place.
Today, the "WITH TEST AFTER."  Tomorrow, the "ALSO. 





Ross
http://community.webshots.com/user/ross_klatte
```

<a id="t-0cb59f7979"></a>
## Thread: PIC S9(9) V99 COMP-3 — 15 this month / 19 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### PIC S9(9) V99 COMP-3

- **From:** Jürgen Borho <borho@tesionmaill.de>
- **Date:** 2002-05-24T11:16:18+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CEE04E2.7020001@tesionmaill.de>`

```
Hello,

I have to extract data from an old btrieve database. I have a small description 
of all the fields, but I don't know, how to read data which is described as 'PIC 
S9(9) V99 COMP-3'. I'm not a COBOL programmer, but somebody told me, that this 
describes a packed decimal number in COBOL. I want to read and convert the data 
from an ASCII-File.

Here are 2 examples (each 6 bytes, in hex):
0C 24 86 1C 01 40
0C 24 86 1C 18 6C

Does somebody know, how I can get a correct ASCII-string representation for such 
numeric data?

Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: PIC S9(9) V99 COMP-3

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-24T14:07:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<BOrH8.2431$jm.489016@bin6.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3CEE04E2.7020001@tesionmaill.de>`

```

"J�rgen Borho" <borho@tesionmaill.de> wrote in message
news:3CEE04E2.7020001@tesionmaill.de...
> Hello,
>
> I have to extract data from an old btrieve database. I have a small
description
> of all the fields, but I don't know, how to read data which is described
as 'PIC
> S9(9) V99 COMP-3'. I'm not a COBOL programmer, but somebody told me, that
this
> describes a packed decimal number in COBOL. I want to read and convert the
data
> from an ASCII-File.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Does somebody know, how I can get a correct ASCII-string representation
for such
> numeric data?

No, sorry. Can't help you.

The values you give are not 'standard packed-decimal' representations. A
'standard' packed number looks like this:

   $12345.67 = 12 34 56 7C (The "C" in the last nibble is the algebraic
sign)

Your samples are not even close.

It may be the case that the numbers are the result of some
'implementor-defined' representation of packed-decimal fields. If so, we'll
need to know the machine and compiler that generated the numbers. The
'Btrieve' thingy is irrelevant; Btrieve is a file system and cares not what
the internal representation of field might be.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: PIC S9(9) V99 COMP-3

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-05-24T14:51:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<acljnh$j7r$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3CEE04E2.7020001@tesionmaill.de> <BOrH8.2431$jm.489016@bin6.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
I wonder if you might have an ASCII representation of EBCDIC characters.  If
it is a copy of a mainframe file, get it translated there.
```


#### ↳ Re: PIC S9(9) V99 COMP-3

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-24T12:32:33-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<DBA7E022F44CA66F.7B30D327645E882C.28A8D83BD489B3D8@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<3CEE04E2.7020001@tesionmaill.de>`

```
On Fri, 24 May 2002 11:16:18 +0200, Jï¿½rgen Borho
<borho@tesionmaill.de> enlightened us:

>Hello,
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thanks

If your data is coming from an IBM or IBM clone mainframe, then a
field described as S9(9)V99  COMP-3 is indeed packed decimal and it
would be 6 bytes long in storage.  However, the two examples you show
here would not be valid.  The sample you showed looks like it could be
3 fields, 2 packed and 1 either packed, display or invalid!  To have a
valid 6 byte packed decimal field, your storage should look like:

NN NN NN NN NN NC  or
NN NN NN NN NN NF  or
NN NN NN NN NN ND where

N = any numeric digit from 0 - 9,
C = valid + sign
F = valid + sign
D = valid - sign

HTH

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Ever notice that people who spend money on beer, cigarettes, 
and lottery tickets are always complaining about being broke 
and not feeling well?
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

```
enlightened us:

>> Hello,
…[60 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Sorry but that is just not true.  There are instances where the sign
in a packed decimal field can be an F particularly if the field was
converted from display to packed and no arithmetic has been performed
on the field.  You can safely add two packed fields, one with a C sign
and one with an F sign with no data exception occurring.

>            
>> > 
>> > HTH
>> > 
Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-
```

```
SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com> wrote in message news:<DBA7E022F44CA66F.7B30D327645E882C.28A8D83BD489B3D8@lp.airnews.net>...
> On Fri, 24 May 2002 11:16:18 +0200, Jï¿½rgen Borho
> <borho@tesionmaill.de> enlightened us:
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> N = any numeric digit from 0 - 9,

     -----------------------------------------
 
       Friends,

       Actually "N" is a hexadecimal digit
       it is from 0 - F . It is not a decimal 
       digit
          
       regards,
       vijay 

     ------------------------------------------
 
> C = valid + sign
> F = valid + sign
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PIC S9(9) V99 COMP-3

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-27T13:18:40-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<acttg9$34e$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3CEE04E2.7020001@tesionmaill.de> <DBA7E022F44CA66F.7B30D327645E882C.28A8D83BD489B3D8@lp.airnews.net> <5cb8574f.0205270947.2160ede9@posting.google.com>`

```
The final note in this response is certainly NOT true in IBM mainframe
environments - or any other environment that I know of.  Packed-Decimal (not
binary) "digit" nibbles *must* be 0-9 and NOT "A-F".

Also, (again in IBM environments - and in reply to another note), the
sign-nibble *may* be A-F.  It is true that C or D are "preferred" but "F" is
tolerated (even in signed-numbers) in many (not all) COBOL cases - and is
often produced by non-COBOL applications.  A, B, and E are *never* produced
by COBOL, but are valid in IBM S/390 (S/360 or S/370) architecture - and
certain non-Assembler applications do produce them. All IBM mainframe
compilers from OS/VS COBOL on (I don't know about earlier) have options for
handling A, B, and E
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PIC S9(9) V99 COMP-3

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2002-05-27T14:39:46-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<actuhi$jh0$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<3CEE04E2.7020001@tesionmaill.de> <DBA7E022F44CA66F.7B30D327645E882C.28A8D83BD489B3D8@lp.airnews.net> <5cb8574f.0205270947.2160ede9@posting.google.com> <acttg9$34e$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
In article <acttg9$34e$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>,
William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:

[snip]

>Also, (again in IBM environments - and in reply to another note), the
>sign-nibble *may* be A-F.  It is true that C or D are "preferred" but "F" is
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>handling A, B, and E

Well, I was going to remain quiet about this but... thanks for mentioning 
this, Mr Klein; I made use of it just recently in an experiment which 
failed but might bear mentioning.

I had a file of... stuff, extracted from a DB2 table, which represented 
reversed charges but which were (for Very Good Reasons, I am sure) stored 
in the tables as positive values.  I had another file of similar... stuff 
that I wanted to balance against the first one... and since this is a part 
of the Never-Ending Problem I mentioned earlier I was working under the 
constraints of of SQL, FileAid and SyncSort.

So... what I did was a FileAid Replace on the sign-byte of the 
reversed-charge file, changing X'0C' to X'0B', X'1C' to X'1B'... X'9C' to 
X'9B'.  I then used SyncSort to SUM these fields and then another FileAid 
to strip out the unmatched reversed charges, leaving behind only 
unreversed charges and matched charge/reversed pairs (with a value of 0 in 
the cost field and a value of 2 in the record-count field).

Anyhow... as I was putting this together another coder asked why I was 
using sign-nibble 'B' instead of 'D'... and I reflexively responded 
'Because when it blows up it'll be obvious where these records came from.'  
His response was a glazed-eye 'Oh... I see' and until your explanation 
above I wasn't sure why mine was... the way it was.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PIC S9(9) V99 COMP-3

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Rod Hewitt <rodders4@NOSPAMntlworld.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-27T19:47:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Xns921BD3DB9DD23rodders4ntlworldcom@80.3.128.4>`
- **References:** `<3CEE04E2.7020001@tesionmaill.de> <DBA7E022F44CA66F.7B30D327645E882C.28A8D83BD489B3D8@lp.airnews.net> <5cb8574f.0205270947.2160ede9@posting.google.com> <acttg9$34e$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <actuhi$jh0$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```

And on at least some systems - all that matters is the least significant 
bit. 0 for positive and 1 for negative, which is, of course, the only 
difference between x'C' and x'D' anyway.

Rod
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PIC S9(9) V99 COMP-3

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamDD@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-29T03:55:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CF45115.8020705@optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3CEE04E2.7020001@tesionmaill.de> <DBA7E022F44CA66F.7B30D327645E882C.28A8D83BD489B3D8@lp.airnews.net> <5cb8574f.0205270947.2160ede9@posting.google.com> <acttg9$34e$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:

> The final note in this response is certainly NOT true in IBM mainframe
> environments - or any other environment that I know of.  Packed-Decimal (not
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> handling A, B, and E


This is the hardware implementation on the IBM side. The following is 
from IBM's "z/Architecture Principles of Operation":

A.1.2 Decimal Integers

Decimal integers consist of one or more decimal digits and a sign. Each 
digit and the sign are represented by a 4-bit code. The decimal digits 
are in binary-coded decimal (BCD) form, with the values 0-9 encoded as 
0000-1001. The sign is usually represented as 1100 (C hex) for plus and 
1101 (D hex) for minus. These are the preferred sign codes, which are 
generated by the machine for the results of decimal-arithmetic 
operations. There are also several alternate sign codes (1010, 1110, and 
1111 for plus; 1011 for minus). The alternate sign codes are accepted by 
the machine as valid in source operands but are not generated for results.

Decimal integers may have different lengths, from one to 16 bytes. There 
are two decimal formats: packed and zoned. In the packed format, each 
byte contains two decimal digits, except for the rightmost byte, which 
contains the sign code in the right half. For decimal arithmetic, the 
number of decimal digits in the packed format can vary from one to 31. 
Because decimal integers must consist of whole bytes and there must be a 
sign code on the right, the number of decimal digits is always odd. If 
an even number of significant digits is desired, a leading zero must be 
inserted on the left.

   -----------------------------------------------------------

8.3.2 COMPARE DECIMAL

CP     D1(L1,B1),D2(L2,B2)     [SS]

________ ____ ____ ____ _/__ ____ _/__ | 'F9' | L1 | L2 | B1 | D1 | B2 | 
D2 | |________|____|____|____|_/__|____|_/__| 0 8 12 16 20 32 36 47


The first operand is compared with the second operand, and the result is 
indicated in the condition code. The operands are in the packed format.

Comparison is algebraic and follows the procedure for decimal 
subtraction, except that both operands remain unchanged. When the 
difference is zero, the operands are equal. When a nonzero difference is 
positive or negative, the first operand is high or low, respectively.

Overflow cannot occur because the difference is discarded.

All sign and digit codes are checked for validity.

Resulting Condition Code:

0 Operands equal
1 First operand low
2 First operand high
3 --

Program Exceptions:

  * Access (fetch, operands 1 and 2)
  * Data

Programming Notes:

1. An example of the use of the COMPARE DECIMAL instruction is given in 
Appendix A, "Number Representation and Instruction-Use Examples."

2. The preferred and alternate sign codes for a particular sign are 
treated as equivalent for comparison purposes.

3. A negative zero and a positive zero compare equal.

ï¿½ Copyright IBM Corp. 1990, 2000

   ----------------------------------------------------------

8.3.9 TEST DECIMAL


TP     D1(L1,B1)        [RSL]

________ ____ ____ ____ _/__ ________ ________ | 'EB' | L1 |////| B1 | 
D1 |////////| 'C0' | |________|____|____|____|_/__|________|________| 0 
8 12 16 20 32 40 47


The first operand is tested for valid decimal digits and a valid sign 
code, and the result is indicated in the condition code. The operand is 
in the packed format.

Resulting Condition Code:

0 All digit codes and the sign valid
1 Sign invalid
2 At least one digit code invalid
3 Sign invalid and at least one digit code invalid

Program Exceptions:

  * Access (fetch, operand 1)
  * Operation (if the extended-translation facility 2 is not installed)

ï¿½ Copyright IBM Corp. 1990, 2000

IBM BookManagerï¿½ BookServer Copyright 1989, 2001 IBM Corporation. All 
rights reserved.

http://publibfp.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/DZ9ZR000/CCONTENTS?DT=20010102160855

   ---------------------------------------------------------

Notice: "2. The preferred and alternate sign codes for a particular sign 
are treated as equivalent for comparison purposes" for the Compare 
Decimal instruction.

I was interesting in the new (to me) TP instruction (with apologies to a 
COBOL NG).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PIC S9(9) V99 COMP-3

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-29T00:47:50-05:00

```
And read  "2.41 NUMCLS" at

 http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igy3c101/2.41

for a discussion of how this "underlying" architecture support may be
MODIFIED by COBOL installation options.

 http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igy3mg00/4.2.1

discusses how this was handled in "earlier" COBOL compilers
```

#### ↳ Re: OT: Delete a GDG base

- **From:** Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-25T07:47:34+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<959ueuc0e0jfauii6mjhoccopm05bh0lup@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<oCxH8.3570$jm.745545@bin6.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
To delete an empty GDG use IDCAMS 

   DELETE MY.GDG  GDG PURGE

If the GDG is not empty, you might try

   DELETE MY.GDG GDG OURGE FORCE

(Which may or may not work due to RACF settings) or

   DELETE MY.GDG.*  NONVSAM PURGE
   DELETE MY.GDG    GDG     PURGE

HTH
```

## Thread: COBOL FAQ — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### COBOL FAQ

- **From:** wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
- **Date:** 2002-05-25T10:34:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,news.answers,comp.answers
- **Message-ID:** `<computer-lang/cobol-faq_1022322829@rtfm.mit.edu>`

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2002/05/06
Version: 3.03
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Spring 2002

The full COBOL FAQ can be viewed at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send
private email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

Toward the top of the FAQ is a pointer to the appendix listing the most
recent significant updates and changes.

This FAQ answers such questions as (and addresses topics such as):

 - Where can I get a COBOL compiler? 
 - Is there a free COBOL compiler? 
 - Where can I contact... ?
 - What is happening with the draft of the next COBOL Standard and what
is in it? 
 - What about OO COBOL? 
 - Books about COBOL
 - COBOL Tools
 - What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
 - What about USAGE? COMP? Storage for data in xyz format? etc?
 - How do I get started with COBOL? Where can I get education?
Tutorials? Etc

and much, MUCH more
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: LPT Ports.

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <frederico_fonseca@eudoramail.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-28T18:27:11+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uef7fuc3vm1a1hgjdfrh7le2btsvg8ug0a@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<uevst6r4cbhgaf@corp.supernews.com> <Xns921A3508AE9Erodders4ntlworldcom@80.3.128.4> <uf6vk02m3v0591@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Tue, 28 May 2002 08:58:00 -0400, "Joe Hunter" <pcs@usaor.net>
wrote:

>
>"Rod Hewitt" <rodders4@NOSPAMntlworld.com> wrote in message
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>Joe Hunter
Adding additional LPT Ports

   1. Start Registry Editor (Regedt32.exe).


    * Click the following registry key. The current LPT ports are
displayed in the right pane:


   2. HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software\Microsoft\Windows
NT\CurrentVersion\Ports On the Edit menu, click Add Value .


    * Add a new value with the following attributes:


      Value Name: LPT # :
      Data Type: REG_SZ
      String: <blank> NOTE : When you create the new value name,
replace " # " in the "LPT # :" value name with the number of the LPT
port you want to add.

    * Quit Registry Editor.


    * Stop and restart the Spooler service by typing net stop spooler
and then net start spooler at a command prompt.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: LPT Ports.

```
Rod
```

```
Joe Hunter wrote:

> It seems to me that you can only print to LPT1, LPT2, and LPT3 in W2K and
> XP.
> 
> 
> Is any one printing to a LPT port higher then 3 using W2k or XP?


Printer ports don't have to be "LPTx". I have a printer port defined 
called "PRTmate". Perhaps you should try some alternate names?
```

<a id="t-d4c48a1e26"></a>
## Thread: power cobol date — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Date and calendar processing`](topics.md#dates)_

### power cobol date

- **From:** "Sim Choon Howe " <schowe@singnet.com.sg>
- **Date:** 2002-05-26T23:21:24+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<acquma$t5m$1@violet.singnet.com.sg>`

```
Hi,
I would like to know what command to use for Powercobol to extract system date?

If the date given is in terms of yyyymmdd
such as 20020526

I would like to change the dd to be always at 01
which will end up as 20020501
If I am not wrong, I can use string delimited and store yyyy to one field, mm to one field and dd to one field. From there I can change dd as 01.

But the main problem is what command should I use to put them back altogether?

Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: power cobol date

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-26T17:58:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Vm9I8.26518$%o.2725502@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<acquma$t5m$1@violet.singnet.com.sg>`

```

"Sim Choon Howe " <schowe@singnet.com.sg> wrote in message
news:acquma$t5m$1@violet.singnet.com.sg...
> Hi,
> I would like to know what command to use for Powercobol to extract system
date?
>
> If the date given is in terms of yyyymmdd
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> If I am not wrong, I can use string delimited and store yyyy to one field,
mm to one field and dd to one field. From there I can change dd as 01.
>
> But the main problem is what command should I use to put them back
altogether?
>
> Thanks

01  TODAYS-DATE-TIME.
   02  TODAYS-DATE   PIC X(8).
   02  FILLER    PIC X(13).


MOVE FUNCTION(CURRENT-DATE) TO TODAYS-DATE-TIME.
MOVE '01' TO TODAYS-DATE(7:2).
```

## Thread: EMPTY OR NULL — 4 msgs

### EMPTY OR NULL

- **From:** "Sim Choon Howe " <schowe@singnet.com.sg>
- **Date:** 2002-05-27T17:50:29+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<acsvlv$2t8$1@violet.singnet.com.sg>`

```
Hi, would like to know what are the command availables to check for empty fields in the picture box of powercobol

I tried doing this way but cannot be accepted by the compiler.

if POW-NUMERIC OF AMOUNT IS EMPTY
. 
.
.
.
I tried empty and null but both command does not seems to work
```

#### ↳ Re: EMPTY OR NULL

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-27T15:08:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8_rI8.45968$Gs.3780779@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<acsvlv$2t8$1@violet.singnet.com.sg>`

```

"Sim Choon Howe " <schowe@singnet.com.sg> wrote in message
news:acsvlv$2t8$1@violet.singnet.com.sg...
> Hi, would like to know what are the command availables to check for empty
fields in the picture box of powercobol

There is no such thing as an 'empty' field. A field always has something in
it. Most applications define SPACES in a field as the absence of data. So:

MOVE 'TEXT' OF SCREEN-FIELD TO TEMP-FIELD.
IF TEMP-FIELD = SPACES
   do something about absence of data
   (i.e., complain to user, set default, etc.)
END-IF.

PowerCOBOL lacks the VB "Validate" method and there is no "Must-Enter"
property.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: EMPTY OR NULL

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-05-27T17:40:37+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3cf25fbe_5@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<acsvlv$2t8$1@violet.singnet.com.sg> <8_rI8.45968$Gs.3780779@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```

JerryMouse <nospam@invalid.com> wrote in message
news:8_rI8.45968$Gs.3780779@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com...
>
>
> PowerCOBOL lacks the VB "Validate" method and there is no "Must-Enter"
> property.
>
Hmmm... There is in my Version ;-)

I have a "Must be entered" Text Box component that I drop on the form
instead of the standard text box, when I want a mandatory field (rare
occasions). It has all the standard text box control properties, methods,
and events, but the underlying Class has been extended to check that data
was entered... Simple OO solution. It won't allow the cursor to move away
from the field until data is entered. I'm thinking of extending it further
to produce a user dialog box if they try three times and still don't enter
data... just tell them it's a mandatory field.

I also have a "Numeric input" Text Box component that automatically
validates for numeric input and sets the number into around 8 different
formats (custom properties) for easy passing to COBOL and non-COBOL
applications. It produces a User message if the data is not numeric and
explains what is required, with examples of valid input (custom methods).

Of course, if I have a "Must Be Entered Numeric Input" field I'm a bit
stuffed at the moment...<G>

(only kidding... the numeric validation component "detaches" from the Text
Box if required and can be dropped directly on the form where it remains
invisible. You can invoke its methods and access its properties just as if
it was a GUI control. Pass it a string and invoke its "CheckNumeric" Method.
Don't need Validate...<G>)

Anyone using PowerCOBOL could easily write these components to extend the
arsenal. I'll happily help anyone who wants to have a go... I won't give you
these components because they took me a lot of effort to develop. Now that I
know how to do it, it is much less effort. You will need to understand
Object Oriented COBOL. Alternatively, send cash...<G>

Pete.



 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

<a id="t-6e9cf19351"></a>
## Thread: IBM Mainframe COBOL "people" — 25 this month / 111 total

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### IBM Mainframe COBOL "people"

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-27T19:36:16-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<acuje5$s4r$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Given some private conversations, I would be interested in some input from
any of "you" who are actually doing "day-to-day" coding in COBOL on IBM
mainframe environments.  I am *particularly* interested in those who are
employees rather than "contractors" or "consultants" - but any input that I
can get will be gratefully received. (Please pass this on, if there are
others you know that you think might provide me with some "real-world"
input.)

It seems to me that few IBM mainframe COBOL programmers are planning on
attending the upcoming (end of June) "COBOL Expo 2002".  I have some guesses
of my own on why this might be, but I am interested in input from others.  I
am also interested in any "relevant" input on any of these topics:

1) It seems to me that VERY few IBM mainframe COBOL programmers
"participate" in the comp.lang.cobol Usenet newsgroup or the IBM-MAIN
list-server.  Why do you think this is?  I can think of a few possibilities
(NOT mutually exclusive), but I don't know how realistic they are:
  - IBM mainframe COBOL programmers want to put in a "day's work" and then
stop thinking about COBOL
 - IBM mainframe COBOL programmers are "old-timers" who are just "waiting"
for the day they can retire and aren't interested in other forums for
discussing "recent" or "new" techniques and issues
 - IBM mainframe COBOL programmers get enough "inter-action" with other
COBOL programmers at work, and don't think they need other venues
 - IBM mainframe COBOL programmers *really* want to get into some other
field/job and see COBOL as a "dead-end"
 - The estimates of how many programmers there really are still doing COBOL
coding on IBM mainframes is really highly exaggerated.
 - others?

2) From what I hear, the COBOL (and LE) projects at SHARE aren't
particularly large/active (but certainly more alive than the now defunct
GUIDE projects).  What *would* interest IBM mainframe COBOL programmers in
the way of "presentations," discussions, forums, etc?

3) How much of the time of IBM mainframe COBOL programmers are spent with
"mainframe-only/primarily" applications and how much is spent with
applications that (now) normally have PC-ish "front-ends"?

4) What do you think can/could be done to "improve" the image (both
self-image and image from outside) of those doing IBM mainframe COBOL
programming?

  ***

*OBVIOUSLY* some/most of this applies to other mainframe COBOL programmers,
but I am particularly interested in those in IBM environments.

Feel free to respond to me publicly via CLC or IBM-MAIN - or via private
email.  This is *not* an official (much less paid for) survey - but is aimed
at my trying to get in touch with those "on the front line" today.  Anything
posted publicly - will obviously be available to others; things sent to me
privately will NOT be generally distributed - unless explicitly allowed by
the sender.
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL "people"

- **From:** "Jean-Ren��� Duval" <jrduval@videotron.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-28T00:15:01-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6yDI8.20752$SQ4.1267311@wagner.videotron.net>`
- **References:** `<acuje5$s4r$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

COBOL
> coding on IBM mainframes is really highly exaggerated.
>  - others?
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> *OBVIOUSLY* some/most of this applies to other mainframe COBOL
programmers,

> at my trying to get in touch with those "on the front line" today.
Anything
> posted publicly - will obviously be available to others; things sent to me
> privately will NOT be generally distributed - unless explicitly allowed by
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Some of your assumptions are true but others are not. At least from my point
of view.
I'm 36 year old, I read alomost every thread on this newsgroup and I don't
consider myself to be an old-timer (at least I don't think so).  I'm not
very active because a lot of the participants are very good if not terrific
on finding answers in record time. Those guys are good. When I first start
working into programming I was into Web development. A year later I got
fired (small company =small budget) and the company witch I'm currently
working for hired me and trained me to do some Y2K work on mainframe.

Years have passed and I'm still working on Mainframe. Stuck with it but
still enjoying it.  Stuck because there are fewer and fewer of us working
and want to work on those relics (in a good sense).
When I talk to poeple from work and when I say at my age that I'm working on
 MAINFRAME there's often a smile from their faces. I often reply with that
kind of phrase. When was the last time that your VB, COOLGEN or Delphi
application freeze your  PC or even some network servers? Doesn't happen
very often with Batch Cobol and CICS. That keeps them quiet for a few hours
:)

Now to answer your questions I spend almost all of my time (90 %) doing
mainframe COBOL applications. No database. Just VSAM and  flat files. The
sofware were working with comes from a company called GEAC (human ressoures
and payroll system) for those who happens to know this software. The rest of
the time is spent doing reports and documentations.

I haven't heard about the project you're talking about (SHARE) but from
where I live there's not a lot of people knowing English either. (I live in
Quebec-City where 98 % of the people speaks french and maybe 50 % can follow
a conversation and speak in English).

The problem with this environment is that (alomost ) nobody knows what we're
doing. It's very easy to show to others when working on PC. Everything is in
pretty windows and poeple can interact with what you've done. Try that with
a mainframe applications.  Honey what have you done today at work ? Well
I've done a JCL that could automatically backup history files from users and
then update the main VSAM database. Heih????. Eventhough you know that
you've done good work the majority won't even know what you've done. CICS
screens are not pretty and eventhough there're working fine, nowadays users
want GUI and I cannot blame them.

There's handfull of people I know who are still doing mainframe COBOL
application from where I work and most of them want to do something else.
All I hear is Oracle, SQL server, VB and COOLGEN. Nobody wants to code with
COBOL, JCL, SAS and CICS and AHHH assembly langage. Those words means boring
and old...

If you like more input I'll be very happy to.
Thank you !

Jean-Ren� Duval

jrduval@xxx.videotron.ca
remove the x to respond.

Se tromper est humain mais pour vraiment tout bousiller il faut un
ordinateur.
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL "people"

- **From:** Joseph Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2002-05-28T20:44:52+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<280520022044520394%josephk@iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<acuje5$s4r$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
In article <acuje5$s4r$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>, William M. Klein
<wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:

> 
> It seems to me that few IBM mainframe COBOL programmers are planning on
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
In my experience:
1. Most mainframe programmers don't even know that the SHARE
organization exists, and what it's about.
2. Most system programmers do know about SHARE.
3. System programmers look upon SHARE as their club.
4. If they were to let mere "programmers" go to SHARE, they might lose
their own spot, as a SHARE conference is fairly expensive, and an
organization might choose to send only one or two delegates.

On a related topic:
Most mainframe programmers don't even know about this new group.
Most mainframe sites, tightly control their access to the Internet
(even for the PC's connected via a different network).
This means that most programmers do not have access to these
news-groups.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL "people"

- **From:** "Alan Neeld" <alan.neeld@btinternet.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-28T19:59:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```

"Joseph Katnic" <josephk@iinet.net.au> wrote in message
news:280520022044520394%josephk@iinet.net.au...
> Most mainframe sites, tightly control their access to the Internet
> (even for the PC's connected via a different network).
> This means that most programmers do not have access to these
> news-groups.
I think that supports one of Bill's original guesses - "IBM mainframe COBOL
programmers want to put in a "day's work" and then stop thinking about
COBOL". And was also true before the rise of the Internet.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL "people"

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-28T22:06:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CF3FF67.59DAB44A@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<acuje5$s4r$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <280520022044520394%josephk@iinet.net.au> <ad0nie$hum$1@helle.btinternet.com>`

```


Alan Neeld wrote:

> "Joseph Katnic" <josephk@iinet.net.au> wrote in message
> news:280520022044520394%josephk@iinet.net.au...
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> COBOL". And was also true before the rise of the Internet.

Certainly Alan's comment is valid. Here in Calgary there is the same corporate
stringent approach to ensure non-computer people don't just spend their days
whistling around the Internet. And of course, you are gonzo if you hit the
X-sites !

Although mainframers in a large installation can swap ideas - without getting
outside your own particular turf, just how do you discover new techniques. Has
the mainframe world reached a situation, (particularly geared to batch
processing), where there are no new techniques to be found ?

However, for those of us, who still put in our eight hour day, and get involved
here - what does that make us, geeks <G>.

One provocative suggestion - for the most part mainframe programming is BORING
- while there are certainly challenges, just like Doc has highlighted with his
DB problem, you can do more 'exciting' stuff with PCs. That's not meant as a
put down - but does it state a reality ?  (How often does a programmer in a
large installation take an application from StartProgram to FinalProgram in an
application.)

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL "people"

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-28T19:59:10-06:00

```
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> news-groups.

Yes!  This is quite true regarding these conferences and system
programmers.  I want to this year's WAVV (World Alliance of VSE and VM)
conference, and I wouldn't be surprised if I was the only applications
programmer there.  Everything was geared toward systems programming.

As for Internet access, you are right again.  We have no newsgroup
access at work.  I believe we can receive mailing lists via email, but I
myself read VSE-L at home.
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL "people"

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-28T11:42:29-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<06680E6E14089DD2.68A2B4ECAB5D2206.97C8A5C7450E1996@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<acuje5$s4r$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
On Mon, 27 May 2002 19:36:16 -0500, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> enlightened us:

>Given some private conversations, I would be interested in some input from
>any of "you" who are actually doing "day-to-day" coding in COBOL on IBM
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

OK that would be me..I am an employee and I support applications on an
IBM mainframe in both Assembler and Cobol.

>It seems to me that few IBM mainframe COBOL programmers are planning on
>attending the upcoming (end of June) "COBOL Expo 2002".  I have some guesses
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>stop thinking about COBOL

I was this way back in the early 80's when personnel computers were
just becoming affordable.  My old adage was, I program all day why do
I want to fool around with a computer and/or programming at home at
night?  It took a year or so, but I came to my senses!

> - IBM mainframe COBOL programmers are "old-timers" who are just "waiting"
>for the day they can retire and aren't interested in other forums for
>discussing "recent" or "new" techniques and issues

Many that I have met are like that...many are not.  It seems the ones
who are PC literate and into the Internet are not that way.

> - IBM mainframe COBOL programmers get enough "inter-action" with other
>COBOL programmers at work, and don't think they need other venues

That's a good one.  I work from home but I still have interaction with
other Cobol programmers and given that I don't have to support any
database application, I seldom need to ask anyone about Cobol.
Assembler is another animal though.

> - IBM mainframe COBOL programmers *really* want to get into some other
>field/job and see COBOL as a "dead-end"

Never came across any veteran Cobol programmer with that attitude.
I've seen it in college students though.

> - The estimates of how many programmers there really are still doing COBOL
>coding on IBM mainframes is really highly exaggerated.
> - others?
>

I think there might be more than you realize.  But the bulk of the
artilces and postings in CLC are geared more to PC and server
programming.  This is not to say that my fellow mainframers don't know
anything about this, it is just not our area of expertise.

>2) From what I hear, the COBOL (and LE) projects at SHARE aren't
>particularly large/active (but certainly more alive than the now defunct
>GUIDE projects).  What *would* interest IBM mainframe COBOL programmers in
>the way of "presentations," discussions, forums, etc?
>

For me personally, I'd like to see technical type presentations
especially in the use of the LE pieces, e.g., trying to read those
damnable dumps that LE now gives!

>3) How much of the time of IBM mainframe COBOL programmers are spent with
>"mainframe-only/primarily" applications and how much is spent with
>applications that (now) normally have PC-ish "front-ends"?
>

For me, 90% of my time is with mainframe-only/primarily application.  

>4) What do you think can/could be done to "improve" the image (both
>self-image and image from outside) of those doing IBM mainframe COBOL
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well I'm a baby boomer and possibly the only way to improve the image
of those doing IBM mainframe Cobol is to get those that would be our
kids/grandkids out there talking about it and talking it up.  Those in
my age group would be looked at as the "old" way of doing things.

>*OBVIOUSLY* some/most of this applies to other mainframe COBOL programmers,
>but I am particularly interested in those in IBM environments.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>the sender.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Blessed are the flexible for they shall not be bent out of shape.
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL "people"

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2002-05-28T17:23:59+00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<20020528132359.06847.00001357@mb-mq.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<06680E6E14089DD2.68A2B4ECAB5D2206.97C8A5C7450E1996@lp.airnews.net>`

```
Steve Wiegand writes ...

[snip]

>I seldom need to ask anyone about Cobol.
>Assembler is another animal though.

and

[snip]

>For me personally, I'd like to see technical type presentations
>especially in the use of the LE pieces, e.g., trying to read those
>damnable dumps that LE now gives!


forewarned: <ad> coming up

Well, Skippy, does your company offer inhouse training, or pay for you to
attend outslide classes?

These might be of interest to you...

For a solid, in depth intro to Assembler, OS/390 Assembler Language: Classic (5
days)

      http://www.trainersfriend.com/C410descrpt.htm


For traditional Assembler techniques, OS/390 Assembler Interfaces (3 days)

     http://www.trainersfriend.com/C411descrpt.htm


For a quick, intensive update on new Assembler instructions, OS/390 Assembler
Language: Update (1 day)

     http://www.trainersfriend.com/C412descrpt.htm


To get the latest hardware and software Assembler stuff, z/OS Assembler for
Applications Programmers (2 days)

     http://www.trainersfriend.com/C500descrpt.htm


For a solid introduction to using LE services in Assembler and / or COBOL,
Application Development Using LE Services (3 days)

     http://www.trainersfriend.com/m212descr.htm

or, the z/OS version: Using LE Services in z/OS (3 days)

    http://www.trainersfriend.com/m512descr.htm


To  work with COBOL and Assembler mixed applications, Inter-Language
Communication in OS/390 (3 days)

    http://www.trainersfriend.com/m220descr.htm



To use those _wonderful_ LE dumps in debugging COBOL programs, COBOL Debugging
and Maintenance in the LE Environment (2 days)

    http://www.trainersfriend.com/D232descr.htm

</ad>


Just some information.

Kind regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

```
On 28 May 2002 17:23:59 GMT, scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
enlightened us:

>Steve Wiegand writes ...
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…

LOL!  They do offer in house training if you are near the main office
(in Arkansas) or you're on a project that would pay for the travel and
the class.  However, they have no language specific classes.  Tons of
application classes.  You're supposed to know the language you program
in.  As for outside classes, the only ones that they've ever sent me
to were project management classes.  Of course they have an education
reimbursement plan if you wanted to attend college and take courses
related to your work, but it is up to you to initiate that effort.
And they only reimburse you for 80% of the tuition cost.

>These might be of interest to you...
>

I've been programming Assembler since 1976 so these courses would not
help me.  There are times when I need to discuss a technique with a
fellow employee or question why someone else did what they did.  But I
know the language well enough to write a program from start to finish
and not have to look at a book and have it assemble, link and run
after correcting typing errors.

As for the new 64 bit instructions, I'm just starting to get familiar
with them via the IBM doc which does a good enough job explaining
them.  I'm sure, given our on-line applications that have some
assembler pieces we'll be making use of these new instructions in the
near future and thus my familiarity will be heightened.

<<shameless plug snipped :)  >>
>
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
```
