#### ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL "people"

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-29T06:45:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CF47911.189318CE@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<acuje5$s4r$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> (snip)
> 
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>  - others?

In my opinion, most mainframe COBOL applications programmers would
probably find comp.lang.cobol not very helpful.  Many of the issues
discussed, such as programming for gui and OO, or for Unix or PC's,
are not relevant to their assignments.  If they're looking for help on
their own problems they would have to wade through too many postings,
and really useful help might take a day or two.  While I have fairly
unrestricted internet access at work, I do not have access to a news
server, other than google (formerly dejanews), which seems to get
postings somewhat late.  I like reading CLC because it does present
other aspects of the COBOL world, and sometimes I try to contribute to
the discussion.

I also read bit.listserv.ibm-main on usenet (I'm not a subscriber),
but I think it's definitely for system programmers rather than
application programmers. 

I suspect many COBOL programmers aren't terribly interested in new
techniques or issues.  There was a large contingent of programmers in
my shop absolutely terrified to switch from OS/VS COBOL to COBOL II. 
They didn't want to learn new features, and they also tended to
believe that OS/VS COBOL produced more efficient code.  So I would
guess a lot of mainframe COBOL programmers are just serving out their
time.  They want to get the job done and forget about COBOL after 5:00
PM.  They probably don't often have a problem they can't solve on
their own, or solve with the help of someone else in the shop.

I don't get to code as much COBOL as I would like to.  They seem to
want to push me into becoming a project leader or analyst.  I worry
sometimes that COBOL might go the way of the buggy whip and the punch
card, but not very often. 

> 
> 2) From what I hear, the COBOL (and LE) projects at SHARE aren't
> particularly large/active (but certainly more alive than the now defunct
> GUIDE projects).  What *would* interest IBM mainframe COBOL programmers in
> the way of "presentations," discussions, forums, etc?

As was said elsewhere, SHARE seems to be oriented primarily towards
System Programmers.  My shop usually has one or two people who attend
(never me), and they usually bring me some powerpoint presentations on
new COBOL, CICS, or O/S features that might help applications
programmers.

> 
> 3) How much of the time of IBM mainframe COBOL programmers are spent with
> "mainframe-only/primarily" applications and how much is spent with
> applications that (now) normally have PC-ish "front-ends"?

100% of my time is spent on mainframe applications.  PC development
seems to be standardized on Visual Basic here, and that's a transfer
to another department.  The situation for Unix platforms is somewhat
similar, except we have a Unix guru who forbids any COBOL development
on any Unix platform.

Of course, my COBOL applications frequently interface to other
platforms.
  
> 
> 4) What do you think can/could be done to "improve" the image (both
> self-image and image from outside) of those doing IBM mainframe COBOL
> programming?

Well, I'm comfortable with my self-image.  But I am a little cheesed
off at Edsger Wybe Dijkstra (Dutch computer scientist, born 1930 and
presumeably still living), who purportedly said, "The use of COBOL
cripples the mind; its teaching should, therefore, be regarded as a
criminal offense."

I suppose the image of COBOL would be improved if management and
academia were more supportive.  I can think of a couple of anecdotes
that illustrate the bad opinion of COBOL.

Around 1995 or 1996, the Vice-President of a division told all the
COBOL programmers that their skills were worthless, and if they didn't
transfer to a Unix department and start coding client-server programs
they would become unemployable.  About a year later (when Y2K became
an issue) they started paying large "legacy retention bonuses" to keep
the remaining COBOL programmers who still understood the business
applications.  That Vice President retired in 1998.  The retention
bonuses continued until early 2002.

There seems to be a common attitude that nothing useful is done in
COBOL, or at least nothing interesting, that COBOL programmers must be
a bunch of drooling, slack-jawed morons.  There also seems to be an
attitude that mainframes are hideously expensive in terms of hardware,
software, and labor, and that a SUN or Wintel box (or perhaps a
handful networked together) could do the same job at 5% of the
mainframe cost.  

Last year a self-employed web developer asked me what kind of work I
did.  When I said COBOL programming, he expressed surprise that COBOL
was still in use, and asked me what it was good for.  

I suppose the reason COBOL is reviled is because it's really designed
for business accounting.  No academic computer science department is
ever going to get excited about business accounting.

It irritates me that COBOL has this image.  But I fully it expect it
to support me until I retire in ten to fifteen years, just as it has
for the last 23 years.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL "people"

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamDD@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-29T07:40:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CF485D0.8090103@optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<acuje5$s4r$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3CF47911.189318CE@worldnet.att.net>`

```
Arnold Trembley wrote:

> "William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
…[89 more quoted lines elided]…
> criminal offense."


This is a fun quote which says more about Edsger than COBOL, besides it 
was probably written based on COBOL on IBM's 2nd generation equipment, 
including the 14xx series (I learned COBOL on a 1400 in 1966).

Many of the current problems/controversies regarding COBOL derive from 
the earliest standards & compilers, and the techies then were trying to 
mimic Autocoder, e.g., Alter Go To (boo, hiss!). I don't know enough 
software development history to know when fully functional higher level 
languages not built around Going To were developed (PL/I in the IBM 
world?); but it surely wasn't around the time of FORTRAN/COBOL.

My .02 cents anyway (pick your currency).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL "people"

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-29T11:36:36-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<DDB1600CF8008320.A59EBC97492D6A6F.FC43E6956D137C6B@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<acuje5$s4r$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3CF47911.189318CE@worldnet.att.net>`

```
On Wed, 29 May 2002 06:45:25 GMT, Arnold Trembley
<arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> enlightened us:


<<snippage>>

>Well, I'm comfortable with my self-image.  But I am a little cheesed
>off at Edsger Wybe Dijkstra (Dutch computer scientist, born 1930 and
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

He actually wrote that in 1975.  Here's a link to the whole paper he
wrote from which that sentence was taken:

http://www.cs.virginia.edu/~evans/cs655/readings/ewd498.html

He also said:

"FORTRAN --"the infantile disorder"--, by now nearly 20 years old, is
hopelessly inadequate for whatever computer application you have in
mind today: it is now too clumsy, too risky, and too expensive to use.

PL/I --"the fatal disease"-- belongs more to the problem set than to
the solution set. 

It is practically impossible to teach good programming to students
that have had a prior exposure to BASIC: as potential programmers they
are mentally mutilated beyond hope of regeneration."

Of course you need to read the whole paper to understand what he was
trying to say.  Taken out of context, these statements look
inflammatory towards the listed computer languages.  But that was not
his intent.

<<more snippage>>

Regards,

 
          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Blessed are the flexible for they shall not be bent out of shape.
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL "people"

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-30T06:52:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CF5CC2A.FF040E5C@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<acuje5$s4r$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3CF47911.189318CE@worldnet.att.net> <DDB1600CF8008320.A59EBC97492D6A6F.FC43E6956D137C6B@lp.airnews.net>`

```
SkippyPB wrote:
> 
> On Wed, 29 May 2002 06:45:25 GMT, Arnold Trembley
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.cs.virginia.edu/~evans/cs655/readings/ewd498.html

Thank you, SkippyPB, for the link.  It's always nice to get the
complete context of a quote, and I truly appreciate it.

> 
> He also said:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> are mentally mutilated beyond hope of regeneration."

He also said, "APL is a mistake, carried through to perfection.  It is
the language of the future for the programming techniques of the past:
it creates a new generation of coding bums."  So Dijkstra has nothing
good to say about FORTRAN, COBOL, PL/I, BASIC, or APL.  In 1975 "C"
was pretty new.  Was he an assembler bigot?  Or perhaps he only liked
Pascal, or ALGOL, or LISP?

> 
> Of course you need to read the whole paper to understand what he was
> trying to say.  Taken out of context, these statements look
> inflammatory towards the listed computer languages.  But that was not
> his intent.

Well, I think his statements are inflammatory, and I still don't
understand his point.  The article you cited (thanks again, by the
way) is titled "How do we tell truths that might hurt?", but I'm not
convinced of the truth value of several of his statements:

"Programming is one of the most difficult branches of applied
mathematics; the poorer mathematicians had better remain pure
mathematicians."  I do not agree that programming is a branch of
applied mathematics, unless you can also call building a house or
cabinet-making a branch of applied mathematics.  I want the machine to
do useful work, so I write code to make it so.  Why is this now his
province and not mine?  Why does he want to prevent me from using a
programming language which I find productive and profitable?

"The easiest machine applications are the technical/scientific
computations."  Give me an example of a technical/scientific
computation, and I can probably name you another that is quite
difficult.

"The tools we use have a profound (and devious!) influence on our
thinking habits, and, therefore, on our thinking abilities."  Pure
horsefeathers.  Human beings created these tools.  They are absolutely
the products of human thought and imagination.  If we don't like them,
then we should build better tools.

"Many companies that have made themselves dependent on IBM-equipment
(and in doing so have sold their soul to the devil) will collapse
under the sheer weight of the unmastered complexity of their data
processing systems."  You could just as easily substitute
"client/server computing" or "Object-oriented programming" for
"IBM-equipment" and not alter the truth value of this statement one
iota.  It's not the paintbrush it's the artist.  And even in 1975 IBM
had *some* competition.

On the same URL there's some links to a University of Virginia class
on Programming Languages.  At least the class is not committing the
"crime" of teaching COBOL.

And why is it that computer science departments will have a class to
teach students how to write a C compiler in C, but never seem to ask
how programming languages should be designed?

I once worked for a programming supervisor who told me the most
incompetent programmer he ever hired had a BS in computer science.  He
claimed he got much better programming out of data processing students
from the local Junior College.

> (rest snipped)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL "people"

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** klatteross@aol.commmm (Ross Klatte)
- **Date:** 2002-05-30T12:18:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020530081808.14056.00001315@mb-ma.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3CF5CC2A.FF040E5C@worldnet.att.net>`

```
>From: Arnold Trembley arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net 
>Date: 5/30/2002 2:52 AM Eastern Daylight Time

>"Programming is one of the most difficult branches of applied
>mathematics; the poorer mathematicians had better remain pure
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

One sees this linking of programming with mathematics quite often. 
It is incomprehensible to me.  One might as well say that 
computer programming is a branch of pornography.  
If I write a program to calculate the trajectory of a rocket to Pluto, 
the problem is clearly mathematical, but the programming itself
is not.  
If I write a program to identify those netsurfers who enjoy pictures
of girls with farm animals, the problem is clearly pornographic, 
but the programming itself is not.  

In the quotation cited, Dijkstra seems to be addressing an audience
of mathematicians.  In that context, he was quite right.  Math
programming tends to be one-shot programming, and there is
little need for enormous master files to be maintained, and not
much more need for printed reports that can be understood by
idiots.  In such an environment, COBOL would probably be a 
poor choice.  

Still, I have never seen anything in C or Java that could not also
have been done with additions to COBOL, if the owners of
COBOL had seen fit to do so in a timely manner. 


Ross
http://community.webshots.com/user/ross_klatte
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL "people"

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2002-05-30T03:42:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020529234213.20473.00001373@mb-cs.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<acuje5$s4r$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
>From: "William M. Klein" wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com 
>Date: 5/27/02 8:36 PM Eastern Daylight Time

I'm what you'd call a contractor (my firm doesn't like this term and calls us
consultants - take your pick).  I've been at my present contract for 6 1/2
years and still going.  Before this I was an employee at an insurance firm and
a Fortune 100 company, plus I've don operations and data entry.  I've worked on
nothing by IBM systems in all that time.  I have an associates degree and am a
few hours away from a BS in Information Processing (been that way for umteen
years :) ).  Had 12 hrs Cobol in college, 4 hrs Fortran, and 4 hrs RPG (to
paraphrase - no f****ing II or III or IV).  I also have a husband who is a
former mainframe Cobol programmer :).  He does substitute teaching now at the
HS level as he decides what he wants to do when he grows up :).

>1) It seems to me that VERY few IBM mainframe COBOL programmers
>"participate" in the comp.lang.cobol Usenet newsgroup or the IBM-MAIN

Well where I work I'm the only one.  I actually went looking for this newsgroup
because I had a question that no one at work could answer.  I did this because
I had used a newsgroup to get an answer to a question on my CD software and
figured there might be one on Cobol.  I don't think the others I work with
would have had a clue.  I do subscribe to IBM-MAIN and CICS-L which I receive
at work.  I used to get this group at work as well but Deja ended up being
almost 5 days behind and waiting 5 days for an answer wasn't exceptable.  I
wish I could have it at work again (but I can't get newsgroups - I think - have
Netscape w/internet access but don't have a clue how to set it up for
newsgroups).   In any event - I find the 2 lists to be very technical but have
received answers to application questions there with one participant on CICS-L
very patiently walking me through setting up EXCI via private e-mail, which I
never would have had a clue about if not for the list.  Solved a very sticky
problem at work and everyone thinks it's slicker than a greased pig.  Here I
learned about instrinsic functions - something I'm still trying to get some of
the others to try.

Why don't the others participate?  Well they have me :).  And most of them when
they go home don't want to look at a computer except for one or two who don't
do much on a PC but play games or burn CD's.  I too consider myself very PC
illiterate, but I do a few more things with my machine.

Do I consider this a deadend?  Well - I had hit the end of the line except for
management at every place of employment previously, and now I'm pretty much at
the end of the line here unless I develop new skills and that doesn't exactly
include Cobol on PCs.  I do have to say that I am getting to the point while
coding to me is interesting etc I'd like some new adventures.  

Estimates of how many mainframers there are?  Well - there are 9 of us at this
location, there were 60 at the insurance company, and probably 60 and the F100
company (we're talking at least 10 years ago - there are none left at the F100,
and maybe 20 at the insurace company now).  In comparison at my current
location there are probably about 75 Unix developers, 30 Oracle developers, and
I don't know how many other things are being supported.  What's our workload
like? Well today we found out we have 300 programs to analyze, make changes to
if necessary, and test in 3 weeks.  I'll make note here that a lot of this is
due to deplorable coding that there is no time to rewrite (so they say) and a
lack of initiative to do so.  If it ain't broke don't fix it rules the day -
trust me - a lot more is broke than they are aware of.


>2) From what I hear, the COBOL (and LE) projects at SHARE aren't
>particularly large/active (but certainly more alive than the now defunct
>GUIDE projects).  What *would* interest IBM mainframe COBOL programmers in
>the way of "presentations," discussions, forums, etc?

I hadn't heard of SHARE until the various lists.  What I'd really like is a
session on how to use LE capabilities in my coding, and presentations on new
additions to the language - in 'human' terms - I find RTFM nice if I'm looking
for something in particular, but a great cure for insomnia otherwise.  I'd also
like presentations on the use of various ancillary tools like Syncsort (sorry
Frank - but I've never used DFSort) and some CA products.

>3) How much of the time of IBM mainframe COBOL programmers are spent with
>"mainframe-only/primarily" applications and how much is spent with
>applications that (now) normally have PC-ish "front-ends"?

I spend 100% of my time on the mainframe. Nothing I support has a PC front end
in any way shape or form.  There is some Websphere work going on but it hasn't
really hit the big time yet, may not if the mainframe is going away like
they've been threatening for the last 5 years (rumors are really heating up
these last few weeks).

>
>4) What do you think can/could be done to "improve" the image (both
>self-image and image from outside) of those doing IBM mainframe COBOL
>programming?
>

If the programmers working on Cobol on the mainframe were continuously trained
in the language (new capabilities etc), and ancillary tools we'd be able to
prove to the PTB that the mainframe is a viable alternative to various glitzy
packages presented to them to replace the old hulk that's been performing with
aclarity yo these many years.  While a lot of firms offer training and tuition
reimbursement there is very little in-house training that would improve both
the performance and morale of their staff.
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL "people"

- **From:** "Leahy, Don" <zzdlf@tdbank.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-30T12:34:09-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5wsJ8.11701$Ed1.1871362@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<acuje5$s4r$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I'm a contractor, but I tend to spend several years at each client, which
gives me plenty of time to observe the local programming culture.   I'm
presently
working at a large bank, which has about 500 programmers, at least half of
whom work in COBOL.  The half that don't work in COBOL are working on JAVA
front ends to provide Web interfaces to traditional host applications, so it
seems (very) unlikely
that COBOL will be going away.

In this organization, COBOL programmers tend to fall into one of the
following categories:

1. Managers in waiting.  To this group, programming is just a step on the
career ladder.   This is a large group (maybe 25%).

2. Business analysts in training.  This group cares more about the company's
business than about the programs that support it.  This is the largest
group,  and they eventually abandon programming to become full-time business
analysts or end users (about 50%).

3. Clerks in training.  This group does what they're told to do, no more no
less.   A smaller group (about 15%).

4. Technicians.  This group doesn't care what their programs are *for*, they
just like to write them.   A small group (10%)..

Of this list, only the technicians are are likely to subscribe to CLC or
IBM-MAIN.  However,
a large percentage of this group, even though they work with COBOL and can
use it creatively, have bought
into the myth that it is a Creaky Obsolescent Boring Old Language, and
they're far more interested in
learning other things.
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL "people"

- **From:** jraben@cascinc.com (Jeff Raben)
- **Date:** 2002-05-30T17:40:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3cf6614e.18390126@news.bullseyetelecom.net>`
- **References:** `<acuje5$s4r$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>Given some private conversations, I would be interested in some input from
>any of "you" who are actually doing "day-to-day" coding in COBOL on IBM
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>input.)
SNIP SNIP

Assuming that you are not a head-hunter, where are you getting stats
that MainFramers do not follow the new groups?  Every MF'n (it aint
MicroFocus) COBOL programmer I know, at least some of the time are
browsing.

Many questions I see are just a waste of time for the "professional". 
They are often problems solved more years agro than I can count
(that's what computers are for). Some are problems caused by compiler
writers (and the solution is illegal). Some are problems that can be
solved with a little diagnostic skill (not well taught in school) and
test code in 5 minutes.  

That doesn't mean I don't help the lost student via e-mail or posting
here.  It just don't see "neat" code posted very often.

I code MainFrame and PC based COBOL using both for testing and
implementation, sometimes interchangably.  Whatever gets the job done
and implemented in a usable way.

jr

and stir with a Runcible spoon...
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL "people"

- **From:** "Gary Warner" <gwarner@Bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-30T23:22:36-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<TXBJ8.65997$Bo3.5571496@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<acuje5$s4r$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I have been a "Contractor" ever since being laid off (after 12 years) in
1995 and have worked for 7 different clients. I'm 100 Mainframe: Primarily
IMS DB/DC, CICS, DB2, and Batch COBOL of Course. I insist on having
Mainframe Express and Revolve and make good use of Visual Basic (and EXTRA!
Basic), Access & Excel to manipulate data and create macros when I do
actually have to interface with TSO. I don't see any of these clients (State
Agencies, Banks, Lenders, Investment firms) getting rid of Cobol any time
soon. They barely [Have|Spend] the money to maintain their current systems
let alone [rewrite|replace] them. My current client is 45% OS/VS. Doesn't
want to migrate because they are afraid their financial calculations will be
different and they don't want to take time to test it all.

Most of the programmers I've worked with are content to do things the way
they were originally taught - even if that means goto loops and never using
inline performs or evaluates. I doubt if many know that the newsgroups
exist. Nobody has time to read them at work, and who wants to at home (just
that 10% techies per Don Leahy). I generally don't contribute - too many
experts (William, SkippyPB, James, Donald, Howard, YukonMama, etc.) that say
it better.

I don't really care what the worlds' perception of COBOL is... In fact, as
far as I'm concerned it's just fine with me: it will keep the rates high for
many years to come since its not going away and few if any are learning it.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL "people"

- **From:** klatteross@aol.commmm (Ross Klatte)
- **Date:** 2002-05-31T23:06:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020531190647.07078.00000157@mb-bd.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<o8OJ8.33040$ib4.1145810@news1.east.cox.net>`

```
>From: "egildone1" egildone1@cox.net 
>Date: 5/31/2002 1:10 PM Eastern Daylight Time
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>> I can get will be gratefully received. 

>Are there any Unisys mainframe COBOL programmers out there?

You rang?  

Based on the original specs, I hardly qualify--I am neither IBM
nor an FTE.  But this is not the first time I have bent the specs. 

Modern mainframe COBOL programming involves an astounding
amount of committee meetings and forms to be filled out and
gotten approved.  In 19 cases out of 20, the Approving Dignitary does
not have the foggiest idea of what he is approving.  In 9 cases out
of 10, the committee meeting spends 60 minutes conveying 
information that could have been contained in a 3-line E-mail.  
The client-server C/Java programmers live in an exciting world
of change requests by telephone, untested turnovers, and
adrenalin-stimulating 2:00 a.m. pages.  I know Java and SQL
and XML and a bunch of other fly-by-night technologies, but
none of their consumers are willing to pay the ridiculous 
salaries I get for trying to stay awake in code review meetings.

As for the 9-to-5-and-forget-it crowd, my chauvinist opinion is
that many females fall into this category.  (Except Yukon Mama,
of course, who according to the nom-de-net is a female, and who
according to the clc postings is deeply involved.)  


Ross
http://community.webshots.com/user/ross_klatte
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL "people"

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2002-05-31T21:18:53-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ad97dt$h2d$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<acuje5$s4r$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
In article <acuje5$s4r$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>,
William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:
>Given some private conversations, I would be interested in some input from
>any of "you" who are actually doing "day-to-day" coding in COBOL on IBM
>mainframe environments.

That's me!

>I am *particularly* interested in those who are
>employees rather than "contractors" or "consultants" - but any input that I
>can get will be gratefully received. (Please pass this on, if there are
>others you know that you think might provide me with some "real-world"
>input.)

I am a consultant/contractor/hired gun, been that way since... oog... 
1987.  I won't bother you with the Whys and Wherefores of that particular 
choice... well, just one of them: it seems to have been written somewhere 
- Leviticus? - that Thou Shalt Not Pay A Good Programmer More Than A Bad 
Manager.  One *is*, however, permitted to pay one's 
Consultants/Contractors/Hired Guns more than one's Managers... so here I 
am.

[snip]

>1) It seems to me that VERY few IBM mainframe COBOL programmers
>"participate" in the comp.lang.cobol Usenet newsgroup or the IBM-MAIN
>list-server.  Why do you think this is?

I barely know why *I* do things, let alone others... but let me  see...

>I can think of a few possibilities
>(NOT mutually exclusive), but I don't know how realistic they are:
>  - IBM mainframe COBOL programmers want to put in a "day's work" and then
>stop thinking about COBOL

I have run across folks like this... in a 8:am, 10:am rolls around and no 
matter *what* they are doing they then... *stop* and grab a cup of mud and 
talk about the sports scores... never work through lunch and at 5:01pm you 
could rent their cubicles out to immigrant families... and as long as 
these families cleared out by 7:59am they would never know the difference.

No enthusiasm, no fire, no interest... but such personalities are found in 
just about every field I've seen, not just COBOL-jockies.

> - IBM mainframe COBOL programmers are "old-timers" who are just "waiting"
>for the day they can retire and aren't interested in other forums for
>discussing "recent" or "new" techniques and issues

This, to me, is a variation of the above, except one substitutes 'putting 
in a retirement's worth of work' instead of 'putting in a day's worth of 
work'.

> - IBM mainframe COBOL programmers get enough "inter-action" with other
>COBOL programmers at work, and don't think they need other venues

I do not find this to be the case at all... rarely, if ever, do I hear 
coding techniques discussed and when they *do* get discussed it is often 
in dismissive tones... 'maybe one of you bit-heads out there knows about 
this but it's too geeky for *me*...'

This is a difficult one to address without seeming to inflate myself 
but... I was taught, long ago, that it takes a large man to admit he is 
wrong and a wise man to admit the limits of his knowledge.  (See how long 
ago it was?  The teachings were gender-specific!)  For whatever the 
reasons - lack of self-confidence, lack of 'size', etc. - many folks, 
COBOL jockies among them, have difficulties saying those three little 
words of 'I don't know'.

Part of learning more in an undirected (in the sense of no teacher, 
professor or other 'guiding personality') group is saying 'Y'know, that's 
a possibility but I Just Don't Know... let me cobb some code together and 
see what comes out of it.'  To learn something new is to admit to 
ignorance or a previous lack... many folks I have met have difficulty 
doing this in the intimate circles of their loved ones, let alone an 
internationally-transmitted newsgroup.

> - IBM mainframe COBOL programmers *really* want to get into some other
>field/job and see COBOL as a "dead-end"

Even worse... some want to get into Management!

> - The estimates of how many programmers there really are still doing COBOL
>coding on IBM mainframes is really highly exaggerated.
> - others?

It has been my experience that the spark, the drive, the Wanna Do More 
that manifests itsself, at times and in part, in participation in 
newsgroups like this is often not found in full-time employees... whether 
this is because it was not there to begin with or because it was beaten 
out of them - or another reason entire, of course - is beyond me.

>
>2) From what I hear, the COBOL (and LE) projects at SHARE aren't
>particularly large/active (but certainly more alive than the now defunct
>GUIDE projects).  What *would* interest IBM mainframe COBOL programmers in
>the way of "presentations," discussions, forums, etc?

Maybe learning something, *anything* new, outside of the environment in 
which they work?... but never mind, it won't happen.  *Training* - 
lifeblood of a technologically-based organisation - is far too frequently 
seen as a benefit, a perk, and something to be given to Management rather 
than to the people who will actually use it.  Things like SHARE *very* 
rarely get to the apps programmer level.

>
>3) How much of the time of IBM mainframe COBOL programmers are spent with
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>programming?

Try to do without it for a month.

DD
```

---

## Thread: NT question — 5 this month / 7 total

### NT question

- **From:** none@none.com (freewheelin)
- **Date:** 2002-05-28T13:48:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3cf3a1fb.7177872@news.earthlink.net>`

```
Hello folks,  I thought this would be a good place to ask this
question.   

Does anyone know of a "standard" or tool for writing and using batch
files in a production NTenvironment, that provides logical step
tracking during execution, logging, re-starting failed batch files by
step (without editing production code), that supports individual and
nested batch files?

In other words, legacy style job control in a production NT
environment.  Not exactly the same as legacy JCL, but similar,
especially from a support point of view.  

Can I get responses, that if this existed, would developers and
support groups be interested?

Thanks.

JF
```

## Thread: dynamic index — 30 this month / 46 total

### dynamic index

- **From:** "Sim Choon Howe " <schowe@singnet.com.sg>
- **Date:** 2002-05-28T22:18:52+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ad03p7$i2b$1@coco.singnet.com.sg>`

```
Hi
I am doing a program on index file. My index file ha a few record similar to
the one described below

11111
22222
11111
33333

I assigned my file as dynamic. When I read the file for key 11111, it should
read record on 1st and third record which contains key 11111.
when it's invalid, I will put 'n' = no-more record.
thus the calling program will stop it's loop since no-more-record is = to
'n'

But the record keeps reading for few more records before it stops. I am very
sure there is nothing wrong with my code.
I have used the debugger . Was very puzzle, why it did not stop as i would
expect it to stop.

What are the possibilities? Could it be my index file that is causing
problem?

Help is appreciated
```

```

"Sim Choon Howe " <schowe@singnet.com.sg> wrote in message
news:ad03p7$i2b$1@coco.singnet.com.sg...
> Hi
> I am doing a program on index file. My index file ha a few record similar
to
> the one described below
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> I assigned my file as dynamic. When I read the file for key 11111, it
should
> read record on 1st and third record which contains key 11111.
> when it's invalid, I will put 'n' = no-more record.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> But the record keeps reading for few more records before it stops. I am
very
> sure there is nothing wrong with my code.
> I have used the debugger . Was very puzzle, why it did not stop as i would
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> problem?

Could be. Index files REQUIRE the keys be unique. Record #3 CANNOT exist in
your file - it would have been discarded.

There are two completely different READ methods for an index file:

READ MYFILE (which attempts to read the record whose key matches the key you
specified)

and

READ MYFILE NEXT (which gets the next record in the file).

Now if you set a key (MOVE "1111"1 TO MYFILE-KEY)

and READ MYFILE, you'll get record 11111. If your next read is:

READ MYFILE

you'll get the same record again.

Bare in mind that if READ MYFILE fails (the record you want doesn't exist),
then subsequent READ MYFILE NEXT statements make no sense and will probably
fail also.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: dynamic index

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-29T15:46:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kJ6J8.61688$%o.5861078@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<ad03p7$i2b$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <49UI8.52393$%o.5150507@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <ad2i48$q3p$1@coco.singnet.com.sg>`

```

"Sim Choon Howe " <schowe@singnet.com.sg> wrote in message
news:ad2i48$q3p$1@coco.singnet.com.sg...
> I just realised that read and read next are 2 different thing, however I
am
> using the correct syntax.
> From my textbook , their sytax used  is " read (filename) next record"
>
> Read next record means red sequentially?
> Read mean randomly?

Yes.

READ MYFILE NEXT (or READ MYFILE PRIOR/PREVIOUS with some compilers)
   AT END do something
   NOT AT END do something else
   END-READ

Gets the next record sequentially from the current location of the 'record
pointer.'

Whereas:

MOVE data-name TO MYFILE-KEY
READ MYFILE
   INVALID KEY do something
   NOT INVALID KEY do something else
   END-READ

Gets the records specified by data-name.

Note the 'record pointer' gets set with AT END, NOT AT END, NOT INVALID KEY
but is meaningless if the INVALID KEY phrase is invoked during a READ
(random). That is, if READ (random) fails, any subsequent READ NEXT commands
generate junk.

Use of the START command on an index file is left as an exercise for the
reader.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: dynamic index

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2002-05-29T12:03:00-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0205291103.612e81d8@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<ad03p7$i2b$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <49UI8.52393$%o.5150507@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <ad2i48$q3p$1@coco.singnet.com.sg>`

```
Try specifying and using/testing the file status for your indexed
file.  I am surprised you even managed to create an indexed file with
duplicate keys and suspect that you didn't use file status in the
program that you used to create the file, so didn't get any messages
to say that duplicate keys were not allowed.  To use file status,
specify a working-storage data item of two bytes and look up the
syntax for the SELECT statement in the File-Control section of the
Environment Division.  Then for each I/O statement for your file test
the file status, which should normally be zero, except for "10" for
end-of-file, "21" for a sequence error when accessing a sequentially
accessed indexed file, "22" for attempting to write a record with the
same key as one that already exists and "23" for attempting to read a
record randomly for a key that does not exist.  If the file status is
not "00" or "10", then display it with the key of the record being
accessed to give a trace of what is happening.  The way your code
fragments are written, your program would think that it had read
subsequent records, when in fact it had failed to read successfully
and the old record was still sitting in the record-description of your
file.  Your manual should give you a full list of file status codes
and their meanings.


Cheers, Robert

"Sim Choon Howe " <schowe@singnet.com.sg> wrote in message news:<ad2i48$q3p$1@coco.singnet.com.sg>...
> I just realised that read and read next are 2 different thing, however I am
> using the correct syntax.
…[110 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>

- **References:** `<ad03p7$i2b$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <49UI8.52393$%o.5150507@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <ad2i48$q3p$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <fcd09c56.0205291103.612e81d8@posting.google.com>`

```
I have been avoiding adding to this thread, but thought that I better add a
"slight" caveat to what others are saying.

A) duplicate ALTERNATE keys are allowed (just in case the original poster
didn't really tell us exactly what was happening). I know that most of those
replying know about this, but I thought it should be mentioned any way.

B) I *hate* it, but some implementations actually *DO* allow for duplicate
primary keys (as an extension).  The semantics for such files often gets
really UGLY and I doubt that this is ever "intentional" in a new
application, but I did want to make certain that everyone in the group
saying that it "couldn't happen" - knew that in SOME environments, it can.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: dynamic index

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-31T11:20:40-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ad87t5$o3i$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<ad03p7$i2b$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <49UI8.52393$%o.5150507@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <ad2i48$q3p$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <fcd09c56.0205291103.612e81d8@posting.google.com> <ad868c$bc8$1@coco.singnet.com.sg>`

```
According to the current ANSI/ISO Standard, you *must* code either a file
status or a declarative.  (Many/most implementations do not enforce this
rule)

Whether or not your compiler requires this, it is HIGHLY recommended that
you always check the file-status after every I/O statement (including OPEN
and CLOSE).  Alternatively, you may want to look at a FILE declarative -
although from my personal experience, this is less often used.

The "traditional" AT END/INVALID KEY phrases only catch *some* file errors.
Although you probably won't experience many of the other types of problems
in beginning course work, they do exist in "production" environments and not
taking explicit programming action may well result in serious (and sometime
undetected) problems.
```

- **References:** `<ad03p7$i2b$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <49UI8.52393$%o.5150507@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <ad2i48$q3p$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <fcd09c56.0205291103.612e81d8@posting.google.com> <ad868c$bc8$1@coco.singnet.com.sg>`

Sim Choon Howe <schowe@singnet.com.sg> wrote in message
news:ad868c$bc8$1@coco.singnet.com.sg...
> Indeed I did not use file status at all.  Is it a must to use file status
> when dealing with index file?

It's not a "MUST" in that no COBOL compiler will force you to do this, but
checking FILE STATUS on "all" disk I-O (indexed or sequential) is IMNSHO an
important part of writing good program code.

MCM

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: dynamic index

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "JamesJ. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-31T16:55:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CF7AB2B.A1F15E7A@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<ad03p7$i2b$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <49UI8.52393$%o.5150507@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <ad2i48$q3p$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <fcd09c56.0205291103.612e81d8@posting.google.com> <ad868c$bc8$1@coco.singnet.com.sg>`

```


Sim Choon Howe wrote:

> Indeed I did not use file status at all.  Is it a must to use file status
> when dealing with index file?

Using file status is *NOT* a must and as noted in the past there are many
here who don't use it. It's a question of choice. There's an aspect of
programming called XP (eXtreme Programming) - use every facility there is to
test for any possible errors - inevitably Murphy's Law will kick in at some
time. Pete Dashwood summed it up best recently when he made the statement,
"The only code that matters is that which hasn't yet crashed".

I definitely use file status all the time. Write yourself a simple program
doing the following :-

    set NotEOF to true
    perform READ-NEXT-RECORD
    perform until EOF
        display MyRecord
        perform READ-NEXT-RECORD
    End-perform

    STOP RUN.

    READ-NEXT-RECORD.
        Read Myfile next record
            at end set EOF to true
        End-read

    if file-status <> "00"
        display "File Status Error"
    End-if.

Now check what the program is doing - I haven't OPENED the file - now see
what the file-status gives you ! As a newcomer, or even an 'old hand', you
could get as frustrated as hell trying to figure out why it isn't working.
The file-status gives you an immediate clue.

Jimmy, Calgary AB

>
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: dynamic index

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-05-31T20:28:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ad8m40$3qr$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<ad03p7$i2b$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <49UI8.52393$%o.5150507@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <ad2i48$q3p$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <fcd09c56.0205291103.612e81d8@posting.google.com> <ad868c$bc8$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <3CF7AB2B.A1F15E7A@shaw.ca>`

```
I work with a system with called programs within called programs that do
status checks.   The inside program does an OPEN of the VSAM file and checks
the status code.  The status code is '97', which means it opened correctly.
Only the programmers didn't anticipate this and only check for '00'.  The
program passes back a different new error code which means "failure".  Heck,
with that level of detail don't pass anything at all - we will know it
failed when the write aborts!

This called program is used all over the place by the vendor who supplied us
with the system.  And it is exactly the reason why mainframe people tend to
fear OO (While this isn't OO, it does have what OO advocates push as OO's
"advantages").  Mainframe people don't want to change the system because of
extensive testing needed and lots of programs using the interface that need
to be changed.  And the problem goes away - if it aborts and nobody knows
why, restart and it will work correctly IF the abort was caused by a status
code of '97'.

The irritation only costs us a few programmer hours per failure while the
fix would be very expensive getting everybody to sign off on extensive
testing of everything that uses it.  So we live with it and curse the
vendor.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: dynamic index

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-31T15:37:04-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ad8mtt$dq3$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<ad03p7$i2b$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <49UI8.52393$%o.5150507@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <ad2i48$q3p$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <fcd09c56.0205291103.612e81d8@posting.google.com> <ad868c$bc8$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <3CF7AB2B.A1F15E7A@shaw.ca> <ad8m40$3qr$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
It is worth mentioning when/why the way IBM (mainframe) COBOL produces the
"97" file-status code has been a "matter of discussion" among those who care
about ANSI/ISO conformance.

In theory, a "9x" file status code *should* indicate a FAILURE (defined by
the implementor).  However, the IBM "97" is actually treated as a
"successful" status code (with an "anomalous" situation).

In the next Standard (oops - here we go again <G>) there is a new "0x"
category of "implementor defined successful file status code" which would be
a "better" place for the existing IBM "97" processing.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: dynamic index

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "JamesJ. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-31T21:30:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CF7EB87.FEE13DCD@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<ad03p7$i2b$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <49UI8.52393$%o.5150507@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <ad2i48$q3p$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <fcd09c56.0205291103.612e81d8@posting.google.com> <ad868c$bc8$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <3CF7AB2B.A1F15E7A@shaw.ca> <ad8m40$3qr$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```


Howard Brazee wrote:

> I work with a system with called programs within called programs that do
> status checks.   The inside program does an OPEN of the VSAM file and checks
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> vendor.

Where angels fear to tread.... <G>.  So OO only adds to the nightmare ? Put a
file access class between YOU and the rotten Vendor's software.

01 SystemType
    88 IfYouReallyMustUseIBM            value 1.
    88 NormalFolks                                value 2.

set IfYouReallyMustUseIBM to true
invoke OurOwnFileHandler "openFile" using SystemType, Filename
            returning Ls-Result

Class - OurOwnFileHandler
method-id. "openFile"
Linkage section.
01 lnk-SystemType                           pic 9.
    88 IfYouReallyMustUseIBM            value 1.
    88 NormalFolks                                value 2.
01 lnk-FileName                            pic x(100).
01 lnk-Result                                   pic 9(03).
    88 ResultOK                               value 0.
    88 FileError                                 value 99.

Procedure Division using  lnk-SystemType, lnk-Filename returning lnk-Result.

set ResultOK to true
call the vendor's rotten software and get his file-status

Evaluate true
    when file-status = "00"
    when file-status = "97" and IfYouReallyMustUseIBM
            continue
    when other
            set FileError to true
End-evaluate

Before WMK jumps in, you could do the above with a conditional compilation :-

    #if.......
     etc...
    #end-if

or are you salivating, waiting for the latest and greatest compiler ?

*******

Don't think USAF have two seats up front in fighters do they ? Gawd, I'd hate to
be your co-pilot <G>.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: dynamic index

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2002-05-29T19:08:19-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0205291808.14b1ead@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<ad03p7$i2b$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <49UI8.52393$%o.5150507@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <ad2i48$q3p$1@coco.singnet.com.sg>`

```
"Sim Choon Howe " <schowe@singnet.com.sg> wrote in message news:<ad2i48$q3p$1@coco.singnet.com.sg>...
> I just realised that read and read next are 2 different thing, however I am
> using the correct syntax.
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
> END-READ.

If ANY-DATA is a  3-character field, 2000-read-file will set it to 'N 
' at the end of the file.  If ANY-DATA is a 1-character field,
2000-READ-FILE will be performed until ANY-DATA is ' '.

When we ask you to post your real code, we are not kidding around. A
typo in your example makes us waste a lot of time.

Stephen J Spiro
Member, ANSI COBOL Standards Committee
```

- **From:** "Ron" <NoSoy@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-28T21:25:40-05:00

- **References:** `<ad03p7$i2b$1@coco.singnet.com.sg>`

```
You cannot have duplicate primary keys. If you wish to read by the keys you
have shown you must use something else, something unique, as your primary
key and define this key as an alternate key which can have duplicates.

There were times in my early programming days I have also been very sure there
was nothing wrong with my code. Only to discover I was wrong. I am a better
COBOL programmer now after 25 years and when something doesn't work I
always ask someone else to check me when I cannot see it myself. It is very easy
to get "too close" to your own work and miss the error that is staring you in the face.
I am sure if post your code, people would be willing to help you.

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------
"Sim Choon Howe " <schowe@singnet.com.sg> wrote in message news:ad03p7$i2b$1@coco.singnet.com.sg...

Process-Rtn.

   EndIf

   Exit

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<ad03p7$i2b$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <3d7858be.0205282233.ef4eacd@posting.google.com> <ad2i5p$pb3$1@coco.singnet.com.sg>`

"Sim Choon Howe " <schowe@singnet.com.sg> wrote in message
news:ad2i5p$pb3$1@coco.singnet.com.sg...
> Is ther a do whiel command for Cobol?
> should I do perform until?

PERFORM UNTIL (in COBOL) = DO WHILE (in less robust languages)

You can even:

PERFORM WITH NO LIMIT

which is just about forever.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<HyqJ8.77581$Oa1.6496297@bin8.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<ad03p7$i2b$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <3d7858be.0205282233.ef4eacd@posting.google.com> <ad2i5p$pb3$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <gL6J8.67127$Gs.6168744@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3CF5BF7E.9B2B71D8@worldnet.att.net>`

"Arnold Trembley" <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
news:3CF5BF7E.9B2B71D8@worldnet.att.net...
> JerryMouse wrote:
> >
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> possibly some vendor's extension?  Which COBOL compiler allows this?

The Fujitsu compiler allows it, along with the Get-Out-Of-Jail-Free command:

   EXIT PERFORM

So,

PERFORM WITH NO LIMIT
   IF some-condition
      EXIT PERFORM
   END-IF
END-PERFORM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: dynamic index

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamDD@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-31T20:37:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CF7DF12.7000007@optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<ad03p7$i2b$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <3d7858be.0205282233.ef4eacd@posting.google.com> <ad2i5p$pb3$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <gL6J8.67127$Gs.6168744@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3CF5BF7E.9B2B71D8@worldnet.att.net>`

```
Arnold Trembley wrote:

> JerryMouse wrote:
> 
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> But I wouldn't necessarily recommend it.


Hmmm, the above is the fundamental processing of many (most?) batch 
COBOL programs, i.e., PERFORM your mainline logic until you're done. The 
mainline logic is built around reading one or more input files and 
INFINITE-LOOP-DONE = EOF.

Perhaps:

PERFORM PROCESS-TRANLOG-FILES
     UNTIL INFINITE-LOOP-DONE

.
.
.

PROCESS-TRANLOG-FILES.
   Read Tranlog-File
     at end  Set INFINITE-LOOP-DONE to True
     not at end  <process the record, which probably includes any other 		 
TRANLOG-FILEs>
   End-Read
   .



I've written/maintained a LOT like that:-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: dynamic index

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-31T21:40:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<G5SJ8.17863$4i.2084778@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<ad03p7$i2b$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <3d7858be.0205282233.ef4eacd@posting.google.com> <ad2i5p$pb3$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <gL6J8.67127$Gs.6168744@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3CF6A332.1030605@optonline.net> <ad86q3$bi1$1@coco.singnet.com.sg>`

```

"Sim Choon Howe " <schowe@singnet.com.sg> wrote in message
news:ad86q3$bi1$1@coco.singnet.com.sg...
> Oh dear,
> Can i say Do until means  do something until the counter reach a certain
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> isn't that the same?

Well, you can't say "DO" (unless you're writing in Fortran).

>
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Can explain a little bit more on PERFORM WITH TEST AFTER?

PERFORM is a very robust and powerful construct and it has lots of little
knobs and switches.

Originally you had:

1. PERFORM procedure-name (where procedure-name was a paragraph somewhere
else in the program). In that regard, it's similar to a VB function or a
subroutine in other languages.

2. PERFORM procedure-name THRU another-name (where another-name was yet
another paragraph, so everything between the beginning of procedure-name and
the last statement of another-name got executed)

3. PERFORM procedure-name nnnn TIMES (do the procedure-name nnnn times)

4. PERFORM procedure-name UNTIL terminal-condition

5. PERFORM procedure-name VARYING x FROM y BY z UNTIL terminal-condition
(example: PERFORM MYLOOP VARYING MYINDX FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL MYINDX > 100)

In #5, the terminal condition is tested at the beginning of the procedure.
So, if the terminal condition is true when the computer hits the PERFORM
statement, the code pointed to by the PERFORM statement is skipped. If you
always want the code executed at least once, you can add the phrase WITH
TEST AFTER to the PERFORM statement. WITH TEST BEFORE is implied.

6. PERFORM (any of the above constructs without a procedure name), followed
by code to execute, followed by END-PERFORM. This is the so-called "In-Line
Perform" and doesn't require a separate procedure somewhere else. For
example:

PERFORM VARYING INDX FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL INDX > 100
   MOVE ZERO TO MYNUM (INDX)
   END-PERFORM.

or, a more useful example:

READ MYFILE.    *>so-called 'priming read'
PERFORM UNTIL MYFILE-STATUS NOT = '00'
    process record
   READ MYFILE
END-PERFORM.

There's more. Much more.
```

---

## Thread: ACUCOBOL Acuthin Thin Client — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### ACUCOBOL Acuthin Thin Client

- **From:** marc <marc.boucher@cofrafer.fr>
- **Date:** 2002-05-28T16:07:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2002528-17756-303839@foorum.com>`

```

Hi Everybody,

We are using the Acuthin Client of ACUCORP Corporation on our Windows NT 4.0
Workstation in order to connect on an aix Server with UNIX 4.3.3 to launch
a business application.

When we are checking the USER_ID of the acuthin we always have "root" as
a result...we would like to know how we can change that...

Even Acucorp don't really know at the moment so if any people here already
managed to change it, it would be very cool if you can help us or even show
us the way to the victory :)

we are searching for many days now without success... :(

Thanks again !
```

#### ↳ Re: ACUCOBOL Acuthin Thin Client

- **From:** "Dennis Edward" <temp020305@ipipeline.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-28T09:26:45-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bcOI8.1620$H67.9249@tor-nn1.netcom.ca>`
- **References:** `<2002528-17756-303839@foorum.com>`

```
The client is running under windows, so you don't actually have a user-id in
the unix sense. If you are looking for the username (i.e. what you logged
onto windows as), we accomplished that by creating a new c call and
recompiling and linking the runtime (also created one for workstation name).
This was the easiest, but not the best way of doing it. If I had the time to
do it over, I'd do an external cobol subroutine that loads and calls a c
dll - that way you don't have to worry about relinking the runtime every
time you get an upgrade.

On that subject, if anyone has actually written a cobol program to call an
external dll, any sample code would be appreciated - the docs that acucorp
provide are thin at best.

"marc" <marc.boucher@cofrafer.fr> wrote in message
news:2002528-17756-303839@foorum.com...

4.0

```

```
I have only used the thin client experimentally (not in production), so I am
not an expert.

When you say you are checking the "USER_ID", what do you mean?  Where are
you getting that value?

Isn't that controlled by the AcuAccess file?  If I recall, there is an
option when setting up AcuAccess that is "same as client", so the user is
the same as the user on the client.

### Help on Net Express COBOL dll and calling the DLL from VB program

- **From:** aravindbabuv@yahoo.com (aravind)
- **Date:** 2002-05-28T10:44:07-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<be69045e.0205280944.76acdd8e@posting.google.com>`

```
Hello All,
I am trying to create a COBOL DLL using NetExpress
3.1.
I have created the class and added methods and
complied.
Now I am trying to access it from VB as follows.

Private Sub Command2_Click()
Dim g As Integer
Dim p As Integer
g = Val(Text4.Text)
p = Val(Text5.Text)
Dim n As Integer

Dim obj2 As babu6.babu6  ' if this code can be
replaced by dim obj2 as
object
Set obj2 = New babu6.babu6 ' then this code will be
set obj2 =
createobject ("babu6")

Text6.Text = obj2.RetriveTotalPayCheck(g, p)

End Sub

I have used 2 input boxes and 1 output text box.
I pass values g, p to the method RetriveTotalPayCheck.
I am getting 438 Runtime Error. Object does not
support this Method or
Property

Possible reasons :
1. Late Bound applies when we used the code given in
VB comments
2. Currently it is used as early bound.

is there any way to fix the 438 run time error. or is
there any option
i should check or select when creating method. I have
used to create
cobol dll as both OLE Automation and Microsoft
Transaction Server.


Thanks
Aravind
```

#### ↳ Re: Help on Net Express COBOL dll and calling the DLL from VB program

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-28T18:32:58-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ad143a029ef@enews2.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<be69045e.0205280944.76acdd8e@posting.google.com>`

```

"aravind" <aravindbabuv@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:be69045e.0205280944.76acdd8e@posting.google.com...
> Hello All,
> I am trying to create a COBOL DLL using NetExpress
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> Property

> Possible reasons :
> 1. Late Bound applies when we used the code given in
> VB comments
> 2. Currently it is used as early bound.

It's probably something more esoteric than this.

Is your ActiveX library/server properly registered?  Even if you intend to
use late binding, you can reference the component long enough to check it
out with the object browser.  Insure that RetriveTotalPayCheck is truly
available in your component's interface.

(Just a tangential thought -- is the method also mispelled in the component?
If not, it's not surprising that it cannot be found.)
```

<a id="t-3876d9617a"></a>
## Thread: Cobol Errors — 2 msgs

### Cobol Errors

- **From:** jct <jct2k2@hfx.eastlink.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-28T21:15:47-03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CF41DB3.80DB0C77@hfx.eastlink.ca>`

```
I've been coding in Cobol for 12 years now and I've never seen anything
like this...ever.

I have a program which executes fine (Microfocus on SunOS...don't
laugh...not my choice).  When the program ends it returns an RC=0 but
for some reason, SunOS is interpreting it as an RC=2 and this is causing
a script to abnormally abend.  Now mind you, if I take out the error
trapping calls, it goes along fine, but the client standard requires the
use of this funky stuff.  Does anyone have any ideas?  I'm big time
under the gun as it's going into UAT tomorrow and unless I can get this
RC crap figured out, I'm going to be stuck.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol Errors

- **From:** "Ron" <NoSoy@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-28T21:54:57-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ad1fu2$otu$1@nntp-m01.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3CF41DB3.80DB0C77@hfx.eastlink.ca>`

```
I ran into a similar problem once doing ODBC on Windows. Problem was the size
of the return-code itself. ODBC was expecting one size but the program
returned a different size causing the value to be misinterepted. This is
controlled by the "rtncode-size" compiler directive. I copied the doc from
MF below for your convenience. Try changing this directive and see if this helps.
This is the only thing I can think of that might be causing your problem.

Let us know what happens.
Good Luck.

     Ron.


RTNCODE-SIZE

Specifies the size of the RETURN-CODE special register and its alignment in memory.

Parameters:

integer 2 or 4.

Properties:

Default: RTNCODE-SIZE"2"
Phase: Syntax check
Environment: All
$SET: Initial

Dependencies:

Set to RTNCODE-SIZE"4" immediately by XOPEN.


Comments:

The possible values of <integer> are:

2 PIC S9(4) COMP: size 2 bytes, aligned on a two-byte boundary.
4 PIC S9(8) COMP: size 4 bytes, aligned on a four-byte boundary.

If a program with a four-byte RETURN-CODE calls a program with a two-byte RETURN-CODE, on return the value from the
called program is in the lower two bytes of the four-byte RETURN-CODE. The content of the upper two bytes is undefined.

If a program with a two-byte RETURN-CODE calls a program with a four-byte RETURN-CODE, on return the lower two bytes of
the value from the called program are in the two-byte RETURN-CODE. The content of the upper two bytes is lost.


==================================================================



"jct" <jct2k2@hfx.eastlink.ca> wrote in message news:3CF41DB3.80DB0C77@hfx.eastlink.ca...
> I've been coding in Cobol for 12 years now and I've never seen anything
> like this...ever.
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

## Thread: MatSql (J.Gavan) — 2 msgs

### MatSql (J.Gavan)

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-28T22:11:35-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ad1gt702tp3@enews2.newsguy.com>`

```
James,

I've been taking a look at the source code you've referenced in our previous
conversations.

Is MATEDIT.CBL a good place to start?  It looks like it's the handler for
the form pictured in material.bmp. os-App/lnk-App appears to be above this,
but I suspect you've (thankfully) excerpted an illustrative slice of your
application.

Please protest if you feel this discussion becomes an endless line of
questions you've grown tired of.  This interests me greatly, but I have no
deadline relying upon it.  And, as long as you're foolish enough to respond,
I'll probably keep asking questions.

Just a few tidbits for now:

1)  I noticed a CLASS-CONTROL section at the top of this module.  It it
mapping namespace identifiers to their respective source files?

2)  Declarations of objects are made with an "object reference" type.  Is
there object typing available, or must all of these references be made with
a generic object type?

3)  You're explicitly finalizing your objects.  Perhaps it's another
assumption gone bad, but garbage collection is such a good match to
factories, I guessed that this would be in OO-COBOL.  Is this not the case?

4)  What does the replacing bit in "copy "dilgretn.cpy" replacing ==(tag)==
by ==lnk==" do?  Is it some sort of template or macro scheme?

_____________

The code is quite readable (to the extent of my fluency.)  Just being able
to scope your code into methods is evolution enough to declare a new epoch.
I'll keep chewing -- thanks again.
```

#### ↳ Re: MatSql (J.Gavan)

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-29T19:52:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CF5319D.ECD0F38B@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<ad1gt702tp3@enews2.newsguy.com>`

```


Grinder wrote:

> James,
>
> I've been taking a look at the source code you've referenced in our previous
> conversations.
>

Just to recap, because you can now access :-

This takes you to the two LRMs(Language Reference Manuals) :-

http://supportline.microfocus.com/documentation/books/nx31sp1/nx31indx.htm

This takes you to the latest draft of COBOL 2002 - and Appendix E covers OO in
some depth :-

    http://www.incits.org/tc_home/j4.htm

And examples of Procedural COBOL calling OO :-

    http://www.microfocus.com/files/whitepapers/wpautomation.pdf


>
> Is MATEDIT.CBL a good place to start?  It looks like it's the handler for
> the form pictured in material.bmp. os-App/lnk-App appears to be above this,
> but I suspect you've (thankfully) excerpted an illustrative slice of your
> application.

Yes application starts with a 'trigger' program (procedural COBOL), which gets
the MyApplication and MyMenu as objects and passes handles between each.
In procedural COBOL, the menu selection would be :-

    CALL 'MATERIALSEDIT' Using etc...

but in OO I have :-

    INVOKE MATERIALSEDIT using etc....

Note that because OO syntax is 90% plus Procedural COBOL, you can both
CALL(procedural program) and INVOKE(a class) in an OO configured application.
Primarily for consistency of style I invoke classes - but from Pascal, you will
be aware that there are other advantages.

From the menu I could also invoke PrintMaterials etc...., which in turn would
invoke the MaterialsSQL as for Editing.

> Please protest if you feel this discussion becomes an endless line of
> questions you've grown tired of.  This interests me greatly, but I have no
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> mapping namespace identifiers to their respective source files?

Yes - MaterialsEdit is class "matedit" = source maedit.cbl.. Also recall this is
early M/F syntax, and that the COBOL 2002 syntax is slightly different using
REPOSITORY. I can include REPOSITORY in a M/F program if I use the "$set ISO
2000"  DIRECTIVE  in the very first line of the source. (Snide aside - see from
the Directive when it was *thought* COBOL 2002 would be ready !!!!). Doesn't do
the 'forward checking' I previously referred to - just gives me future syntax. I
believe Fujitsu already has REPOSITORY syntax (????)

>
> 2)  Declarations of objects are made with an "object reference" type.  Is
> there object typing available, or must all of these references be made with
> a generic object type?

Yes - an object class/dialog/collection etc. is referenced using an OBJECT
REFERENCE. There are properties in the COBOL 2002 syntax which allow different
features on invoking - but as yet not part of M/F. Just as I'm sure you can do
in Pascal, I can pass the reference/handle for os-ObjectA via Linkage Section to
other classes where it becomes ObjectB, C, D etc... all unique handle references
but all 'pointing' to the one object. Destroy Object C and it destroys the rest.
Back to the duality in COBOL

    01 Flag                    pic 9.
        88 ResultOk        value 0.
        88 RecNotFound value 1.
        88 etc...

either MOVE ZERO TO FLAG or SET RESULTOK TO TRUE

OO does the additional :-

Linkage section.
01 lnk-ObjectA                         object reference.

SET OBJECTB TO LNK-OBJECTA ('receiving' object is FIRST)

>
> 3)  You're explicitly finalizing your objects.  Perhaps it's another
> assumption gone bad, but garbage collection is such a good match to
> factories, I guessed that this would be in OO-COBOL.  Is this not the case?
>

Again early version - J4 have now produced a TR(Technical Report) which can be
added to the Standard. I thought they had finished, but apparently what I saw
was not so - they were discussing this in their current May meeting. One of the
beefs about COBOL - it takes too bloody long to crystallize features - this one
has been going on for over two years.

M/F offers the interim (a) "finalize" a single, (b) 'deepfinalize" a collection
and (c) "deepFinalizeWithDepth" an n-dimensional collection. The purpose of the
J4 paper is to introduce the spec for auto garbage collection.

>
> 4)  What does the replacing bit in "copy "dilgretn.cpy" replacing ==(tag)==
> by ==lnk==" do?  Is it some sort of template or macro scheme?
>

Copyfiles are a big feature of COBOL - I have 150 class source files and 141
accompanying copyfiles. You can get real nifty in COBOL using the syntax COPY
REPLACE. Take the one you have hit on :-

------------------dilgretn.cpy ------------------------------

*> "Dialog return" -
*> Parameters returned from MyDialog - indicating receiving
*> Method Name plus Table Line and Column positions when
*> appropriate

01 (tag)-ReturnValues.
   05 (tag)-Line                    pic 9(03).
   05 (tag)-Column                  pic 9(03).
   05 (tag)-length                  pic x(4) comp-5.
   05 (tag)-text.
      10 occurs 1 to TextLength
         depending on (tag)-length  pic x(01).

----------------------------------------------------------------
"Sending" CLASS - MYDIALOG
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "returnEntryObject".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Local-storage section.
copy "dilgretn.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==ls==.

*> Using "replace etc... - each field prefixed "(tag)" becomes "ls"

01 n                             pic x(4) comp-5.
01 ls-object                     object reference.
01 ls-string                     object reference.

Procedure Division.

perform varying n from 1 by 1 until n > MaxEntryFields

    invoke os-Collection(1) "at"
           using n returning ls-object

    if  os-CurrentObject = ls-object
        invoke os-CurrentObject "getText" returning ls-string
        invoke ls-string "trimblanks" returning ls-string
        invoke os-CurrentObject "Getlength" returning ls-length
        invoke ls-string "getValueWithSize"
             using ls-Length returning ls-text
        invoke ls-string "finalize" returning ls-string

        invoke os-Collection(2) "at"
             using n returning ws-MethodRec
        move ws-Line   to ls-Line
        move ws-Column to ls-Column
        invoke os-Caller ws-MethodName using ls-ReturnValues
        EXIT METHOD
    End-if
End-perform

End Method "returnEntryObject".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------

"Receiving" CLASS : MATEDIT
*>---------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "EF-Stress".
*>---------------------------------------------------------------
Linkage section.
copy "dilgretn.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==lnk==.

*> Using "replace etc... - each field prefixed "(tag)" becomes "lnk"

Procedure Division using lnk-ReturnValues.

*>++++++++++
No reason to do so, but I could move the Lnk-ReturnValues to Global Working
storage :-

    move lnk-ReturnValues to ws-ReturnValues

in which case I would want the following extra line in Workig Storage :-

copy "dilgretn.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==ws==.
*> Using "replace etc... - each field prefixed "(tag)" becomes "ws"

*>++++++++++++

set RecordChanged to true
compute MATERIAL-Stress(lnk-Column) =
        ( function numval (lnk-text) / 10 )
invoke self "passStressField" using lnk-Column

Evaluate true
   when NoStressValues
        add 1 to lnk-Column

        if   lnk-Column < 25
             move lnk-Column to Dialog-column
             compute Dialog-Field = lnk-Column + MaxEntryFields
             invoke os-Dialog "setNextfocusEmpty"
                    using Dialog-Params

        else  set YesStressValues to true
              invoke os-Dialog "setFocusDefault"
        End-if

   when other
        invoke os-Dialog "setFocusDefault"
End-evaluate

End Method "EF-Stress".
*>---------------------------------------------------------------

>
> _____________
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> I'll keep chewing -- thanks again.
```
