<a id="t-e2adc5501f"></a>
## Thread: OLE, Word & NetExpress: changing fonts, bold etc — 4 this month / 6 total

### OLE, Word & NetExpress: changing fonts, bold etc

- **From:** philhickling@compuserve.com (Phil Hickling)
- **Date:** 2002-05-29T06:05:04-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6a371be2.0205290505.6cefa309@posting.google.com>`

```
Can anyone please advise on how to change fonts using OLE calling
Word2000 from MicroFocus NetExpress.

I am able to do many things, such as open a file, add text, mailmerge,
print, paste clipboard, save file, but I cannot find the correct
syntax for changing fonts, size, bold, italics etc.

I am enclosing some code I have got working, in the hope it may be of
help to others.  If anyone wants the full source they are welcome to
email me.

      **** Create an instance of Word AND MAKE IT VISIBLE
           invoke wordapp "new" returning wordServer
           invoke wordServer "SetVisible" using by value 1
      **** Get the Word documents collection object
           invoke wordServer "getDocuments" returning theDocuments
      **** EITHER open existing file OR create new document
      *    invoke theDocuments "Open" using "C:\XX.DOC ".
           invoke theDocuments "add".
      **** INSERT SOME TEXT (EQUIVALENT TO Selection.InsertAfter)
           invoke wordServer "getSelection" returning theSelection
           invoke theSelection "InsertAfter" using Z"BEFORE macros ".
      **** SELECT THE WHOLE DOCUMENT (Selection.WholeStory)
      *    invoke theSelection "WholeStory".
      *    invoke theSelection "InsertAfter" using Z"AT END? ".
      **** GO TO END OF DOCUMENT (Selection.EndKey Unit:=wdStory)
           invoke theSelection "WholeStory".
           invoke theSelection "EndKey".
      **** PASTE A SCREEN PRINT (Selection.Paste)
      *    invoke theSelection "Paste".
      **** PRINT THE CURRENT DOCUMENT (Application.PrintOut)
      *    invoke wordServer "PrintOut".
      **** RUN A MACRO Application.Run
MacroName:="Normal.NewMacros.x4")
           invoke wordServer "Run" using "Macro1". *> it works
      **** INSERT SOME TEXT (EQUIVALENT TO Selection.InsertAfter
           invoke wordServer "getSelection" returning theSelection
           invoke theSelection "InsertAfter" using Z"between macros ".
```

#### ↳ Printing in Net Express

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-29T18:09:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CF5195E.D0F2AAFB@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<6a371be2.0205290505.6cefa309@posting.google.com>`

```


Phil Hickling wrote:

> Can anyone please advise on how to change fonts using OLE calling
> Word2000 from MicroFocus NetExpress.
>

graham wrote:

> We have a MF COBOL package that can produce printed output...currently
> plain and simple text but we have plans to improve this in the not too
> distant future.

This is in response to your separate messages. One other *possibilty* that
may help you both, go take a look at :-

     http://www.microfocus.com/files/whitepapers/wpautomation.pdf

Wayne Rippin's examples spell it out pretty neatly using MS Word as the
OLE. Read through and you will see you need to access from the IDE -->
Tools -->  the Type Library Assistant which shows you what you can invoke
per OLE

In Graham's case he wants to print continuous, so there *might* be a
restriction in the Word software on maximum file size. As a suggestion,
perhaps you can create a fixed page (continuous page) template in Word and
'merge' entries from COBOL ?

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: OLE, Word & NetExpress: changing fonts, bold etc

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-29T15:01:02-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ad3c1v01rkj@enews1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<6a371be2.0205290505.6cefa309@posting.google.com>`

```

"Phil Hickling" <philhickling@compuserve.com> wrote in message
news:6a371be2.0205290505.6cefa309@posting.google.com...
> Can anyone please advise on how to change fonts using OLE calling
> Word2000 from MicroFocus NetExpress.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> syntax for changing fonts, size, bold, italics etc.

I'm not sure of the exact syntax, but here's a speck of VB code that will
open a document and change all of it's text to use "Tahoma"  I've included
the analagous COBOL commands from your code -- hope that helps.

  Dim wordServer As Word.Application

  'invoke wordapp "new" returning wordServer
  Set wordServer = New Word.Application

  'invoke wordServer "SetVisible" using by value 1
  wordServer.Visible = True

  'invoke wordServer "getDocuments" returning theDocuments
  'invoke theDocuments "Open" using "C:\XX.DOC ".
  wordServer.Documents.Open "c:\xx.doc"

  'invoke theSelection "WholeStory".
  wordServer.Selection.WholeStory


  'I'm not sure what this will look like in COBOL, though...
  wordServer.Selection.Font.Name = "Tahoma"

__________________

One suggestion worth making, concerns the Documents.Open and Documents.Add
methods.  These routines return a document reference.  It's worth hanging
onto that reference and applying your command to it, instead of the word
application itself.  This removes the requirement that your document be the
active one in that application.

Translated, the code looks like:

  Dim wordServer As Word.Application
  Dim wordDoc As Word.Document

  'invoke wordapp "new" returning wordServer
  Set wordServer = New Word.Application

  'invoke wordServer "SetVisible" using by value 1
  wordServer.Visible = True

  'invoke wordServer "getDocuments" returning theDocuments
  'invoke theDocuments "Open" using "C:\XX.DOC " returning wordDoc
  Set wordDoc = wordServer.Documents.Open("c:\xx.doc")

  'invoke wordDoc "getRange" returning theRange
  'invoke theRange "WholeStory".
  wordDoc.Range.WholeStory

  'I'm not sure what this will look like in COBOL, though...
  wordDoc.Range.Font.Name = "Tahoma"
```

#### ↳ Re: OLE, Word & NetExpress: changing fonts, bold etc

- **From:** jean.villemaire@microfocus.com (Jean Villemaire)
- **Date:** 2002-05-30T05:35:30-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b9b30ab.0205300435.2cfd6aa0@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<6a371be2.0205290505.6cefa309@posting.google.com>`

```
Phil,

An example demonstrating this can be found at
http://supportline.microfocus.com, then click on Self-Service on the
left and choose Net Express Samples & Utilities and on the page click
Samples.

A demo called wordsetup.zip contains the code that you are looking
for.

Hope this helps.
```

<a id="t-fa9f209222"></a>
## Thread: Page printer adjustments — 2 this month / 4 total

### Page printer adjustments

- **From:** graham@olympic.co.uk (graham)
- **Date:** 2002-05-29T06:16:36-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a02b0a1.0205290516.684fa0c1@posting.google.com>`

```
We have a MF COBOL package that can produce printed output...currently
plain and simple text but we have plans to improve this in the not too
distant future.

At the moment we are looking for a quick improvement to our support of
page printers with pre-printed stationery - and this simply relates to
micro-adjustments to ensure alignment within the pre-printed boxes
etc. Obviously on continuous stationery there can be as much
adjustment, by tractor positioning etc., as the user can be bothered
to make, but that's not the case with page printers. And so we're
looking for a software solution. The output data is simple text
without control characters (so it should print anywhere) and it's easy
to introduce an extra character(s) or line(s) at the left and top of
the page (which we have done), but are there any printer controls that
allow for finer control ?

I've looked through some Epson ESC P and HP PCL specifications but
without success.

There doesn't seem to be an easy route to the full specs. on the web
(although I suppose buying a manual may get more information but I
don't want to do that if the controls we want aren't available!) but
while I have found a lot of information nothing appears to relate to
our requriements.

Ideally some control command to start printer "y" things down from
standard top of page and "x" things in from the left (either globally,
or we could issue this with each data line).

Any suggestions gratefully received.

(BUT, please don't mention PDF, XSLfo etc - I did mention longer-terms
plans, didn't I?)

graham
Olympic Computers Ltd.
```

#### ↳ Re: Page printer adjustments

- **From:** jmindoc@mclaneco.com (John Mindock)
- **Date:** 2002-05-30T14:12:12-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<18bd37b1.0205301312.50cdfaf8@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<8a02b0a1.0205290516.684fa0c1@posting.google.com>`

```
graham@olympic.co.uk (graham) wrote in message > 
> (snipped - he is looking for print solution)> 

There is an IBM product called Advanced Function Presentation (AFP).
Runs on the mainframe and does all the fanciest print things. Search
for 'PPFA' on the IBM site.

John
```

```
Hi,
 I have a problem to move a alphanumeric variable into a numeric
variable. If i move the alphanumeric variable into  a numeric field ,
the last digit of the numric variable become zero. for example....

alphanumeric variable contain value = '7             '     x(10)
numeric variable is 9(10).

after move the alphanumeric into numeric the numeric variable contain
value as
7         0

I have properly initialize the numeric variable. still the zero is
coming.

I got the alphanumeric variable value by doing the UNSTRING operation.

can anyone help me to solve this strange problem.

pls reply back to 
nithyanandan.r@polaris.co.in
or
nithi_rajagopal@rediffmail.com

Thanks in Advance
Nithi
```

```
COJ
```

#### ↳ Re: problem in alphanumeric to numeric move........

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-30T14:17:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<RvqJ8.77235$Gs.7018820@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<fb8f6b1a.0205300509.3885075c@posting.google.com>`

```

"Nithya Nandan Rajagopal" <nithi_rajagopal@rediffmail.com> wrote in message
news:fb8f6b1a.0205300509.3885075c@posting.google.com...
> Hi,
>  I have a problem to move a alphanumeric variable into a numeric
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Sure.

Don't do it.

That is, don't move alphanumeric fields to numeric fields. (What do you
suppose happens if you move "APPLE" to a numeric field?)

There is no primitive command to do what you want. You have to right-justify
the string, fill with leading zeros, and test for numeric before you do the
move. Here's one way:

MOVE FUNCTION REVERSE(MYFIELD) TO REV-FIELD.
INSPECT REV-FIELD REPLACING ALL LEADING ' ' BY '0'.
MOVE FUNCTION REVERSE(REV-FIELD) TO MYFIELD.
IF MYFIELD NOT NUMERIC
    you're screwed
ELSE
   MOVE MYFIELD TO NUMERIC-FIELD.
```

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-05-31T12:45:46+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<fb8f6b1a.0205300509.3885075c@posting.google.com> <RvqJ8.77235$Gs.7018820@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <ad5cmc$ui86f$1@ID-134254.news.dfncis.de> <UBwJ8.10925$4i.1157966@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
JerryMouse wrote:
> 
> If you have X = '123   ' and Y = PIC 9(5).
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 4. Do a relative addressing move? (MOVE X(1:3) TO Y(3:3)

MF has a nice extension since CIS Cobol (1979) where it could de-edit
without bothering with the ANS rules.  eg

      01  Input-Field                    PIC X(12).
      01  Input-Z REDEFINES Input-Field  PIC --------9.99

      01  Input-Value                    PIC S9(8)V99.

          ACCEPT Input-Field
          MOVE Input-Z       TO Input-Value

Cobol/2 needs compiler option DE-EDIT"1" to allow this.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: problem in alphanumeric to numeric move........

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamDD@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2002-05-31T00:30:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CF6C41C.90506@optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<fb8f6b1a.0205300509.3885075c@posting.google.com> <RvqJ8.77235$Gs.7018820@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <ad5cmc$ui86f$1@ID-134254.news.dfncis.de>`

```
COJ wrote:

> It's easy to say...but if this was a bit map in CICS/Cobol and you have all
> screen fields as char for some reason, like multiple input, this is the way
> to do it. We used this at least until mid 90' when I changed job.


I take "bit map" to be "BMS map". There's no reason to define all your 
BMS map fields as character. BMS permits the use of PICIN & PICOUT if 
you're generating a map for a COBOL program and I would urge you to use 
them. Saves a lot of monkey motion editing fields in WS then moving them 
to your output map field.

If "multiple input" means you have a mapset containing more than one 
map, you have to keep track of which one you're using to specify the 
correct mapname in the RECEIVE command.

Many people mistakenly believe all BMS fields must be character and the 
programmer is responsible for all editing, but that's not the case with 
current versions and hasn't been for more than 10 years, or so.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: problem in alphanumeric to numeric move........

```
 > I take "bit map" to be "BMS map".
Yes bit map means BMS map.

There's no reason to define all your
> BMS map fields as character.
No but somebody have decide it should be that way

 > If "multiple input" means you have a mapset containing more than one
> map,

number in the same field for a search but not simultanius of course.

> Many people mistakenly believe all BMS fields must be character and the
> programmer is responsible for all editing, but that's not the case with
> current versions and hasn't been for more than 10 years, or so.
Many applications in CICS/Cobol is far older than that.

COJ
```

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamDD@optonline.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CF70FB2.8040502@optonline.net>`

```
COJ wrote:

>  > I take "bit map" to be "BMS map".
> Yes bit map means BMS map.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> number in the same field for a search but not simultanius of course.


Of course, but this approach is inherently confusing, IMHO.

Not intended as a put down or flame, but when is the last time you 
looked at a CICS Application Programming manual?


>>Many people mistakenly believe all BMS fields must be character and the
>>programmer is responsible for all editing, but that's not the case with
>>current versions and hasn't been for more than 10 years, or so.
>>
> Many applications in CICS/Cobol is far older than that.


Most programmers, too, I started working with CICS in 1972 when we had 
the Standard versions OS & DOS), plus DOS-ENTRY, i.e., CICS lite. 
Macros, of course, and no journaling/recovery. Aye, those were the days:-)

Few applications have never had any maintenance applied (otherwise most 
of us would be looking for work).
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A8335AF2538CBCB.2C47BA7B4936EF2A.D352E41FEEAFAC33@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<fb8f6b1a.0205300509.3885075c@posting.google.com>`

```
On 30 May 2002 06:09:01 -0700, nithi_rajagopal@rediffmail.com (Nithya
Nandan Rajagopal) enlightened us:

>Hi,
> I have a problem to move a alphanumeric variable into a numeric
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>Nithi

Ask here answered here.

You will have to "jump through hoops", i.e. lot of instructions, to
achieve what you want.  A better solution, if possible, look at where
the data is coming from and see if that field can be made right
justified instead of left.  Thus your PIC X(10) field might contain,
 '         7'.  You could then using INSPECT change all spaces to zero
and then move it to a PIC 9(10) field.  Or change the way your
UNSTRING instruction works so that you PIC X(10) field is right
justified.

Regards,


Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Blessed are the flexible for they shall not be bent out of shape.
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

- **Message-ID:** `<4csJ8.10501$t97.1999222@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<fb8f6b1a.0205300509.3885075c@posting.google.com> <3A8335AF2538CBCB.2C47BA7B4936EF2A.D352E41FEEAFAC33@lp.airnews.net>`

```
"SkippyPB" <swiegand@neo.rr.com> wrote in message
news:3A8335AF2538CBCB.2C47BA7B4936EF2A.D352E41FEEAFAC33@lp.airnews.net...
> On 30 May 2002 06:09:01 -0700, nithi_rajagopal@rediffmail.com (Nithya
> Nandan Rajagopal) enlightened us:
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Or you can use the intrinsic function

 COMPUTE CONTRACT-TICKETS = FUNCTION NUMVAL (SAP-CONT-TICKETS).

where CONTRACT-TICKETS is an internal numeric field, and SAP-CONT-TICKETS is
some weird and wonderfull formated data coming from a completely unreliable
source, and defined as an X of field.  Much easier than jumping through
hoops.

Donald.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: problem in alphanumeric to numeric move........

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-30T11:37:45-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ad5kh4$41f$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<fb8f6b1a.0205300509.3885075c@posting.google.com> <3A8335AF2538CBCB.2C47BA7B4936EF2A.D352E41FEEAFAC33@lp.airnews.net> <4csJ8.10501$t97.1999222@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
I agree that Function NumVal may well be the way to go.  HOWEVER, be aware
that (currently - will be fixed in next Standard) there is no way to
validate that the input to this function is "correct".  So if your input was
not
  "7      "
but instead
  "7    x"

"results are unpredictable" - and may result in ABENDs or similar "nasty"
results.  This means that you MUST do a check of input (or somehow KNOW that
your input is correct) before safely using this function.

P.S.  Usual Bill Klein suggestion ...
   Contact your compiler vendor of choice to see when they plan on
implementing the TEST-NUMVAL intrinsic function
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: problem in alphanumeric to numeric move........

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-30T13:06:56-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f4tJ8.8350$eJ5.902896@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<fb8f6b1a.0205300509.3885075c@posting.google.com> <3A8335AF2538CBCB.2C47BA7B4936EF2A.D352E41FEEAFAC33@lp.airnews.net> <4csJ8.10501$t97.1999222@news20.bellglobal.com> <ad5kh4$41f$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:ad5kh4$41f$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net...
> I agree that Function NumVal may well be the way to go.  HOWEVER, be aware
> that (currently - will be fixed in next Standard) there is no way to
> validate that the input to this function is "correct".  So if your input
was
> not
>   "7      "
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> results.  This means that you MUST do a check of input (or somehow KNOW
that
> your input is correct) before safely using this function.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>  wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

I should probably have stated the platform and compiler.  I am using
Fujitsu, which has a fully implimented declaratives.

I would disagree, slightly on your first statement, though.  You imply that
the purpose of the function is to validate input.  Input can never be
gauranteed to be correct.  What can be gauranteed is output.  The purpose of
the function is to guarantee that the result is the best arithmetic
interpetation of the data that can be arrived at, and that the output will
always be valid Cobol numeric, regardless of input.  By definition, it
should be able to handle anything that can be written by a Cobol program,
and come up with the correct answer.  I doubt that it could handle PICTURE
$$$$,$$9.99DB into S9(7)v99, but it should.  Regardless, the receiving field
should end up as correct Cobol data for the picture clause ... data that can
cause no further problems like ABEND.

The real problem with home brew solutions is that they solve the wrong
problem.  The real problem is that you have to be able to gaurantee that
foreign data coming in cannot screw up processing later in the run.

Donald



> "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
> news:4csJ8.10501$t97.1999222@news20.bellglobal.com...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: problem in alphanumeric to numeric move........

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-30T12:41:33-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ad5o8o$ka3$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<fb8f6b1a.0205300509.3885075c@posting.google.com> <3A8335AF2538CBCB.2C47BA7B4936EF2A.D352E41FEEAFAC33@lp.airnews.net> <4csJ8.10501$t97.1999222@news20.bellglobal.com> <ad5kh4$41f$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <f4tJ8.8350$eJ5.902896@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
To clarify:

In the next Standard, each of the NumVal-? functions has a "corresponding"
TEST-NumVal-? function that *does* validate that the input meets the
"requirements" for the function.  (It does NOT return the numeric or
numeric-edited version of the argument - it just returns a "good" or "bad"
indicator.)  Once this function is "tested", it is then GUARANTEED to be
"safe" to use the NumVal-? function.  If you do not SOMEHOW "validate" your
input to any NumVal-? function for meeting the "documented" requirements for
such an argument, then results are (and always will be) "undefined" - but it
is true than an EC-ARGUMENT error condition will be raised - if/when your
compiler supports such.

P.S.  According to the current ('89) Intrinsic Function definition, a value
"created" by a PICTURE $$$$,$$9.99DB field *must* be handled correctly by
NumVal-C.  If your compiler doesn't - then it is "non-conforming"
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: problem in alphanumeric to numeric move........

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-05-30T19:22:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ad5tsh$ntb$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<fb8f6b1a.0205300509.3885075c@posting.google.com> <3A8335AF2538CBCB.2C47BA7B4936EF2A.D352E41FEEAFAC33@lp.airnews.net> <4csJ8.10501$t97.1999222@news20.bellglobal.com> <ad5kh4$41f$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <f4tJ8.8350$eJ5.902896@news20.bellglobal.com> <ad5o8o$ka3$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

On 30-May-2002, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:

> In the next Standard, each of the NumVal-? functions has a "corresponding"
> TEST-NumVal-? function that *does* validate that the input meets the
> "requirements" for the function.

One problem with CoBOL in general is that it wasn't designed with good
trapping mechanism.  Later languages have been better at this - and CoBOL
has tried to correct this fault.  TEST-NumVal type functions will be
appreciated.

I would rather have had ON ERROR or STATUS CODE type checks that can be done
with my commands.  Instead of constantly doing a IF NUMERIC or TEST-Num-Val,
or if valid-date, etc, we do exception checking based upon a particular
statement throwing an error, some efficiency gains would result.

Or I guess we could make every sentence an object...

At any rate, when you can't depend upon the input data - the Num-Val
function really can't be used without this TEST ability.
```

- **References:** `<fb8f6b1a.0205300509.3885075c@posting.google.com> <3A8335AF2538CBCB.2C47BA7B4936EF2A.D352E41FEEAFAC33@lp.airnews.net> <4csJ8.10501$t97.1999222@news20.bellglobal.com> <ad5kh4$41f$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <f4tJ8.8350$eJ5.902896@news20.bellglobal.com> <ad5o8o$ka3$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <ad5tsh$ntb$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
With respect to the next standard, I think VALIDATE would help here, as
would the new Exception Handling mechanism.


"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:ad5tsh$ntb$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> I would rather have had ON ERROR or STATUS CODE type checks that can be
done
> with my commands.  Instead of constantly doing a IF NUMERIC or
TEST-Num-Val,
> or if valid-date, etc, we do exception checking based upon a particular
> statement throwing an error, some efficiency gains would result.

With respect to the next standard, I'd consider the new Exception Handling
mechanism as providing much of this, e.g., USE AFTER EXCEPTION CONDITION
EC-DATA-INCOMPATIBLE, with calls on  EXCEPTION-LOCATION  and
EXCEPTION-STATEMENT in the body.   VALIDATE might also be very useful in
this arena as well.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: problem in alphanumeric to numeric move........

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-30T22:18:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CF6A565.716F29DE@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<fb8f6b1a.0205300509.3885075c@posting.google.com> <3A8335AF2538CBCB.2C47BA7B4936EF2A.D352E41FEEAFAC33@lp.airnews.net> <4csJ8.10501$t97.1999222@news20.bellglobal.com> <ad5kh4$41f$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <f4tJ8.8350$eJ5.902896@news20.bellglobal.com> <ad5o8o$ka3$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <ad5tsh$ntb$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <ad61nu$tj1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```


Chuck Stevens wrote:

> With respect to the next standard, I think VALIDATE would help here, as
> would the new Exception Handling mechanism.
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>     -Chuck Stevens

Ironically,  just about to look at the draft and read up on the above. With
regard to OO, Thane was making the same claim that you are. Using current OO -
without going into the nitty gritty, it appears to be a real pig, that having
trapped errors which may have occurred in a class "two or more steps removed"
from the class which is controlling the Business Logic, it is not *easily* done
to advise the Business Logic that the error occurred, (i.e., the Controlling
Class needs to do 'close-down' housekeeping). Be interesting to see what I
interpret from the draft.

Coupled with a current suggestion from Bill, until I read up, I still remain
doubtful the above works for OO - but probably works very smoothly for
Procedural programs. To illustrate :-

    Procedural
    Perform PARA-A (or Section-A)
    Perform PARA-B (or...
    Perform PARA-C (or...

Each of the above, using the checking you mentioned, could create a critical
condition where you want to get out of the program real quick with either GO TO
or PERFORM EXIT-PARAGRAPH - Bingo - Done !

OO - ??????????? - EXIT PROGRAM  - no such animal !

To try and illustrate the problem simply - assume you have a Dialog Box - user
starts filling in entries or changing them. At any stage he/she can hit the
EXIT Pushbutton. You immediately pass the event/callback to say Method
"PB-Exit" - dead easy, the program(class) is not in the throes of performing
other actions and is being *instructed* to exit - execute your close-down
housekeeping.

***************

OK - update to the above. I've now checked out the draft. Essentially in
'Procedural' code the Exception Handling does three things :-

   ECs for files - extension of checking file access syntax
   ECs for R/W
   ECs for Arithmetic - extension of On Size Error ( a separate feature in
Java)

(Just to ensure that code is non portable I see that the draft text for EC
messages uses my favourite bete noir "implementor defined" !).

Obviously does  more than I've listed above - but that's it essentially. It
triggers Exception Conditions and allows you to use them via Declaratives or
even override them with subsequent code, if non-fatal. But the important thing
is a triggered Declarative Section/Pagaraph can be coded to tell the program to
GO TO or PERFORM EXIT-PARAGRAPH.

I still think OO COBOL has missed the boat !

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: problem in alphanumeric to numeric move........

- **From:** "Ron" <Ron@NoSpamForMe.Com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-30T13:32:20-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ad5r7n$1qb$1@nntp-m01.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<fb8f6b1a.0205300509.3885075c@posting.google.com>`

```
Assuming you know this field is always numeric - the answer is
just UNSTRING it directly into a numeric field and you
will get you want.

Example:
05  input-field pic x(80) value '123|7'
05  A           pic 9(10).
05  B           pic 9(10).

unstring input-field delimited by '|' into A B.

A will contain '0000000123'
B will contain '0000000007'.


------------------------------------------------------------

"Nithya Nandan Rajagopal" <nithi_rajagopal@rediffmail.com> wrote in message
news:fb8f6b1a.0205300509.3885075c@posting.google.com...
> Hi,
>  I have a problem to move a alphanumeric variable into a numeric
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> Nithi
```

#### ↳ Re: problem in alphanumeric to numeric move........

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-30T18:26:29-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ad68bo$ijc$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<fb8f6b1a.0205300509.3885075c@posting.google.com>`

```
The compiler is just being friendly towards its display-numeric receiving
field. Normally (in the IBM world) an "OI" Assembler instruction, using a
X'F0' character is issued after the actual move. This is just compiler
insurance.

Try moving the data, but use reference modification against both the sending
alphanumeric (1:10) and the receiving display-numeric field (1:10).

I believe this should do the trick.

HTH....

Bill

"Nithya Nandan Rajagopal" <nithi_rajagopal@rediffmail.com> wrote in message
news:fb8f6b1a.0205300509.3885075c@posting.google.com...
> Hi,
>  I have a problem to move a alphanumeric variable into a numeric
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> Nithi
```

<a id="t-ebd2e82003"></a>
## Thread: Group level move vs sub-group level move. — 7 msgs

### Group level move vs sub-group level move.

- **From:** nivas_shyam@indiatimes.com (shyam nivas)
- **Date:** 2002-05-30T06:46:07-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c02fd744.0205300546.d335e11@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,
            I am facing editing problem in cobol.In my cobol program I have two 
area in working storage as follows:

01 W-COMPUTE-AREA.
   05 FILLER              PIC X(10).
   05 W-COMPUTE-DATA-1    PIC 9(12)V9(02).
   05 W-COMPUTE-DATA-2    PIC 9(12)V9(02).
   05 W-COMPUTE-DATA-3    PIC 9(12)V9(02).
   05 W-COMPUTE-DATA-4    PIC 9(12)V9(02).
   05 W-COMPUTE-DATA-5    PIC 9(12)V9(02).

01 W-EDIT-AREA.
   05 FILLER              PIC X(10).
   05 W-EDIT-DATA-1       PIC $$$$$$$$$$$9.99.
   05 W-EDIT-DATA-2       PIC $$$$$$$$$$$9.99.
   05 W-EDIT-DATA-3       PIC $$$$$$$$$$$9.99.
   05 W-EDIT-DATA-4       PIC $$$$$$$$$$$9.99.
   05 W-EDIT-DATA-5       PIC $$$$$$$$$$$9.99.
  
      In PROCEDURE DIVISION  if I am moving group level variable 
            (like 
              MOVE W-COMPUTE-AREA TO W-EDIT-AREA.
             )
      I am not getting the field edited in W-EDIT-AREA for example 

 W-COMPUTE-DATA-1 has value 000000000012.05 then 
 W-EDIT-DATA-1    has value 00000000001205

     whereas if I am moving sub-group level variable individually
             ( like 
                   MOVE W-COMPUTE-DATA-1 TO W-EDIT-DATA-1.
                   MOVE W-COMPUTE-DATA-2 TO W-EDIT-DATA-2.
                   MOVE W-COMPUTE-DATA-3 TO W-EDIT-DATA-3.
                   MOVE W-COMPUTE-DATA-4 TO W-EDIT-DATA-4.
                   MOVE W-COMPUTE-DATA-5 TO W-EDIT-DATA-5.
             )
  then I am getting the edited data for example 

 W-COMPUTE-DATA-1 has value 000000000012.05 then 
 W-EDIT-DATA-1    has value          $12.05                  


                 I am not getting why this is happening?
Thanks in advance.

shyam
```

#### ↳ Re: Group level move vs sub-group level move.

- **From:** "COJ" <christer.o.jonsson@home.se>
- **Date:** 2002-05-30T13:58:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ad5b5d$u1sdo$1@ID-134254.news.dfncis.de>`
- **References:** `<c02fd744.0205300546.d335e11@posting.google.com>`

```
I think that it has to do with the fact that a move is either a numeric move
or a alphanumeric one. It's the reciveing field that decide wich and in this
case group level makes it a alpanumeric move and sub level makes it a
numeric move.

COJ
--Sorry for spelling and gramar, being ESL--
"shyam nivas" <nivas_shyam@indiatimes.com> skrev i meddelandet
news:c02fd744.0205300546.d335e11@posting.google.com...
> Hi,
>             I am facing editing problem in cobol.In my cobol program I
have two
> area in working storage as follows:
>
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
> shyam
```

#### ↳ Re: Group level move vs sub-group level move.

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-30T14:07:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1nqJ8.77537$Oa1.6488154@bin8.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<c02fd744.0205300546.d335e11@posting.google.com>`

```

"shyam nivas" <nivas_shyam@indiatimes.com> wrote in message
news:c02fd744.0205300546.d335e11@posting.google.com...
> Hi,
>             I am facing editing problem in cobol.In my cobol program I
have two
> area in working storage as follows:
>
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks in advance.

Group moves are treated as elementary alphnumeric moves. The entire sending
group is copied without change to the receiving group. It matters not what
the PIC definitions of the sending or receiving surbordinate fields contain
(other than the implied length).

In your case, a MOVE W-COMPUTE-AREA TO W-EDIT-AREA proceeds as if you had
defined:

01  W-COMPUTE-AREA  PIC X(80).
01  W-EDIT-AREA  PIC X(85).

Note your receiving field is five bytes bigger than the sending field, so
the move blank-fills the last five bytes.

So, yes, you have to do it the hard way.

Hint: You can define W-EDIT-x as PIC $(11)9.99.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Group level move vs sub-group level move.

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-30T12:07:21-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<E4F88E5E40E2B43E.77AC6A7341DC3E77.FBCB696EF090E8D5@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<c02fd744.0205300546.d335e11@posting.google.com> <1nqJ8.77537$Oa1.6488154@bin8.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
On Thu, 30 May 2002 14:07:57 GMT, "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com>
enlightened us:

>
>"shyam nivas" <nivas_shyam@indiatimes.com> wrote in message
…[65 more quoted lines elided]…
>

To add on to what Jerry has written, here's another way to make the
task a little easier (if this change is allowed):

Change you data descriptions to:

01 W-COMPUTE-AREA.
     05 FILLER              PIC X(10).
     05  W-COMPUTE-DATA-AREA.
           07 W-COMPUTE-DATA-1    PIC 9(12)V9(02).
           07 W-COMPUTE-DATA-2    PIC 9(12)V9(02).
           07 W-COMPUTE-DATA-3    PIC 9(12)V9(02).
           07 W-COMPUTE-DATA-4    PIC 9(12)V9(02).
           07 W-COMPUTE-DATA-5    PIC 9(12)V9(02).
     05  W-COMPUTE-DATA-TBL REDEFINES W-COMPUTE-DATA-AREA
           OCCURS 5 TIMES               PIC 9(12)V99.

01 W-EDIT-AREA.
     05 FILLER              PIC X(10).
     05  W-EDIT-DATA-AREA.
           07 W-EDIT-DATA-1       PIC $$$$$$$$$$$9.99.
           07 W-EDIT-DATA-2       PIC $$$$$$$$$$$9.99.
           07 W-EDIT-DATA-3       PIC $$$$$$$$$$$9.99.
           07 W-EDIT-DATA-4       PIC $$$$$$$$$$$9.99.
           07 W-EDIT-DATA-5       PIC $$$$$$$$$$$9.99.
      05  W-EDIT-DATA-TBL REDEFINES W-EDIT-DATA-AREA
            OCCURS 5 TIMES        PIC $(11)9.99.

Then add somewhere in Working Storage:

01  WS-SUBSCRIPT                 PIC S9999   VALUE +0  COMP.

Then in your procedure division where appropriate you can put the
following inline perform:

PERFORM VARYING WS-SUBSCRIPT FROM +1 BY +1
                   UNTIL WS-SUBSCRIPT GREATER THAN +5
                       MOVE W-COMPUTE-DATA-TBL (WS-SUBSCRIPT) TO
                                   W-EDIT-DATA-TBL (WS-SUBSCRIPT)
END-PERFORM.

Note:  This example assumes mainframe IBM Cobol or compatible
compiler.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Blessed are the flexible for they shall not be bent out of shape.
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Group level move vs sub-group level move.

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-31T13:45:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<G7LJ8.21712$fY4.3643033639@newssvr30.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<c02fd744.0205300546.d335e11@posting.google.com> <1nqJ8.77537$Oa1.6488154@bin8.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <E4F88E5E40E2B43E.77AC6A7341DC3E77.FBCB696EF090E8D5@lp.airnews.net>`

```
"SkippyPB" <swiegand@neo.rr.com> wrote in message
news:E4F88E5E40E2B43E.77AC6A7341DC3E77.FBCB696EF090E8D5@lp.airnews.net...
[much snippage]
> 01 W-EDIT-AREA.
>      05 FILLER              PIC X(10).
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> 01  WS-SUBSCRIPT                 PIC S9999   VALUE +0  COMP.

And one way to define somewhere is right there with the OCCURS, viz:

       05  W-EDIT-DATA-TBL REDEFINES W-EDIT-DATA-AREA
             OCCURS 5 TIMES  INDEXED BY WS-SUBSCRIPT
             PIC $(11)9.99.
```

#### ↳ Re: Group level move vs sub-group level move.

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-30T10:17:47-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VwqJ8.11650$Ed1.1842441@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<c02fd744.0205300546.d335e11@posting.google.com>`

```

"shyam nivas" <nivas_shyam@indiatimes.com> wrote in message
news:c02fd744.0205300546.d335e11@posting.google.com...
> Hi,
>             I am facing editing problem in cobol.In my cobol program I
have two
> area in working storage as follows:
>
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks in advance.


That is what is suppose to happen, so your program is working correctly.

The 01 level data names are not edited fields.  They can be considered as
X(80) fields ... the 80 is the total of the data fields in them.  The group
move is a move from one X(80) field to another. The individual moves are
moves of numeric data to edit fields, and will trigger the data conversion.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Group level move vs sub-group level move.

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>

- **Message-ID:** `<_DrJ8.45429$R_4.9348@nwrddc02.gnilink.net>`
- **References:** `<c02fd744.0205300546.d335e11@posting.google.com> <VwqJ8.11650$Ed1.1842441@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
You may need to rename some variables, but MOVE CORRESPONDING might be
promising....

MCM
```

## Thread: Evaluate readability — 11 this month / 32 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Evaluate readability

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-05-31T13:16:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ad7sq7$kco$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
I recently came across the following code:

 Evaluate printSnapshot Also sendEmail
   When true also true
     move both-snapshots-and-email       to retcode
     move ' both snapshots and email'    to retcode-text
   When true also false
     move snapshots-only                 to retcode
     move ' snapshots only          '    to retcode-text
   When false also true
     move email-only                     to retcode
     move ' email only              '    to retcode-text
   When Other
     move neither-snapshots-nor-email    to retcode
     move ' neither snapshots nor email' to retcode-text
 End-Evaluate

Most programmers I have worked with have a hard time with complex Evaluate
conditions ( with ALSO's).

I found this interesting - Do you like this code or dislike this code?
```

```
:>>   When true also true
:>>     move both-snapshots-and-email       to retcode
:>>     move ' both snapshots and email'    to retcode-text
:>>   When true also false
:>>     move snapshots-only                 to retcode
:>>     move ' snapshots only          '    to retcode-text
:>>   When false also true
:>>     move email-only                     to retcode
:>>     move ' email only              '    to retcode-text
:>>   When Other
:>>     move neither-snapshots-nor-email    to retcode
:>>     move ' neither snapshots nor email' to retcode-text
:>> End-Evaluate

:>>Most programmers I have worked with have a hard time with complex Evaluate
:>>conditions ( with ALSO's).

:>>I found this interesting - Do you like this code or dislike this code?

:>To paraphrase a saying from an old movie, "Frankly my dear, it
:>sucks!".  Can you imagine getting called at 2 am and having to come
:>into the shop and debug that statement?   Please folks..KISS

How would you code this statement?

Are nested IFs that much more understandable?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Evaluate readability

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-31T13:15:07-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ad8ejo$qds$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<ad7sq7$kco$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <11966306B74003A5.5E7FB3612828790A.2EC6442A4EAA6531@lp.airnews.net> <obcffu8qifgda4483c1r01ds3nd7hqc1bm@4ax.com>`

```
Like others, I don't find anything particularly difficult with the original
code.  It *might* be clearer/more self-documenting (from a maintenance
perspective) to code:

Evaluate TRUE Also TRUE
    When printSnapshot also sendEmail
> :>>     move both-snapshots-and-email       to retcode
> :>>     move ' both snapshots and email'    to retcode-text
     When printSnapshot also NOTsendEmail
> :>>     move snapshots-only                 to retcode
> :>>     move ' snapshots only          '    to retcode-text
     When NOT sendEmailalso sendEmail
> :>>     move email-only                     to retcode
> :>>     move ' email only              '    to retcode-text
     When NOT sendEmail also NOT sendEmail
> :>>     move neither-snapshots-nor-email    to retcode
> :>>     move ' neither snapshots nor email' to retcode-text
 End-Evaluate

to *SOME* this may more accurately reflect the underlying "truth table".
(Remember that there is no "requirement" to always code a "WHEN OTHER"
clause - if logically, only one option is left)
```

#### ↳ Re: Evaluate readability

- **From:** "JamesJ. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-05-31T17:43:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CF7B64F.ED003FB7@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<ad7sq7$kco$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```


Howard Brazee wrote:

> I recently came across the following code:
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> I found this interesting - Do you like this code or dislike this code?

Like Steve I don't find the above difficult to follow - and I can't see why it
would give a maintenance programmer the eebie-jeebies at 2am <G>

It's a question of preference. I personally don't like multiple True/False
tests, keeping them simple :-

        invoke os-Collection "isEmpty" returning ls-bool
        if isTrue
            do this...
        else ...
        End-if

Reworking above code slightly, given there are four conditions from a Decision
Table :-

    Evaluate true
        when SnapshotAndEmail
            .....
        when SnapshotOnly
            ........
        when EmailOnly
            .......
        when Other (e.g. 88 = Neither )
            ........
    End-Evaluate

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Evaluate readability

- **From:** "Schroeder" <jfriedman@nc.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-05-31T23:57:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<D5UJ8.22308$lM2.592393@twister.southeast.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<ad7sq7$kco$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Actually, I only think it really poor depending on where it came from.  The
Evaluate verb was (partly) designed to implement  decision tables.  If this
was automatically generated from a table, keeping it in sync with the table
makes a lot of sense.   But since that was probably not the case, I guess I
side with all of you who had something negative to say about it! <g>

Partial-expressions in Evaluate (in the next Standard) is going to help the
evaluate statement a LOT.

Jeff Friedman





"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:ad7sq7$kco$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
> I recently came across the following code:
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> I found this interesting - Do you like this code or dislike this code?
```
